# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any four unique characteristics that distinguish it from a traditional wireless ad-hoc network.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-organizing, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and communicate wirelessly.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Data-Centric Architecture:** WSNs are interested in the *data value* (e.g., "temperature in region A is 50°C"), not in *which node* sent the data. This is opposite to MANETs, which route based on node identity.
2. **Severe Energy Constraints:** Nodes are powered by non-rechargeable batteries with lifetimes of months to years. MANET nodes are typically recharged frequently.
3. **Denser, Mostly Static Deployment:** WSNs deploy hundreds to thousands of nodes in a small area; most are stationary. MANETs are sparser and more mobile.
4. **Many-to-One Traffic Pattern:** Data flows from many sources to a single sink/base station. MANETs use peer-to-peer traffic.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Briefly explain the four major subsystems of a sensor node and identify the subsystem that consumes the maximum energy.*

**Model Answer:**

The four subsystems are:

1. **Sensing Subsystem:** Contains physical sensors and ADC. Converts environmental analog signals to digital data. Typical consumption: **5–10%** of total energy.
2. **Processing Subsystem:** Microcontroller running the protocol stack, performing local computation, and managing duty cycles. Typical consumption: **15–25%** of total energy.
3. **Communication Subsystem:** Radio transceiver (transmit/receive/sleep). Typical consumption: **60–80%** of total energy.
4. **Power Supply Subsystem:** Battery with optional energy harvesting. Provides energy to all other subsystems.

**Maximum energy consumer: Communication Subsystem (the radio).** Therefore, all major WSN protocol optimizations focus on minimizing radio active time, e.g., by aggressive duty cycling and in-network data aggregation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the WSN protocol stack in detail. Compare it with the OSI 7-layer model. What is cross-layer design and why is it used in WSNs?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN with 100 nodes is deployed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Each node has an initial energy of 1 J. The first-order radio model is used with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. The base station is located at a distance of 75 m from the cluster heads. If each node transmits a 2000-bit message per round directly to the base station, compute:
- (i) The crossover distance $d_0$.
- (ii) The energy consumed by a single node per round.
- (iii) The total network energy per round.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The WSN protocol stack has **5 layers**:

| Layer | Function | Example Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| Application | User program | Target tracking |
| Transport | Reliable delivery | PSFQ, RMST |
| Network | Multi-hop routing | LEACH, PEGASIS |
| Data Link | Framing, MAC, error control | S-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 |
| Physical | Modulation, frequency selection | O-QPSK, DSSS |

**Comparison with OSI:** OSI has 7 layers (adds Session and Presentation). WSN omits these because WSNs are application-specific and do not need session management in the traditional sense.

**Cross-Layer Design:** It is a deliberate violation of the strict layering principle where information from one layer is shared with another (e.g., a routing decision at the Network Layer informs the MAC duty cycle at the Data Link Layer). It is used in WSNs because **energy is the primary constraint** and a strictly layered approach wastes energy on redundant processing at each boundary.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute crossover distance $d_0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{7692.31} \\
&\approx 87.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify which model applies:**

Distance to BS = 75 m $< d_0 = 87.7$ m, so use **free-space model** ($n=2$).

**Step 3: Energy consumed by a single node per round:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx} &= k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}) \\
&= 2000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 75^{2}) \\
&= 2000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 5625) \\
&= 2000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 56.25 \times 10^{-9}) \\
&= 2000 \cdot 106.25 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 2.125 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \\
&= 0.2125 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total network energy per round (100 nodes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 100 \cdot 0.2125 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.02125 \text{ J} \\
&= 21.25 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Number of rounds until first node dies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{rounds} &= \frac{E_0}{E_{per-node}} = \frac{1 \text{ J}}{0.2125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}} \\
&\approx 4705 \text{ rounds}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the major design challenges of WSNs? Explain any five in detail, with one real-world example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the energy consumption equation for the first-order radio model. Why is multi-path fading (n=4) used for longer distances? What is the practical significance of the crossover distance $d_0$?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Five Major Design Challenges of WSNs:**

1. **Limited Energy / Battery Power:** Energy is non-rechargeable. *Example:* Nodes deployed in a forest cannot be physically accessed for battery replacement. Energy harvesting is partial.
2. **Scalability:** Protocols must scale to thousands of nodes. *Example:* A precision agriculture deployment may have 5000 nodes across a 100-hectare farm.
3. **Self-Configuration:** Nodes must auto-organize without infrastructure. *Example:* Air-dropped sensors in a disaster zone forming a network without any prior planning.
4. **Harsh Environmental Conditions:** Nodes must survive temperature, humidity, dust. *Example:* Volcano monitoring sensors operating in 300°C+ ambient conditions.
5. **Data Security:** Wireless communication is susceptible to eavesdropping. *Example:* Military battlefield sensors must encrypt data to prevent enemy interception.
6. **Hardware Constraints:** Limited CPU, memory, and radio range. *Example:* MICAz motes have only 4 KB RAM.
7. **Real-Time Constraints:** Some applications (e.g., fire detection) need millisecond response. *Example:* Industrial control loops using WSNs for closed-loop feedback.

#### Model Solution to (b):

The derivation was performed in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key points to highlight in the answer:

**Free-space vs Multi-path:**
- For short distances ($d \leq d_0$), the line-of-sight path dominates, and energy falls off as $d^{2}$.
- For longer distances ($d > d_0$), obstacles and reflections cause destructive interference, and energy falls off as $d^{4}$.

**Practical significance of $d_0$:**
- $d_0$ is the **break-even distance** at which the choice of path loss model changes.
- $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m for the standard parameters ($10$ pJ vs $0.0013$ pJ).
- Network designers use $d_0$ to decide **transmission power levels** and **multi-hop routing decisions**: if a node is more than $d_0$ away, it is more energy-efficient to use a multi-hop route via intermediate nodes than to transmit directly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks in PECAT634:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_{elec}$ units:** $E_{elec}$ is in **J/bit** (or nJ/bit), not J. Mixing up units leads to a calculation error worth **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $E_{elec} \cdot k$ term** in $E_{Tx}$: Always include BOTH the electronics term and the amplifier term. Omitting the electronics term costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong path-loss exponent selection:** Always check whether $d \le d_0$ or $d > d_0$ before applying $n=2$ or $n=4$. Wrong selection is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Not stating units in the final answer:** KTU examiners explicitly require units. "$E_{Tx} = 0.2125$" without "mJ" or "J" loses **0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing MANET and WSN characteristics in definitions:** WSNs are *data-centric* and *energy-constrained*; MANETs are *address-centric* and *mobility-focused**. Mixing these costs **2–3 marks** in definition questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This Module 1 topic is foundational. Before moving to Module 2 (Routing Protocols), ensure you can confidently answer the following rapid-revision checklist:

- **Definition:** A WSN is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of low-power, low-cost, multi-functional sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, process, and communicate data wirelessly.
- **Four Subsystems:** Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power. **Radio is the biggest energy consumer.**
- **Five-Layer Stack:** Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. **Cross-layer design is essential.**
- **First-Order Radio Model:** $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$. Always include **both** terms.
- **Path Loss Exponent:** $n=2$ for free space ($d \le d_0$), $n=4$ for multi-path ($d > d_0$).
- **Crossover Distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$. Typical value is **~87.7 m** for standard parameters.
- **WSN vs MANET:** WSN = data-centric, static, energy-constrained, many-to-one. MANET = address-centric, mobile, peer-to-peer.
- **Standard Constants:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal.
- **Duty Cycle:** Typically 0.1% – 5%. Sleep mode is the key to energy efficiency.
- **Design Challenges:** Energy, Scalability, Self-Configuration, Harsh Environment, Security, Hardware Limits, Real-time.
- **Application Domains:** Environmental, Healthcare, Military, Industrial, Agriculture, Structural Health.
- **Key Metrics:** Network lifetime (FND/HND/LND), Coverage, Latency, PDR, Jitter.
- **Why WSN exists:** To enable *unattended, large-scale, dense, long-duration monitoring* of physical phenomena at a low per-node cost.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers.
- **Always justify** the choice of $n=2$ or $n=4$ by computing $d_0$ explicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Tip for Module 1 (PECAT634):** KTU 2024 scheme questions on this topic fall into three buckets — (1) *Definition & comparison* (3-mark level), (2) *Architecture & subsystem explanation* (7-mark level), and (3) *Numerical energy calculation using the first-order radio model* (7-mark level). Master all three, and you will secure full marks in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Architecture and Protocol Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Network Architecture and Protocol Stack ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Network Architecture of a WSN > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Akyildiz & Vuran, Standard WSN Reference):** > A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) architecture** is a distributed, self-organizing, multi-hop network consisting of spatially dispersed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, motion, or pollutants) and forward their sensed data through wireless links to a central entity known as the **Sink** (or **Base Station / Gateway**) via a layered **protocol stack** that integrates sensing, communication, computation, and energy management. ### 1.2 The Five Canonical Architectural Components According to the KTU 2024 PECAT634 syllabus, a WSN architecture is formally decomposed into the following **five functional components**: | # | Component | Functional Role | |---|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | **Sensor Nodes (Motes)** | Sensing, local processing, wireless communication | | 2 | **Radio / Wireless Link** | Physical medium of communication | | 3 | **Sink / Base Station (BS)** | Data aggregation, gateway to external networks | | 4 | **Internet / Task Manager / End User** | Data consumer, query originator | | 5 | **Protocol Stack** | Coordinated layered communication and management | ### 1.3 Concept Mapping — A Sensor Node's Internal Architecture A single sensor node (mote) is itself an **embedded system** with four classical sub-units, plus the **power unit**: $$\text{Node} = \{S, P, C, T\} \cup \{E_{pwr}\}$$ where each sub-unit serves a distinct purpose: - **Sensing Unit (S):** Comprises *sensors* (analog/digital transducers) and an *Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)*. Converts physical phenomena into digital samples. - **Processing Unit (P):** A *microcontroller* (e.g., MSP430) or *microprocessor* coupled with local *memory* (Flash + RAM). Runs the OS (typically TinyOS / Contiki) and middleware. - **Transceiver Unit (T):** A short-range *radio* (e.g., CC2420 at 2.4 GHz, IEEE 802.15.4) operating in transmit (TX), receive (RX), idle, or sleep modes. - **Power Unit (E_pwr):** Typically a coin-cell or 2× AA battery; energy budget is the *fundamental constraint* of every WSN design decision. ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Postal Network" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Student-Friendly):** > Imagine a **village postal system** without any roads or vehicles: > - Each house has a **letter-writer** (Sensing unit) who observes the weather. > - The house has a **clerk** (Processing unit) who decides *what* to write, *to whom*, and *when*. > - The house has a **short-range walkie-talkie** (Transceiver) — it can only talk to neighboring houses within ~100 m. > - The house has a **limited fuel tank** (Power unit) — once fuel is gone, the house goes silent. > - The letters must be **relay-hopped** (multi-hop forwarding) through neighbors to reach the **Post Office** (Sink/BS), which then uploads the mail to the **internet** (Task Manager). > - There is a strict **chain-of-command protocol** (Protocol Stack) — how to write, when to talk, how to listen, who sleeps when, etc. This analogy is the **mental scaffold** for every WSN design problem. ### 1.5 Operational Lifecycle of a WSN > [!IMPORTANT] > The WSN operates through five recurring phases: > **① Deployment → ② Discovery / Neighbour Identification → ③ Organization / Clustering → ④ Data Sensing & Multi-hop Forwarding → ⑤ Sink Delivery / Sleep Cycling** --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The KTU WSN Protocol Stack (5-Layer + 3-Plane) The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the **cross-layer Akyildiz protocol stack** (often attributed to *Akyildiz, Su, Sankarasubramaniam, & Cayirci, 2002*), which consists of **5 layers** and **3 management planes**: #### 2.1.1 The Five Layers (Top to Bottom) **Layer 5 — Application Layer** - Provides **task-specific services** to the end user. - Applications include: habitat monitoring (e.g., Great Duck Island), industrial control, target tracking, health monitoring, military surveillance. - Protocols: SMP (Sensor Management Protocol), TADAP (Task Assignment and Data Advertisement Protocol), SQF (Sensor Query and Data Dissemination Protocol). **Layer 4 — Transport Layer** - Ensures **reliable end-to-end delivery** of data when the WSN interfaces with external networks (e.g., Internet). - Key requirement: *Reliability* (no packet loss) and *congestion avoidance*. - Protocols: **TCP (legacy for sink-to-Internet)**, **PSFQ (Pump-Slowly, Fetch-Quickly)** for downstream reliability, **ESRT (Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport)** for upstream reliability. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic — "ASTPL":** Application → Sink/Transport → Physical (bottom-up data flow). Reverse "LPTSA" for top-down. **Layer 3 — Network Layer** - The most researched layer in WSNs. - Responsibilities: **routing of sensed data** from source nodes to the sink, typically through **multi-hop paths** since direct long-range transmission is energy-impractical. - Classes of routing protocols (essential for exam): - **Data-centric:** SPIN, Directed Diffusion, Rumor Routing - **Hierarchical / Cluster-based:** LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED, TEEN - **Location-based:** GPSR, GEAR - **QoS-aware:** SAR, SPEED - Also performs **addressing** (often attribute-based, not IP-based) and **data aggregation** to suppress redundant packets. **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer** - Performs **Medium Access Control (MAC)** — arbitrating *who* may transmit *when* on the shared wireless medium, and *Error Control* (FEC/ARQ). - Critical duty: **energy conservation** via duty cycling. - Energy-efficient MAC protocols (favorites in KTU papers): - **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC)** - **T-MAC (Timeout-MAC)** - **B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC)** - **Sift MAC** for event-driven WSNs - **IEEE 802.15.4** (basis of ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread) **Layer 1 — Physical Layer** - Defines **frequency band**, **modulation scheme**, **data rate**, and **transceiver characteristics**. - Common bands: **2.4 GHz (ISM)**, 868/915 MHz, UWB. - Modulation: O-QPSK (IEEE 802.15.4), BPSK, DSSS, CSS. - Key challenges: **path loss**, **fading**, **interference**, **energy budget**. #### 2.1.2 The Three Management Planes (Orthogonal to Layers) | Plane | Layer-Spanning Duty | Why It Matters | |-------|---------------------|----------------| | **Power Management Plane** | Governs *how a node uses its battery* (sleep/wake, TX power, duty cycle) across *all* layers | Extends network lifetime — the **#1 WSN design metric** | | **Mobility Management Plane** | Tracks *node and sink mobility*; updates routes when topology changes | Critical for mobile sinks (e.g., animal tracking, UAV-mounted sinks) | | **Task Management Plane** | Allocates sensing tasks among nodes to balance load; balances energy consumption | Enables **energy-aware task scheduling** | ### 2.2 Why Multi-Hop Communication? The Energy Argument > [!IMPORTANT] > A single-hop long-range transmission from a node at distance $d$ consumes energy proportional to $d^{n}$ (where $n \geq 2$ is the path-loss exponent), whereas $k$ short hops of length $d/k$ consume energy proportional to $k \cdot (d/k)^{n}$. For $n > 2$, multi-hop is **strictly more energy-efficient**. **Optimal hop count (continuous approximation):** $$\boxed{k_{opt} = \sqrt[\,n-1\,]{\dfrac{d^{n}}{(n-1)\,E_{elec}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{amp}}}}$$ But for KTU 2024, the canonical energy formula you must memorize is the **first-order radio model** (Heinzelman): $$E_{TX}(k,d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{0} \quad \text{(free space)} \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d \geq d_{0} \quad \text{(multipath)} \end{cases}$$ $$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$ **Critical cross-over distance:** $$\boxed{d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}}$$ **Energy consumed in amplifying $k$ bits over distance $d$:** $$E_{amp}(k, d) = k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |--------|---------|--------------| | $E_{elec}$ | Per-bit electronics (coding, filtering, spreading) | **50 nJ/bit** | | $\varepsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier coefficient | **10 pJ/bit/m²** | | $\varepsilon_{mp}$ | Multipath-fading amplifier coefficient | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** | | $d_0$ | Crossover distance | typically **~87.7 m** | | $k$ | Packet size (bits) | usually **4000 bits** | | $d$ | Transmission distance (m) | variable | | $n$ | Path-loss exponent (free space: 2, multipath: 4) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** This energy model is used to evaluate **network lifetime**, **hot-spot analysis** (energy hole problem), **cluster-head selection** (LEACH), **transmission power control**, and **optimal route selection** — making it the **single most important formula in WSN** from a KTU valuation standpoint. ### 2.3 Network Topologies WSNs support three principal topologies: 1. **Star (Single-hop):** Every node talks directly to the sink. *Pros:* simple, low latency. *Cons:* energy-inefficient for distant nodes; no scalability. 2. **Mesh / Multi-hop (Peer-to-Peer):** Nodes forward each other's data. *Pros:* self-healing, energy-efficient, scalable. *Cons:* complex routing, more overhead. 3. **Hierarchical (Cluster-based):** Nodes grouped into clusters with **Cluster Heads (CHs)**; CHs aggregate and forward to sink. *Pros:* maximum scalability, energy efficiency. *Cons:* CH selection overhead, hot-spot near sink. ### 2.4 Design Goals vs. Constraints | **Design Goals (Aspirational)** | **Design Constraints (Hard Limits)** | |----------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | Long network lifetime | Limited battery energy | | Self-organization | No fixed infrastructure | | Cooperative sensing | Variable wireless link quality | | Query-based operation | Tiny node size, limited memory/compute | | Scalability (1000s of nodes) | Harsh deployment environments | | Fault tolerance | Mobility (sometimes), security threats | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Energy Dissipated in a Single-Hop Transmission > This derivation is **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Part B 14-mark questions. We will not skip any algebraic step. **Problem Statement:** A source node transmits a $k$-bit packet over a distance $d$ to the sink. Using the first-order radio model, compute the **total energy** dissipated by the source. **Step 1 — Decompose total energy.** The transmitter dissipates energy in two sub-stages: - Running the **radio electronics** (encoding, modulation, filtering) — cost: $k \cdot E_{elec}$ - Running the **power amplifier** to push the radio signal over distance $d$ — cost: $k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ **Step 2 — Formal expression.** $$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$ **Step 3 — Two-channel model.** For $d < d_0$ the channel behaves as **free space** ($n=2$), so $\varepsilon_{amp} = \varepsilon_{fs}$: $$E_{TX}^{fs}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$ For $d \geq d_0$ the channel suffers **multipath fading** ($n=4$), so $\varepsilon_{amp} = \varepsilon_{mp}$: $$E_{TX}^{mp}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$ **Step 4 — Receiver energy.** The receiver only runs electronics (no amplifier); therefore: $$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$ **Step 5 — Round-trip per-hop energy.** The total per-hop energy (one TX + one RX) is therefore: $$E_{hop}(k, d) = E_{TX}(k, d) + E_{RX}(k) = 2k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical illustration (KTU-favored).** Take $k = 4000$ bits, $d = 100$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. First, the crossover distance: $$d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{0.0013}} \approx \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$$ Since $d = 100$ m $> d_0$, we use the **multipath** formula: $$E_{TX}^{mp} = 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 4000 \cdot (0.0013 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (100)^{4}$$ $$= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 4000 \cdot 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8}$$ $$= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 4000 \cdot 1.3 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 10^{8}$$ $$= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.2 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\boxed{E_{TX}^{mp} = 2.52 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.252 \text{ mJ}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Hop vs. Single-Hop Energy Comparison **Setup:** A node at distance $D$ from the sink chooses between (a) one direct hop and (b) $k$ equal hops of length $D/k$. **Step 1 — Single-hop energy (multipath, $n=4$):** $$E_{single} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4}$$ **Step 2 — Multi-hop energy (each hop of length $D/k$):** Per hop: $k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot (D/k)^{4}$ Total over $k$ hops (plus $k$ receptions): $$E_{multi} = k \cdot \bigl[k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot (D/k)^{4}\bigr] + k \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** $$E_{multi} = 2k^{2} E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k^{2} \cdot \frac{D^{4}}{k^{4}} = 2k^{2} E_{elec} + \frac{\varepsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{k^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Compare.** $$E_{multi} < E_{single} \iff 2k^{2} E_{elec} + \frac{\varepsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{k^{2}} < k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} D^{4}$$ This inequality is satisfied for sufficiently large $k$ when $n > 2$, confirming **multi-hop is energy-superior** in the multipath regime. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Stack Cross-Reference | Layer | KTU-Favorite Protocol | Energy-Saving Mechanism | Layer-Specific Metric | |-------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------| | Application | SMP, SQF | In-network query processing | Task completion time | | Transport | PSFQ, ESRT | Rate-controlled reliability | Loss ratio, latency | | Network | LEACH, SPIN, Directed Diffusion | Data aggregation, cluster rotation | Hop count, PDR | | Data Link (MAC) | S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC | Duty cycling, sleep modes | Duty cycle %, throughput | | Physical | IEEE 802.15.4 (O-QPSK) | Adaptive TX power, O-QPSK | BER, RSSI, range | ### 3.4 Network Lifetime Definitions (Kernels for KTU Problems) | Lifetime Metric | Definition | |-----------------|-----------| | **FND** (First Node Dies) | Time until the first node's battery exhausts | | **HNA** (Half Nodes Alive) | Time until 50% of nodes die | | **LND** (Last Node Dies) | Time until the entire network is dead | | **Network Lifetime (operational)** | Time until WSN can no longer satisfy coverage/connectivity KPI | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level WSN Architecture (End-to-End System View) ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Sensor Field - Cluster 1] --> A2[Cluster Head CH1] A3[Sensor Field - Cluster 2] --> A4[Cluster Head CH2] A5[Sensor Field - Cluster 3] --> A6[Cluster Head CH3] A2 -->|Multi-hop Wireless| A4 A4 -->|Multi-hop Wireless| A6 A6 -->|Single-hop| BS[Base Station / Sink Gateway] BS -->|Internet Backhaul| INT[Internet / Cloud Server] INT --> TM[Task Manager / End User GUI] TM -->|Query Injection| BS BS -->|Query Dissemination| A2 BS -->|Query Dissemination| A4 BS -->|Query Dissemination| A6 ``` ### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single Sensor Node ```mermaid flowchart LR PSU[Power Unit - Battery] -->|DC supply| SUNIT PSU -->|DC supply| PUNIT PSU -->|DC supply| TUNIT SUNIT[Sensing Unit - Sensor + ADC] -->|Digital Samples| PUNIT PUNIT[Processing Unit - Microcontroller + Memory] -->|Encoded Bits| TUNIT TUNIT[Transceiver Unit - Radio TX RX] -->|Radio Waves over air| AIR((Wireless Channel)) AIR -->|Incoming Radio Waves| TUNIT PUNIT -.->|Control Plane - Sleep Wake| TUNIT PUNIT -.->|Sampling Rate Control| SUNIT ``` ### 4.3 WSN Protocol Stack with Management Planes (Cross-Layer View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph layers[Protocol Stack Layers] L5[Layer 5 - Application Layer
SMP SQF TADAP] L4[Layer 4 - Transport Layer
TCP PSFQ ESRT] L3[Layer 3 - Network Layer
LEACH SPIN Directed Diffusion] L2[Layer 2 - Data Link Layer
S-MAC T-MAC IEEE 802.15.4] L1[Layer 1 - Physical Layer
O-QPSK DSSS 2.4 GHz ISM] end subgraph planes[Management Planes - Span all layers] P1[Power Management Plane
Sleep Wake Duty Cycle] P2[Mobility Management Plane
Route Updates Location] P3[Task Management Plane
Task Allocation Energy Balance] end P1 -.- L1 P1 -.- L2 P1 -.- L3 P1 -.- L4 P1 -.- L5 P2 -.- L1 P2 -.- L2 P2 -.- L3 P2 -.- L4 P2 -.- L5 P3 -.- L1 P3 -.- L2 P3 -.- L3 P3 -.- L4 P3 -.- L5 ``` ### 4.4 Topology Comparison — Star vs. Mesh vs. Hierarchical ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph star[Star Topology] S1[Node 1] -->|Direct| SB[BS] S2[Node 2] -->|Direct| SB S3[Node 3] -->|Direct| SB S4[Node 4] -->|Direct| SB end subgraph mesh[Mesh Topology] M1[Node 1] <-->|Multi-hop| M2[Node 2] M2 <-->|Multi-hop| M3[Node 3] M3 <-->|Multi-hop| M4[Node 4] M4 <-->|Multi-hop| M1 M1 -.->|Any path| MSB[BS] end subgraph hier[Hierarchical Topology] C1[Node a] --> CH1A[CH1] C2[Node b] --> CH1A CH1A -->|Aggregated| CH2[CH2] C3[Node c] --> CH2 C4[Node d] --> CH2 CH2 -->|Aggregated| HBS[BS] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Block Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `CH1A`, `HBS`) and label text is plain uppercase — fully compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards in the protocol specification. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **List and briefly explain the five layers of the WSN protocol stack. Why is the transport layer often considered optional in single-sink WSNs?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The WSN protocol stack consists of **five layers**: 1. **Application Layer** — Provides task-specific services (e.g., habitat monitoring, target tracking) and supports application-level protocols such as SMP, SQF, and TADAP. 2. **Transport Layer** — Ensures reliable end-to-end data delivery, handles congestion control, and is essential when the WSN interfaces with external IP networks. Uses protocols like **PSFQ** and **ESRT**. 3. **Network Layer** — Performs routing of sensed data from source nodes to the sink through **multi-hop** paths; implements data-centric, hierarchical, or location-based routing (e.g., LEACH, SPIN). 4. **Data Link Layer** — Provides **Medium Access Control (MAC)** and error control; key energy-efficient MACs include S-MAC, T-MAC, and B-MAC. 5. **Physical Layer** — Defines the **radio frequency band (e.g., 2.4 GHz ISM)**, modulation (e.g., O-QPSK), encoding, and bit-rate. The transport layer is considered **optional** in a single-sink WSN where all traffic flows *upstream* to one destination — congestion and reliability can be handled by MAC and network layers, and TCP overhead is unnecessary for low-rate sensor traffic. It becomes mandatory when the WSN interconnects to the Internet. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Explain the three management planes in the WSN protocol stack. How do they differ from the five layers?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three management planes are **orthogonal** to the five layers and span *across* all of them: 1. **Power Management Plane** — Manages how a node uses its limited energy. It controls sleep/wake cycles, TX power level, and duty cycle, interacting with physical, MAC, and routing layers. It is the **#1 design concern** in WSNs. 2. **Mobility Management Plane** — Detects and registers node movement; updates routes and neighbour tables when topology changes. Essential for mobile sinks (e.g., UAV-mounted gateways) and mobile sensor scenarios (animal tracking). 3. **Task Management Plane** — Allocates sensing tasks among nodes to balance the energy load across the network, avoiding premature death of any single node. **Key Difference:** The **5 layers** are *vertical*, governing the *flow of data* from the physical medium up to the application. The **3 planes** are *horizontal*, governing *how resources and tasks* are managed at every layer. Together they form a **cross-layer architecture** that distinguishes WSNs from traditional layered network stacks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B, 14 marks each) --- #### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network. List and describe the responsibilities of each major component (sensor node, sink, gateway, and task manager).* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition and WSN Architecture Diagram.** (3 marks) A Wireless Sensor Network is a self-organizing, multi-hop, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively sense physical phenomena and forward data to a central **Sink (Base Station)**. The architecture consists of four principal components (drawn below): ``` ┌──────────┐ │ Task │ (End-user query/data consumer) │ Manager │ └─────┬────┘ │ Internet / external network ┌─────▼────┐ │ Sink / │ (Base Station / Gateway) │ BS │ └─────┬────┘ │ Multi-hop wireless (downstream query, upstream data) ┌──────┴──────┐ │ Sensor │ Cluster 1 Cluster 2 Cluster 3 │ Field │ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ │ │ │N1│ │N3│ │N5│ │ │ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ │ │ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ │ │ │N2│ │N4│ │N6│ │ │ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └─────────────┘ ``` **Step 2 — Component Responsibilities.** (4 marks) - **Sensor Nodes (Motes):** *Sense* physical phenomena, *locally process* the data (data aggregation, compression), and *wirelessly transmit* it. Each node has four sub-units: sensing, processing, transceiver, and power. - **Sink / Base Station (BS):** Acts as the *data concentrator* and *gateway* between the WSN field and the external world. It receives all upstream traffic, may perform further aggregation, and forwards to the Internet. - **Gateway:** Often synonymous with the BS in single-sink deployments; performs *protocol translation* between the WSN's link-layer protocols and the Internet's TCP/IP stack. - **Task Manager / End User:** The *consumer* of the data and the *originator* of queries. It issues high-level tasks (e.g., "report all temperatures > 30 °C in zone 2") and visualizes results. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** *Derive an expression for the total energy consumed in transmitting a 4000-bit packet from a source node to a sink at 100 m, using the first-order radio model with the multipath-fading channel. Use $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. Comment on why multi-hop transmission is preferred in WSNs.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Energy equation for multipath channel** (1 mark) $$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values** (2 marks) $$E_{TX} = 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 4000 \cdot (0.0013 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (100)^{4}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate term by term** (2 marks) Electronics term: $4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J Amplifier term: $4000 \cdot 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8} = 4.0 \times 10^{3} \cdot 1.3 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 10^{8} = 5.2 \times 10^{-5}$ J **Step 4 — Final answer** (1 mark) $$\boxed{E_{TX} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.2 \times 10^{-5} = 2.52 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.252 \text{ mJ}}$$ **Step 5 — Why multi-hop is preferred** (1 mark) In the multipath regime, transmit energy scales as $d^4$. A direct 100 m transmission costs $d^4 = 10^8$. If we instead transmit in 4 hops of 25 m each, the amplifier cost per hop drops to $25^4 = 3.9 \times 10^5$, yielding a total amplifier cost of $4 \times 3.9 \times 10^5 = 1.56 \times 10^6$ — roughly **64× less**. Hence multi-hop is energy-superior in WSNs. --- #### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** *Explain the WSN protocol stack with all five layers and three management planes. Discuss the role of the MAC sublayer in detail, citing two energy-efficient MAC protocols.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — The 5 Layers (3 marks)** | Layer | Function | Example Protocol | |-------|----------|------------------| | Application | Task-specific services to user | SMP, SQF | | Transport | Reliability, congestion control | PSFQ, ESRT | | Network | Multi-hop routing, data aggregation | LEACH, SPIN | | Data Link | MAC + error control | S-MAC, T-MAC | | Physical | Radio, modulation, frequency | IEEE 802.15.4, O-QPSK | **Step 2 — The 3 Management Planes (2 marks)** - **Power Management Plane** — Sleep/wake scheduling; energy harvesting. - **Mobility Management Plane** — Detects node movement; updates routes. - **Task Management Plane** — Balances sensing load and energy consumption. **Step 3 — Role of MAC Sublayer (1 mark)** The MAC sublayer is the **energy-critical** sublayer of the data link layer. Radio idle listening is one of the largest sources of energy waste. The MAC sublayer minimizes idle listening via **duty cycling** — periodically putting the radio into sleep mode. **Step 4 — Two Energy-Efficient MAC Protocols (1 mark)** - **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC):** Uses synchronized sleep/wake schedules among neighbouring nodes. Nodes form virtual clusters that share a common sleep schedule. Reduces idle listening at the cost of latency. - **B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC):** Uses Clear Channel Assessment (CCA) and Low Power Listening (LPL). Each node wakes briefly to sample the channel; if no preamble is detected, it returns to sleep. Achieves very low duty cycle. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** *With suitable diagrams, compare and contrast the three WSN topologies: star, mesh, and hierarchical. Identify the topology that best suits a 1000-node deployment in a large agricultural field and justify your answer.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Star Topology (1.5 marks)** Every node transmits directly to the sink. **Pros:** simplicity, low latency, no routing overhead. **Cons:** distant nodes deplete their battery very fast; not scalable beyond ~50 nodes; complete failure of the link kills that node. **Step 2 — Mesh Topology (1.5 marks)** Every node forwards for every other node; multiple paths exist between any source-destination pair. **Pros:** self-healing, fault-tolerant, high reliability. **Cons:** routing complexity, redundant transmissions can waste energy; suffers from hidden-terminal problem. **Step 3 — Hierarchical (Cluster-based) Topology (1.5 marks)** Nodes are grouped into clusters; each cluster elects a Cluster Head (CH). Member nodes send to CH; CHs aggregate and forward to sink. **Pros:** highly scalable, energy-efficient through aggregation and CH rotation. **Cons:** CH selection overhead; CHs near the sink deplete faster (the "hot-spot" problem). **Step 4 — Choice for 1000-node Agricultural Deployment (1.5 marks)** **Hierarchical topology** is the best fit. Justifications: - **Scalability:** With 1000 nodes, flat (mesh) routing tables become infeasible; clustering groups nodes into ~25–50 clusters. - **Energy efficiency:** Cluster heads (LEACH) rotate, balancing load across all 1000 nodes — critical when batteries cannot be replaced mid-season. - **Data aggregation:** Agricultural data is highly redundant (similar temperature/humidity in adjacent plots); aggregating at CHs reduces 1000s of packets to a handful. - **Coverage:** Cluster boundaries naturally map to field sub-zones, simplifying task allocation (e.g., "irrigate cluster 5"). A *single-tier* star is rejected because 1000 nodes cannot all be within radio range of a single sink. A *pure mesh* is rejected due to routing overhead and uneven energy drain. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on WSN Architecture questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the three management planes** — Many students draw only the 5 layers and ignore the orthogonal Power / Mobility / Task planes. KTU rubric explicitly allocates marks for these. > 2. **Mixing up the path-loss exponent** — Using $n=2$ (free space) instead of $n=4$ (multipath) for distances $> d_0$ results in an answer that is **off by orders of magnitude**. > 3. **Unit inconsistency in energy calculations** — $E_{elec}$ is in nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{mp}$ in pJ/bit/m⁴. Failing to convert to joules before adding causes a **silent error of $10^9$**. > 4. **Confusing "Sink" with "Gateway"** — The Sink is the WSN-side data collector; the Gateway bridges to the Internet. Sometimes they are co-located, sometimes not. State the assumption. > 5. **Skipping the crossover distance $d_0$ check** — Always compute $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ first to decide which channel model applies. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **WSN Architecture** has 5 functional components: sensor nodes, radio link, sink, gateway/task manager, and protocol stack. - ✅ **Sensor node** = Sensing + Processing + Transceiver + Power units. - ✅ **Protocol Stack = 5 Layers + 3 Management Planes** (Akyildiz model) — this is the **#1 most-asked topic** in KTU 2024 PECAT634. - ✅ **5 Layers (top → bottom):** Application, Transport, Network, Data Link (MAC), Physical. - ✅ **3 Management Planes (orthogonal):** Power, Mobility, Task. - ✅ **Multi-hop is energy-superior** for $n > 2$ — direct long-distance transmission is a critical design antipattern. - ✅ **First-order radio model formulas** — must be memorized: $E_{TX} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon d^n$, $E_{RX} = kE_{elec}$, $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$. - ✅ **Topologies:** Star (simple, short-range), Mesh (robust, complex), Hierarchical (scalable, energy-efficient — best for large WSNs). - ✅ **MAC is the energy-critical sublayer** — duty cycling, S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 are KTU favorites. - ✅ **Network lifetime metrics:** FND, HNA, LND — FND is most commonly used in KTU problems. - ✅ **Architecture is application-driven:** habitat monitoring ≠ military tracking ≠ industrial control — each picks a different protocol and topology. ---

topics.title: MAC Access Control - Fundamental MAC Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** MAC Access Control - Fundamental MAC Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medium Access Control (MAC) Protocol** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) is a sub-layer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2 of the OSI / IEEE 802.15.4 model) that governs how multiple sensor nodes share the limited, error-prone, and broadcast-nature wireless communication channel without destructive interference.

In the context of WSNs, the MAC layer is not merely about *who transmits when* — it is the **primary energy governor** of the entire network. Because radio communication is the single largest source of energy drain (often accounting for **70% – 80%** of total node energy consumption), the MAC protocol directly determines the **lifetime, scalability, and reliability** of the sensor deployment.

The **fundamental objective** of a WSN MAC protocol can be formally stated as:
> To maximize **network lifetime** by minimizing **energy consumption** while maintaining acceptable **latency, throughput, and fairness** across the sensor field.

## 1.2 Why WSN MAC is Fundamentally Different from Traditional MAC

| Traditional Wireless MAC (Wi-Fi, Cellular) | WSN MAC |
|---|---|
| Designed for high data rate, continuous traffic | Designed for low data rate, bursty/event-driven traffic |
| Energy is abundant (mains powered or rechargeable) | Energy is **scarce and non-rechargeable** (battery powered) |
| Optimized for throughput & QoS | Optimized for **energy efficiency & longevity** |
| Mobility is a key concern | Nodes are largely **stationary** after deployment |
| Nodes are addressable and identifiable | Nodes are **resource-constrained** (CPU, RAM, Radio) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Town Hall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Town Hall Meeting with One Microphone**
>
> Imagine **200 villagers (sensor nodes)** sitting in a large town hall, but there is only **one microphone (the shared wireless channel)**. If everyone speaks simultaneously, the message becomes unintelligible (a **collision**). 
>
> A **MAC protocol** is the **moderator's rulebook** that decides:
> - *Who* gets the mic and *when* (channel access)
> - *How long* they may speak (frame length)
> - Whether they should listen before speaking (carrier sensing)
> - Whether the microphone should be turned *off* when no one is speaking (duty cycling)
>
> In a village powered by a single candle (battery), the moderator's most important job is to **turn the mic OFF** whenever possible — because keeping it open wastes energy. This "smart switching off" is the soul of every WSN MAC protocol.

## 1.4 The Four Classical Sources of Energy Waste in WSN MAC

Every fundamental MAC protocol in WSN literature attempts to combat the same four energy wastage causes identified by Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (2002):

1. **Idle Listening** — Radio ON while no packet is being sent. *The single largest energy drain in low-traffic WSNs.*
2. **Collision** — Two nodes transmit simultaneously, packets corrupted, retransmission required.
3. **Overhearing** — A node receives packets destined for other neighbours.
4. **Control Packet Overhead** — Energy spent transmitting/receiving RTS/CTS/ACK/SYNC signalling.

A fifth, less-discussed but important source is:
5. **Overemitting** — Transmission begins while the receiver's radio is not yet awake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Any answer on "Fundamental MAC Protocols" MUST begin by identifying which of the above five energy wastage mechanisms the protocol is designed to mitigate. Examiners specifically look for this classification in 7-mark and 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Taxonomy of Fundamental WSN MAC Protocols

Every classical WSN MAC protocol can be classified into one of three broad families. The taxonomy is the **single most important diagram** you will draw in KTU Module 1.

### A. Contention-Based (Random Access) Protocols
Nodes compete for the channel using CSMA/CA-style rules. Simple, scalable, but prone to collision and idle listening.
- **Examples:** S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, WiseMAC, X-MAC, Sift MAC, R-MAC, RL-MAC

### B. Schedule-Based (TDMA / FDMA / CDMA) Protocols
Channel time/frequency/code is pre-allocated or negotiated. Collision-free but requires tight synchronization.
- **Examples:** L-MAC, BMA, TRAMA, FLAMA, DMAC, DEANA, LEACH (cluster-scheduled)

### C. Hybrid (Contention + Schedule) Protocols
Combine the flexibility of contention with the determinism of scheduling.
- **Examples:** Z-MAC, Funneling-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 (slotted CSMA/CA + GTS)

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of the Core Fundamental Protocols

### 2.2.1 S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) — Ye, Heidemann & Estrin, 2002
The **foundational paper** of WSN MAC and a compulsory KTU topic.

**Core Design Idea:** Nodes form **virtual clusters** based on common **sleep/wake schedules**, achieving synchronized periodic duty cycling.

**Key Mechanisms:**
- Periodic **Listen + Sleep** cycle (Listen interval: typically 10% of frame time).
- **SYNC packets** exchanged between neighbours to maintain schedule alignment.
- **CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS** during the Listen window.
- **Overhearing avoidance** via NAV (Network Allocation Vector) tracking.
- **Message passing** — long messages fragmented and sent as burst in a single duty cycle.

**Advantages:** Simple, energy efficient, good for uniform periodic traffic.
**Drawbacks:** Fixed duty cycle wastes energy under variable load; sleep latency high; synchronization overhead.

### 2.2.2 T-MAC (Timeout-MAC) — van Dam & Langendoen, 2003
**Direct evolution of S-MAC.**

**Core Idea:** Instead of a *fixed* listen interval, the node **stops listening early** if no activation event (data, RTS) occurs for a duration **TA** (Time Active).
- If **TA > (C + R + T)** where C = contention time, R = RTS transmission, T = turnaround time, the node sleeps immediately.
- This **adaptive early-sleeping** saves energy under variable load.

**Drawback:** The famous **"early sleeping problem"** — a node may sleep before hearing an RTS intended for it, reducing throughput under multi-hop traffic. Solutions proposed: **D-MAC, DBP, R-MAC, RL-MAC**.

### 2.2.3 B-MAC (Berkeley MAC) — Polastre, Hill & Culler, 2004
**One of the most cited "clean-slate" WSN MAC designs.**

**Core Idea:** Drops SYNC/RTS/CTS, replaces them with **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** + **preamble sampling (long preamble)**.
- Sender transmits a long preamble (≥ full sleep interval).
- Receiver wakes periodically, samples channel, detects preamble, stays awake to receive.
- Uses **LPL (Low Power Listening)** for ultra-low idle current (~few µA).

**Advantages:** Highly energy efficient under very low traffic; no synchronization required; simple.
**Drawbacks:** Long preamble is energy-expensive for *sender*; high latency; overhearing of preamble.

### 2.2.4 WiseMAC — El-Hoiydi & Decotignie, 2004
**Optimizes B-MAC by making preamble length adaptive.**
- Each node learns the **wake-up schedule of its neighbours** through overheard ACKs.
- Preamble is shortened to just *cover the receiver's next wake-up + clock drift*.
- Achieves **1–2% duty cycle** under typical loads.

### 2.2.5 Sift MAC — Jamieson, Patel & Kermode, 2003
**Designed for event-driven WSNs where N nodes may all detect an event simultaneously.**
- Uses **slotted CSMA** with a **non-uniform, geometrically increasing** transmission probability.
- If *r* of *N* nodes have a packet, the protocol is designed so that the **first successful transmission occurs within a few slots** regardless of *r*.
- Optimal for spatial contention correlation (e.g., fire detection in a region).

### 2.2.6 L-MAC (Lightweight MAC) — van Hoesel & Havinga, 2004
**Schedule-based, TDMA-style.**
- Time divided into **fixed slots**; each node owns a unique slot.
- Node transmits in its slot, sleeps in all others.
- Distributed **slot election** via random backoff to resolve conflicts.
- Very low duty cycle; collision-free; no SYNC/RTS/CTS.
- Drawback: Slot assignment does not scale well to mobile networks.

### 2.2.7 DMAC — Lu, Krishnamachari & Raghavendra, 2004
**Designed for data-gathering trees (convergecast).**
- Schedule is staggered so a node wakes when its child has data to forward — forming an **"active path"** up the tree.
- Eliminates the "data forwarding interruption" problem of S-MAC.
- Uses **More-To-Send (MTS) flag** for data prediction.

### 2.2.8 Z-MAC (Zebra MAC) — Rhee, Warrier, Min & Xu, 2005
- Uses TDMA slot assignment as a *base*, but allows **CSMA contention within a slot** under low contention (called "local framing").
- Switches behaviour dynamically — collision-free under high load, flexible under low load.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **mandatory quantitative backbone** for any 7-mark or 14-mark numerical in KTU Module 1. Memorize both formula and units.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Node Battery Lifetime** | $T_{life} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | $E_{bat}$ = total battery energy, $P_{total}$ = avg power | seconds / days |
| 2 | **Total Average Power** | $P_{total} = P_{tx} + P_{rx} + P_{idle} + P_{sleep}$ | Power spent in each radio state | mW |
| 3 | **Duty Cycle (DC)** | $DC = \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{frame}}$ | $T_{listen}$ = active window, $T_{frame}$ = full cycle | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | **S-MAC Energy per Frame** | $E_{frame} = P_{listen} \cdot T_{listen} + P_{sleep} \cdot (T_{frame}-T_{listen})$ | Sleep/listen radio power | Joules |
| 5 | **Energy per Bit (B-MAC)** | $E_{bit} = \frac{P_{preamble} \cdot T_{preamble} + P_{tx} \cdot T_{data}}{L}$ | $L$ = packet size in bits | Joules/bit |
| 6 | **WiseMAC Preamble Length** | $T_{p} = T_{w} + 4 \cdot \theta \cdot T_{interval}$ | $T_{w}$ = wake-up sampling, $\theta$ = clock drift, $T_{interval}$ = listen interval | seconds |
| 7 | **T-MAC Active Timeout** | $TA = C + R + T$ | $C$=contention, $R$=RTS time, $T$=turnaround | seconds |
| 8 | **Sift Success Probability (slot $i$)** | $p_i = \frac{(1-\alpha)\alpha^{N-i}}{1-\alpha^N}$, $0<\alpha<1$ | $\alpha$ = distribution parameter, $N$ = slots | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Idle Listening Penalty** | $E_{idle} = P_{rx} \cdot T_{frame} \cdot (1-DC)$ | Energy wasted if DC = 0 | Joules |
| 10 | **Throughput (S-MAC)** | $S = \frac{L \cdot N_{succ}}{T_{frame}}$ | $N_{succ}$ = successful frames/cycle | bits/sec |
| 11 | **L-MAC Slot Fraction** | $N_{slots} = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{slot}}$ | Each node owns 1 slot | slots |
| 12 | **Clock Drift Bound (WiseMAC)** | $\vert \Delta f_{drift} \vert \leq \frac{T_{p} - T_{w}}{2 \cdot T_{interval}}$ | Required oscillator accuracy | ppm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Notice the **absolute value expressions** like $\vert \Delta f_{drift} \vert$ above. In KTU answer sheets, use `\vert ... \vert` (single-line vertical bars) in LaTeX — **never** write `|x|` inside a markdown table cell or the cell may break. We will use this convention throughout the derivation section.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **S-MAC →** Realistic indoor environmental monitoring (HVAC, smart building sensors).
- **T-MAC →** Variable-traffic animal habitat monitoring (ZebraNet).
- **B-MAC →** TinyOS default MAC — used in **MicaZ, TelosB motes** for industrial research.
- **WiseMAC →** Adopted in **IEEE 802.15.4 / WirelessHART** precursor designs.
- **L-MAC →** Industrial WSN deployments needing deterministic latency.
- **Sift →** Critical-event detection: **forest fire alarms, gas leak detection, structural collapse**.
- **Z-MAC →** Hybrid industrial mesh networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: S-MAC Duty Cycle Energy Model

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark part-a style):**
A WSN node uses S-MAC with frame time $T_{frame} = 500$ ms, listen interval $T_{listen} = 50$ ms. Radio parameters: $P_{rx} = 30$ mW, $P_{sleep} = 5$ μW. Compute the average power consumption and the fraction of energy saved vs always-on mode.

### Step 1 — Define the Duty Cycle

The **duty cycle (DC)** is the fraction of the frame period the radio is active.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DC &= \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{frame}} \\
DC &= \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{500 \text{ ms}} \\
DC &= 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The radio is awake for 10% of every cycle. This is the typical "10% S-MAC" operating point from Ye's original paper.

### Step 2 — Compute the Sleep Duration

The sleep interval is the remainder of the frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sleep} &= T_{frame} - T_{listen} \\
T_{sleep} &= 500 \text{ ms} - 50 \text{ ms} \\
T_{sleep} &= 450 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The radio must be ON for 50 ms and OFF for 450 ms each cycle.

### Step 3 — Write the Average Power Equation

For S-MAC, the average power $P_{avg}$ is the **time-weighted sum** of listen power and sleep power:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{(P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen}) + (P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep})}{T_{frame}}
$$

*Logic:* Total energy in one frame divided by the frame duration gives the long-term average power.

### Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{(30 \text{ mW} \cdot 50 \text{ ms}) + (5 \, \mu\text{W} \cdot 450 \text{ ms})}{500 \text{ ms}} \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{1500 \, \mu\text{J} + 2.25 \, \mu\text{J}}{500 \text{ ms}} \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{1502.25 \, \mu\text{J}}{500 \text{ ms}} \\
P_{avg} &\approx 3.0045 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $30 \text{ mW} \cdot 50 \text{ ms} = 1500 \text{ mW·ms} = 1500 \, \mu\text{J}$ since $1 \text{ mW·ms} = 1 \, \mu\text{J}$. The sleep power term is negligible (4 orders of magnitude smaller) but included for rigour.

### Step 5 — Compare with Always-On Power

If the node never sleeps, $P_{always} = P_{rx} = 30$ mW. The **energy saving factor** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{avg}}{P_{always}} \\
\eta &= \frac{3.0045 \text{ mW}}{30 \text{ mW}} \\
\eta &\approx 0.10015
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The node now consumes ~10% of the always-on power, confirming that the duty cycle is the dominant energy governor.

### Step 6 — Compute the Lifetime Multiplier

For a 2 × AA battery pack, $E_{bat} \approx 2880$ J. The lifetime is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life}^{always} &= \frac{2880 \text{ J}}{30 \text{ mW}} = \frac{2880}{0.030} \text{ s} = 96000 \text{ s} \approx 26.67 \text{ hr} \\
T_{life}^{SMAC} &= \frac{2880 \text{ J}}{3.0045 \text{ mW}} = \frac{2880}{0.0030045} \text{ s} \approx 958765 \text{ s} \approx 11.1 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** S-MAC extends lifetime by a factor of **~10×** with a 10% duty cycle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: WiseMAC Minimum Preamble Length

**Problem Setup:**
Receiver samples channel every $T_{interval} = 500$ ms. Clock drift $\theta = 30$ ppm. CCA sampling duration $T_{w} = 5$ ms. Compute the minimum preamble length that guarantees detection.

### Step 1 — State the Design Equation

WiseMAC's preamble must cover:
- The receiver's next wake-up moment
- Plus a guard band equal to **twice the maximum clock drift** accumulated since the last synchronization.

$$
T_{p} = T_{w} + 2 \cdot \theta \cdot T_{interval}
$$

*Logic:* The factor 2 appears because both sender and receiver clocks can drift in opposite directions, doubling the worst-case uncertainty.

### Step 2 — Substitute Drift in Fractional Form

For $\theta = 30 \text{ ppm} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p} &= 5 \text{ ms} + 2 \cdot (30 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 500 \text{ ms} \\
T_{p} &= 5 \text{ ms} + 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ms} \\
T_{p} &= 5 \text{ ms} + 0.030 \text{ ms} \\
T_{p} &= 5.030 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The drift contribution is small (30 μs) but accumulates over long listen intervals. For $T_{interval} = 1$ hr, the same drift would add 216 ms.

### Step 3 — Compare with B-MAC

In B-MAC, preamble length equals the full listen interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p}^{BMAC} &= T_{interval} = 500 \text{ ms} \\
T_{p}^{WiseMAC} &= 5.030 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Reduction factor} &= \frac{T_{p}^{WiseMAC}}{T_{p}^{BMAC}} = \frac{5.030}{500} \approx 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* WiseMAC cuts the preamble to **~1%** of B-MAC's — a ~100× reduction in sender energy and channel occupancy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sift MAC Transmission Probability Distribution

**Problem Setup:**
N = 8 slots in a contention window, N = 16 potential contenders. Find the transmission probability in slot $i$ that maximizes the probability that **exactly one node** picks slot $i$.

### Step 1 — Sift's Non-Uniform Probability Function

In Sift MAC, node *k* picks slot *i* with probability:

$$
p_{i,k} = \frac{(1-\alpha) \cdot \alpha^{N-i}}{1 - \alpha^{N}}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1
$$

*Logic:* As $i \to 1$ (first slot), the exponent becomes smaller, making the probability **highest in the first slot** — encouraging early transmission. This is the geometrically biased "winner-takes-all-early" effect.

### Step 2 — Probability that Slot i Receives Exactly One Transmission

$$
P_{1}(i) = N \cdot p_i \cdot (1-p_i)^{N-1}
$$

*Logic:* Out of N contenders, choose 1 to transmit in slot $i$ (N ways) and the remaining N-1 to *not* pick slot $i$.

### Step 3 — Maximization

For a given N, the optimal $\alpha$ is selected to maximize the probability of **at least one success in the first few slots** for any $r \le N$ (where $r$ = number of active nodes). In Jamieson's original paper, for N=16, the optimal choice is $\alpha \approx 0.55$ to $0.65$, giving the slot-1 transmission probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{(1-0.6) \cdot 0.6^{15}}{1 - 0.6^{16}} \\
p_1 &= \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.00047018}{1 - 0.00028211} \\
p_1 &= \frac{0.00018807}{0.99972} \\
p_1 &\approx 0.000188
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The probability per node is small, but with N=16 contenders, the **expected number of transmissions in slot 1** is $N \cdot p_1 \approx 0.003$ — intentionally *low* — because if no node picks slot 1, the geometric distribution heavily favours early slots for the remaining contenders in the next round (after the first failed slot, contention restarts with a fresh window).

### Step 4 — Real-World Interpretation

Sift is designed so that whether **1 node** or **100 nodes** are trying to report an event, the **first reporter wins within a small, bounded number of slots**. This is critical in **event-driven WSNs** (e.g., fire detection) where hundreds of sensors may fire simultaneously.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Must-Memorize for KTU 14-Mark)

| Property | S-MAC | T-MAC | B-MAC | WiseMAC | Sift | L-MAC | Z-MAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Access Type | Contention | Contention | Contention | Contention | Contention | Schedule | Hybrid |
| Synchronization | Required | Required | Not required | Not required | Required (slot) | Required | Required |
| Idle Listening | Reduced | Reduced (more) | Minimal (LPL) | Minimal | Moderate | None | Minimal |
| Collision Handling | CSMA/CA | CSMA/CA | CCA only | CCA only | Slotted CSMA | None (TDMA) | CSMA in slot |
| Typical Duty Cycle | 10–20% | 5–15% | 1–5% | 1–2% | ~5% | 1–2% | 2–10% |
| Scalability | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Latency | High (sleep) | High (early sleep) | Very High | Medium | Low | Low (bounded) | Medium |
| Best For | Periodic monitoring | Variable load | Research testbeds | Battery-critical | Event detection | Industrial | Mixed load |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "B-MAC is better than S-MAC because duty cycle is lower". This is *partially* true but **incomplete** — you must also mention the **preamble overhead** that B-MAC pays per packet. Always present a **trade-off**, not a winner.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Fundamental WSN MAC Protocols

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["WSN MAC Protocols"]
    A --> B["Contention Based"]
    A --> C["Schedule Based"]
    A --> D["Hybrid"]
    B --> B1["S MAC"]
    B --> B2["T MAC"]
    B --> B3["B MAC"]
    B --> B4["WiseMAC"]
    B --> B5["Sift MAC"]
    B --> B6["X MAC"]
    C --> C1["L MAC"]
    C --> C2["TRAMA"]
    C --> C3["DMAC"]
    C --> C4["DEANA"]
    D --> D1["Z MAC"]
    D --> D2["Funneling MAC"]
    D --> D3["IEEE 802.15.4 GTS"]
    B1 --> E1["Sync Listen Sleep"]
    B2 --> E2["Adaptive TA timeout"]
    B3 --> E3["LPL Long Preamble"]
    B4 --> E4["Adaptive Short Preamble"]
    B5 --> E5["Geometric slot bias"]
    C1 --> E6["TDMA slot per node"]
    D1 --> E7["TDMA base with CSMA fallback"]
```

## 4.2 S-MAC Listen/Sleep Cycle Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title S-MAC Duty Cycle Timing
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Node A
    Listen_A   :a1, 0, 50
    Sleep_A    :a2, 50, 450
    Listen_A2  :a3, 500, 550
    Sleep_A2   :a4, 550, 950
    section Node B (synchronized)
    Listen_B   :b1, 0, 50
    Sleep_B    :b2, 50, 450
    Listen_B2  :b3, 500, 550
    Sleep_B2   :b4, 550, 950
    section Node C (different schedule)
    Listen_C   :c1, 200, 250
    Sleep_C    :c2, 250, 700
    Listen_C2  :c3, 700, 750
    Sleep_C2   :c4, 750, 1200
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Node A and Node B share a virtual cluster schedule (synchronized). Node C follows a different schedule — when a border node belongs to two clusters, it follows both. The 10% listen window (50 ms / 500 ms) is the classical S-MAC operating point.

## 4.3 S-MAC Per-Frame Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SYNC
    SYNC --> LISTEN : Send Receive SYNC
    LISTEN --> CSMA_CA : Carrier Sense
    CSMA_CA --> RTS : Channel Free
    RTS --> CTS_WAIT : Transmit RTS
    CTS_WAIT --> DATA_TX : CTS Received
    CTS_WAIT --> SLEEP : CTS Timeout
    DATA_TX --> ACK_WAIT : Transmit Data
    ACK_WAIT --> SLEEP : ACK Received
    ACK_WAIT --> RETRY : ACK Timeout
    RETRY --> RTS : Attempt less than max
    RETRY --> DROP : Max Retries Reached
    SLEEP --> LISTEN : Frame Timer Expiry
    DROP --> SLEEP
```

## 4.4 B-MAC Preamble Sampling — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Application Layer Packet Ready"] --> B["B-MAC Send Module"]
    B --> C["Preamble Generator"]
    C --> D["Transmit Long Preamble"]
    D --> E["Transmit Data Packet"]
    E --> F["Wait for ACK"]
    F --> G{ACK Received}
    G -->|Yes| H["Transmit Complete"]
    G -->|No| I["Backoff and Retry"]
    A2["Node Receiving"] --> J["Wake up Sample Channel"]
    J --> K{Carrier Detected}
    K -->|No| L["Return to Sleep"]
    K -->|Yes| M["Stay Awake Receive Data"]
    M --> N["Send ACK"]
    L --> J
```

**Interpretation:** The receiver's CCA loop is the energy-critical section. B-MAC's elegance is in making the *check* extremely short (~hundreds of μs) while the *preamble* is long enough to be detected. WiseMAC shortens the preamble block by reusing the schedule learning from block N.

## 4.5 L-MAC Time-Slot Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Slot 1 Node A"] --- S2["Slot 2 Node B"]
    S2 --- S3["Slot 3 Node C"]
    S3 --- S4["Slot 4 Node D"]
    S4 --- S5["Slot 5 Node E"]
    S5 --- S6["Slot 6 Node F"]
    S6 --- S7["Slot 7 Node G"]
    S7 --- S8["Slot 8 Node H"]
    S8 --- S1B["Slot 1 Next Frame"]
    style S1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#cfc,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#ccf,stroke:#333
    style S4 fill:#ffc,stroke:#333
    style S5 fill:#cff,stroke:#333
    style S6 fill:#fcf,stroke:#333
    style S7 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style S8 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** Each colour = a different sensor node. Each node owns **one slot per TDMA frame**; the rest of the time, its radio is OFF. Collision-free and deterministic, but the **scaling cost** is $O(N)$ slots per frame.

## 4.6 Energy-Wastage → Protocol Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1["Idle Listening"] --> P1["S MAC Sleep"]
    W1 --> P2["T MAC Early Sleep"]
    W1 --> P3["B MAC LPL"]
    W1 --> P4["L MAC TDMA Sleep"]
    W2["Collision"] --> P5["CSMA CA in S MAC"]
    W2 --> P6["RTS CTS handshake"]
    W2 --> P7["Sift geometric bias"]
    W3["Overhearing"] --> P8["S MAC NAV tracking"]
    W3 --> P9["802.15.4 GTS"]
    W4["Control Overhead"] --> P10["B MAC removes SYNC"]
    W4 --> P11["WiseMAC piggyback on ACK"]
    W5["Overemitting"] --> P12["Long Preamble B MAC"]
    W5 --> P13["Schedule staggered DMAC"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This mapping diagram is the **highest-yield visual** for a KTU 14-mark "Discuss the MAC protocols designed to address energy wastage in WSNs" question. It is recommended to redraw this in the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four major sources of energy waste in a WSN MAC layer.**

**Model Answer:**
The four classical sources of energy waste at the MAC layer of a WSN are:
1. **Idle Listening** — Radio ON while no traffic exists; the dominant energy drain in low-traffic WSNs.
2. **Collision** — Simultaneous transmissions corrupt packets, causing costly retransmissions.
3. **Overhearing** — A node receives packets intended for other neighbours.
4. **Control Packet Overhead** — Energy spent on RTS, CTS, ACK, SYNC signalling.

A fifth source often added in modern literature is **Overemitting** — transmitting while the receiver's radio is still asleep.

*(Expected answer length: 4–5 lines. Award full 3 marks if all four are named and briefly described.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between contention-based and schedule-based MAC protocols in WSNs. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Contention-Based | Schedule-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Access | Random competition (CSMA) | Pre-allocated TDMA/FDMA slots |
| Synchronization | Not always required (e.g., B-MAC) | Strictly required |
| Collision | Possible (handled by backoff) | Collision-free by design |
| Scalability | High | Limited (slot exhaustion) |
| Latency | Variable | Bounded & deterministic |
| Examples | S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, WiseMAC, Sift | L-MAC, TRAMA, DMAC, DEANA |

*(Award 3 marks: 1 for clear distinction, 1 for two examples of each, 1 for additional correct technical detail.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write paragraph-only answers. Examiners award step marks for *numbered points, sub-headings, and diagrams*.
> - **Always** open a 14-mark MAC answer with a one-line **definition** of the protocol before listing features.
> - **Always** close with a comparison table or a "merits/demerts" block — this typically fetches the final 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse "duty cycle" with "data rate". Duty cycle = fraction of time the radio is ON.
> - **Do NOT** misattribute the S-MAC paper. S-MAC was proposed by **Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (2002)** — T-MAC by **van Dam & Langendoen (2003)** — B-MAC by **Polastre et al. (2004)**. Examiner will cut 1 mark for wrong attribution.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **S-MAC protocol** in detail. Discuss its listen/sleep mechanism, synchronization, and the message-passing technique. Show with a timing diagram how virtual clusters are formed.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) is a contention-based MAC protocol proposed by Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (2002) specifically for energy-constrained wireless sensor networks. Unlike IEEE 802.11, it is built on the premise that the *dominant energy cost is idle listening*, not transmission.

**2. Listen/Sleep Duty Cycle (2 marks):**
- Each node follows a periodic cycle: **Listen interval** (active) followed by **Sleep interval** (radio OFF).
- Typical operating point: $T_{frame} = 1$ s, $T_{listen} = 100$ ms → **10% duty cycle**.
- During sleep, the radio is OFF, saving ~99% of idle-listening energy.
- All nodes within a *virtual cluster* follow the **same schedule**, enabling synchronized communication.

**3. Synchronization (2 marks):**
- Nodes exchange **SYNC packets** at the start of each Listen window.
- SYNC contains: sender ID, next sleep time, schedule.
- A node that hears multiple schedules becomes a **border node** and alternates between the two.
- Neighbouring clusters with drifting schedules re-synchronize periodically to prevent drift.

**4. Message Passing (1 mark):**
- Long messages are **fragmented** and sent as a **burst** within a single Listen window.
- Uses **RTS/CTS/ACK** between every fragment to avoid collision.
- Other nodes hearing RTS/CTS enter **NAV-based sleep** (overhearing avoidance).

**5. Timing Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
gantt
    title S-MAC Virtual Cluster Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Cluster 1
    Listen1     :a1, 0, 100
    Sleep1      :a2, 100, 1000
    section Cluster 2 (offset)
    Listen2     :b1, 300, 400
    Sleep2      :b2, 400, 1300
    section Border Node
    ListenB1    :c1, 0, 100
    SleepB1     :c2, 100, 300
    ListenB2    :c3, 300, 400
    SleepB2     :c4, 400, 1000
```

**6. Merits & Demerits (built into the above):**
- Merits: Simple, energy efficient, avoids overhearing.
- Demerits: Fixed duty cycle is wasteful under variable load; sleep latency high; broadcast packets must be repeated across all Listen windows.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **T-MAC protocol** and show how it overcomes the fixed-duty-cycle limitation of S-MAC. Also discuss the **"early sleeping problem"** and any one solution to it.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):**
In S-MAC, the listen interval is fixed. If no event occurs, the node still listens for the full interval — wasting energy. T-MAC (Timeout-MAC) by van Dam & Langendoen (2003) replaces the *fixed* listen with an *adaptive* timeout.

**2. Active Timeout TA (2 marks):**
After the end of contention, the node starts a timer TA. If **no activation event** (any reception of data, RTS, or ACK) occurs within TA, the node goes to sleep.
The condition is:
$$
TA > C + R + T
$$
where C = contention slot time, R = RTS transmission, T = radio turnaround.

**3. Energy Gain (1 mark):**
- T-MAC achieves **5× energy reduction** vs S-MAC under variable load (as reported in the original paper on the EYES sensor node).
- Average idle listening time drops from 100% to a small fraction per cycle.

**4. Early Sleeping Problem (2 marks):**
- If node A sends an RTS to node B at the moment B has just timed out and gone to sleep, B misses the RTS.
- This is **most severe under multi-hop traffic** where a chain of forwarding nodes is active.
- Demonstrated in the original paper: throughput collapse at traffic > 10 packets/sec.

**5. Solution — D-MAC (1 mark):**
- D-MAC (Lu, Krishnamachari & Raghavendra, 2004) **stagger the wake-up times** of nodes along a data-gathering tree so that a node is awake exactly when its child has data to forward.
- This forms an "**active path**" up the tree, eliminating the interruption.

**6. Comparison Table (built into the discussion):**

| Property | S-MAC | T-MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Listen interval | Fixed | Adaptive (timeout) |
| Energy under variable load | High | Low |
| Multi-hop performance | Good | Suffers early-sleep problem |
| Complexity | Low | Medium |

*(Total: 7 marks. Examiner will allocate 2 for the explanation of TA, 2 for the early-sleep problem, 1 for the solution, 2 for the rest.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **B-MAC protocol** with its **Low Power Listening (LPL)** and **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** mechanisms. Show how preamble sampling saves energy. Compare it briefly with **WiseMAC**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Background (1 mark):**
B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC) by Polastre, Hill & Culler (2004) is a CSMA-based protocol for low-power, low-rate WSNs, widely deployed in TinyOS on Mica2/MicaZ motes.

**2. Low Power Listening (LPL) — 2 marks:**
- Receiver wakes up periodically to **sample** the channel.
- Sampling duration is very short (typ. **100–500 μs**).
- Between samples, the radio is OFF.
- Duty cycle can be as low as **0.1% to 1%**.

**3. Long Preamble — 2 marks:**
- The sender transmits a **continuous preamble** whose length $\ge$ receiver's listen interval.
- The receiver detects the preamble, stays awake, and receives the data packet.
- After the data, the receiver sends an ACK containing its **next wake-up time** (a key detail — this is what WiseMAC later exploits).

**4. CCA — 1 mark:**
- B-MAC uses **RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)** based CCA.
- If RSSI exceeds a threshold during the sample, the channel is declared busy and the node stays awake.

**5. Comparison with WiseMAC (1 mark):**

| Property | B-MAC | WiseMAC |
|---|---|---|
| Preamble length | Full listen interval | Just covers next wake-up + drift |
| Overhearing | Receives every preamble | Sends minimal preamble |
| Energy at sender | High (long TX) | Low (short TX) |
| Best for | Research testbeds | Production battery-critical WSN |

**6. Conclusion (built-in):**
WiseMAC is the optimized successor of B-MAC — preamble length adaptive based on the learned schedule of each neighbour.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Sift MAC protocol** for event-driven WSNs. Show why non-uniform slot selection is necessary, and derive the success probability in slot 1.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):**
In event-driven WSNs (e.g., fire detection, structural monitoring), when an event occurs, **many spatially-correlated nodes** detect it simultaneously. Traditional CSMA would suffer massive collisions. Sift, by Jamieson, Patel & Kermode (2003), solves this by designing the *probability distribution* itself, not the contention count.

**2. Non-Uniform Probability (2 marks):**
- N = total contenders (potentially), r = actual active nodes.
- Sift uses a **geometrically decreasing** probability across slots:
$$
p_i = \frac{(1-\alpha) \cdot \alpha^{N-i}}{1 - \alpha^{N}}
$$
- The first slot has the **highest** probability; the last slot has the lowest.
- Intuition: If many nodes are competing, we want the **first** one to transmit **fast** — so they all bias toward early slots.

**3. Derivation of Slot-1 Success Probability (2 marks):**
Probability that **exactly one** of the N nodes picks slot 1:

$$
P_{1}(1) = N \cdot p_1 \cdot (1-p_1)^{N-1}
$$

For $N = 16$, $\alpha = 0.6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.6^{15}}{1 - 0.6^{16}} \\
    &= \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.0004702}{0.9997} \\
    &\approx 0.000188
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1}(1) &= 16 \cdot 0.000188 \cdot (1-0.000188)^{15} \\
         &\approx 16 \cdot 0.000188 \cdot 0.9972 \\
         &\approx 0.003
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The probability per node is small, but if the first slot fails, Sift **re-uses the same window** with a fresh geometric bias — this time even more nodes are forced to choose the first slot. Within **3–4 slots**, the protocol guarantees that one node wins.

**4. Property of Spatial Correlation (1 mark):**
Sift's key insight is that the **number of active nodes r is unknown**. By biasing toward early slots regardless of $r$, Sift works equally well for r = 1 or r = 100. This is its **major innovation** over CSMA/CA.

**5. Comparison with S-MAC (1 mark):**
- S-MAC: optimized for **periodic** traffic.
- Sift: optimized for **bursty, spatially-correlated** event traffic.

**6. Conclusion (built-in):**
Sift is the de-facto choice for **critical event detection WSNs** where the first reporter must reach the sink in bounded time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Five energy wastage mechanisms:** Idle listening, collision, overhearing, control overhead, overemitting.
- [x] **S-MAC:** 10% duty cycle, virtual clusters, SYNC, message passing, RTS/CTS, NAV.
- [x] **T-MAC:** Adaptive TA = C + R + T, early sleeping problem, D-MAC solution.
- [x] **B-MAC:** LPL + long preamble, RSSI-CCA, no synchronization.
- [x] **WiseMAC:** Adaptive short preamble $T_p = T_w + 2 \theta T_{interval}$, schedule learning from ACK.
- [x] **Sift:** Geometric probability $p_i = \frac{(1-\alpha)\alpha^{N-i}}{1-\alpha^N}$, for event-driven WSNs.
- [x] **L-MAC:** TDMA, one slot per node, distributed slot election, O(N) scaling cost.
- [x] **DMAC:** Data-gathering tree, staggered active path, MTS flag.
- [x] **Z-MAC:** TDMA base with CSMA in slot, hybrid.
- [x] **Key formula:** $DC = T_{listen} / T_{frame}$, $P_{avg} = (P_{rx} T_{listen} + P_{sleep} T_{sleep})/T_{frame}$.
- [x] **Paper attribution:** S-MAC = Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (2002); T-MAC = van Dam & Langendoen (2003); B-MAC = Polastre, Hill & Culler (2004); Sift = Jamieson, Patel & Kermode (2003); WiseMAC = El-Hoiydi & Decotignie (2004); L-MAC = van Hoesel & Havinga (2004); DMAC = Lu, Krishnamachari & Raghavendra (2004); Z-MAC = Rhee, Warrier, Min & Xu (2005).
- [x] **Three family classification:** Contention-based / Schedule-based / Hybrid.
- [x] **Examiner cues:** Always end a 14-mark answer with a comparison table.
- [x] **Mermaid fallback:** If unsure of any diagram, draw the taxonomy graph or the duty-cycle Gantt chart — both are KTU-friendly.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — MAC Access Control: Fundamental MAC Protocols**
