topics.title: MAC Design for WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** MAC Design for WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Medium Access Control (MAC) layer** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is the sub-layer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2 in the OSI / IEEE 802 reference model) that governs *how multiple sensor nodes share a single, common, broadcast wireless radio channel* without mutual destructive interference.

In the context of WSNs, the MAC protocol is not merely a "collision avoidance" mechanism; it is the **primary energy-management engine** of the node. Because sensor motes are battery-operated, energy-constrained, and typically deployed in inaccessible environments, the MAC protocol is explicitly designed to maximize network lifetime by aggressively minimizing radio usage while still meeting application-level Quality of Service (QoS) requirements such as latency, throughput, and fairness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A *WSN MAC protocol* is a set of rules that schedule or arbitrate access to the shared radio medium, schedule radio transitions between the **active (transmit/receive/listen) state** and the **sleep (low-power) state**, and control the topology-specific framing of packets so that the *per-bit energy cost* and *per-bit delay* are jointly optimized.

The major classes of WSN MAC protocols are:

- **Contention-based (CSMA/CA family):** S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, WiseMAC, X-MAC.
- **Schedule-based (TDMA / FDMA hybrids):** LEACH, TRAMA, BMA, LMAC.
- **Hybrid:** Z-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 (which is the de-facto standard for WSNs and is the foundation of ZigBee).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **huge conference hall full of 500 sleeping students** who must occasionally whisper a short fact to a neighbor. If every student shouted all day long, the room would be noisy, no one would be heard, and everyone would die of exhaustion. Instead, the "MAC protocol" is the *rule book* the students follow:

1. **Most of the time, students sleep** (radio in sleep mode — saves battery).
2. **At agreed moments, they briefly wake up, listen, and speak** (synchronized duty-cycled active periods).
3. **If two students want to talk at once, one yields** (carrier sensing / backoff).
4. **They use short handshakes ("You there?" — "Yes, go ahead.") before long speech** (RTS/CTS to avoid expensive collisions).

The *energy efficiency* of the rule book is measured by how long students sleep versus how long they are awake. The *fairness* is measured by whether everyone gets a chance to speak. The *latency* is the average time a student has to wait before speaking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** In WSNs, *energy efficiency* is the primary design goal — **not** throughput, **not** delay, and **not** fairness (in the classical wireline sense). Every other metric is a *secondary constraint*.

## 1.3 Why WSN MAC ≠ Traditional Wi-Fi / Cellular MAC

| Property | Traditional MAC (e.g., IEEE 802.11) | WSN MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Throughput, fairness, QoS | **Energy efficiency**, lifetime |
| Node Power | Mains or rechargeable | **Single non-rechargeable battery** |
| Traffic Pattern | Continuous, bursty | Low-rate, event-driven, periodic |
| Node Count | 10s – 100s | **100s – 10,000s** |
| Mobility | Mobile hosts | **Mostly static** |
| Computation | Powerful CPUs | **8/16-bit microcontrollers** |
| Sleep Modes | Optional / aggressive wakeup | **Mandatory duty cycling** |
| Channel Errors | Modest, FEC present | **High, lossy wireless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most defining trait that distinguishes a WSN MAC protocol is **duty cycling** — the periodic, coordinated toggling of the radio between a low-power sleep state and a brief active state. Without duty cycling, a sensor node's battery would drain within days; with proper duty cycling, the same node can survive **months to years**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used in KTU 2024 WSN MAC design and must be memorized:

- **Radio states:** Transmit (TX), Receive (RX), Idle Listening (IDLE), Sleep (SLEEP).
- **Power draw hierarchy (typical CC2420 mote):** $P_{TX} \approx 35\text{–}52 \text{ mW}$, $P_{RX} \approx 38 \text{ mW}$, $P_{IDLE} \approx 38 \text{ mW}$, $P_{SLEEP} \approx 1.4 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$.
- **Duty Cycle:**

$$DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{frame}}$$

- **Per-bit Energy:**

$$E_{bit} = \frac{P_{avg}}{R_{b}} \quad \text{[Joules/bit]}$$

where $R_b$ is the bit rate in bits/second.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Sources of Energy Waste in WSN MAC

Every WSN MAC protocol must address the following four energy wastage causes. These were identified by Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (USC/ISI) in the seminal S-MAC paper and form the **backbone of KTU 2024 Module 1 questions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hint:** Whenever a KTU question says *"Discuss the energy waste problem in WSNs"* or *"Why is a WSN MAC protocol required?"*, your answer **must** begin with the four sources below.

### 2.1.1 Collisions
When two nodes transmit simultaneously on the same channel, the packets overlap at the receiver and both become corrupted. The energy spent in transmitting the corrupted packets is **completely wasted**. The retransmission further doubles, triples, or quadruples the energy cost. **Mitigation:** CSMA/CA, RTS/CTS, randomized backoff.

### 2.1.2 Overhearing
A node receives packets destined for **other** nodes. Because the wireless medium is broadcast, every node within radio range hears every transmission. The receiving node expends $P_{RX} \cdot T_{packet}$ energy for each such unwanted packet. In dense networks, overhearing can account for **30–50 %** of total energy consumption. **Mitigation:** Sleep after hearing RTS not addressed to self, address filtering.

### 2.1.3 Control Packet Overhead
Sending RTS, CTS, ACK, SYNC, beacon, and other MAC-layer headers consumes bandwidth and energy. For a tiny 20-byte data packet, the MAC overhead may exceed 30 bytes — meaning **>50 % of transmitted energy goes to non-data bits**. **Mitigation:** Piggybacking, in-band signaling, reducing preamble length.

### 2.1.4 Idle Listening
This is the **single largest wastage source** in many WSN deployments. Listening to the channel to detect an incoming packet when **no packet is actually being transmitted** consumes nearly the same power as receiving. In many WSNs with low traffic, idle listening accounts for **50–90 %** of total energy. **Mitigation:** Duty cycling, scheduled wake-ups, low-power listening (LPL).

## 2.2 Energy Model of a Sensor Node (First-Order Linear Model)

The average power consumption of a node's radio subsystem is:

$$P_{avg} = P_{TX} \cdot \tau_{TX} + P_{RX} \cdot \tau_{RX} + P_{IDLE} \cdot \tau_{IDLE} + P_{SLEEP} \cdot \tau_{SLEEP}$$

where $\tau_{TX}, \tau_{RX}, \tau_{IDLE}, \tau_{SLEEP}$ are the **fractional time** the radio spends in each state, and:

$$\tau_{TX} + \tau_{RX} + \tau_{IDLE} + \tau_{SLEEP} = 1$$

Because $P_{SLEEP} \ll P_{IDLE} \approx P_{RX} \approx P_{TX}$, the optimization is to drive $\tau_{IDLE} \to 0$ and $\tau_{SLEEP} \to 1$.

The per-frame energy is then:

$$E_{frame} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}$$

and the node lifetime in frames is:

$$L = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{frame}} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $DC$ | Duty Cycle | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of time radio is active |
| $T_{frame}$ | One full listen + sleep cycle | seconds | $T_{frame} = T_{active} + T_{sleep}$ |
| $T_{active}$ | Active (listen) duration | seconds | MAC-dependent (S-MAC ≈ 300 ms) |
| $T_{sleep}$ | Sleep duration | seconds | MAC-dependent (S-MAC ≈ 700 ms) |
| $P_{avg}$ | Average radio power | Watts (W) | Weighted sum of state powers |
| $E_{bit}$ | Energy per useful bit | Joules/bit | $E_{bit} = P_{avg} / R_b$ |
| $E_{battery}$ | Total node energy | Joules (J) | AA battery ≈ 2.85 kJ = 2850 J |
| $R_b$ | Bit rate | bits/second (bps) | CC2420: 250 kbps |
| $\tau_i$ | Fractional time in state $i$ | dimensionless | Sum to 1 |
| $T_{preamble}$ | Preamble length | seconds | LPL uses long preamble |
| $N$ | Number of neighbors | integer | Affects overhearing |
| $L$ | Node lifetime | frames or seconds | $L = E_{battery} / E_{frame}$ |

## 2.4 Contention-Based vs Schedule-Based MAC — Comparative Analysis

| Criterion | Contention-Based (S-MAC, B-MAC) | Schedule-Based (TDMA, LEACH) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Carrier sense + backoff | Pre-assigned time slots |
| Scalability | High (no global state) | Low (needs cluster head / coordinator) |
| Collision Handling | Probabilistic (CSMA/CA) | Collision-free by construction |
| Energy Efficiency | Good with LPL, overhearing is issue | Excellent (no idle, no collision) |
| Synchronization | Loose (S-MAC) or none (B-MAC) | Tight (GPS or beacon-based) |
| Latency | Variable, depends on contention | Predictable, bounded by slot |
| Overhead | Control packets in channel | Schedule distribution overhead |
| Best Suited For | Event-driven, low traffic | Periodic, high traffic, data fusion |

## 2.5 The Concept of Virtual Clustering in S-MAC

A **virtual cluster** is a set of nodes that share the *same* listen/sleep schedule. Nodes that cannot hear each other may still belong to the same virtual cluster (and thus sleep in unison). This is the *S-MAC solution* to the hidden-terminal problem of decentralized synchronization: rather than maintaining a global schedule, nodes broadcast a small **SYNC packet** at the start of every listen period. Border nodes (those that can hear two clusters) follow both schedules, effectively bridging the clusters.

## 2.6 Why IEEE 802.15.4 (and thus ZigBee) Matters for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The IEEE 802.15.4 standard is the *only* MAC standard explicitly designed for low-rate wireless personal area networks (LR-WPANs) and is the underlying MAC for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART, Thread, and MiWi. The 2024 scheme often asks for a comparison between classical research MACs (S-MAC, B-MAC) and the standardized 802.15.4 MAC.

Key 802.15.4 features relevant to WSN MAC design:

- **Two channel-access modes:** Beacon-enabled (slotted CSMA/CA with superframe, GTS) and Non-beacon-enabled (unslotted CSMA/CA).
- **Superframe structure:** Active period (CAP + GTS) + Inactive period (sleep).
- **Duty cycling natively supported** via Beacon Interval (BI) and Superframe Duration (SD).
- **Slotted CSMA/CA** with binary exponential backoff over 5 slots per backoff stage.
- **Guaranteed Time Slots (GTS)** for collision-free, deterministic latency delivery.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** A soil-moisture sensor may wake once every 10 minutes, transmit 16 bytes, and sleep. The MAC design directly determines whether the node lasts 1 year or 10 years.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** MAC must support bursty event-driven traffic (earthquake detection) with bounded latency.
- **Medical Body Area Networks (BANs):** MAC must minimize specific absorption rate (SAR) and avoid heating tissue, while still guaranteeing heartbeat data.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT) / Smart Grid:** MAC must coexist with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and microwave ovens in the 2.4 GHz ISM band.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per-Frame Energy with Linear Radio Model

We model the radio as a 4-state device. For one MAC frame of duration $T_{frame}$, the node spends time $T_{TX}$ transmitting, $T_{RX}$ receiving, $T_{IDLE}$ idle-listening, and $T_{SLEEP}$ sleeping.

**Step 1 — Write the time conservation equation:**

$$T_{frame} = T_{TX} + T_{RX} + T_{IDLE} + T_{SLEEP}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total energy spent in one frame:**

$$E_{frame} = P_{TX} \cdot T_{TX} + P_{RX} \cdot T_{RX} + P_{IDLE} \cdot T_{IDLE} + P_{SLEEP} \cdot T_{SLEEP}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to fractional times $\tau_i = T_i / T_{frame}$:**

$$E_{frame} = T_{frame} \cdot \left( P_{TX}\tau_{TX} + P_{RX}\tau_{RX} + P_{IDLE}\tau_{IDLE} + P_{SLEEP}\tau_{SLEEP} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the bracketed term as the average power $P_{avg}$:**

$$\boxed{E_{frame} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}}$$

**Step 5 — Express the energy-per-bit by dividing by the useful bits $B = R_b \cdot T_{frame}$:**

$$\boxed{E_{bit} = \frac{P_{avg}}{R_b}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** To minimize $E_{bit}$, we either lower $P_{avg}$ (by sleeping more) or raise $R_b$ (by using higher modulation). In WSNs, $R_b$ is fixed by the radio hardware (e.g., 250 kbps for CC2420), so the **only** knob is $P_{avg}$, controlled by the MAC duty cycle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Duty Cycle for Bounded Latency

Suppose an application has a *worst-case delay tolerance* $D_{max}$ seconds (the maximum time a packet may wait before delivery). The MAC uses a periodic listen/sleep schedule with frame length $T_{frame}$.

**Step 1 — Worst-case wake-up latency:**
A packet arriving just after a listen period ends must wait almost an entire $T_{frame}$ for the next listen period:

$$D_{worst} = T_{frame}$$

**Step 2 — Bound the frame length to meet delay:**

$$T_{frame} \le D_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the duty-cycle definition $DC = T_{active}/T_{frame}$:**

$$T_{active} = DC \cdot T_{frame} \le DC \cdot D_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Express average power using only the active and sleep contributions:**

$$P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot DC + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the duty cycle that minimizes $P_{avg}$ subject to $T_{frame} \le D_{max}$:**

Since $P_{active} \gg P_{sleep}$, $P_{avg}$ is a monotonically increasing function of $DC$. So the minimum is achieved at:

$$\boxed{DC_{min} = \frac{T_{active,min}}{D_{max}}}$$

where $T_{active,min}$ is the minimum time needed to send/receive one packet and its ACK.

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Given $D_{max} = 1 \text{ s}$, $T_{active,min} = 100 \text{ ms}$, $P_{active} = 40 \text{ mW}$, $P_{sleep} = 0.001 \text{ mW}$:

$$DC_{min} = \frac{0.1}{1.0} = 0.1 \quad (10\%)$$

$$P_{avg} = 40 \cdot 0.1 + 0.001 \cdot 0.9 = 4.0009 \text{ mW}$$

Compared to no duty cycling ($DC = 1$): $P_{avg} = 40 \text{ mW}$. That is a **10× energy reduction**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Saved by Overhearing Avoidance in S-MAC

In a dense network with $N$ neighbors, a node using **plain CSMA** hears every transmission of every neighbor. In S-MAC, after hearing a packet addressed to *another* node, the node returns to sleep for the remainder of the frame.

**Step 1 — Energy with no overhearing avoidance (CSMA baseline):**
Assume each neighbor generates $\lambda$ packets per second, each of duration $T_{p}$:

$$E_{overhear,noMAC} = N \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{RX} \cdot T_{p} \cdot T_{frame}$$

**Step 2 — Energy with S-MAC overhearing avoidance:**
Only the RTS/CTS to *other* nodes is heard; the S-MAC node goes to sleep after determining the destination is not itself. The overhearing time is reduced to roughly the RTS/CTS duration $T_{ctrl} \ll T_{p}$:

$$E_{overhear,SMAC} \approx N \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{RX} \cdot T_{ctrl} \cdot T_{frame}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of energies (savings factor):**

$$\eta_{save} = \frac{E_{overhear,SMAC}}{E_{overhear,noMAC}} = \frac{T_{ctrl}}{T_{p}}$$

For a typical 20-byte data packet with 200 µs RTS: $\eta_{save} \approx 200/1600 \approx 12.5\%$, i.e., an **87.5 % reduction** in overhearing energy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 2024 Numerical Setup

The most common numerical problem in Module 1 of PECAT634 has the following structure:

| Given Quantity | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Battery capacity | 2850 J (2 × AA alkaline) | $E_{bat}$ |
| Bit rate | 250 kbps | $R_b$ |
| TX current | 17.4 mA @ 3 V | $I_{TX}$ |
| RX current | 18.8 mA @ 3 V | $I_{RX}$ |
| Sleep current | 1 µA | $I_{S}$ |
| Duty cycle | 1 % to 10 % | $DC$ |
| Frame length | 1 s | $T_{frame}$ |
| Packet size | 50 bytes | $L_{pkt}$ |
| Overhead | 20 bytes | $L_{oh}$ |

Using these values, a KTU numerical typically asks for *node lifetime* or *total bits transmittable per battery*.

**Worked template (for student practice):**

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{avg}$:**

$$P_{avg} = (P_{TX} \cdot \tau_{TX} + P_{RX} \cdot \tau_{RX}) + P_{SLEEP} \cdot (1 - DC)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_{frame}$:**

$$E_{frame} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}$$

**Step 3 — Compute number of frames in lifetime:**

$$L = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{frame}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total operational time:**

$$t_{life} = L \cdot T_{frame} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If a question gives currents instead of powers, multiply by supply voltage $V_{DD}$ to get powers. For a MICAz mote, $V_{DD} = 3.0 \text{ V}$. The conversions are: $P_{TX} = I_{TX} \cdot V_{DD} = 17.4 \text{ mA} \cdot 3 \text{ V} = 52.2 \text{ mW}$.

## 3.5 Closed-Form Solution for the B-MAC Preamble Length

In Low-Power Listening (LPL), the sender transmits a long *preamble* of length $T_{preamble}$ that is just long enough to overlap with at least one of the receiver's periodic channel checks (CCAs). The receiver checks the channel every $T_{check}$ seconds; to guarantee overlap:

$$T_{preamble} \ge T_{check}$$

**Step 1 — Required preamble length (minimum):**

$$\boxed{T_{preamble,min} = T_{check}}$$

**Step 2 — Energy cost per transmission in LPL:**

$$E_{LPL} = P_{TX} \cdot T_{preamble} + P_{RX} \cdot T_{packet} + P_{IDLE} \cdot T_{check}$$

**Step 3 — Overhead ratio of LPL preamble to useful data:**

$$OH_{LPL} = \frac{T_{preamble}}{T_{packet}}$$

For $T_{check} = 100 \text{ ms}$ and a 1 ms packet: $OH_{LPL} = 100$, i.e., **99 % of transmitted energy is preamble**. This is the key weakness of B-MAC, addressed in X-MAC by strobed preambles.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*"List and briefly explain the four major sources of energy waste in a Wireless Sensor Network's MAC layer."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme Aligned):**

The four major sources of energy waste identified in WSN MAC design are:

1. **Collisions (½ mark):** Two or more nodes transmit simultaneously; both packets are corrupted and retransmissions waste energy. Mitigated by CSMA/CA and RTS/CTS handshakes.

2. **Overhearing (½ mark):** A node receives packets destined for other nodes within its radio range. Energy is wasted in receiving and decoding unwanted frames. Mitigated by sleep after hearing a non-self RTS.

3. **Control Packet Overhead (½ mark):** MAC headers, beacons, SYNC packets, RTS/CTS, ACKs consume bandwidth and energy. For small data packets, the overhead can exceed the payload. Mitigated by piggybacking and short headers.

4. **Idle Listening (1 mark — emphasized):** Listening to the channel while no packet is being transmitted consumes energy nearly equal to receiving. In low-traffic WSNs, this is the dominant energy wastage. Mitigated by duty cycling and low-power listening (LPL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocated Marks:** 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks. Most students lose the third mark by failing to mention the *mitigation* alongside the *cause*.

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Differentiate between contention-based and schedule-based MAC protocols in WSNs. Give one example of each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Contention-Based | Schedule-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Access | Random, on-demand via CSMA/CA | Pre-assigned time slots (TDMA) |
| Collisions | Possible (probabilistic) | Collision-free by construction |
| Synchronization | Loose or none | Tight (GPS or beacon) |
| Example | **S-MAC** (or B-MAC) | **LEACH / TRAMA** |
| Best For | Event-driven, low traffic | Periodic, predictable traffic |

(1½ marks for differences + ½ mark each for examples = 3 marks)

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*"With the help of a neat diagram, explain the design of the **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) protocol** for Wireless Sensor Networks. Discuss how it reduces energy waste due to idle listening, collisions, and overhearing. Also derive the expression for the average power consumption of a node operating under S-MAC with duty cycle $DC$."*

#### Part (a) — S-MAC Design Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):**
In a sensor network with low traffic, the radio spends most of its time *idle listening*, which is as expensive as actually receiving. S-MAC, proposed by **Ye, Heidemann and Estrin (2002, USC/ISI)**, introduces a periodic **listen/sleep cycle** to reduce idle listening.

**2. Frame Structure (2 marks):**
Each S-MAC frame is divided into:
- **Listen period** (duration $T_{listen}$): subdivided into *SYNC*, *RTS*, *CTS*, *DATA*, *ACK* sub-slots.
- **Sleep period** (duration $T_{sleep}$): the radio is turned off.

$$T_{frame} = T_{listen} + T_{sleep}$$

A typical S-MAC configuration: $T_{listen} = 300 \text{ ms}$, $T_{sleep} = 700 \text{ ms}$, giving a duty cycle of 30 %. For aggressive energy savings, $T_{sleep}$ can be 5–10 s.

**3. Synchronization (2 marks):**
All nodes in a *virtual cluster* share the same schedule. A node broadcasts a **SYNC packet** at the start of every listen period containing the *time until next sleep*. Border nodes (those with two or more cluster neighbors) maintain *multiple schedules* to bridge clusters. The SYNC packet uses CSMA/CA to avoid collision with data traffic.

**4. Collision and Overhearing Avoidance (2 marks):**
- **Collision:** S-MAC uses 802.11-style RTS/CTS/DATA/ACK with virtual and physical carrier sensing. The 4-way handshake ensures the channel is reserved before long DATA transmission.
- **Overhearing:** After a node overhears an RTS/CTS, it computes the *Network Allocation Vector (NAV)* — the time until the exchange will end — and goes to sleep for exactly that duration. This is the key S-MAC innovation over 802.11.

**5. Periodic Sleeping (½ mark):**
Nodes that have no traffic to send and are not addressed by an overheard RTS/CTS enter the sleep state for the remainder of the frame.

**6. Diagram (½ mark — for neatness):** (See the S-MAC timing diagram in Section 4.3 above.)

#### Part (b) — Average Power Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define state powers (½ mark):**
Let $P_{active}$ be the average power when the radio is awake (TX, RX, or IDLE — all similar), and $P_{sleep}$ be the sleep-mode power.

**Step 2 — Define duty cycle (1 mark):**

$$DC = \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{listen} + T_{sleep}}$$

**Step 3 — Write the average power (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot DC + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $T_{listen}$ and $T_{sleep}$ (1 mark):**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_{active} \cdot T_{listen} + P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep}}{T_{listen} + T_{sleep}}$$

**Step 5 — Discuss limiting cases (2 marks):**
- If $DC = 1$ (always awake): $P_{avg} = P_{active}$ (no savings).
- If $DC = 0$ (always asleep): $P_{avg} = P_{sleep}$ (no communication possible).
- For $P_{active} \gg P_{sleep}$: $P_{avg} \approx P_{active} \cdot DC$, i.e., $P_{avg}$ scales **linearly** with duty cycle. A 10 % duty cycle yields a 10× energy reduction.

**Step 6 — Compute node lifetime (½ mark):**

$$t_{life} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}}$$

For $E_{battery} = 2850 \text{ J}$, $P_{active} = 50 \text{ mW}$, $P_{sleep} = 0.001 \text{ mW}$, $DC = 0.1$:

$$P_{avg} = 50 \cdot 0.1 + 0.001 \cdot 0.9 \approx 5.001 \text{ mW}$$

$$t_{life} = \frac{2850}{5.001 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 5.7 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \approx 6.6 \text{ days (continuous)} $$

Wait — correction: $5.7 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \div 86400 \text{ s/day} \approx 6.6 \text{ days}$. Compared to $DC = 1$: $t_{life} = 2850 / 0.05 = 57000 \text{ s} \approx 15.8 \text{ hours}$. So S-MAC at $DC = 0.1$ yields a **10× lifetime extension** (15.8 hours → 6.6 days).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Choice A):**
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you write $P_{IDLE} \neq P_{RX}$; in S-MAC derivation they are equal.
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you forget to include the SYNC packet overhead when calculating $T_{listen}$.
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you do not draw or describe the frame structure (timing diagram is mandatory for 7-mark questions).
> - **Loss of 2 marks** if you derive the average power without first *defining* the duty cycle and the two state powers explicitly.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Explain the **B-MAC (Berkeley MAC) protocol** for WSNs. Compare it with S-MAC in terms of preamble length, synchronization requirement, and energy efficiency. With a neat block diagram, describe the B-MAC architecture and explain the concept of Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)."*

#### Part (a) — B-MAC Architecture and CCA (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Background and Motivation (1 mark):**
B-MAC (Polastre, Hill, Culler — UC Berkeley, 2004) was designed as a *minimalist* MAC for WSNs running on the Mote platform. It removes all per-packet state and complex synchronization in favour of a *purely channel-sensing* approach called **Low-Power Listening (LPL)**.

**2. Core Idea — Low-Power Listening (2 marks):**
- The receiver **wakes up briefly to perform a Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** every $T_{check}$ seconds (e.g., every 100 ms).
- The sender **prepends a long preamble** to every data packet, of length $T_{preamble} \ge T_{check}$.
- The receiver's CCA finds the channel busy, so it stays awake to receive the data.
- This achieves **asynchronous** low-power operation — *no schedule synchronization* is needed.

**3. CCA Implementation (2 marks):**
CCA is a *single-sample* channel-busy check. The receiver measures the received signal strength indicator (RSSI) over a short window and compares it to a threshold. If the energy exceeds the threshold, the channel is "busy." This is the "B-MAC architecture" — simple, stateless, and low-overhead.

**4. B-MAC Block Diagram (2 marks):** (See Section 4.5 above for the sequence diagram.)

```
Sender:  [App] → [Packet Queue] → [Preamble Generator] → [Radio TX]
Receiver: [Radio RX] → [CCA Sampler] → [Sleep/Wake Controller] → [App]
```

**5. Strengths and Weaknesses (½ mark each — for completeness):**
- *Strengths:* No synchronization, simple, scalable, works with any physical layer.
- *Weaknesses:* Long preamble wastes energy at the sender; overhearing is not addressed; high overhead ratio $T_{preamble}/T_{packet}$.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table: B-MAC vs S-MAC (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (as a structured table — 7 marks):**

| Criterion | S-MAC | B-MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Authors** | 2002, Ye et al., USC/ISI | 2004, Polastre et al., UC Berkeley |
| **Synchronization** | Required (SYNC packets, virtual clusters) | None (asynchronous via LPL) |
| **Idle Listening** | Reduced by scheduled sleep | Reduced by short CCA sampling |
| **Overhearing Avoidance** | Yes (NAV-based sleep) | No (relies on lower layers) |
| **Collision Avoidance** | RTS/CTS + CSMA | CSMA only (no RTS/CTS) |
| **Preamble Length** | Short (data packet only) | **Long** (length $\ge T_{check}$) |
| **Energy per Bit** | Lower for moderate traffic | Higher due to long preamble |
| **Latency** | Bounded by frame length | Bounded by $T_{check}$ |
| **Scalability** | Moderate (schedule propagation) | **High** (no schedule) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Medium | **Low** |
| **Best Suited For** | Periodic, multi-hop | Event-driven, sparse traffic |

(½ mark per significant row, with 1 mark for the table header / overall conclusion = 7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Choice B):**
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you state that B-MAC *uses* RTS/CTS (it does NOT — only CSMA).
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you claim B-MAC *requires* cluster synchronization (it does NOT — that is S-MAC's hallmark).
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you do not draw the block diagram or CCA flow — the question explicitly asks for one.
> - **Loss of 1 mark** if you confuse *preamble length* with *packet length* in the comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 1 — MAC Design for WSNs — Final Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **MAC's primary goal in WSNs is energy efficiency**, not throughput, not fairness.
- ✅ The **four energy wastage causes** are: Collisions, Overhearing, Control Overhead, **Idle Listening** (the dominant one).
- ✅ **Duty cycle** $DC = T_{active}/(T_{active} + T_{sleep})$ — the central tunable knob.
- ✅ **$E_{bit} = P_{avg} / R_b$** is the figure of merit.
- ✅ **S-MAC** = scheduled listen/sleep + SYNC + virtual clusters + NAV-based overhearing avoidance.
- ✅ **T-MAC** = S-MAC + adaptive early sleep using a TA timeout (saves energy under variable load).
- ✅ **B-MAC** = asynchronous LPL + long preamble + simple CCA (no synchronization).
- ✅ **WiseMAC** = minimizes preamble by learning the receiver's sampling offset (minimizes B-MAC's overhead).
- ✅ **X-MAC** = strobed (interrupted) preamble — receiver replies with an early ACK to clip the preamble.
- ✅ **LEACH / TRAMA** = TDMA-style cluster-based MAC; collision-free but synchronization-heavy.
- ✅ **Z-MAC** = hybrid; uses CSMA in high contention and TDMA in low contention.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4** = the standardized WSN MAC, with slotted CSMA/CA in beacon-enabled mode, GTS for QoS, and a superframe structure with active + inactive (sleep) periods.
- ✅ **Linear radio model:** $P_{avg} = P_{TX}\tau_{TX} + P_{RX}\tau_{RX} + P_{IDLE}\tau_{IDLE} + P_{SLEEP}\tau_{SLEEP}$.
- ✅ **Energy hierarchy:** $P_{TX} \approx P_{RX} \approx P_{IDLE} \gg P_{SLEEP}$ (sleep is ~10⁻⁵ of active).
- ✅ **Lifetime** $t_{life} = E_{battery}/P_{avg}$; for a 2850 J AA battery and $P_{avg} = 5$ mW, lifetime $\approx 6.6$ days continuous.
- ✅ **Hidden terminal problem** in WSNs is addressed by RTS/CTS (in S-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4) and by physical carrier sensing + backoff.
- ✅ **Virtual cluster** = set of nodes sharing a schedule; border nodes follow multiple schedules.
- ✅ **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** = virtual carrier sense counter; set after overhearing RTS/CTS.
- ✅ **Preamble length in LPL** must be at least $T_{check}$ — the receiver's CCA interval.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favorite numerical** = given $E_{bat}$, $V_{DD}$, $I_{TX}$, $I_{RX}$, $I_{S}$, $R_b$, $DC$, compute lifetime, $E_{bit}$, or required duty cycle for a latency bound.
- ✅ **Always** include a *diagram* in any 7-mark explanation question (S-MAC timing, B-MAC block diagram, IEEE 802.15.4 superframe, energy-waste tree).
- ✅ **Memorize the comparison tables** — S-MAC vs B-MAC, contention vs schedule, IEEE 802.15.4 vs 802.11.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MAC Protocols for WSN (Contention Based)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MAC Protocols for WSN (Contention Based)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) is a set of deterministic or stochastic rules that govern how multiple sensor nodes share a common, scarce, and error-prone wireless communication channel without destructive mutual interference. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, MAC protocols for WSN are broadly classified into two families:

1. **Contention-Based MAC Protocols** (also called *Random Access* or *CSMA-based* protocols)
2. **Schedule-Based / TDMA-based MAC Protocols** (Contention-Free)

A **Contention-Based MAC Protocol** is defined as a distributed channel access mechanism where nodes compete for the shared wireless medium using carrier sensing, random back-off, and handshake signaling (RTS/CTS), rather than being allocated deterministic time slots. Nodes that wish to transmit first "contend" for the channel; if a collision occurs, they retransmit after a random waiting period. The dominant variants in WSN literature are **S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, WiseMAC, and Sift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase "Contention Based" specifically points to CSMA/CA-family protocols that rely on *Listen-Before-Talk (LBT)*, *Random Back-off*, and *Synchronized/Random Sleep-Wake Cycles* — NOT pure ALOHA or Slotted-ALOHA.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Polite Roundtable Discussion"**
>
> Imagine **10 people (sensor nodes)** sitting around a single round table in a quiet library. They all need to speak occasionally to share information, but **only one microphone (the wireless channel)** is available.
>
> * **Schedule-Based (TDMA) Analogy:** The moderator gives each person a fixed 1-minute slot — orderly, but most people sit idle for 59 minutes wasting their energy.
> * **Contention-Based Analogy (S-MAC):** Everyone agrees on a rule: *"We will all listen for 10 seconds (SYNC period). If someone speaks, others stay quiet. Then we ALL sleep for 50 seconds to save energy, and wake up together to listen again."*
> * **Contention-Based Analogy (B-MAC):** Each person wakes up at *random* times, listens briefly (CCA — Clear Channel Assessment), and if no one is speaking, immediately starts talking. They use short "preambles" to wake up the intended listener (like knocking on a door to check if the receiver is awake).
>
> **Key Takeaway:** Contention-based protocols trade *guaranteed determinism* for *flexibility, scalability, and energy efficiency through duty-cycling*.

## 1.3 Why Contention-Based MAC is the Default for WSN

| Property | WSN Requirement | Contention-Based Solution |
|----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **Scalability** | Hundreds–thousands of nodes | Distributed, no central coordinator needed |
| **Topology Changes** | Nodes may die, new nodes added | Self-organizing CSMA handles mobility gracefully |
| **Traffic Pattern** | Bursty, low-duty-cycle event-driven | Random access suits sporadic transmissions |
| **Hardware Cost** | Cheap, low-power radios (e.g., CC2420) | Simple CSMA logic fits within MCU constraints |
| **Energy Budget** | Months–years on 2× AA batteries | Sleep/listen duty cycling achieves $\mu\text{A}$ idle current |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Mark-Worthy Definitions)

- **Duty Cycle ($D_C$):** Ratio of active (listen+transmit) time to total cycle time. Expressed as a **%** or fraction. WSN target: $D_C \leq 1\%$.
- **Energy per Bit ($E_b$):** Joules consumed to successfully deliver 1 bit, measured in **J/bit** or **nJ/bit**.
- **Throughput ($S$):** Successful packet deliveries per unit time, measured in **packets/sec** or **kbps**.
- **Latency ($L$):** End-to-end packet delivery delay, measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Collision Probability ($P_c$):** Probability that two or more nodes transmit simultaneously in the same slot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Contention-Based MAC Protocols for WSN

```
                    Contention-Based MAC
                            │
        ┌───────────────────┼────────────────────┐
   Synchronous (with SYNC)    Asynchronous (No SYNC)
        │                            │
   ┌────┴─────┐               ┌──────┴──────┐
 S-MAC       T-MAC           B-MAC       WiseMAC
```

## 2.2 Protocol-by-Protocol Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) — Ye et al., 2002

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-MAC is the most frequently examined protocol in KTU Module 1 questions. Memorize its frame structure.**

**Core Design Principles:**
1. **Periodic Listen and Sleep** — Nodes alternate between a *Listen* state and a *Sleep* state. The complete cycle is called a **frame**, consisting of:
   - **Listen Interval ($T_L$):** All neighboring nodes wake up to exchange SYNC + RTS/CTS packets.
   - **Sleep Interval ($T_S$):** Nodes turn off their radio to conserve energy.

2. **Collision Avoidance** — Uses physical/virtual carrier sense + RTS/CTS handshake to mitigate the hidden terminal problem.

3. **Overhearing Avoidance** — Nodes that overhear an RTS/CTS destined for another node go to sleep immediately.

4. **Message Passing** — Long messages are fragmented and transmitted as a burst using a single RTS/CTS handshake, reducing per-packet overhead.

**S-MAC Frame Structure (KTU diagram-favorite):**
```
|<-------- Listen Interval (T_L) -------->|<------- Sleep (T_S) ------->|
| SYNC |  CS   |   RTS/CTS/Data/ACK         |         RADIO OFF          |
|<-T_sync->|<-T_cs->|                         |                            |
```

**Mathematical Model:**
The **Duty Cycle** of S-MAC is given by:

$$
D_C^{S\text{-}MAC} = \frac{T_L}{T_L + T_S}
$$

If $T_{frame}$ = total frame duration, then $T_{frame} = T_L + T_S$, and the *energy savings* relative to always-on operation is $1 - D_C$.

### 2.2.2 T-MAC (Timeout-MAC) — van Dam & Langendoen, 2003

**Key Innovation:** T-MAC introduces an **adaptive listen window** that ends early if no activation event (data reception, channel busy) occurs for a timeout period $T_A$. This solves the *early sleeping problem* of S-MAC when traffic is variable.

**Timeout Logic:**
- After a synchronization or RTS event, the node starts a timer $T_A$.
- If no activity is detected within $T_A$, the node goes to sleep.
- $T_A$ is chosen such that: $T_A = C \cdot (R + T_{turn} + T_{frame})$

  Where:
  - $C$ = safety constant (typically 1.5–3)
  - $R$ = radio contention time
  - $T_{turn}$ = time to turn radio from Rx to Tx
  - $T_{frame}$ = time to transmit a single data frame

**Advantage over S-MAC:** Up to **5× energy reduction** under variable load.

### 2.2.3 B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC) — Polastre et al., 2004

**Core Idea:** B-MAC is a **CSMA-based asynchronous protocol** that uses **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** and **preamble sampling** (also called *Low Power Listening — LPL*).

**Operating Principle:**
- Each node wakes up briefly to sample the channel.
- The sender transmits a *long preamble* (length = sleep interval of receiver) to ensure the receiver detects traffic.
- The receiver, upon detecting the preamble, stays awake to receive the data frame.

**B-MAC Preamble Duration:**
$$
T_{preamble} = T_{sleep} = T_{CCA} \times N_{samples}
$$

Where $N_{samples}$ is the number of channel samples taken per cycle.

### 2.2.4 WiseMAC — El-Hoiydi & Decotignie, 2004

**Key Optimization:** WiseMAC reduces the preamble length by *learning* the receiver's sampling schedule from previous ACKs. After the first packet exchange, the sender knows exactly when the receiver will wake up and can transmit a **minimally-sized preamble**:

$$
T_{preamble}^{WiseMAC} = \theta \cdot L
$$

Where $\theta$ = clock drift rate (typically $\pm 50$ ppm) and $L$ = time since last synchronization. This is the most energy-efficient preamble-based scheme.

## 2.3 Energy Consumption Model (Unified)

For any duty-cycled MAC, the **average power consumption** is:

$$
P_{avg} = D_C \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D_C) \cdot P_{sleep}
$$

Where:
- $P_{active}$ = power during listen+tx+rx (typically **30–60 mW**)
- $P_{sleep}$ = power during sleep (typically **< 1 $\mu$W**)

The **total energy per bit** delivered:

$$
E_b = \frac{P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}}{L_{payload} \cdot (1 - P_e)}
$$

Where $P_e$ = packet error probability, $L_{payload}$ = payload size in bits.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize)

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Typical Value** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|-------------------|
| $D_C = \frac{T_L}{T_L + T_S}$ | S-MAC Duty Cycle | % (or fraction) | 1% – 10% |
| $T_{frame} = T_L + T_S$ | S-MAC Frame Duration | **seconds (s)** | 0.5 – 2 s |
| $P_{avg} = D_C \cdot P_{active} + (1-D_C) \cdot P_{sleep}$ | Average Power | **Watts (W)** or **mW** | $\mu$W to mW range |
| $E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy (battery model) | **Joules (J)** | Battery: 2× AA = 2200 mAh @ 3V |
| $T_{preamble} = T_{sleep}$ | B-MAC preamble length | s | equal to receiver sleep |
| $T_A = C(R + T_{turn} + T_{frame})$ | T-MAC timeout | s | C = 1.5 – 3 |
| $E_b = \frac{P_{avg} \cdot T_{frame}}{L_{payload}(1-P_e)}$ | Energy per successful bit | **J/bit** | 10–100 nJ/bit |
| $P_c = 1 - (1 - \tau)^n$ | Collision prob. (n nodes, prob $\tau$ of transmit) | dimensionless | depends on $\tau, n$ |
| $S = \frac{P_s \cdot L_{payload}}{T_{frame}}$ | Throughput | **bps** | kbps range |
| $L_{end\text{-}end} = h \cdot T_{frame}$ | End-to-end latency (h hops) | s | scales with $h$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The duty-cycle optimization is the single most influential technique that enables a MicaZ mote (powered by 2× AA batteries) to survive **>1 year** in a deployment such as **Great Duck Island habitat monitoring** (UC Berkeley, 2002) — the canonical WSN field test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: S-MAC Duty Cycle and Energy Savings

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A WSN node uses S-MAC with $T_L = 50$ ms listen interval and $T_S = 950$ ms sleep interval. The active power $P_{active} = 30$ mW and sleep power $P_{sleep} = 1.5\ \mu$W. Compute the duty cycle, average power, and energy saved over 1 hour versus an always-on node.

### Step 1: Compute Total Frame Duration

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= T_L + T_S \\
&= 50 \text{ ms} + 950 \text{ ms} \\
&= 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ second}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The listen and sleep intervals together form one complete duty-cycle period. A frame of 1 s is convenient for many WSN deployments.

### Step 2: Compute the Duty Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_C &= \frac{T_L}{T_{frame}} \\
&= \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{1000 \text{ ms}} \\
&= 0.05 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The radio is active only 5% of the time, which is the foundation of S-MAC's energy efficiency.

### Step 3: Compute the Average Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= D_C \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D_C) \cdot P_{sleep} \\
&= (0.05)(30 \text{ mW}) + (0.95)(1.5\ \mu\text{W}) \\
&= 1.5 \text{ mW} + 1.425\ \mu\text{W} \\
&\approx 1.5014 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The first term dominates because $P_{active} \gg P_{sleep}$ by **4 orders of magnitude** (30 mW vs 1.5 $\mu$W). The sleep term is nearly negligible.

### Step 4: Energy Consumed in 1 Hour (3600 s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{WSN} &= P_{avg} \cdot t \\
&= 1.5014 \text{ mW} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\
&= 5405.04 \text{ mJ} \approx 5.4 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Energy for Always-On Reference

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{always\text{-}on} &= P_{active} \cdot t = 30 \text{ mW} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 108 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Energy Savings Percentage

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \frac{E_{always\text{-}on} - E_{WSN}}{E_{always\text{-}on}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{108 - 5.4}{108} \times 100\% \\
&= 95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** S-MAC achieves **95% energy savings** with a 5% duty cycle — this is why duty cycling is the *single most important* concept in WSN MAC design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: S-MAC Latency with Multi-Hop Forwarding

**Problem:** A packet must traverse $h = 5$ hops. Each hop incurs a worst-case delay of $T_{frame}$ (sender wakes, sends, receiver wakes in next cycle). Compute the end-to-end latency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{end\text{-}end} &= h \cdot T_{frame} \\
&= 5 \times 1 \text{ s} \\
&= 5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the *fundamental trade-off* of duty cycling — energy efficiency is bought at the cost of multi-hop latency. KTU examiners love this trade-off question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: B-MAC Preamble Energy Optimization

**Problem:** A B-MAC node sleeps for $T_{sleep} = 500$ ms. Compute the optimal preamble length and the energy spent on the preamble.

**Step 1: Preamble Length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{preamble} &= T_{sleep} = 500 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The preamble must be at least as long as the receiver's sleep interval, so that no matter *when* the receiver wakes up, it will detect the preamble.

**Step 2: Preamble Energy (assuming Tx power $P_{tx} = 50$ mW)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{preamble} &= P_{tx} \cdot T_{preamble} \\
&= 50 \text{ mW} \times 0.5 \text{ s} \\
&= 25 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the *cost* of asynchronous B-MAC — the long preamble wastes energy. **WiseMAC** mitigates this by learning the receiver schedule.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison Table (HIGH-YIELD for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| **Parameter** | **S-MAC** | **T-MAC** | **B-MAC** | **WiseMAC** |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Sync Required** | Yes (SYNC packets) | Yes (SYNC packets) | No (Asynchronous) | No (Asynchronous) |
| **Clock Sync Overhead** | High | High | None | Minimal |
| **Preamble Length** | Short | Short | **Long** (= sleep time) | **Short** (learned) |
| **Energy Efficiency (Low Load)** | Moderate | Good | **Good** | **Best** |
| **Energy Efficiency (High Load)** | Moderate | Better | Poor (preamble overhearing) | Good |
| **Latency** | High (multi-frame) | Medium (early sleep) | Variable | Low (predictive wake) |
| **Scalability** | Good | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| **Hidden Terminal Handling** | RTS/CTS | RTS/CTS | CCA only | CCA only |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | **Very Low** | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice Problem (Full Solution)

**Q (KTU-style):** A 10-node WSN cluster uses T-MAC. Each node's timeout is set to $T_A = 25$ ms. The radio Rx-to-Tx turnaround is $T_{turn} = 2$ ms, frame transmission time $T_{frame} = 5$ ms, and contention time $R = 3$ ms. Find the safety constant $C$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_A &= C(R + T_{turn} + T_{frame}) \\
C &= \frac{T_A}{R + T_{turn} + T_{frame}} \\
C &= \frac{25 \text{ ms}}{3 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms}} \\
C &= \frac{25}{10} = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C = 2.5$ lies in the recommended range $[1.5, 3]$, the T-MAC configuration is **valid**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 S-MAC Listen/Sleep Cycle Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Node Boot / Power On] --> B[Initialize Radio at Listen State]
    B --> C[Listen Phase Begins T sub L]
    C --> D[Receive and Decode SYNC Packet]
    D --> E{Channel Busy Check CSMA}
    E -- Yes --> F[Wait Random Backoff]
    F --> E
    E -- No --> G[Transmit RTS or Receive CTS]
    G --> H[Exchange Data and ACK]
    H --> I[Enter Sleep Phase T sub S]
    I --> J[Radio Power OFF - Energy Saving]
    J --> K[Timer Expires after T sub S]
    K --> B

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.2 S-MAC Frame Timeline (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "S-MAC FRAME PERIOD"
        direction LR
        P1["SYNC Phase - All nodes exchange schedule - 10 ms"]
        P2["CS Phase - Contention window Carrier Sense - 5 ms"]
        P3["RTS / CTS / DATA / ACK Burst - 35 ms"]
        P4["SLEEP PHASE - Radio OFF - 950 ms"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P4 -.->|Timer Wake Up| P1

    style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style P2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style P3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style P4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 B-MAC Preamble Sampling Operation (Asynchronous Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Sender Has Data to Transmit] --> S2[Send Long Preamble T sleep duration]
    S2 --> S3[Receiver Wakes and Performs CCA]
    S3 --> S4{Channel Clear?}
    S4 -- No CCA Busy --> S5[Go Back to Sleep Retry Later]
    S4 -- Yes CCA Idle --> S6[Stay Awake - Preamble Detected]
    S6 --> S7[Receive Data Frame from Sender]
    S7 --> S8[Send ACK to Sender]
    S8 --> S9[Both Nodes Return to Sleep]

    R1[Receiver in Sleep] --> S3
    S5 -.->|Wait T sleep| R1

    style S1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style S2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style S3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style S6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style S9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.4 Hidden Terminal Problem and S-MAC Solution (Functional Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Node A Wishes to Send to Node B]
    B[Node C Hidden from A but in B Range]
    C[A Senses Channel - IDLE]
    D[A Transmits RTS to B]
    E[B Replies with CTS - Heard by A and C]
    F[C Defers Transmission After Hearing CTS]
    G[A and B Exchange DATA and ACK]
    H[Node C Remains Silent During Exchange]

    A --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    B -.->|Within Range| E
    B -.->|Aware of B Traffic| H
    G --> H

    style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style E fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.5 Energy vs. Latency Trade-off Block (Why Contention-Based MAC Exists)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AlwaysOn[Always On Radio - 100 percent duty cycle]
    SMAC[S-MAC 5 percent duty cycle - 95 percent energy saved - 5 s latency per hop]
    TMAC[T-MAC Adaptive - 3 to 5 percent duty cycle - early sleep]
    BMAC[B-MAC Asynchronous - 1 to 2 percent duty cycle - long preamble overhead]
    WiseMAC[WiseMAC Predictive - less than 1 percent duty cycle - low latency]

    AlwaysOn -->|Trade| SMAC
    SMAC -->|Trade| TMAC
    TMAC -->|Trade| BMAC
    BMAC -->|Trade| WiseMAC

    style AlwaysOn fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style SMAC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style TMAC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style BMAC fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style WiseMAC fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```
