# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define contention-based MAC protocol. List any two contention-based MAC protocols used in WSN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key-point style):**

> A contention-based MAC protocol is a distributed channel access mechanism in which multiple sensor nodes compete for the shared wireless medium using carrier sensing and random back-off procedures, without pre-assigned time slots.
>
> *Definition (1 mark):* Contention-based MAC = CSMA/CA family protocols where nodes "listen-before-talk" and use random back-off upon collision.
>
> *Examples (2 marks, 1 each):*
> 1. **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC)** — Uses periodic synchronized listen/sleep cycles with SYNC, RTS/CTS handshakes.
> 2. **B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC)** — Uses asynchronous Clear Channel Assessment (CCA) and long preamble sampling for low power listening.
>
> *(Optional valid alternative: T-MAC, WiseMAC, Sift-MAC)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is duty cycling in WSN MAC protocols? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Duty Cycling (1 mark):** Duty cycling is the technique by which a sensor node periodically switches its radio between an *active* (listen/transmit) state and a *sleep* (radio off) state to minimize energy consumption.
>
> **Importance (2 marks — any two):**
> 1. **Energy Efficiency:** Idle listening consumes nearly as much power as reception. Sleeping reduces average power by orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Network Lifetime:** With $D_C \leq 5\%$, a MicaZ mote (2× AA battery, 2200 mAh) can operate for 1+ years instead of days.
> 3. **Heat/Thermal Management:** Lower duty cycle = less radio heating = stable oscillator performance.
> 4. **Collision Reduction:** Sleeping nodes cannot contend, reducing $P_c$ (collision probability) in dense deployments.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the design principles and operation of the S-MAC protocol. Discuss its energy-saving mechanisms and limitations. [14 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): S-MAC Design Principles and Operation [7 marks]

S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) was proposed by Ye, Heidemann, and Estrin (2002) specifically for energy-constrained WSN. It introduces four core design principles:

**1. Periodic Listen and Sleep (2 marks):**
Each node independently chooses a listen/sleep schedule. Neighboring nodes synchronize their schedules by exchanging **SYNC packets** during the listen interval. The frame structure is:

$$
T_{frame} = T_{Listen} + T_{Sleep}
$$

Typical values: $T_{Listen} = 50$ ms, $T_{Sleep} = 950$ ms, giving a duty cycle of 5%.

**2. Collision Avoidance using RTS/CTS (2 marks):**
Within the contention window (CS phase) of the listen interval, nodes perform physical carrier sensing and exchange Request-To-Send (RTS) and Clear-To-Send (CTS) handshakes. The hidden terminal problem is mitigated because:
- Node A sends RTS to B.
- B's CTS is heard by all neighbors (including hidden terminals).
- Hidden nodes defer their transmissions until the exchange ends.

**3. Overhearing Avoidance (1.5 marks):**
When a node overhears an RTS or CTS not addressed to itself, it immediately enters sleep mode for the duration indicated in the Duration field of the frame. This saves energy in dense networks.

**4. Message Passing (1.5 marks):**
Long application messages are fragmented into smaller MAC frames. All fragments are transmitted as a single burst with only one RTS/CTS handshake, reducing control overhead and per-packet energy cost.

#### Part (b): Energy-Saving Mechanisms and Limitations [7 marks]

**Energy-Saving Mechanisms (4 marks):**

| Mechanism | Energy Saved From |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Sleep state | Idle listening (biggest energy sink) |
| Overhearing avoidance | Receiving packets for other nodes |
| Message passing | Per-packet RTS/CTS overhead |
| Carrier sensing | Collisions and retransmissions |

The average power is:

$$
P_{avg} = D_C \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D_C) \cdot P_{sleep} \approx D_C \cdot P_{active}
$$

For $D_C = 5\%$, $P_{active} = 30$ mW: $P_{avg} \approx 1.5$ mW — a **95% reduction**.

**Limitations of S-MAC (3 marks):**

1. **Energy cost of synchronization:** SYNC packets consume bandwidth and energy; clocks drift between SYNC intervals.
2. **Fixed listen interval:** Cannot adapt to variable traffic → leads to *idle listening* (low load) or *early sleeping* (high load).
3. **High multi-hop latency:** Worst-case delay = $h \times T_{frame}$, where $h$ is the number of hops.
4. **Scalability issue with virtual clusters:** Border nodes belonging to two schedules lose more energy due to overlapping wake-ups.
5. **Difficult to deploy across heterogeneous networks** with different traffic patterns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With neat block diagrams, explain the operation of B-MAC and WiseMAC protocols. Compare their performance. [14 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): B-MAC Operation [7 marks]

**B-MAC (Berkeley MAC)** is an asynchronous, CSMA-based protocol developed at UC Berkeley (Polastre et al., 2004). It uses **Low Power Listening (LPL)** with preamble sampling.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
1. Each node independently chooses a sleep duration $T_{sleep}$ (e.g., 500 ms).
2. After sleeping, the node wakes briefly to perform **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** — typically a single RSSI sample lasting ~250 $\mu$s.
3. **Sender side:** When a node has data, it transmits a long preamble of duration $T_{preamble} = T_{sleep}$ followed by the data frame.
4. **Receiver side:** The receiver's CCA samples detect the preamble → node stays awake → receives data → sends ACK.
5. If the channel is idle, the node goes back to sleep.

**Block Diagram of B-MAC Cycle:**
```
[Node A: Sleep] --(T_sleep = 500 ms)--> [CCA Sample] --(Idle)--> [Sleep again]
[Node B: Sender] --(Begin Tx)--> [Preamble: 500 ms] --[Data]--> [ACK]
```

**Key Features (2 marks):**
- No synchronization overhead.
- Excellent scalability — works in dense deployments.
- Configurable sleep interval per application.

**Drawback (2 marks):**
- Long preamble wastes energy at sender.
- Heavily asymmetric: sender pays much more energy than receiver.
- Latency = $T_{sleep}/2$ on average (variable).

#### Part (b): WiseMAC Operation and Comparison [7 marks]

**WiseMAC Operation (4 marks):**
WiseMAC optimizes B-MAC by *learning* the receiver's sampling schedule:

1. Each ACK from the receiver contains the time-stamp of its next scheduled CCA.
2. The sender records this in a table indexed by destination address.
3. On next transmission, the sender computes when the receiver will wake and transmits a **minimally-sized preamble**:

$$
T_{preamble}^{WiseMAC} = \theta \cdot L + T_{processing}
$$

Where $\theta$ = clock drift rate, $L$ = elapsed time since last ACK. Clock drift in typical WSN oscillators is **±50 ppm**, so the preamble can be as short as a few milliseconds — a **100× reduction** vs B-MAC.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | B-MAC | WiseMAC |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| Preamble length | $T_{sleep}$ (long) | $\theta \cdot L$ (short) |
| Energy per packet (sender) | High | **Much lower** |
| Energy per packet (receiver) | Low | Low (same) |
| Clock sync required | No | No (only drift estimate) |
| Adaptability to load | Poor | Good |
| Storage | None | Per-destination schedule table |
| Best for | Periodic traffic | **Event-driven / variable traffic** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Contention-Based MAC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing S-MAC with S-ALOHA or Pure ALOHA** — S-MAC uses *CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS*, not pure ALOHA. Writing "S-MAC is a pure ALOHA protocol" costs **2 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Forgetting the SYNC phase in S-MAC diagrams** — Every S-MAC frame MUST show the SYNC sub-phase separately. Generic "listen/sleep" diagrams lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Not justifying the duty cycle formula** — A formula alone is worth 1 mark; the *physical interpretation* (e.g., "sleep state saves 95% energy") is worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up B-MAC and WiseMAC** — B-MAC uses a *fixed* long preamble; WiseMAC uses a *learned short* preamble. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Numerical units** — Always specify $D_C$ in **% or fraction**; never leave it dimensionless.
> 6. **Hidden Terminal explanation** — When asked, draw the triangle (A, B, hidden C) and explicitly mention that *CTS solves it* because CTS is broadcast.
> 7. **Last-page summary omitted** — In 14-mark answers, the conclusion table comparing protocols is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap: Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 MUST-KNOW bullet points for KTU Module 1 — Contention-Based MAC:**
>
> ✅ **Contention-based MAC = CSMA/CA family with listen-before-talk + random back-off** (NOT TDMA, NOT pure ALOHA).
>
> ✅ **S-MAC (2002):** Synchronous, periodic listen/sleep, 4 design principles (Periodic sleep, Collision avoidance, Overhearing avoidance, Message passing). Duty cycle $D_C = T_L / (T_L + T_S)$.
>
> ✅ **T-MAC (2003):** Adaptive timeout $T_A = C(R + T_{turn} + T_{frame})$; solves early-sleeping problem; $C \in [1.5, 3]$.
>
> ✅ **B-MAC (2004):** Asynchronous, CCA-based, Low Power Listening with long preamble $T_{preamble} = T_{sleep}$. No sync overhead.
>
> ✅ **WiseMAC (2004):** Preamble optimization via receiver schedule learning; $T_{preamble} = \theta L$ where $\theta$ = clock drift (50 ppm).
>
> ✅ **Duty Cycling** is THE foundational WSN energy-saving technique — 95% energy savings achievable with $D_C = 5\%$.
>
> ✅ **Hidden Terminal Problem:** Solved by RTS/CTS handshake (CTS is broadcast and reaches hidden nodes).
>
> ✅ **Average Power Formula:** $P_{avg} = D_C \cdot P_{active} + (1-D_C) \cdot P_{sleep}$.
>
> ✅ **Latency Trade-off:** $L_{end\text{-}end} = h \cdot T_{frame}$ — energy savings bought with multi-hop delay.
>
> ✅ **Protocol Selection Rule of Thumb:**
>   * Synchronized, periodic traffic → **S-MAC**
>   * Variable/bursty traffic → **T-MAC**
>   * Asynchronous, no-sync needed → **B-MAC**
>   * Energy-critical + predictive traffic → **WiseMAC**
>
> ✅ **Key WSN MAC metrics:** Duty Cycle (%), Energy per bit (nJ/bit), Latency (s), Throughput (kbps), Collision Probability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contention Free
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Contention Free # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), **Contention-Free Medium Access Control (MAC) protocols** are a class of channel access mechanisms in which the shared wireless medium is partitioned among sensor nodes using a **deterministic, pre-scheduled, or centrally coordinated allocation scheme**. Each node is granted an exclusive transmission opportunity (time slot, frequency band, or code) such that **no two nodes transmit simultaneously on the same channel resource**, thereby eliminating collisions by construction rather than by recovery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Contention-free protocols are categorized under **Scheduled MAC Protocols** in the KTU 2024 PECAT634 syllabus, fundamentally relying on **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)**, **Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)**, or **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** slot/code allocation. Mathematically, a contention-free MAC protocol guarantees: $$ P_{collision} = 0 \quad \text{(Zero collision probability by design)} $$ This is a strict contrast to contention-based protocols (e.g., CSMA/CA, S-MAC, B-MAC) where: $$ P_{collision} > 0 \quad \text{with avoidance/recovery mechanisms active} $$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: A Class Timetable (Indian Engineering Classroom)** Imagine an engineering classroom with **50 students (sensor nodes)** and **only 1 microphone (the shared wireless channel)**. - **Contention-Based (CSMA/CA):** Every student shouts "I want to talk!" and waits for silence. If two students talk at once, they both stop and retry. This is chaotic but doesn't need a timetable. Lots of wasted energy arguing. - **Contention-Free (TDMA):** The professor creates a **timetable**. Student 1 speaks from 9:00–9:01, Student 2 from 9:01–9:02, and so on. No one ever collides. Energy is **only** spent when it's your slot — the radio sleeps the rest of the time. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Sensor nodes spend most of their life sleeping to conserve battery. Contention-free protocols are **naturally compatible with duty cycling** because a node knows *exactly* when to wake up — it doesn't have to keep listening for a clear channel. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in WSN The following parameters are critical benchmarks in KTU board examinations for contention-free WSN protocols: | Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Engineering Significance | |-----------|------------------------|--------------------------| | Channel Bandwidth | **2.4 GHz ISM band** | Unlicensed industrial band | | Data Rate (IEEE 802.15.4) | **250 kbps** | O-QPSK modulation baseline | | Frame Duration ($T_{frame}$) | Variable (ms to seconds) | Total round of slots | | Slot Duration ($T_{slot}$) | $T_{frame}/N$ | Per-node allocation unit | | Duty Cycle | **< 1%** in WSN | Sleep-to-active ratio | | Node Energy (typical) | **0.5 Ah, 1.2 V** | AA battery baseline | | Idle Listening Cost | **~50–100%** of TX cost | Avoided by scheduling | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Contention-Free Access The contention-free MAC protocol operates in **four logical phases**. Understanding these steps is mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivation questions. 1. **Topology Discovery / Cluster Formation** - Nodes self-organize (peer-to-peer) or form clusters around a **Cluster Head (CH)**. - Each node acquires a unique identity (Node ID, $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$). 2. **Schedule Construction (Slot Assignment)** - A central entity (Base Station / CH) or distributed algorithm assigns each node $i$ a **slot number** $s_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$. - Constraints enforced: **no two neighbors share the same slot** (interference avoidance). 3. **Synchronized Transmission** - All nodes maintain a common time reference (via beacon frames or sync packets). - Node $i$ transmits **only** during slot $s_i$; radio is OFF in all other slots. 4. **Reception & Sleep** - Intended receiver wakes up in the transmitter's slot; all other nodes sleep. - Frame repeats every $T_{frame}$ seconds. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Contention-Free"?** The word *contention* literally means "arguing over who gets to speak." By pre-allocating turns, we **remove the argument entirely** — hence "contention-free." --- ## 2.2 Classification of Contention-Free Protocols (KTU High-Yield) | Protocol | Type | Slot Allocation Method | Special Feature | |----------|------|------------------------|-----------------| | **SMACS** | Hybrid (contention + scheduled) | Distributed neighbor discovery | Self-organizing | | **LEACH-TDMA** | Clustered TDMA | Centralized (CH) | Energy-balanced clustering | | **TRAMA** | Distributed TDMA | Traffic adaptive | NP-based election | | **DEE-MAC** | Distributed | Energy-aware | Dynamic slot reuse | | **EMACS** | Clustered | Static / Hybrid | Multi-channel support | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important revision asset** for KTU board exams on this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions. | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes | |---------|---------|----------------|---------------| | Number of slots per frame | $N_{slots} = N$ (for N nodes) | $N$ = total nodes in cluster | Dimensionless integer | | Frame duration | $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot}$ | $T_{slot}$ = per-slot transmission time | **seconds (s)** | | Slot duration | $T_{slot} = T_{frame} / N$ | — | **s** | | Per-node throughput | $\eta = \frac{L}{N \cdot T_{slot}}$ | $L$ = packet size in bits | **bits/s** | | Maximum channel utilization | $U_{max} = 1$ (ideal, no overhead) | — | Dimensionless | | Effective utilization | $U_{eff} = \frac{T_{data}}{T_{frame}}$ | $T_{data}$ = useful TX time | $\leq 1$ | | Energy per bit | $E_b = \frac{I \cdot V \cdot T_{slot}}{L}$ | $I$ = TX current, $V$ = supply voltage | **Joules/bit** | | Total frame energy (TX only) | $E_{frame} = N \cdot I \cdot V \cdot T_{slot}$ | All nodes transmit once | **Joules** | | Duty cycle | $DC = \frac{T_{slot}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{N}$ | Fraction of time node is active | $\ll 1$ | | Collision probability | $P_c = 0$ | By design | — | | Throughput cap (TDMA) | $S_{max} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot R$ | $R$ = raw channel rate | **bps** | | Spectral efficiency (FDMA) | $\eta_f = \frac{N \cdot \log_2(1+SNR)}{B_{total}}$ | $B_{total}$ = total bandwidth | **bps/Hz** | | Code capacity (CDMA) | $N_{users} \leq G_p / E_b$ | $G_p$ = processing gain | Approx. limit | **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:** - $N \geq 1$ (integer) - $T_{frame} \gg T_{slot}$ (frame must be large enough for clock drift) - $P_c = 0$ is the **defining constraint** — never violated in a true contention-free protocol. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Contention-free MAC protocols power: - **Medical Body Area Networks (MBANs):** Pacemakers and insulin pumps transmit on strict schedules to avoid interference with life-critical signals. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT) WirelessHART / ISA100.11a:** Use TDMA over 2.4 GHz with channel hopping. - **Smart Grid AMI:** Smart meters report via scheduled slots to avoid mass collision events. - **Underwater Acoustic WSNs:** Long propagation delays (≈1500 m/s) make CSMA inefficient; scheduled access is mandatory. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Throughput and Delay in Pure TDMA **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):** A WSN cluster has $N = 10$ sensor nodes operating in TDMA mode. Each node generates one packet of $L = 128$ bytes per frame. The raw channel rate is $R = 250$ kbps. Compute the frame duration, slot duration, and average packet delay. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.** $$ N = 10, \quad L = 128 \text{ bytes} = 128 \times 8 = 1024 \text{ bits}, \quad R = 250 \text{ kbps} = 250 \times 10^3 \text{ bps} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the slot duration $T_{slot}$.** The time required to transmit one packet at the raw channel rate is: $$ T_{slot} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1024 \text{ bits}}{250 \times 10^3 \text{ bps}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{slot} &= \frac{1024}{250000} \text{ s} \\[4pt] &= 0.004096 \text{ s} \\[4pt] &= 4.096 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* Dividing bits by bits-per-second yields seconds (by dimensional analysis: $\frac{[\text{bits}]}{[\text{bits/s}]} = [\text{s}]$). **Step 3 — Compute the frame duration $T_{frame}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{frame} &= N \cdot T_{slot} \\[4pt] &= 10 \times 4.096 \text{ ms} \\[4pt] &= 40.96 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* Each of the 10 nodes occupies one exclusive slot within the frame, so the frame is the sum of all individual slot times. **Step 4 — Compute per-node throughput $\eta$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{L}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1024 \text{ bits}}{40.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} \\[4pt] &= 25 \times 10^3 \text{ bps} = 25 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* This is exactly $R/N$, confirming the per-node throughput cap of a pure TDMA system. **Step 5 — Compute the average packet delay $D_{avg}$.** In a uniformly scheduled TDMA, a packet generated uniformly at random must wait on average half a frame: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{avg} &= \frac{T_{frame}}{2} = \frac{40.96 \text{ ms}}{2} \\[4pt] &= 20.48 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* A packet arriving just after its assigned slot must wait a near-full frame; one arriving just before waits almost zero. Uniform distribution over the frame gives the mean of $T_{frame}/2$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Consumption Comparison (Contention-Free vs. Contention-Based) **Setup:** A node with TX current $I_{tx} = 20$ mA, RX current $I_{rx} = 10$ mA, and sleep current $I_{sleep} = 1$ $\mu$A, powered by $V = 3$ V. Frame period $T_{frame} = 1$ s, $T_{slot} = 0.1$ s. ### Energy in Contention-Free Mode Node is active for **1 slot**, sleeps for **9 slots**: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{CF} &= V \cdot I_{tx} \cdot T_{slot} + V \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot (T_{frame} - T_{slot}) \\[4pt] &= 3 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.1 + 3 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 0.9 \\[4pt] &= 6 \times 10^{-3} + 2.7 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt] &\approx 6.003 \text{ mJ per frame} \end{aligned} $$ ### Energy in Contention-Based (CSMA) Mode Node must listen for an average of **0.5 frame** (50% idle listening) before transmitting: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{CB} &= V \cdot I_{rx} \cdot 0.5 \cdot T_{frame} + V \cdot I_{tx} \cdot T_{slot} + V \cdot I_{rx} \cdot 0.4 \cdot T_{frame} \\[4pt] &\approx V \cdot I_{rx} \cdot 0.9 \cdot T_{frame} + V \cdot I_{tx} \cdot T_{slot} \\[4pt] &= 3 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.9 + 3 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.1 \\[4pt] &= 27 \times 10^{-3} + 6 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt] &= 33 \text{ mJ per frame} \end{aligned} $$ ### Energy Saving Ratio $$ \text{Saving} = \frac{E_{CB} - E_{CF}}{E_{CB}} = \frac{33 - 6.003}{33} \approx 81.8\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inference for KTU:** Contention-free MAC protocols save **~80% energy** in typical WSN by eliminating idle listening — the dominant energy waste source. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Contention-Free vs. Contention-Based | Parameter | Contention-Free (TDMA) | Contention-Based (CSMA) | |-----------|------------------------|-------------------------| | Collision Probability | **$P_c = 0$** | $P_c > 0$ | | Idle Listening | **None** | High (50–100%) | | Energy Efficiency | **High** | Low to Moderate | | Scalability | Limited by slot count $N$ | Naturally scalable | | Synchronization | **Strictly Required** | Not required | | Latency | Bounded ($T_{frame}/2$) | Variable | | Throughput @ High Load | **Predictable** | Collapses | | Throughput @ Low Load | Underutilized (slots empty) | Efficient | | Complexity | Schedule management | Carrier sensing | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 TDMA Frame Structure (Mermaid Gantt-style Flow) ```mermaid gantt title TDMA Frame Structure for N=8 Sensor Nodes dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Frame Slot 0 (Node 1) :node1, 0, 1 Slot 1 (Node 2) :node2, 1, 1 Slot 2 (Node 3) :node3, 2, 1 Slot 3 (Node 4) :node4, 3, 1 Slot 4 (Node 5) :node5, 4, 1 Slot 5 (Node 6) :node6, 5, 1 Slot 6 (Node 7) :node7, 6, 1 Slot 7 (Node 8) :node8, 7, 1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Contention-Free MAC Operational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Network Boot / Topology Discovery] --> B{Cluster Head Exists?} B -- Yes --> C[CH Collects Node IDs] B -- No --> D[Self-Organize Distributed Schedule] C --> E[Centralized Slot Assignment] D --> E E --> F[Slot Map Broadcast to All Nodes] F --> G[Time Synchronization via Beacon] G --> H{My Slot Active?} H -- Yes --> I[Transmit Packet] H -- No --> J[Radio in SLEEP Mode] I --> K[Wait for ACK or Next Frame] J --> L[Next Frame Starts] K --> L L --> H ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Contention-Free WSN Node ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX_Path A1[Application Layer
Sensor Data] --> A2[MAC Layer
Slot Scheduler] A2 --> A3[Radio Frontend
2.4 GHz TX] end subgraph RX_Path B1[Radio Frontend
RX only in assigned slot] --> B2[MAC Layer
Slot Filter] B2 --> B3[Application Layer
Data Sink] end subgraph CTRL[Controller] C1[Real-Time Clock
Slot Timer] --> A2 C1 --> B2 end subgraph PWR[Power Management Unit] P1[Battery 3V] --> P2[Voltage Regulator] P2 --> TX_Path P2 --> RX_Path P2 --> CTRL end ``` **Block Interpretation for KTU Viva:** - The **Slot Scheduler** is the heart of the contention-free design — it consults the **Real-Time Clock** and only energizes the radio at the assigned slot. - All other blocks remain in **deep sleep** ($I_{sleep} \approx 1\,\mu$A), maximizing battery life. --- ## 4.4 Slot Reuse Concept (Spatial Reuse for Scalability) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CL_A[Cluster A - Far Region] NA1[Node 1A] --- NA2[Node 2A] NA2 --- NA3[Node 3A] end subgraph CL_B[Cluster B - Far Region] NB1[Node 1B] --- NB2[Node 2B] end CL_A -- Interference = 0 --> CL_B NA1 -. Same Slot 0 Reuse .-> NB1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept:** Two nodes in geographically separated clusters may reuse the same time slot if their transmissions **do not interfere** (i.e., they are out of radio range). This is called **spatial TDMA reuse** and is a 14-mark favorite topic in KTU exams. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define contention-free MAC protocol. Give two examples.** **Model Answer:** A contention-free MAC protocol is a medium access mechanism in which channel resources (time slots, frequencies, or codes) are **pre-allocated to sensor nodes**, ensuring that no two nodes transmit simultaneously. This eliminates collisions by design. **Two Examples:** 1. **TDMA-based LEACH** — clusters use scheduled slots. 2. **TRAMA (Traffic-Adaptive Medium Access)** — distributed contention-free scheduling. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why are contention-free protocols preferred in energy-constrained WSNs?** **Model Answer:** Contention-free protocols are preferred because they **eliminate idle listening** and **collision-induced retransmissions**, the two dominant energy wastes in WSNs. By knowing the exact transmission slot, a node can keep its radio in sleep mode for nearly the entire frame, achieving duty cycles below 1%. They also provide **bounded latency** and **deterministic throughput**, which are essential for monitoring and control applications. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a TDMA-based contention-free MAC protocol for WSN with a neat frame structure diagram. Derive the expression for frame duration and per-node throughput. **(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN cluster has 20 nodes, each transmitting a 256-byte packet. Channel rate is 250 kbps. Compute the frame duration, slot duration, per-node throughput, and average delay. Comment on scalability. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Working of TDMA-based Contention-Free MAC:** 1. **Topology Setup:** The cluster head (CH) or base station collects node IDs $\{1, 2, \dots, N\}$. 2. **Slot Assignment:** Each node $i$ is assigned a unique slot $s_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$. 3. **Frame Construction:** A frame of duration $T_{frame}$ consists of $N$ consecutive slots: $$ T_{frame} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{slot,i} = N \cdot T_{slot} $$ 4. **Transmission:** Node $i$ transmits in slot $s_i$; sleeps in all others. 5. **Reception:** The destination node wakes only in $s_i$ to receive. 6. **Throughput Derivation:** The per-node throughput is: $$ \eta = \frac{L}{T_{frame}} = \frac{L}{N \cdot T_{slot}} = \frac{R}{N} $$ where $R = L/T_{slot}$ is the raw channel rate. The system is **collision-free by construction**. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $N = 20$, $L = 256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits}$, $R = 250 \text{ kbps}$. **Step 1 — Slot Duration:** $$ T_{slot} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2048}{250 \times 10^3} = 8.192 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Frame Duration:** $$ T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot} = 20 \times 8.192 = 163.84 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Per-Node Throughput:** $$ \eta = \frac{R}{N} = \frac{250 \text{ kbps}}{20} = 12.5 \text{ kbps} $$ **Step 4 — Average Delay:** $$ D_{avg} = \frac{T_{frame}}{2} = \frac{163.84}{2} = 81.92 \text{ ms} $$ **Scalability Comment:** As $N$ increases, $\eta$ decreases linearly and $D_{avg}$ grows linearly. For $N = 1000$, $\eta$ drops to **250 bps** — impractical for bandwidth-hungry applications. **Spatial TDMA reuse** (clustering + slot reuse) is the standard scalability remedy. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare contention-free and contention-based MAC protocols for WSN. Which is more energy-efficient and why? **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable energy model, show analytically that a contention-free TDMA protocol consumes significantly less energy than a CSMA-based protocol in a typical WSN scenario. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | Contention-Free (TDMA) | Contention-Based (CSMA) | |-----------|------------------------|-------------------------| | Collision | $P_c = 0$ | $P_c > 0$ | | Idle Listening | Zero (radio sleeps) | High | | Synchronization | Required | Not required | | Scalability | Limited (slot count) | High | | Latency | Bounded, deterministic | Variable | | Energy Efficiency | **High** | Low | | Complexity | Schedule management | Carrier sensing | | Suitability | Periodic monitoring | Event-driven | **Conclusion:** Contention-free is **more energy-efficient** in WSNs because the dominant energy waste — idle listening — is structurally eliminated. **Part (b) — Analytical Energy Comparison (7 marks):** Consider a frame $T_{frame} = 1$ s, $N = 10$ nodes, $V = 3$ V, $I_{tx} = 20$ mA, $I_{rx} = 10$ mA, $I_{sleep} = 1\,\mu$A, packet time $T_{slot} = 0.1$ s. **TDMA (Contention-Free):** Node active for 1 slot, sleeping for 9 slots. $$ E_{TDMA} = V I_{tx} T_{slot} + V I_{sleep} (T_{frame} - T_{slot}) $$ $$ E_{TDMA} = 3 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.1 + 3 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 0.9 = 6.003 \text{ mJ} $$ **CSMA (Contention-Based):** Node listens for ~50% of frame, transmits, then waits for ACK. $$ E_{CSMA} = V I_{rx} \cdot 0.5 T_{frame} + V I_{tx} T_{slot} + V I_{rx} \cdot 0.4 T_{frame} = 33 \text{ mJ} $$ **Energy Saving:** $$ \Delta E = \frac{E_{CSMA} - E_{TDMA}}{E_{CSMA}} = \frac{33 - 6.003}{33} \approx 81.8\% $$ Thus, contention-free TDMA saves ~82% energy per frame per node — a **strong analytical proof** of its superiority for energy-constrained WSNs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting units:** Marks deducted if you write $T_{slot} = 4.096$ without stating "ms" or "seconds." > 2. **Confusing $T_{frame}$ with $T_{slot}$:** Frame = sum of all slots. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Claiming CSMA is "collision-free":** Strictly false — CSMA only *avoids* collisions; it does not guarantee zero. > 4. **Ignoring idle listening energy:** Many students compute only TX energy and skip the RX/listen component — this loses 3 marks in the energy derivation. > 5. **Forgetting synchronization cost:** Real TDMA requires sync beacons; neglecting this loses the practical reasoning mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Contention-Free MAC** - **Definition:** Contention-free MAC = pre-allocated channel access, $P_c = 0$ by design. - **Core Mechanism:** TDMA / FDMA / CDMA-based slot or resource allocation. - **Key Protocol Examples:** SMACS, LEACH-TDMA, TRAMA, DEE-MAC. - **Frame Formula:** $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot}$ — non-negotiable. - **Throughput Formula:** $\eta = R / N$ — per-node cap. - **Delay:** $D_{avg} = T_{frame}/2$ — uniform arrival assumption. - **Energy Edge:** Contention-free saves **~80% energy** by killing idle listening. - **Drawback:** Strict time synchronization required; clustering needed for scalability. - **Spatial Reuse:** Geographically separated nodes can reuse slots — key scalability lever. - **Comparison Hook:** $P_c = 0$ (CF) vs. $P_c > 0$ (CB); bounded (CF) vs. variable (CB) delay. - **Real-World:** WirelessHART, ISA100.11a, MBANs all use contention-free scheduled access. - **Standard Band:** 2.4 GHz ISM, 250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4 baseline) — quote these in numerical answers. - **Duty Cycle Target:** $< 1\%$ in WSN — achievable only with scheduled access. - **Exam Trick Question:** "Is TDMA collision-free?" — Answer: Yes, by construction ($P_c = 0$). - **Last-Line Motto:** *"In contention-free MAC, you do not ask for the channel — you are told when to speak."*

topics.title: Hybrid Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**, **Hybrid Routing Protocols** are a class of data dissemination and routing algorithms that strategically combine the operational principles of two or more fundamental routing paradigms — namely **proactive (table-driven)**, **reactive (on-demand)**, **location-based**, and **hierarchical (cluster-based)** schemes — within a single unified protocol stack to overcome the individual shortcomings of each pure approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Hybrid protocols in WSN are defined as routing mechanisms that exploit the strengths of both *flat routing* (proactive/reactive) and *hierarchical/cluster-based routing* (LEACH-family), or the synergy of *proactive + reactive* routing (ZRP-family), to deliver energy-aware, scalable, and low-latency data delivery across a resource-constrained sensor field.

The three principal hybrid families studied in KTU Module 1 are:

| Hybrid Family | Core Hybridization Strategy | Representative Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone-Based Hybrid** | Proactive *inside* a zone, Reactive *across* zones | **ZRP** (Zone Routing Protocol) |
| **Cluster-Hierarchical Hybrid** | Reactive event-driven + Proactive periodic cluster rotation | **LEACH, TEEN, APTEEN** |
| **Data-Centric Negotiation Hybrid** | Meta-data negotiation + On-demand data query | **SPIN, Directed Diffusion** |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postal System"

Imagine a city where every household is a **sensor node**:

- **Pure Proactive (Like a daily newspaper)** — Every household receives a complete route map of the entire city *every day*, even if they only need one address. **Wasteful** for battery-powered nodes.
- **Pure Reactive (Like a one-time courier)** — A courier is summoned *only* when a delivery is needed, but the courier has to *search* the entire city from scratch. **Slow** and energy-expensive for far destinations.
- **Hybrid (Like a regional courier hub)** — Each neighborhood has a **local postmaster** (proactive within the zone) who knows every house in his area. For deliveries *outside* the neighborhood, the package is forwarded to a **regional hub** (reactive inter-zone lookup), which efficiently routes it to the right local postmaster at the destination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Hybrid protocols are designed on the engineering maxim: *"Spend energy where it pays off; save energy where it doesn't."* Proactive overhead is contained to a small neighborhood, while reactive scanning is restricted to a sparse backbone.

---

### 1.3 Why Hybrid Protocols? — Motivation

Pure routing paradigms suffer from well-known deficiencies:

- **Proactive** → Continuous route maintenance → High control overhead → Unsuitable for energy-constrained, large-scale WSN.
- **Reactive** → Route discovery floods the network per query → High latency → Not suitable for time-critical monitoring.
- **Hierarchical/Clustered** → Requires cluster-head election overhead and synchronization.

**Hybrid protocols** resolve this by **adaptive scope selection** — applying the right paradigm at the right network layer or geographical region.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently award marks for explicitly stating *why* a hybrid design is necessary. Always cite the **energy-latency trade-off** as the principal motivation.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard WSN Metrics (Bolded)

The following constants are the de-facto standard values adopted in the **First-Order Radio Model** (Heinzelman, 2000), universally used in KTU numerical problems:

- **Transceiver Electronics Energy:** $E_{elec} = 50 \; nJ/bit$
- **Free-Space Amplifier Energy:** $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \; pJ/bit/m^2$
- **Multipath Fading Amplifier Energy:** $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \; pJ/bit/m^4$
- **Data Aggregation Energy:** $E_{DA} = 5 \; nJ/bit/signal$
- **Default Packet Size:** $k = 4000 \; bits$
- **Threshold Distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}} = 87.7 \; m$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Hybrid Protocol Families — Deep Analysis

#### **Family 1: ZRP (Zone Routing Protocol)**

ZRP, proposed by *Haas & Pearlman (1999)*, partitions the network into **overlapping routing zones** of radius $\rho$ (measured in hops).

- **Intra-Zone (IARP — Intrazone Routing Protocol):** Uses a **proactive** strategy. Every node maintains a routing table for all nodes within its $\rho$-hop neighborhood. Updated periodically.
- **Inter-Zone (IERP — Interzone Routing Protocol):** Uses a **reactive** strategy. Route queries are propagated outward until they hit a peripheral node that has the destination in its proactive routing table.
- **Bordercast Resolution Protocol (BRP):** A query-control mechanism that prevents flooding by using a multicast tree of *border nodes* (nodes exactly $\rho$ hops away).

**Why it works:**
- Proactive cost is bounded by zone size, not the whole network → $O(\rho^2)$ overhead per node instead of $O(N)$.
- Reactive cost is paid only for cross-zone traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> The **zone radius $\rho$** is a critical tunable parameter. Small $\rho$ → behaves reactively (high delay). Large $\rho$ → behaves proactively (high energy cost). Optimal $\rho$ is typically **2–5 hops** for dense WSNs.

---

#### **Family 2: LEACH (Low Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)**

LEACH (Heinzelman et al., 2000) is a **cluster-based, self-organizing, adaptive** hybrid protocol. It rotates cluster-head (CH) responsibility to distribute energy dissipation.

**Two Operational Phases:**

1. **Setup Phase (Proactive/Cluster-Formation):**
   - Each node generates a random number $r \in [0,1]$.
   - If $r < T(n)$, the node becomes a CH for the current round $r$.
   - The threshold function:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left(r \bmod \dfrac{1}{p}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $p$ is the desired CH percentage (typically 5%), $r$ is the current round, and $G$ is the set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds.

2. **Steady-State Phase (Reactive/Data-Transfer):**
   - Non-CH nodes transmit data to their CH using **TDMA** slots (reactive on-demand scheduling).
   - CH aggregates/fuses data and transmits to the BS using **CDMA** to reduce inter-cluster interference.

---

#### **Family 3: TEEN & APTEEN (Threshold-Sensitive Protocols)**

**TEEN (Threshold Sensitive Energy Efficient Sensor Network):**
- **Proactive component:** Cluster formation identical to LEACH.
- **Reactive component:** Nodes transmit *only* when sensed attributes cross a **hard threshold (HT)** or change by a **soft threshold (ST)**.
- Suitable for **time-critical, reactive** applications (e.g., temperature alarm, intruder detection).

**APTEEN (Adaptive Periodic TEEN):**
- Hybridizes TEEN with periodic data transmission.
- Captures both **time-critical events** (TEEN-style) and **periodic snapshots** (LEACH-style).
- Best balance of energy + responsiveness.

---

#### **Family 4: SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation)**

A data-centric **negotiation-based** hybrid protocol using 3 message types:

- **ADV** (Advertisement) — node advertises new data via meta-data.
- **REQ** (Request) — interested neighbor requests actual data.
- **DATA** — full data packet sent only upon request.

Avoids the **implosion**, **overlap**, and **resource-blindness** problems of classic flooding.

---

#### **Family 5: Directed Diffusion**

- **Proactive component:** Sink floods *interests* (named attribute-value pairs) periodically to set up gradients.
- **Reactive component:** Sources send data along reinforced paths only when matching event occurs.
- Data is attribute-named, not node-addressed.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula / Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Units / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ | Transmit energy (free-space) | Joules |
| $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^4$ | Transmit energy (multipath) | Joules |
| $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Receive energy | Joules |
| $E_{total} = N \cdot (E_{TX} + E_{RX})$ | Total network energy per round | Joules |
| $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}$ | Optimal number of clusters (LEACH) | Integer |
| $T(n) = \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)}$ | LEACH CH election threshold | $[0,1]$ |
| $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters |
| $\rho$ (ZRP) | Zone radius | hops |
| $E_{DA}$ | Data aggregation energy | $5 \; nJ/bit$ |
| $f$ | Frame size (frames per round) | integer |
| $\alpha$ | Path loss exponent | 2 (FS) or 4 (MP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** Always state the **threshold distance $d_0$** when applying the free-space vs. multipath rule. KTU examiners expect: *"if $d \leq d_0$, use $\epsilon_{fs}$; else use $\epsilon_{mp}$."*

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEACH** → Adopted in commercial **agricultural sensor deployments** (e.g., vineyard microclimate monitoring).
- **TEEN** → Used in **forest fire detection** and **structural health monitoring** (alert on threshold breach).
- **ZRP** → Foundation for **mobile ad-hoc military networks** (MANETs) and **vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V)** hybrid routing.
- **SPIN / Directed Diffusion** → Pioneered the **data-centric paradigm** now used in **IoT publish-subscribe brokers (MQTT)**.
- **APTEEN** → Deployed in **hybrid industrial monitoring** where both alarms and periodic logs are required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: LEACH Optimal Cluster Count $k_{opt}$

**Setup:** $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ field, single base station at distance $d_{toBS}$ from the field center.

**Step 1 — Expected cluster count per node:**
Each cluster contains on average $N/k$ nodes. The area of each cluster is approximately $M^2/k$.

**Step 2 — Expected distance from a non-CH node to its CH:**
For uniformly distributed nodes, the expected distance squared from a node to its CH within a cluster is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E[d_{toCH}^2] &= \iint (x^2 + y^2) \cdot \rho(x,y) \, dx \, dy \\[4pt]
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{M/\sqrt{\pi k}} r^2 \cdot \frac{k}{M^2} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \\[4pt]
&= \frac{k}{M^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \int_{0}^{M/\sqrt{\pi k}} r^3 \, dr \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\pi k}{M^2} \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_{0}^{M/\sqrt{\pi k}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\pi k}{M^2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{M^4}{\pi^2 k^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}
\end{aligned}$$

Here the cluster radius was obtained assuming a circular cluster area $M^2/k$, giving radius $M/\sqrt{\pi k}$.

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated by a non-CH node (transmitting $k$ bits to CH):**
Assume $d_{toCH} < d_0$ (free-space):

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k} = k \cdot E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated by a CH (receiving + aggregating + forwarding to BS):**
CH receives $(N/k - 1)$ messages, aggregates them, then transmits to BS at distance $d_{toBS}$ (assumed multipath):

$$E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4$$

**Step 5 — Total energy per round (sum over all $N$ nodes):**

$$E_{round} = k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot N + E_{DA} \cdot N + k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot N + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2 \cdot N}{2\pi k} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate $E_{round}$ with respect to $k$ and set to zero** to find the optimum:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} &= -\epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2 \cdot N}{2\pi k^2} + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4 = 0 \\[4pt]
k_{opt}^2 &= \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^2 \cdot N}{2\pi \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4} \\[4pt]
k_{opt} &= \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $k_{opt}$ increases with $\sqrt{N}$ (more nodes → more clusters) and **decreases** with $d_{toBS}^2$ (farther BS → fewer, larger clusters to conserve CH transmission energy).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Dissipated in a Single Frame of LEACH

For $N$ nodes, $k$ clusters, and $k_{bits}$ data bits per node per frame:

**Transmitter side (each node → CH):**

$$E_{TX,node} = k_{bits} \cdot E_{elec} + k_{bits} \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^2$$

**Receiver side (CH receives $N/k - 1$ messages):**

$$E_{RX,CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k_{bits} \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Aggregation energy at CH:**

$$E_{agg,CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k}\right) \cdot k_{bits} \cdot E_{DA}$$

**CH → BS transmission (multipath, distance $d_{toBS}$):**

$$E_{TX,CH\to BS} = k_{bits} \cdot E_{elec} + k_{bits} \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4$$

**Frame total:**

$$E_{frame} = N \cdot k_{bits} \cdot E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k_{bits} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{i,toCH(i)}^2 + k \cdot E_{RX,CH} + k \cdot E_{agg,CH} + k \cdot E_{TX,CH\to BS}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \, m \times 100 \, m$ field. The BS is at $d_{toBS} = 150 \, m$ from the field center. Using the first-order radio model with $E_{elec} = 50 \, nJ/bit$, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \, pJ/bit/m^2$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \, pJ/bit/m^4$, packet size $k = 4000$ bits. **Find the optimal number of clusters.**

**Step 1:** Apply the $k_{opt}$ formula derived above.

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \cdot \frac{100}{150^2}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each factor.

$$\sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{15.915} = 3.989$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} = 87.71$$

$$\frac{100}{150^2} = \frac{100}{22500} = 0.00444$$

**Step 3:** Multiply.

$$k_{opt} = 3.989 \times 87.71 \times 0.00444 = 1.554$$

**Step 4:** Round to a physically realizable integer.

$$k_{opt} = 2 \; \text{clusters (or 1 if rounding conservatively)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU expects you to round to the nearest *practical* integer (1, 2, 3, 5) and justify based on $p = k_{opt}/N$. Always show the **intermediate numerical substitution** for full marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Hybrid Protocols

| **Metric / Protocol** | **LEACH** | **TEEN** | **APTEEN** | **ZRP** | **SPIN** | **Directed Diffusion** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Routing Type | Cluster-based hybrid | Cluster + Threshold | Cluster + Periodic | Zone hybrid | Data-centric | Data-centric |
| Energy Efficiency | High | Very High | High | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Latency | Medium | Low (event-driven) | Low–Medium | Medium (intra-zone) / High (inter-zone) | Medium | Medium |
| Data Aggregation | Yes (at CH) | Yes | Yes | No | No | Yes (in-network) |
| Scalability | Good | Good | Good | Good | Medium | Limited |
| Application Fit | Periodic monitoring | Time-critical alarms | Hybrid monitoring | Mobile / mixed traffic | Generic WSN | Query-driven WSN |
| Overhead Type | CH election per round | Threshold broadcast | Threshold + TDMA schedule | Zone table maintenance | ADV/REQ handshake | Interest flood |
| Failure Recovery | Periodic rotation | Threshold re-broadcast | Periodic | Bordercast retry | Re-negotiate | Reinforce alternate path |
| KTU Mark Weightage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ZRP Zone Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS["Source Node S"] --> nodeC1["CH 1 Node"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeN1["Member Node N1"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeN2["Member Node N2"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeB1["Border Node B1"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2["CH 2 Node"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeN3["Member Node N3"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeN4["Member Node N4"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeN5["Member Node N5"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeB2["Border Node B2"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC3["CH 3 Node"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeN6["Member Node N6"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeN7["Member Node N7"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD["Destination Node D"]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows a ZRP-style network partitioned into overlapping zones. Within each zone, **proactive** routing tables (IARP) are maintained. Communication between zones (e.g., $S \to D$) traverses **border nodes** ($B1 \to B2$) using **reactive** queries (IERP).

---

### 4.2 LEACH Operational Phase Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start of Round R"] --> decideA["Each node generates random r in 0 1"]
    decideA --> checkA{"r less than threshold T of n"}
    checkA --> yesA["Node elected as Cluster Head CH"]
    checkA --> noA["Node acts as Member"]
    yesA --> broadcastA["CH broadcasts ADV message"]
    noA --> joinA["Members join nearest CH based on RSSI"]
    broadcastA --> joinA
    joinA --> tdmSched["CH assigns TDMA schedule"]
    tdmSched --> senseA["Members sense and transmit in their slots"]
    senseA --> aggA["CH aggregates received data"]
    aggA --> txBS["CH transmits aggregated packet to BS using CDMA"]
    txBS --> endR["End of Round R"]
    endR --> startA
```

**Interpretation:** This is the canonical LEACH round, decomposed into **Setup** (election → join) and **Steady-State** (TDMA → aggregation → BS upload) phases. The closed loop indicates periodic CH rotation for **energy load balancing**.

---

### 4.3 TEEN Threshold Logic Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    senseA["Sensor reads current value Vc"] --> checkHard{"Vc greater than Hard Threshold HT"}
    checkHard --> noA["Do not transmit stay idle"]
    checkHard --> yesA["Transmit and set Vc as stored value Vs"]
    yesA --> waitA["Wait for next sample"]
    waitA --> senseA
    senseA --> checkSoft{"abs Vc minus Vs greater than Soft Threshold ST"}
    checkSoft --> yesA
    checkSoft --> noA
    noA --> waitA
```

**Interpretation:** TEEN's two-stage threshold logic. **Hard Threshold (HT)** triggers the *first* transmission. **Soft Threshold (ST)** triggers *subsequent* transmissions *only if* the attribute has changed by more than $ST$. The expression $\vert V_c - V_s \vert > ST$ uses the absolute value rendered as plain text to avoid Mermaid syntax issues.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hybrid WSN Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    appLayer["Application Layer Query Sink Interface"] --> netLayer["Network Layer Hybrid Routing Engine"]
    netLayer --> proactive["Proactive Sublayer IARP Intra-Zone Tables"]
    netLayer --> reactive["Reactive Sublayer IERP On-Demand Queries"]
    netLayer --> cluster["Cluster Sublayer LEACH TEEN APTEEN CH Logic"]
    netLayer --> dataC["Data-Centric Sublayer SPIN Directed Diffusion"]
    proactive --> macLayer["MAC Layer CSMA TDMA SCDMA"]
    reactive --> macLayer
    cluster --> macLayer
    dataC --> macLayer
    macLayer --> phyLayer["Physical Layer Radio Transceiver"]
    phyLayer --> channel["Wireless Channel"]
```

**Interpretation:** A single WSN node architecture showing how multiple hybrid routing sub-modules can coexist and be selected based on application requirements, traffic type, and energy budget. This addresses the examiner's frequent question: *"How are multiple routing strategies combined in a hybrid WSN?"*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define hybrid routing protocols in WSN. Why are they preferred over pure proactive or reactive protocols?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Hybrid routing protocols in WSN combine the operational features of two or more routing paradigms — typically proactive (table-driven) and reactive (on-demand), or hierarchical/cluster-based — within a single protocol to deliver energy-efficient, low-latency, and scalable data routing across resource-constrained sensor nodes.
>
> **Justification (1.5 marks):** Pure proactive protocols waste energy maintaining routes that may never be used in large WSNs. Pure reactive protocols incur high latency and flooding cost per query. Hybrid protocols achieve the optimal trade-off: proactive scope is **limited to a local zone or cluster** (e.g., ZRP, LEACH) and reactive lookup is applied **only for inter-zone or rare events** (e.g., TEEN).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the ZRP (Zone Routing Protocol) architecture and the role of IARP and IERP.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ZRP Architecture (1.5 marks):** In ZRP, every node defines a **routing zone of radius $\rho$ hops**. The protocol consists of three sub-components:
>
> - **IARP (Intrazone Routing Protocol):** Proactively maintains routing tables for all nodes **within** the $\rho$-hop zone.
> - **IERP (Interzone Routing Protocol):** Reactively discovers routes for destinations **outside** the local zone using route queries.
> - **BRP (Bordercast Resolution Protocol):** Uses border nodes (at exact radius $\rho$) to **multicast** route queries efficiently, avoiding redundant flooding.
>
> **Diagram & Role (1.5 marks):** (Draw three concentric circles around source $S$; mark border nodes at radius $\rho$; show route query from $S$ → border → destination $D$). The diagram establishes that **proactive cost scales with $\rho^2$** (zone size), while **reactive cost scales with the number of zones** traversed, not the total node count.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **LEACH protocol** in detail. Describe its setup and steady-state phases, the cluster-head election algorithm, and the use of TDMA/CDMA.

**(b) [7 marks]** A WSN has $N = 200$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in a $200 \, m \times 200 \, m$ field. The base station is located $d_{toBS} = 100 \, m$ from the field center. Each node transmits $k = 4000$ bits per frame. Use the first-order radio model with $E_{elec} = 50 \, nJ/bit$, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \, pJ/bit/m^2$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \, pJ/bit/m^4$, and $E_{DA} = 5 \, nJ/bit$. **Calculate the optimal number of clusters and the total energy dissipated per round.**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

> **LEACH Overview (2 marks):** LEACH (Low Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) is a self-organizing, adaptive, cluster-based hybrid protocol. It uses **randomized rotation of cluster-heads** to evenly distribute the energy load among all sensor nodes, preventing the energy depletion of a single static CH.
>
> **Setup Phase (2.5 marks):**
> 1. Each node picks a random number $r \in [0,1]$.
> 2. If $r < T(n)$, the node becomes a CH, where $T(n) = \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)}$.
> 3. CHs broadcast an **ADV** message using CSMA.
> 4. Non-CH nodes join the CH with the **strongest received signal** (RSSI).
> 5. CH creates a **TDMA schedule** and assigns each member a transmission slot.
>
> **Steady-State Phase (2.5 marks):**
> 1. Members sense and transmit in their assigned TDMA slots → **collision-free** intra-cluster communication.
> 2. CHs apply **data aggregation/fusion** to reduce payload size.
> 3. CHs transmit aggregated data to BS using **CDMA codes** to avoid inter-cluster interference.
> 4. The frame is repeated $f$ times before a new CH election round begins.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

> **Step 1 — Calculate $k_{opt}$ using the derived formula (3 marks):**
>
> $$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}$$
>
> $$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \cdot \frac{200}{100^2}$$
>
> $$k_{opt} = \sqrt{31.831} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot \frac{200}{10000}$$
>
> $$k_{opt} = 5.642 \cdot 87.71 \cdot 0.02 = 9.90 \approx 10 \; \text{clusters}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Calculate $E_{round}$ (4 marks):**
>
> Substituting $N = 200$, $k = 10$, $M = 200$, $d_{toBS} = 100$:
>
> $$E_{TX,nonCH} = k(E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}) = 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \frac{200^2}{2\pi \cdot 10})$$
>
> $$= 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 636.62) = 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 6.366 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.255 \times 10^{-4} \; J$$
>
> $$E_{RX,CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} = 19 \cdot 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 3.8 \times 10^{-3} \; J$$
>
> $$E_{agg,CH} = \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} = 20 \cdot 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \; J$$
>
> $$E_{TX,CH\to BS} = k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4) = 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 100^4)$$
>
> $$= 4000 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9} + 1.3 \times 10^{-8}) = 4000 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-7} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \; J$$
>
> **Step 3 — Total per round (multiplied by $k$ clusters and summed):**
>
> $$E_{round} = N \cdot E_{TX,nonCH} + k \cdot (E_{RX,CH} + E_{agg,CH} + E_{TX,CH\to BS})$$
>
> $$E_{round} = 200 \cdot 2.255 \times 10^{-4} + 10 \cdot (3.8 \times 10^{-3} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 7.2 \times 10^{-4})$$
>
> $$E_{round} = 0.0451 + 10 \cdot 4.92 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0451 + 0.0492 = 0.0943 \; J$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $k_{opt} = 10$ clusters; $E_{round} \approx 0.0943 \; J$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **TEEN and APTEEN** protocols. Discuss the role of **Hard Threshold (HT)** and **Soft Threshold (ST)** with a suitable application scenario.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **SPIN protocol family** with ADV, REQ, and DATA message types. How does SPIN solve the **implosion, overlap, and resource-blindness** problems of classical flooding?

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

> **TEEN Protocol (2.5 marks):** TEEN (Threshold sensitive Energy Efficient sensor Network) is a cluster-based hybrid protocol designed for **time-critical, reactive** applications. Cluster formation mirrors LEACH. However, unlike LEACH, members transmit *only* when:
> - The current sensed value $V_c$ exceeds the **Hard Threshold (HT)** → first transmission.
> - The current value differs from the last transmitted value $V_s$ by more than the **Soft Threshold (ST)** → subsequent transmissions.
>
> **APTEEN Protocol (2.5 marks):** APTEEN (Adaptive Periodic TEEN) augments TEEN with a **Count Time (CT)** parameter, allowing nodes to send periodic reports even without threshold breach. This makes APTEEN suitable for applications requiring **both periodic snapshots and event-driven alerts**. APTEEN thus captures the energy savings of TEEN with the data fidelity of LEACH.
>
> **Application Scenario (2 marks):** TEEN is ideal for **forest fire detection** — a sensor must alert *only* when temperature crosses HT (e.g., 60 °C) *and* keeps changing by more than ST (e.g., 2 °C), saving substantial energy between events. APTEEN is suited to **industrial process monitoring** where the system must log periodic pressure readings *and* raise alarms on sudden spikes.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

> **SPIN Protocol Family (3.5 marks):** SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation) is a data-centric hybrid protocol that disseminates data using **meta-data negotiation** between neighbors. Three message types:
>
> 1. **ADV** — A node that has new data advertises it by broadcasting a short **meta-data descriptor** (size, type, etc., not the actual data).
> 2. **REQ** — A neighbor that needs the data replies with a request.
> 3. **DATA** — Only upon REQ does the original node send the full data packet.
>
> **Solution to Flooding Problems (3.5 marks):**
>
> | **Problem in Flooding** | **How SPIN Solves It** |
> |---|---|
> | **Implosion** (same data sent to one node via multiple paths) | Negotiation ensures the node requests each unique data item *once* from *one* neighbor. |
> | **Overlap** (redundant data from overlapping regions) | Meta-data lets neighbors *check content* before requesting → no redundant transmission. |
> | **Resource Blindness** (ignores node energy) | Nodes with low energy simply do not ADV/REQ, conserving resources. |
>
> SPIN variants include **SPIN-PP** (point-to-point), **SPIN-EC** (energy-conservative), **SPIN-BC** (broadcast), and **SPIN-RL** (reliable).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hybrid Protocol Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the threshold distance $d_0$:** Students often use $\epsilon_{fs}$ for *every* distance, but the model switches to $\epsilon_{mp}$ when $d > d_0 = 87.7 \, m$. This single error can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Misapplying the LEACH threshold formula:** $T(n)$ uses $(r \bmod 1/p)$, not just $r$. Writing $T(n) = p/(1 - p \cdot r)$ is a frequent wrong simplification.
> 3. **Conflating ZRP and LEACH:** ZRP is **zone-based** (no clusters, hop-based), LEACH is **cluster-based** (geographical). Examiners deduct for swapping their core mechanisms.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** $E_{round}$ must be expressed in **Joules (J)** with correct prefixes ($nJ$, $\mu J$, $mJ$). Missing units = 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram:** A hybrid protocol question without a block diagram or Mermaid-style flow chart typically loses **1–2 marks** under the "neat diagram" instruction.
> 6. **Confusing SPIN message types:** ADV ≠ DATA. The negotiation *precedes* the actual data transfer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hybrid Protocols (Module 1)**

- **Hybrid protocols** combine ≥ 2 routing paradigms to optimize the **energy–latency–scalability** trade-off in WSN.
- **ZRP** = Proactive *intra-zone* (IARP) + Reactive *inter-zone* (IERP); zone radius $\rho$ is the tuning knob.
- **LEACH** = Randomized CH rotation using threshold $T(n) = \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)}$; uses **TDMA** inside clusters and **CDMA** between clusters.
- **Optimal LEACH clusters:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}$.
- **TEEN** is event-driven: transmits only on **Hard Threshold (HT)** breach *or* on **Soft Threshold (ST)** change.
- **APTEEN** = TEEN + periodic reports via **Count Time (CT)** — hybrid of reactive + proactive.
- **SPIN** uses **meta-data negotiation** (ADV → REQ → DATA) to defeat flooding's implosion, overlap, and resource-blindness.
- **Directed Diffusion** = sink *floods interests* → sources *reinforce* data paths along gradients; data is **attribute-named**, not address-named.
- **First-order radio model constants:** $E_{elec} = 50 \, nJ/bit$, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \, pJ/bit/m^2$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \, pJ/bit/m^4$, $E_{DA} = 5 \, nJ/bit$.
- **Threshold distance $d_0 = 87.7 \, m$** determines free-space vs. multipath energy model selection.
- **Total energy per LEACH round:** $E_{round} = N \cdot E_{TX,nonCH} + k(E_{RX,CH} + E_{agg,CH} + E_{TX,CH\to BS})$.
- **Always draw a diagram** — ZRP zone circles, LEACH round flow, or TEEN threshold logic — to secure full marks.
- **Compare tabularly** when asked: LEACH vs. TEEN vs. APTEEN vs. ZRP vs. SPIN on **energy, latency, scalability, data aggregation, application fit**.
- **Hybrid ≠ hierarchical:** A protocol can be hybrid without being cluster-based (e.g., ZRP). Be precise in definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 802.15.4
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IEEE 802.15.4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: IEEE 802.15.4 — FOUNDATION STANDARD FOR LOW-RATE WPANs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 802.15.4** is a standard developed by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** that specifies the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sub-layer protocols for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**. It targets low-cost, low-power, short-range communication devices with relaxed throughput requirements — making it the de-facto PHY/MAC foundation for modern Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) and IoT.

The standard defines:
- **PHY Layer:** Radio frequency (RF) characteristics, channel allocation, modulation, and transmit/receive timing.
- **MAC Sub-layer:** Frame structure, device addressing, beacon management, channel access (CSMA-CA), GTS allocation, and association/disassociation procedures.

The 2020 revision (IEEE 802.15.4-2020) consolidates prior amendments (802.15.4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, 4f, 4g, 4n, 4p, 4q, 4s, 4t, 4u, 4v, 4w, 4x, 4y, 4z). For WSN contexts, the **2003/2006 versions** remain the most cited in board examinations.

### 1.2 Real-World Intuition (The Plug & Socket Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie Village"**
> Imagine a village where every house has a low-cost **walkie-talkie** that runs on a single AA battery for **months**. People don't need high-fidelity music — they only need to send short messages like "Temperature = 38 °C" or "Motion detected" every few seconds. **IEEE 802.15.4** is the "language" these walkie-talkies use to share airtime politely without interrupting one another. It's the **rule book**, not the hardware. Without it, every device would shout at once, and nobody's data would arrive.

The standard is a **rule book for low-power radio chitchat** between sensor motes. Above this rule book (the lower two layers of the OSI model), upper layers like **6LoWPAN**, **Zigbee**, **Thread**, or **ISA100.11a** provide networking smarts — but they all rely on 802.15.4 as their bedrock.

### 1.3 Why IEEE 802.15.4 Matters in WSN

| Property | Value / Description | WSN Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Range** | **10 m – 100 m** typical (line-of-sight) | Indoor/short-haul sensing |
| **Data Rate** | **20 kbps** (868 MHz), **40 kbps** (915 MHz), **250 kbps** (2.4 GHz) | Sufficient for telemetry, not multimedia |
| **Power Profile** | Target battery life of **months to years** | Enables unattended deployment |
| **Device Cost** | Target under **a few USD** per radio IC | Enables dense node deployment |
| **Latency** | Deterministic via GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots) | Real-time control support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module-1 explicitly maps IEEE 802.15.4 to the **PHY + MAC layers** of the WSN protocol stack. Students must be able to draw the **superframe structure**, list the **three ISM frequency bands**, and explain **CSMA-CA** with and without slotted contention.

---

## 2. Network Topologies Defined by IEEE 802.15.4

The standard supports **two principal network topologies** — every WSN deployment based on this standard falls into one of them.

### 2.1 Star Topology
- A central device called the **PAN Coordinator** orchestrates all communication.
- **End devices** communicate *only* with the PAN coordinator — never directly with each other.
- Simple, low-latency, but single point of failure (coordinator).

### 2.2 Peer-to-Peer Topology
- Every device can communicate with any other device within radio range.
- Forms the basis of more complex topologies like **mesh** and **cluster-tree** networks.
- Enables multi-hop routing — essential for WSN coverage extension.

### 2.3 Cluster-Tree Topology
- A special case of peer-to-peer where devices are organized in a hierarchical tree.
- Each cluster has a **cluster head** (acting as coordinator for that cluster).
- Provides a balance of scalability and power efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> In a WSN-based **forest fire detection** system, you would typically deploy a **cluster-tree**: clusters of sensor motes in different fire-risk zones, each reporting to a cluster head, with cluster heads forming a peer-to-peer backhaul to a central sink.

---

## 3. Device Roles in IEEE 802.15.4

The standard classifies every node into one of two functional categories:

| Device Type | Acronym | Capability | Power | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Function Device** | **FFD** | Can serve as PAN coordinator, coordinator, or end device | Mains / battery | Any role; relays traffic |
| **Reduced-Function Device** | **RFD** | Limited to end-device role only (typically sleep most of the time) | Battery only | Sensors/actuators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An FFD can talk to FFDs *and* RFDs. An RFD can **only** talk to an FFD — never to another RFD. This constraint dramatically simplifies RFD hardware/firmware, slashing cost and energy use — exactly what WSN motes need.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 1. IEEE 802.15.4 Physical Layer (PHY) — The Radio Itself

### 1.1 Operating Frequency Bands (The 3 ISM Bands)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IEEE 802.15.4 operates in **three unlicensed Industrial-Scientific-Medical (ISM)** bands. The exact channel count, data rate, and modulation vary per band. KTU examiners *love* this table.

| Band | Frequency Range | Region of Use | Channels (k) | Data Rate (R) | Modulation (Original 2003 Spec) | Chip Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **868 MHz** | **868 – 868.6 MHz** | Europe | **1** (k=0) | **20 kbps** | BPSK | 300 kchips/s |
| **915 MHz** | **902 – 928 MHz** | North America, Australia | **10** (k=1,2,…,10) | **40 kbps** | BPSK | 600 kchips/s |
| **2400 MHz** | **2.4 – 2.4835 GHz** | Worldwide (most popular) | **16** (k=11,12,…,26) | **250 kbps** | O-QPSK | 2 Mchips/s |

**Channel Centre Frequency Formula (kTU High-Yield):**

For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 10$ (sub-GHz bands):

$$f_c = 868.3 \text{ MHz} \quad (k=0)$$

$$f_c = 906 + 2(k-1) \text{ MHz} \quad (k=1,2,\ldots,10)$$

For $k = 11, 12, \ldots, 26$ (2.4 GHz band):

$$f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11) \text{ MHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Mini-Example:** What is the centre frequency of channel **k = 15**?
>
> Since 11 ≤ 15 ≤ 26, we use the 2.4 GHz formula:
>
> $$f_c = 2405 + 5(15-11) = 2405 + 20 = 2425 \text{ MHz}$$
>
> This is channel 15 — a valid 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi-adjacent Zigbee channel.

### 1.2 PPDU (PHY Protocol Data Unit) Format

The PHY receives a MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) and wraps it in a **PPDU** for transmission:

| Field | Length (Octets) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Preamble** | **4** (32 bits) | Synchronization of receiver |
| **SFD** (Start of Frame Delimiter) | **1** (8 bits, value `0xA7`) | Marks the start of useful data |
| **Frame Length** | **1** (7 bits) | Number of octets in PSDU (0–127) |
| **PSDU** (PHY Service Data Unit) | **≤ 127** | Carries the MAC frame |
| **Total PPDU** | **≤ 133 octets** | Whole frame over the air |

> [!NOTE]
> The 127-octet PSDU ceiling is the reason IEEE 802.15.4 is "low-rate" — frames are tiny by design to minimize airtime and energy.

### 1.3 PHY Layer Functions Summary
- **Activation/deactivation** of the radio transceiver
- **Channel frequency selection**
- **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** — used by CSMA-CA
- **Transmit/Receive packets** based on PPDU structure
- **Link Quality Indication (LQI)** measurement (e.g., from RSSI)
- **Energy Detection (ED)** within the operating channel

---

## 2. IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Layer — The Traffic Cop

### 2.1 MAC Sub-Layer Key Features
1. **Beacon management** (in beacon-enabled mode)
2. **Channel access** — slotted CSMA-CA or unslotted CSMA-CA
3. **GTS management** — guaranteed time slots
4. **Frame validation**, acknowledgment, retransmission
5. **Association & disassociation** of devices
6. **Security** (optional AES-128, in 2006+)

### 2.2 The Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **#1 most-asked diagram** on IEEE 802.15.4 in KTU exams. Memorize and reproduce it cleanly.

The PAN coordinator broadcasts **beacons** periodically to synchronize the network. The time between two consecutive beacons is the **Beacon Interval (BI)**. It contains an **Active Period** and an **Inactive Period** (during which devices may sleep).

The superframe is described by two parameters:
- **SO (Superframe Order)**: $0 \le SO \le 14$
- **BO (Beacon Order)**: $0 \le BO \le 14$ and $BO \ge SO$

**Active Period Duration:**

$$SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} \text{ symbols}$$

**Beacon Interval Duration:**

$$BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO} \text{ symbols}$$

where the **base superframe duration** is the duration of the minimum superframe (SO = 0):

$$aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 \text{ symbols} = 15.36 \text{ ms (at 62.5 ksymbol/s)}$$

**Number of Slots in Active Period:**

$$N_{slots} = 16$$

**Slot Duration:**

$$slot\_duration = SD / 16 = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} / 16 \text{ symbols}$$

**Inactive Period Duration:**

$$Inactive = BI - SD$$

**Duty Cycle (Active Fraction):**

$$Duty Cycle = \frac{SD}{BI} = \frac{2^{SO}}{2^{BO}} = 2^{SO-BO} \quad \text{when } BO > SO$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** When $BO = SO$, the inactive period is zero — the network is always active (100% duty cycle). When $BO > SO$, devices can sleep in the inactive period to save power. This is the primary power-saving mechanism in beacon-enabled IEEE 802.15.4.

### 2.3 Composition of the Active Period

The Active Period is divided into **16 equal time slots**, allocated as follows:

| Portion | Number of Slots | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Beacon** | **1** (slot 0) | PAN coordinator broadcasts synchronization + superframe spec |
| **Contention Access Period (CAP)** | **≤ 15** | All devices compete via **slotted CSMA-CA** |
| **Contention-Free Period (CFP)** | **0 to ≤ 15 (max 7 GTS)** | Reserved Guaranteed Time Slots for time-critical traffic |

> [!TIP]
> Up to **7 GTSs** may be allocated by the PAN coordinator (each GTS may occupy one or more slots). GTSs occupy the CFP, which always trails the CAP.

---

## 3. CSMA-CA: The Channel Access Mechanism

The standard offers **two CSMA-CA variants** based on whether beacons are used.

### 3.1 Unslotted CSMA-CA (Non-Beacon Networks)
- No timing synchronization. A device **waits a random backoff**, performs **CCA**; if channel is busy, retries after another random backoff.
- Used in star and RFD-heavy networks.

### 3.2 Slotted CSMA-CA (Beacon-Enabled Networks)
- Aligned to slot boundaries.
- Maintains three variables: **NB (Number of Backoffs)**, **BE (Backoff Exponent)**, **CW (Contention Window length)**.

**Algorithm Skeleton (slotted CSMA-CA):**
1. Initialize $NB = 0$, $CW = 2$, $BE = macMinBE$ (default 3).
2. Wait for a random backoff in $[0, 2^{BE} - 1]$ slots.
3. Perform CCA. If idle, decrement CW; if CW = 0, transmit.
4. If channel busy, increment NB, BE = min(BE+1, macMaxBE=5); if $NB > macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4$, abort with channel access failure.
5. Else, go to step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two CCA slots (CW = 2)?** To avoid collisions from hidden terminals — a second CCA confirms the channel is still free immediately before transmission.

---

## 4. Frame Types at the MAC Layer

| Frame Type | Subtype Examples | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Beacon Frame** | — | Coordinator → All | Sync, superframe spec, GTS allocation |
| **Data Frame** | — | Any → Any | Payload transfer |
| **Acknowledgment (ACK)** | — | Receiver → Sender | Confirms successful frame reception |
| **MAC Command Frame** | Association Request/Response, Data Request, Disassociation, GTS Request, etc. | Any → Any | Network management |

### 4.1 General MAC Frame Format

| Field | Octets | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Control** | 2 | Frame type, addressing, security, ACK request |
| **Sequence Number** | 1 | Unique ID for the frame |
| **Addressing Fields** | 0–20 | Source & destination PAN ID + 16/64-bit addresses |
| **Auxiliary Security Header** | 0/5/6 | Security key identifier & counter |
| **Payload (MSDU)** | Variable | Upper-layer data |
| **FCS** | 2 | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-16) |

**Maximum MAC payload (MSDU):**
- Without security: 127 − (2+1+0+0) = **124 octets** (when no addressing or security used; actual lower in practice)
- With short (16-bit) addresses, no security: **≤ 81 octets**
- With 64-bit addresses, no security: **≤ 51 octets**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The MAC payload ceiling is one of the key reasons 6LoWPAN/IPv6 fragmentation is required for WSNs using IEEE 802.15.4.

---

## 5. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this consolidated table for all IEEE 802.15.4 numerical problems in KTU exams. All symbols use the standard 802.15.4 notation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Channel centre (868 MHz) | $f_c = 868.3$ MHz | k = 0 |
| 2 | Channel centre (915 MHz) | $f_c = 906 + 2(k-1)$ MHz | k = 1,..,10 |
| 3 | Channel centre (2.4 GHz) | $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz | k = 11,..,26 |
| 4 | Symbol period (2.4 GHz O-QPSK) | $T_s = 16 \text{ μs}$ | 62.5 ksymbol/s |
| 5 | Bit → symbol mapping (2.4 GHz) | **4 bits/symbol** | O-QPSK with 16-ary orthogonal |
| 6 | Bit period (2.4 GHz) | $T_b = 4 \text{ μs}$ | 250 kbps |
| 7 | Bit period (868 MHz BPSK) | $T_b = 50 \text{ μs}$ | 20 kbps |
| 8 | Bit period (915 MHz BPSK) | $T_b = 25 \text{ μs}$ | 40 kbps |
| 9 | Base superframe duration | $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ symbols | 15.36 ms |
| 10 | Active period (SD) | $SD = 960 \times 2^{SO}$ symbols | |
| 11 | Beacon interval (BI) | $BI = 960 \times 2^{BO}$ symbols | |
| 12 | Slots in active period | $N_{slots} = 16$ | |
| 13 | Slot duration | $slot = SD / 16$ | |
| 14 | Inactive period | $Inactive = BI - SD$ | |
| 15 | Duty cycle | $DC = 2^{SO-BO}$ | When BO > SO |
| 16 | Max GTS allocated | **7** | |
| 17 | Max PSDU size | **127 octets** | |
| 18 | Max MSDU (short addr, no sec) | **81 octets** | |
| 19 | Max MSDU (ext addr, no sec) | **51 octets** | |
| 20 | CSMA-CA backoff exponent initial | $macMinBE = 3$ | |
| 21 | CSMA-CA max backoff exponent | $macMaxBE = 5$ | |
| 22 | CSMA-CA max backoffs | $macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4$ | |

---

## 6. Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> IEEE 802.15.4 is the silent workhorse behind:
> - **Zigbee** (home automation, lighting, smart meters)
> - **6LoWPAN** (IPv6 over 802.15.4 — used in industrial IoT)
> - **Thread** (Nest/Google home mesh networking)
> - **ISA100.11a & WirelessHART** (industrial process automation)
> - **MiWi** (Microchip's proprietary WSN protocol)
> - **SNAP / Synapse Wireless** (M2M)
> - Most **smart agriculture** and **environmental monitoring** WSN deployments.
>
> Understanding 802.15.4 unlocks every higher-layer WSN protocol built atop it.
