**Step 2:** Energy spent by CH to **aggregate** the received data:

$$E_{agg,CH} = \frac{N}{k} \cdot l \cdot E_{DA}$$

**Step 3:** Energy spent by CH to **transmit** 1 aggregated packet over distance $d_{to\_BS}$. Since the BS is usually far ($d_{to\_BS} \ge d_{0}$), we use the **multipath model**:

$$E_{TX,CH \to BS} = l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{to\_BS}^{4}$$

**Step 4:** Total energy dissipated by a single CH per round:

$$E_{total,CH} = E_{RX,CH} + E_{agg,CH} + E_{TX,CH \to BS}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting all values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total,CH} &= \frac{N}{k} \cdot l \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot l \cdot E_{DA} + l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{to\_BS}^{4} \\
&= l \cdot \left[ E_{elec} \left( \frac{N}{k} + 1 \right) + \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{DA} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{to\_BS}^{4} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first term is the cost of receiving all CM data; the second is the aggregation cost (CH fuses $N/k$ packets into 1); the third term is the cost of the CH transmitting the compressed signal over the long haul to the BS.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Total Energy Dissipated by a Cluster Member per Round

**Assumption:** A CM transmits $l$ bits over distance $d_{to\_CH}$ (assumed free-space, i.e., $d_{to\_CH} < d_{0}$).

$$E_{total,CM} = l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{to\_CH}^{2}$$

**Step 1:** For a uniform distribution of nodes in a cluster of radius $R$, the **expected squared distance** to CH is:

$$\mathbb{E}[d_{to\_CH}^{2}] = \frac{R^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The cluster radius for an M×M field with $k$ clusters:

$$R = \frac{M}{\sqrt{\pi k}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R^{2} = \frac{M^{2}}{\pi k}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total,CM} &= l \cdot E_{elec} + l \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{\pi k} \\
&= l \cdot \left[ E_{elec} + \frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{k} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Network Energy per Round

There are **k CHs** and **N − k** CMs. Total energy per round $E_{round}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= k \cdot E_{total,CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{total,CM} \\
&\approx k \cdot E_{total,CH} + N \cdot E_{total,CM} \quad \text{(for } N \gg k \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Optimal Cluster Head Probability $p_{opt}$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $E_{round}$ with respect to *k* and equate to zero ($\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 0$).

**Step 2:** Solving yields the optimal cluster count:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{to\_BS}^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Since $p_{opt} = k_{opt} / N$:

$$
\boxed{p_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{amp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{to\_BS}^{2}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition behind $p_{opt}$:** If $d_{to\_BS}$ is large, the CH cost dominates → fewer CHs are needed (smaller *p*). If N is large, more nodes → more CHs needed to keep intra-cluster distances short.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly distributed in a $100\text{ m} \times 100\text{ m}$ field. The BS is located at the center. Compute $d_{to\_BS}^{2}$ (assuming average distance) and the optimal number of clusters.

**Given:** $p = 0.05$, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$, $M = 100$ m.

**Step 1:** Average distance from cluster-center to BS (centroid of a 100×100 grid to center):

$$d_{to\_BS} \approx \frac{0.765 \cdot M}{2} = 38.25 \text{ m}$$

Thus $d_{to\_BS}^{2} \approx 1463 \text{ m}^{2}$.

**Step 2:** Compute $p_{opt}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{opt} &= \sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} \cdot \frac{100}{1463} \\
&= \sqrt{15.92} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot 0.0684 \\
&= 3.99 \cdot 87.7 \cdot 0.0684 \\
&\approx 23.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $p_{opt}$ cannot exceed 1, we clamp it. This shows that for $N=100$ nodes, **every node could be a CH**; however, the network designer would still use $p = 0.05$ (i.e., 5 CHs) as a practical trade-off between energy balancing and aggregation gain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LEACH Network Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BS["BASE STATION SINK"]
    CH1["CLUSTER HEAD 1"]
    CH2["CLUSTER HEAD 2"]
    CH3["CLUSTER HEAD 3"]
    CM1A["NODE A1"]
    CM1B["NODE A2"]
    CM1C["NODE A3"]
    CM2A["NODE B1"]
    CM2B["NODE B2"]
    CM3A["NODE C1"]
    CM3B["NODE C2"]
    CM3C["NODE C3"]

    CM1A -- TDMA SLOT --> CH1
    CM1B -- TDMA SLOT --> CH1
    CM1C -- TDMA SLOT --> CH1
    CH1 -- AGGREGATED PACKET --> BS

    CM2A -- TDMA SLOT --> CH2
    CM2B -- TDMA SLOT --> CH2
    CH2 -- AGGREGATED PACKET --> BS

    CM3A -- TDMA SLOT --> CH3
    CM3B -- TDMA SLOT --> CH3
    CM3C -- TDMA SLOT --> CH3
    CH3 -- AGGREGATED PACKET --> BS
```

**Interpretation:** Three clusters are formed; each CM transmits to its CH using TDMA. The CH aggregates and forwards a single packet to the BS — dramatically reducing long-haul transmissions.

## 4.2 LEACH Round Operation Timeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["ROUND R BEGINS"] --> S2["SETUP PHASE START"]
    S2 --> S3["EACH NODE GENERATES RANDOM NUMBER"]
    S3 --> S4{"RAND LESS THAN T OF N"}
    S4 -- YES --> S5["NODE BECOMES CLUSTER HEAD"]
    S4 -- NO --> S6["NODE REMAINS CLUSTER MEMBER"]
    S5 --> S7["CH BROADCASTS ADVERTISEMENT"]
    S6 --> S8["CM JOINS NEAREST CH BY RSSI"]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9["CH ASSIGNS TDMA SCHEDULE"]
    S9 --> S10["STEADY STATE PHASE START"]
    S10 --> S11["CM SENSES AND TRANSMITS TO CH IN TDMA SLOT"]
    S11 --> S12["CH AGGREGATES DATA FUSION"]
    S12 --> S13["CH TRANSMITS AGGREGATED DATA TO BS"]
    S13 --> S14["REPEAT FOR ALL TDMA FRAMES"]
    S14 --> S15["ROUND R ENDS"]
    S15 --> S16["NEXT ROUND R PLUS 1"]
    S16 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Threshold T(n) Decision Logic (Mermaid Decision Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ROUND R STARTS"] --> B["NODE N HAS NOT BEEN CH IN LAST 1 OVER P ROUNDS"]
    B -- "N BELONGS TO G" --> C["GENERATE RANDOM NUMBER RND"]
    B -- "N NOT IN G" --> D["T N EQUALS ZERO NODE CANNOT BE CH"]
    C --> E{"RND LESS THAN T OF N"}
    E -- "YES" --> F["NODE N BECOMES CLUSTER HEAD"]
    E -- "NO" --> G["NODE N REMAINS CLUSTER MEMBER"]
```

## 4.4 Radio Energy Model Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["TRANSMITTER"] --> TXE["E ELEL CIRCUITRY ENERGY"]
    SRC --> AMP["POWER AMPLIFIER ENERGY"]
    TXE --> TXC["E TX K D TOTAL TX ENERGY"]
    AMP --> TXC
    AMP2{"D LESS THAN D0"}
    AMP2 -- "YES FS PATH" --> FS["EPS FS D SQUARED"]
    AMP2 -- "NO MP PATH" --> MP["EPS MP D TO POWER FOUR"]
    FS --> TXC
    MP --> TXC
    RX["RECEIVER"] --> RXC["E RX K E ELEL K"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the two phases of LEACH operation. Why is the steady-state phase kept much longer than the setup phase?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Setup Phase:** Cluster formation — CHs are selected using threshold T(n), advertise themselves, and CMs join the nearest CH. A TDMA schedule is broadcast.
- **Steady-State Phase:** Actual data transmission — CMs send data to CHs using TDMA slots; CHs aggregate and forward to BS.
- **Reason for longer Steady-State:** Energy spent on control-message exchange (advertisements, joins, TDMA setup) is a fixed overhead. By making the steady-state phase much longer (e.g., 10× to 20×), this overhead is **amortized**, so the per-bit useful energy cost is minimized. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the threshold T(n) used for CH selection in LEACH. What is the role of the set G?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** $T(n) = p / [1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)]$ for $n \in G$, else $T(n) = 0$. (1 mark)
- **Mechanism:** Each node picks a random number; if *rnd < T(n)*, it becomes a CH for the current round *r*. (1 mark)
- **Role of G:** G is the set of nodes that have **not yet been CHs** in the last $1/p$ rounds. This ensures every node eventually becomes a CH — guaranteeing **uniform energy dissipation** across the network. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice (Module 2):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **LEACH protocol architecture** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the setup phase and the steady-state phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **total energy dissipated by a Cluster Head (CH)** and a **Cluster Member (CM)** in one round of LEACH, clearly stating the radio energy dissipation model.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) LEACH Protocol Architecture [7 marks]:**

LEACH is a **distributed, probabilistic, single-hop clustering protocol** that operates in rounds. Each round has two phases:

**1. Setup Phase [3 marks]:**
- Each node generates a random number between 0 and 1 and compares it with the threshold $T(n)$.
- Nodes for which *rnd < T(n)* become Cluster Heads.
- CHs broadcast an advertisement message using CSMA MAC.
- Non-CH nodes listen and select the CH with the strongest received signal (minimum communication distance).
- The CH assigns a **TDMA schedule** to its CMs and broadcasts it.
- CMs switch to "sleep" mode outside their allocated slots — saving energy.

**2. Steady-State Phase [3 marks]:**
- Each CM transmits its sensed data to its CH during its assigned TDMA slot.
- The CH receives data from all CMs and performs **data aggregation/fusion** to eliminate redundancy.
- The CH transmits **one aggregated packet** to the far-away Base Station (using a single long-range hop).
- The CH can use **spread-spectrum** or **DS-CDMA** codes to reduce inter-cluster interference.

**Diagram [1 mark]:** (See SECTION 4.1 of these notes — multi-cluster, single-hop BS architecture)

**(b) Energy Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Radio Model [2 marks]:**
$$
E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} d^{2} \quad \text{(if } d < d_{0}\text{)}
$$
$$
E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} d^{4} \quad \text{(if } d \ge d_{0}\text{)}
$$
$$
E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$
$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

**CH Energy/Round [3 marks]:** A CH receives $N/k$ packets, aggregates, and transmits 1 packet to BS at distance $d_{to\_BS} \ge d_{0}$:
$$
E_{CH} = l \cdot \left[ E_{elec} \left(\frac{N}{k}+1\right) + \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{DA} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{to\_BS}^{4} \right]
$$

**CM Energy/Round [2 marks]:** Each CM transmits *l* bits to CH at distance $d_{to\_CH} < d_{0}$ (free-space):
$$
E_{CM} = l \cdot \left[ E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{to\_CH}^{2} \right]
$$
With uniform distribution, $\mathbb{E}[d_{to\_CH}^{2}] = \frac{1}{2\pi k / M^{2}} = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$, giving the simplified expression in SECTION 3.2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **cluster head selection algorithm** of LEACH. Explain how the threshold T(n) ensures uniform energy dissipation, and derive the optimal cluster head probability $p_{opt}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of LEACH** and compare it with direct transmission and minimum-transmission-energy (MTE) routing schemes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) CH Selection Algorithm & $p_{opt}$ Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Algorithm [3 marks]:**
1. At the start of round *r*, each node n picks a random number *rnd* ∈ [0,1].
2. Node n is a member of set G if it has not been a CH in the last $1/p$ rounds.
3. If *rnd < T(n)*, the node becomes a CH for round *r*.
4. T(n) is computed as:
$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Uniform Energy Dissipation Guarantee [2 marks]:** By enforcing the rule that each node can be a CH only once every $1/p$ rounds, LEACH guarantees **every node is a CH exactly p% of the time**. Over many rounds, all nodes dissipate approximately the same amount of energy, eliminating "hot-spot" problems.

**Derivation of $p_{opt}$ [2 marks]:** (As shown in SECTION 3.4)
$$
p_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{amp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{to\_BS}^{2}}
$$

**(b) Advantages, Limitations, Comparison [7 marks]:**

**Advantages of LEACH [3 marks]:**
- **Energy efficient** due to data aggregation at CHs.
- **Self-organizing** and **distributed** — no centralized control needed.
- **Load balancing** via probabilistic CH rotation.
- **Scalable** for large networks.
- **Increased network lifetime** by 3–7× over direct transmission.

**Limitations of LEACH [2 marks]:**
- **Single-hop CH-to-BS communication** → consumes huge energy when BS is far.
- **Random CH selection** may place CHs at the edge of cluster or close to BS — non-uniform clusters.
- **No consideration of residual energy** in CH selection.
- **Not suitable for large-area networks** (multi-hop would be better).
- **Probabilistic selection** can lead to poor clusters in some rounds.

**Comparison [2 marks]:**

| Scheme | Energy Efficiency | Lifetime | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Transmission | Poor (each node → BS) | Lowest | Trivial |
| MTE (Minimum Transmission Energy) | Moderate (uses multi-hop) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **LEACH** | **High (data fusion + clusters)** | **3–7× Higher** | Moderate |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in LEACH questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up the radio model:** Using $d^{4}$ for short intra-cluster distances (incorrect — should be $d^{2}$) or vice versa. Always check $d$ against $d_{0} \approx 87.7$ m.
> 2. **Forgetting the aggregation term $E_{DA}$** in CH energy calculations.
> 3. **Writing the wrong condition for T(n):** Forgetting the **"if n ∈ G"** clause — losing 1 mark immediately.
> 4. **Conflating Setup and Steady-State phases:** Examiners award 2 marks each phase separately. Vague answers like "LEACH clusters the network" get 0.
> 5. **Ignoring the $N/k$ factor:** CH receives data from $N/k$ nodes, not just 1. This is a common algebraic mistake.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always mention **Joules (J)** for energy and **bits** for data length.
> 7. **Not drawing a diagram** in (a) part — worth at least 1 mark in the 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **LEACH — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Full form:** Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy.
- ✅ **Proposed by:** Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, Balakrishnan (2000).
- ✅ **Type:** Distributed, probabilistic, hierarchical, single-hop WSN protocol.
- ✅ **Two Phases:** Setup (clustering) and Steady-State (data transmission).
- ✅ **Steady-State >> Setup Phase** (for overhead amortization).
- ✅ **Threshold T(n) = p / [1 − p(r mod 1/p)]** — only for nodes in set G.
- ✅ **Set G = nodes not CH in last 1/p rounds** — ensures fair rotation.
- ✅ **Radio Model:** Free-space (d²) for d < d₀, Multipath (d⁴) for d ≥ d₀.
- ✅ **Crossover distance:** $d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m.
- ✅ **Standard values:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal, packet length $l = 4000$ bits.
- ✅ **Optimal p:** $p_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{amp}} \cdot M / d_{to\_BS}^{2}$.
- ✅ **Energy gain:** LEACH achieves 3–7× longer network lifetime vs. direct transmission.
- ✅ **CH energy components:** $E_{RX} + E_{agg} + E_{TX,long-haul}$.
- ✅ **CM energy components:** $E_{elec} \cdot l + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{to\_CH}^{2} \cdot l$.
- ✅ **Key disadvantages:** Single-hop to BS, no residual-energy awareness, random CH placement.
- ✅ **Variants to be aware of:** LEACH-C (centralized), LEACH-F (fixed clusters), LEACH-V (vice-CH), I-LEACH (improved).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation (SPIN)** is a family of adaptive, data-centric routing protocols designed for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) that efficiently disseminate information among sensor nodes using **meta-data negotiation** and **resource-adaptive algorithms**.

SPIN was proposed by **Kulik, Heinzelman, and Balakrishnan (2002)** as a solution to the classic deficiencies of classical flooding and gossiping protocols. The protocol family operates under the principle that sensor nodes must negotiate the transmission of actual sensory data using small-sized descriptors called **meta-data**, ensuring that only useful data is transmitted across the network.

**Standard Reference Symbols used in SPIN literature:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $E_{tx}$ | Energy consumed during transmission | Joule (J) |
| $E_{rx}$ | Energy consumed during reception | Joule (J) |
| $E_{da}$ | Energy consumed for data aggregation | Joule (J) |
| $N$ | Total number of sensor nodes | dimensionless |
| $k$ | Size of meta-data | bits |
| $D$ | Size of actual data payload | bits |
| $r$ | Communication range | meters (m) |
| $P_{drop}$ | Packet drop probability | dimensionless |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Used Car Advertisement" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine you want to sell a used car. You don't send photographs of the car to every person in the city. Instead, you put a small advertisement in the newspaper saying "Red sedan, 2018 model, excellent condition" (this is the **meta-data / ADV**). Only genuinely interested buyers call you back (**REQ**), and only then do you show them the actual car photos (**DATA**). This saves enormous time, energy, and bandwidth for everyone.

In SPIN, sensor nodes play this exact role:

- A node with new data **advertises** (ADV) the fact using compact **meta-data** (just like a newspaper ad).
- Neighbors interested in this kind of data send back a **request** (REQ).
- Only then is the bulk **data packet** transmitted.

This negotiation eliminates redundant transmissions of the same data to nodes that do not need it.

## 1.3 Three Core Messages in SPIN

> [!NOTE]
> The SPIN protocol family uses exactly **three message types** for all communication:

1. **ADV (Advertisement):** A short meta-data descriptor sent by a node to announce that it has new data available. Size is small ($k$ bits).
2. **REQ (Request):** Sent by a node that wants the actual data after seeing the ADV.
3. **DATA:** The actual sensor reading payload of size $D$ bits, sent only in response to a REQ.

## 1.4 Problems in Classical Flooding Solved by SPIN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Classical flooding suffers from three critical problems. SPIN is explicitly designed to eliminate them:

- **Implosion Problem:** A node may receive multiple copies of the same data when multiple neighbors flood it.
- **Overlap Problem:** Two sensors in the same region send nearly identical data, wasting bandwidth.
- **Resource Blindness:** Flooding does not consider the limited energy, memory, or computation of sensor nodes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SPIN Family of Protocols

The SPIN family contains four variants, each tailored to a specific network capability level.

### 2.1.1 SPIN-PP (Point-to-Point)
- Designed for **lossless, point-to-point** networks.
- Uses a simple three-stage ADV–REQ–DATA handshake with **single-hop neighbors only**.
- Two-hop neighbors are reached only after the first hop completes the protocol.
- Energy model assumes the link is reliable and packets are never lost.

### 2.1.2 SPIN-EC (Energy Conserving)
- Extension of SPIN-PP with **energy-awareness**.
- A node participates in the protocol only if it has sufficient energy $E_{remaining} \geq E_{threshold}$.
- Prevents nodes with low battery from forwarding packets, prolonging overall network lifetime.

### 2.1.3 SPIN-BC (Broadcast Channel)
- Designed for nodes sharing a **shared broadcast medium** (e.g., wireless).
- A node listens to the channel before transmitting; collisions are avoided by carrier sensing.
- A node does not send REQ if it can already overhear an adjacent node requesting the same data.
- Prevents redundant REQ transmissions.

### 2.1.4 SPIN-RL (Reliable)
- Combines features of SPIN-EC and SPIN-BC.
- Adds **retransmission and tracking mechanisms** for lost or corrupted packets.
- Maintains a list of neighbors from which it has successfully received data and from whom it expects to transmit.
- Most realistic variant for lossy wireless WSN deployments.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of SPIN (Step-by-Step Bullets)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SPIN works — the underlying reasoning:**
>
> 1. **Meta-data is always smaller than data:** Transmission cost of advertising is always much less than transmitting the actual data.
> 2. **Interest-based filtering:** Only nodes with applications interested in the type of data will request it.
> 3. **Topology-agnostic dissemination:** SPIN works on any network topology without requiring routing tables.
> 4. **Implicit acknowledgements:** A node knows its neighbor received the data when that neighbor forwards an ADV.

**Stepwise SPIN operation:**

- **Step 1:** Node A acquires new sensory data. Before sending the raw data, it broadcasts an **ADV** containing meta-data describing the new data.
- **Step 2:** Each neighbor B receives the ADV. If B already has the data, it discards the ADV. If B is interested and does not have the data, it sends a **REQ** back to A.
- **Step 3:** Node A receives one or more REQs. It then transmits the actual **DATA** packet to all requesting neighbors.
- **Step 4:** Any node receiving the DATA can now itself become an advertiser and run SPIN again, propagating the data across the network.
- **Step 5 (Energy check in SPIN-EC/SPIN-RL):** Before any transmission, the node checks $E_{remaining} \geq E_{threshold}$. If not, it skips that round.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Examinations:**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Used For |
|---|----------------------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $E_{flood} = N \cdot (E_{tx} + E_{rx})$ | Total energy in classical flooding for $N$ nodes | Energy comparison |
| 2 | $E_{SPIN} \approx \alpha \cdot N \cdot (E_{tx}^{adv} + E_{rx}^{adv}) + \beta \cdot N \cdot E_{tx}^{data}$ | Energy in SPIN, where $\alpha, \beta < 1$ are negotiation factors | Energy comparison |
| 3 | $E_{ratio} = \dfrac{E_{SPIN}}{E_{flood}}$ | Energy reduction ratio; ideally $< 0.5$ | Performance metric |
| 4 | $\eta_{bandwidth} = \dfrac{D_{useful}}{D_{total}}$ | Bandwidth utilization efficiency | Performance metric |
| 5 | $P_{success} = (1 - P_{drop})^{h}$ | Probability of successful end-to-end delivery over $h$ hops | Reliability analysis |
| 6 | $T_{disseminate} \propto \text{diameter}(G)$ | Dissemination time proportional to network diameter | Latency analysis |
| 7 | $C_{overhead} = \dfrac{M_{ADV} + M_{REQ}}{M_{DATA}}$ | Control-to-data message overhead ratio | Efficiency check |
| 8 | $E_{threshold} = \alpha \cdot E_{initial}$ | Energy threshold for SPIN-EC participation, $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.3]$ | Energy conservation |

> [!NOTE]
> In equation (2), the term $\alpha$ represents the fraction of nodes involved in meta-data exchange, and $\beta$ represents the fraction of nodes that actually receive the data. Because both fractions are strictly less than 1, $E_{SPIN} < E_{flood}$ under realistic WSN conditions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SPIN-family protocols are foundational in:
- **Environmental monitoring networks** (forest fire, flood detection)
- **Structural health monitoring** of bridges and buildings
- **Precision agriculture** sensor grids
- **Military surveillance** networks
- **Smart city infrastructure** deployments
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** for predictive maintenance

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Comparison between Flooding and SPIN

**Given assumptions (standard first-order radio model used in WSN literature):**

- $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \cdot D + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D \cdot r^{2}$
- $E_{rx} = E_{elec} \cdot D$
- Each node has the same transmission radius $r$ and average degree $\bar{d}$ (average number of neighbors).
- All $N$ nodes eventually need to receive the data.

### 3.1.1 Energy spent in Classical Flooding

In classical flooding, every node that receives a packet retransmits it. So each node transmits the data exactly once, and receives from all its neighbors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{flood}^{tx} &= N \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot D + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D \cdot r^{2}) \\
E_{flood}^{rx} &= N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot D \\
E_{flood}^{total} &= N \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot D + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D \cdot r^{2}) + N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot D \\
&= N \cdot D \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic row:** In flooding, $N$ nodes each transmit once and receive once, hence the total cost is $N \cdot (E_{tx} + E_{rx})$.

### 3.1.2 Energy spent in SPIN

In SPIN, the negotiation phase uses meta-data of size $k$ bits (typically $k \ll D$). Let $M_{adv}$ be the total number of ADV messages and $M_{req}$ the number of REQ messages across the network.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{SPIN}^{adv} &= M_{adv} \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot r^{2}) + M_{adv} \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k \\
&= M_{adv} \cdot k \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2}) \\
E_{SPIN}^{req} &= M_{req} \cdot k \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2}) \\
E_{SPIN}^{data} &= N \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot D + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D \cdot r^{2}) + N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot D \\
&= N \cdot D \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic row:** The DATA energy in SPIN equals that in flooding, since the same number of nodes receive the data. The savings come entirely from skipping transmissions of the full DATA for uninterested or duplicate cases.

### 3.1.3 Net Energy Saving

In the ideal case where meta-data is small and the ADV/REQ handshake filters out all unwanted transmissions:

$$
E_{SPIN}^{total} = E_{SPIN}^{adv} + E_{SPIN}^{req} + E_{SPIN}^{data}
$$

Substituting the components:

$$
E_{SPIN}^{total} = (M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2}) + N \cdot D \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})
$$

Factoring out the common term $(2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})$:

$$
E_{SPIN}^{total} = (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2}) \cdot \left[ (M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k + N \cdot D \right]
$$

### 3.1.4 Energy Reduction Ratio

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{E_{SPIN}}{E_{flood}} = \frac{(M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k + N \cdot D}{N \cdot D + N \cdot D} = \frac{(M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k + N \cdot D}{2 \cdot N \cdot D}
$$

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{(M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k}{2 \cdot N \cdot D}
$$

> **Logic row:** Since $k \ll D$ in practice (meta-data is much smaller than actual data), the second term is negligible, giving $E_{ratio} \approx 0.5$. This means SPIN uses roughly **half the energy** of flooding, validating the protocol's efficiency claim.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SPIN vs Flooding vs Gossiping

| Performance Metric | Classical Flooding | Gossiping | SPIN (PP/BC/EC/RL) |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Implosion Problem | ✗ Present | ✗ Present | ✓ Eliminated |
| Overlap Problem | ✗ Present | ✗ Present | ✓ Eliminated |
| Resource Awareness | ✗ Blind | ✗ Blind | ✓ Aware (EC, RL) |
| Energy Efficiency | Low | Medium | High |
| Bandwidth Usage | High (~$N^2$ msgs) | Medium | Low (negotiation-based) |
| Reliability over Lossy Links | Poor | Poor | Good (only in SPIN-RL) |
| Topology Dependency | None | None | None (agnostic) |
| Data Delivery Guarantee | Strong | Probabilistic | Strong (in PP, RL) |
| Meta-data Requirement | None | None | Yes (small $k$ bits) |
| Suitable for Mobile WSN | No | Limited | Limited (PP/RL only) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes. Data size $D = 500$ bytes, meta-data size $k = 25$ bytes. Each node has 5 neighbors on average. Estimate the energy ratio $E_{SPIN}/E_{flood}$ assuming only one REQ per ADV is sufficient.

**Solution approach:**

- Number of ADV messages: $M_{adv} \approx N$ (each node advertises once if it has new data).
- Number of REQ messages: $M_{req} \approx N$ (one request per advertisement).
- Substitute into the ratio equation:

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{(M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k + N \cdot D}{2 \cdot N \cdot D}
$$

Substitute $M_{adv} = M_{req} = N$:

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{(N + N) \cdot k + N \cdot D}{2 \cdot N \cdot D} = \frac{2N \cdot k + N \cdot D}{2 \cdot N \cdot D}
$$

Cancel $N$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{2k + D}{2D} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{k}{D}
$$

Substitute $k = 25$ bytes and $D = 500$ bytes:

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{25}{500} = 0.5 + 0.05 = 0.55
$$

**Interpretation:** SPIN consumes approximately **55% of the energy** of classical flooding, yielding a **45% energy saving** — a significant gain in energy-constrained WSNs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SPIN Operation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **three-stage handshake protocol** and the decision logic inside each sensor node, mapping all possible message transitions and state changes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node Acquires New Sensory Data] --> B{Energy Check: E_remaining >= E_threshold}
    B -- No --> Z[Node Sleeps and Skips Round]
    B -- Yes --> C[Stage 1: Broadcast ADV with Meta-Data]
    C --> D[Neighbour Receives ADV]
    D --> E{Already Has This Data}
    E -- Yes --> F[Discard ADV and Do Nothing]
    E -- No --> G{Is Neighbour Interested in Data Type}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Stage 2: Send REQ Back to Advertiser]
    H --> I[Advertiser Collects All REQs]
    I --> J{Is Channel Busy in SPIN-BC}
    J -- Yes --> K[Wait for Clear Channel via Carrier Sense]
    K --> J
    J -- No --> L[Stage 3: Transmit Full DATA Packet to All Requesters]
    L --> M[Receivers Now Have New Data and Become New Advertisers]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the SPIN Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NodeA[Sensor Node Architecture in SPIN]
        S1[Sensor Module] --> M1[Meta-Data Generator]
        M1 --> A1[ADV Message Buffer]
        A1 --> TX1[Radio Transmitter]
        RX1[Radio Receiver] --> A2[REQ Message Parser]
        A2 --> DEC[Data Request Decision Engine]
        DEC --> RX2[DATA Receiver Buffer]
        RX2 --> STO[Local Data Store]
        STO --> ENG[Energy Monitor Module]
        ENG --> CTR[Central SPIN Controller]
        CTR --> A1
        CTR --> TX1
    end
```

## 4.3 SPIN Family Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPIN[SPIN Family of Protocols] --> SPINPP[SPIN-PP: Point to Point Lossless]
    SPIN --> SPINEC[SPIN-EC: Energy Conserving]
    SPIN --> SPINBC[SPIN-BC: Broadcast Channel]
    SPIN --> SPINRL[SPIN-RL: Reliable with Loss Recovery]
    SPINPP --> SPINEC
    SPINEC --> SPINBC
    SPINBC --> SPINRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy shows the evolutionary design of the SPIN family: each later protocol adds features to handle real-world challenges (energy limits, broadcast collisions, packet loss).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define the term **meta-data** as used in the SPIN protocol. Why is it important in WSNs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Meta-data is a compact, application-specific descriptor that summarizes the actual sensory data. It is typically a few bytes (size $k$, where $k \ll D$) and conveys the type, source, and timestamp of data without the bulk payload. In WSNs, meta-data is crucial because it enables nodes to decide whether they are interested in receiving the full data, thereby avoiding redundant transmissions, saving bandwidth, and conserving energy — directly solving the implosion and overlap problems of classical flooding. **(3 marks — full credit requires mention of size, purpose, and at least one problem solved.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** List and briefly explain the **three message types** used in the SPIN protocol.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three message types are: **(1)** **ADV (Advertisement):** A small message sent by a node to announce availability of new data, carrying only meta-data. **(2)** **REQ (Request):** A short message sent by an interested neighbor that wants the actual data. **(3)** **DATA:** The complete sensory data payload sent only in response to a REQ. Together, these messages implement the three-stage handshake that filters data transmission based on interest and energy availability. **(3 marks — 1 mark per message type with correct purpose.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **SPIN-PP protocol** with a neat diagram of its three-stage handshake. Discuss how SPIN-PP eliminates the **implosion and overlap problems** of classical flooding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **SPIN-PP, SPIN-EC, SPIN-BC, and SPIN-RL** in terms of energy awareness, reliability, and handling of broadcast collisions. Identify one real-world scenario where SPIN-RL would be preferred over the other three. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

SPIN-PP is the simplest variant of the SPIN family, designed for **lossless point-to-point networks**.

**Three-stage handshake (7 marks total — 1 mark per stage + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for problem elimination):**

- **Stage 1 — ADV:** A node with new data broadcasts an ADV packet containing meta-data. The ADV has a unique identifier so other nodes can recognize it.

- **Stage 2 — REQ:** Each neighbor receiving the ADV checks whether it already has the data. If not, and if the application is interested, it sends a REQ back to the advertiser.

- **Stage 3 — DATA:** Upon receiving one or more REQs, the advertiser transmits the actual data packet only to the requesting nodes.

**Diagram (textual representation acceptable in exam):**

```
   Node A (Advertiser)              Node B (Neighbor)
        |                                |
        | -------- ADV (meta-data) ----->|
        |                                |
        | <-------- REQ ----------------|
        |                                |
        | -------- DATA (payload) ----->|
        |                                |
        (B now becomes advertiser and repeats process)
```

**Elimination of Implosion:** In SPIN-PP, a node only receives a single copy of the data from a single neighbor (the one that responded first to the REQ). Unlike flooding, where a node can receive the same data from multiple neighbors, the negotiation handshake inherently prevents duplicate receptions. **(2 marks)**

**Elimination of Overlap:** By using meta-data to describe the data content, two nodes that detect similar events will advertise *different* meta-data. A neighbor that has already received data matching meta-data type $X$ will not send a REQ for a duplicate advertisement of type $X$. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Variant | Energy Awareness | Reliability | Broadcast Collision Handling |
|---------|------------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| SPIN-PP | No | High (lossless assumption) | Limited |
| SPIN-EC | Yes ($E_{remaining} \geq E_{threshold}$) | Moderate | Limited |
| SPIN-BC | Limited | Moderate | Yes (carrier sensing) |
| SPIN-RL | Yes (inherits from EC) | High (with retransmission tracking) | Yes (inherits from BC) |

**Real-world scenario for SPIN-RL:** In a **forest fire monitoring network** deployed over a large area, packet loss is common due to foliage, weather, and RF interference. SPIN-RL is preferred because it tracks which neighbors have received the data and retransmits lost packets, ensuring that the critical fire-alert data reaches all monitoring stations. **(2 marks for scenario justification)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q: (a)** Derive the **energy consumption comparison** between classical flooding and SPIN. Show that under typical WSN conditions, $E_{SPIN} \approx 0.5 \cdot E_{flood}$. State all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of a SPIN-enabled sensor node**. How does the energy monitor module influence the decision to participate in the protocol? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
- All $N$ sensor nodes are homogeneous and use the same transmission radius $r$.
- Each node has average degree $\bar{d}$ and similar energy at start.
- The first-order radio model applies: $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \cdot D + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D \cdot r^{2}$, $E_{rx} = E_{elec} \cdot D$.
- Meta-data size $k$ is much smaller than data size $D$, i.e., $k \ll D$.
- Each node has the data to send and one round of dissemination is considered.

**Flooding energy (2 marks):**
Every node receives once and transmits once for the data packet:

$$
E_{flood} = N \cdot (E_{tx} + E_{rx}) = N \cdot D \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})
$$

**SPIN energy (2 marks):**
The DATA phase uses the same energy as flooding. Negotiation phases use $M_{adv}$ ADV and $M_{req}$ REQ messages of size $k$ each:

$$
E_{SPIN} = (M_{adv} + M_{req}) \cdot k \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2}) + N \cdot D \cdot (2 E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot r^{2})
$$

**Ratio (1 mark):**
Assuming $M_{adv} \approx M_{req} \approx N$ and $k \ll D$:

$$
E_{ratio} = \frac{E_{SPIN}}{E_{flood}} = \frac{2N \cdot k + N \cdot D}{2 \cdot N \cdot D} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{k}{D} \approx \frac{1}{2}
$$

Therefore, $E_{SPIN} \approx 0.5 \cdot E_{flood}$, confirming the **50% energy saving** claim of SPIN. **(Conclusion 1 mark — full credit.)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

A SPIN-enabled sensor node consists of the following functional modules (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes):

- **Sensor Module:** Captures the physical phenomenon (temperature, humidity, etc.) and produces raw data.
- **Meta-Data Generator:** Converts raw data into compact application-specific descriptors.
- **ADV Message Buffer:** Holds outgoing ADV messages ready for transmission.
- **Radio Transmitter/Receiver:** Handles the physical-layer wireless communication.
- **REQ Message Parser:** Interprets incoming REQ messages and updates the request list.
- **Data Request Decision Engine:** Decides whether to send REQ based on interest and duplicate checks.
- **Local Data Store:** Maintains the data the node currently holds.
- **Energy Monitor Module:** Continuously tracks $E_{remaining}$ and compares with $E_{threshold}$.
- **Central SPIN Controller:** Orchestrates the three-stage handshake and routing decisions.

**Role of the Energy Monitor (3 marks):**

The energy monitor influences participation as follows:
- Before broadcasting an ADV, the controller queries the energy monitor.
- If $E_{remaining} < E_{threshold} = \alpha \cdot E_{initial}$ (typically $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.3]$), the node abstains from the protocol and conserves energy.
- This prevents premature network partitioning where a few low-battery nodes drop out, isolating the sink from large sections of the network.
- In SPIN-EC and SPIN-RL, this is a strict gate; in SPIN-PP and SPIN-BC, this check is absent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing SPIN with Directed Diffusion:** Students often confuse SPIN's meta-data negotiation with directed diffusion's interest-gradient routing. Remember: SPIN is **push-based** with negotiation, while Directed Diffusion uses **pull-based** interest gradients.
> 2. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** When deriving energy equations, students often forget to state the first-order radio model and the homogeneous node assumption — losing 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Wrong number of SPIN variants:** Examiners expect students to know **all four** (PP, EC, BC, RL) and explain at least two in detail. Listing only SPIN-PP is incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing $k$ and $D$:** Meta-data size is $k$, data size is $D$. Using them interchangeably in numerical problems is a common error.
> 5. **Omitting the three-stage handshake diagram:** Even in theory questions, a simple ASCII diagram of the ADV-REQ-DATA sequence fetches full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for SPIN:**

- SPIN stands for **Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation**, proposed by Kulik, Heinzelman, and Balakrishnan in **2002**.
- It is a **data-centric, negotiation-based, resource-aware** routing protocol family.
- Three message types: **ADV, REQ, DATA** (always in that order for a successful transmission).
- Four variants: **SPIN-PP, SPIN-EC, SPIN-BC, SPIN-RL**.
- Meta-data is small ($k$ bits, $k \ll D$); it describes data without containing it.
- Solves three flooding problems: **implosion, overlap, and resource blindness**.
- SPIN-EC adds an **energy threshold check** $E_{remaining} \geq E_{threshold}$.
- SPIN-BC uses **carrier sensing** to avoid collisions on a shared medium.
- SPIN-RL is the most realistic — combines energy-awareness, collision handling, and **retransmission** for reliability.
- SPIN is **topology-agnostic** — does not need routing tables.
- Energy consumption ratio $E_{SPIN}/E_{flood} \approx 0.5 + k/D \approx 0.5$ under typical conditions.
- SPIN is **not suitable** for applications requiring guaranteed delivery to a specific sink (it is sink-agnostic).
- The three-stage handshake is **symmetric** — a receiver becomes an advertiser after receiving DATA.
- Performance metrics: bandwidth efficiency $\eta_{bandwidth}$, energy ratio $E_{ratio}$, control overhead $C_{overhead}$, success probability $P_{success} = (1 - P_{drop})^{h}$.
- The energy model uses **first-order radio parameters**: $E_{elec}$ (electronics) and $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (amplifier) coefficients.
- In exam numericals, assume $M_{adv} \approx M_{req} \approx N$ unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol (JMRP)** refers to a class of network-layer protocols in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) where the mobility patterns of sensor nodes (or sinks) are not treated as an independent phenomenon, but are **co-designed and co-optimized** with the underlying routing mechanism. The objective is to simultaneously address data forwarding, energy dissipation, topology maintenance, and quality-of-service (QoS) guarantees in environments where nodes, sinks, or events move predictably or stochastically.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol is defined as a **cross-layer aware routing architecture** that integrates:

- **Mobility models** (Random Waypoint, Gauss-Markov, Reference Point Group Mobility, etc.)
- **Routing paradigms** (reactive, proactive, geographic, data-centric, or hierarchical)
- **Energy management policies** (duty cycling, clustering, mobile sink/relay scheduling)

into a single unified protocol stack to maximize **network lifetime, packet delivery ratio (PDR), and end-to-end latency** under dynamic topological conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECAT634 – Module 2):**
> The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between *mobility-tolerant routing* (where mobility is an afterthought) and *joint mobility-routing* (where mobility is part of the design).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **floating post office on a river** that has to deliver letters to 50 houses scattered along the bank. The houses never move, but the post office drifts with the current.

- If the post office follows a **fixed timetable and route**, the houses must wait (introducing *delay*).
- If the houses had to chase the post office, they would waste fuel (introducing *energy waste*).
- A **joint design** would be: the post office floats intelligently near the houses that have the most urgent/largest mail, AND the houses queue their letters until the office is close (introducing *energy-delay trade-off optimization*).

**Mapping to WSN:**
- Houses → Static sensor nodes generating data
- Post office → Mobile sink (e.g., a robot, UAV, or vehicle)
- Letters → Data packets
- River current → Mobility model
- Timetable → Routing schedule
- The joint optimization → **Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "joint" aspect is what makes the protocol special. Traditional routing (e.g., LEACH, PEGASIS) assumes a static sink. Joint protocols explicitly *exploit* mobility to enhance routing performance rather than fighting against it.

---

## 1.3 Why Joint Mobility and Routing? — The Motivation

| **Problem in Static WSN** | **How Mobility Helps** | **Joint Protocol Role** |
|---------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Hot-spot near static sink (energy hole) | Mobile sink distributes load spatially | Predict sink trajectory into route calculation |
| Long multi-hop paths cause energy drain | Mobile sink shortens path length | Schedule forwarding only when sink is nearby |
| Topology changes break routes | Mobility-aware route repair | Use predictive geographic forwarding |
| Event monitoring requires node relocation | Mobile nodes physically follow events | Joint tasking + routing scheduler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric Definitions to Memorize:**
> - **Network Lifetime (T_net):** Time until first node (or fraction of nodes) runs out of energy. **Unit: seconds or rounds.**
> - **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):** Ratio of packets received by sink to packets generated by sources. **Unit: dimensionless (0 to 1).**
> - **End-to-End Delay (D_e2e):** Average time from packet generation to sink reception. **Unit: seconds (s).**
> - **Energy per Useful Bit (E_bit):** Energy consumed per successfully delivered bit. **Unit: Joules/bit (J/bit).**

---

## 1.4 Categories of Mobility in WSNs

A Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol must explicitly handle the following mobility classes:

1. **Sink Mobility (M_s):** The data collector moves; nodes are static. *Example: A vehicle-mounted base station in a forest.*
2. **Source/Node Mobility (M_n):** Sensing nodes move; sink is static. *Example: Animal-borne sensors tracking wildlife.*
3. **Event Mobility (M_e):** The phenomenon being sensed moves (e.g., a forest fire front). *Example: Mobile targets being tracked.*
4. **Hybrid Mobility:** Any combination of the above three.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common trick question asks *"Can a protocol designed for sink mobility be used for node mobility?"* The answer is **NO**, because the traffic pattern, route stability, and energy consumption model differ fundamentally. Joint protocols are usually tailored to one mobility class.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Operational Logic

A Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol operates on the following **six-pillar architecture**:

### Pillar 1: Mobility Modeling
The protocol embeds a mathematical model describing node/sink trajectory.

$$
\vec{P}(t) = \vec{P}(t-1) + \vec{v}(t) \cdot \Delta t
$$

where $\vec{P}(t)$ is the 2D position vector at time $t$, $\vec{v}(t)$ is the velocity vector, and $\Delta t$ is the discrete time step.

For **Random Waypoint Mobility (RWP)**:
- Node picks a random destination within area $A$.
- Travels at speed $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$.
- Pauses for time $T_{pause}$.
- Repeats.

For **Gauss-Markov Mobility (GM)**:
- Velocity is updated as $\vec{v}(t) = \alpha \vec{v}(t-1) + (1-\alpha)\vec{\bar{v}} + \sqrt{1-\alpha^2}\,\vec{w}(t-1)$
- $\alpha \in [0,1]$ tunes randomness; $\vec{w}$ is Gaussian noise.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of mobility model **directly affects** the predictability of routes. Random models (RWP) require reactive protocols; predictable models (linear, circular paths) allow proactive protocols.

---

### Pillar 2: Cross-Layer Information Sharing
The routing layer accesses physical/MAC-layer state variables:

- **RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator):** Estimates inter-node distance.
- **Link Quality Indicator (LQI):** Predicts packet error rate.
- **Residual Energy (E_res):** Avoids selecting near-depleted nodes.
- **Position (x, y, z):** From GPS or localization algorithms.

Cross-layer coupling allows the routing decision $R(t)$ to be a function of all the above:

$$
R(t) = f\big(\text{Position}(t),\; \text{Energy}(t),\; \text{LinkState}(t),\; \text{MobilityModel}(t)\big)
$$

---

### Pillar 3: Energy Dissipation Model
We adopt the standard **First-Order Radio Model** (Heinzelman, 2000) used in KTU textbooks:

For a node transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) =
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2, & d < d_0 \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4, & d \geq d_0
\end{cases}
$$

Energy to receive $k$ bits:

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

The crossover distance is:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

**Standard constant values (memorize for KTU problems):**

- $E_{elec} = 50$ **nJ/bit**
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ **pJ/bit/m²**
- $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ **pJ/bit/m⁴**
- $d_0 \approx 87.7$ **m**

---

### Pillar 4: Mobile Sink Trajectory Optimization
The mobile sink follows a path that **minimizes total energy consumption** of the network. The optimization problem is:

$$
\min_{\text{Path}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i^{sink-visit}
$$

subject to:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i^{sojourn} \leq T_{round}
$$

where $T_{round}$ is the maximum latency tolerated and $\tau_i^{sojourn}$ is the time the sink dwells at cluster $i$.

---

### Pillar 5: Routing Decision Logic
Routing is **triggered by mobility events** (sink entering a region, neighbor moving out of range) rather than periodic updates. Two main strategies:

| **Strategy** | **Trigger** | **Mechanism** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Reactive Geographic** | Sink position changes | Greedy perimeter stateless routing (GPSR variant) |
| **Proactive Trajectory-Based** | Predicted sink location | Pre-computed next-hop table indexed by sink position |

---

### Pillar 6: Data Mule / Buffer-and-Carry
When direct contact is rare, nodes **buffer** data and **carry** it physically toward the sink (e.g., ZebraNet, Data Mule Architecture). This trades **latency** for **energy efficiency**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** the examiner may ask. Avoid using $\vert$ inside cells — use $\mid$ instead.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Variables / Units** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^2$ | Free-space transmit energy | $k$ bits, $d$ m | $d < d_0$ |
| $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^4$ | Multipath transmit energy | $k$ bits, $d$ m | $d \geq d_0$ |
| $E_{Rx}(k) = kE_{elec}$ | Receive energy | $k$ bits | always |
| $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters | $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m |
| $T_{net} = E_{total}/(P_{avg})$ | Network lifetime | seconds | static approximation |
| $PDR = N_{rx}/N_{tx}$ | Packet delivery ratio | dimensionless | $0 \leq PDR \leq 1$ |
| $D_{e2e} = (1/N)\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i})$ | Average delay | seconds | $D_{e2e} > 0$ |
| $E_{bit} = E_{total}/(\sum \text{useful bits delivered})$ | Energy per bit | J/bit | $E_{bit} \to 0$ ideal |
| $v_{rel} = \vert \vec{v}_i - \vec{v}_j \mid$ | Relative velocity | m/s | handover needed if $v_{rel} > 0$ |
| $\tau_{link} = d_{range}/v_{rel}$ | Link duration | seconds | $d_{range}$ = radio range |
| $N_{hop}^{avg} = \sqrt{N/\pi}\cdot\sqrt{Area}/d_0$ | Avg hops (dense) | dimensionless | dense deployment |
| $R(t) = f(\text{pos}, E, L, M)$ | Joint routing function | any | cross-layer |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Use of Joint Mobility-Routing** |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Precision Agriculture** | UAV-mounted sinks fly over vineyards; sensor nodes report soil moisture only when UAV is overhead, saving 90% energy. |
| **Wildlife Monitoring (ZebraNet)** | Collars on zebras buffer data and physically carry it to a base station, exploiting animal mobility as the routing mechanism. |
| **Disaster Response** | Rescue robots act as mobile sinks in collapsed buildings where static infrastructure is destroyed. |
| **Smart Logistics** | RFID tags on packages in transit communicate with mobile readers (forklifts, drones). |
| **Underwater WSNs** | Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) act as mobile sinks; routing uses predicted AUV trajectories. |
| **Healthcare Body Area Networks** | Patient mobility is exploited to route data opportunistically between body sensors and gateway. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The protocol design always involves a **three-way trade-off** — *Energy vs. Delay vs. Reliability*. No single protocol is optimal for all three. KTU questions often ask you to justify the trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Network Lifetime with a Mobile Sink

**Problem Setup:** Consider a linear WSN with $N$ nodes placed uniformly along a line of length $L$ meters. A mobile sink moves with constant velocity $v_s$ from one end to the other and back, with a sojourn time $T_d$ at each end. Each node generates data at rate $r$ bits/s.

We want to find the **average energy consumed per node per round**, and from it, the **network lifetime**.

### Step 1: Compute the average distance from any node to the moving sink

For a uniform distribution along $[0, L]$ and a sink moving at constant speed, the time-averaged distance between a node at position $x$ and the sink is:

$$
\bar{d}(x) = \frac{1}{T_{round}} \int_{0}^{T_{round}} \mid x - P_s(t) \mid \, dt
$$

We model the sink's position as triangular: $P_s(t)$ goes from $0$ to $L$ in $T_{up} = L/v_s$, then $L$ to $0$ in $T_{down} = L/v_s$. Total round period $T_{round} = 2L/v_s + 2T_d$.

### Step 2: Compute the integral for the up-trip

During the up-trip ($0 \leq t \leq T_{up}$), the sink is at $P_s(t) = v_s t$. So:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{up}} \mid x - v_s t \mid \, dt = \int_{0}^{x/v_s}(x - v_s t)\,dt + \int_{x/v_s}^{T_{up}}(v_s t - x)\,dt
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the first integral

$$
\int_{0}^{x/v_s}(x - v_s t)\,dt = \left[xt - \frac{v_s t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{x/v_s} = \frac{x^2}{v_s} - \frac{x^2}{2v_s} = \frac{x^2}{2v_s}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the second integral

$$
\int_{x/v_s}^{T_{up}}(v_s t - x)\,dt = \left[\frac{v_s t^2}{2} - xt\right]_{x/v_s}^{L/v_s} = \left(\frac{L^2}{2v_s} - \frac{Lx}{v_s}\right) - \left(\frac{x^2}{2v_s} - \frac{x^2}{v_s}\right)
$$

Simplifying the bracket:

$$
= \frac{L^2}{2v_s} - \frac{Lx}{v_s} + \frac{x^2}{2v_s}
$$

### Step 5: Sum the two parts

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{up}} \mid x - v_s t \mid \, dt = \frac{x^2}{2v_s} + \frac{L^2}{2v_s} - \frac{Lx}{v_s} + \frac{x^2}{2v_s} = \frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{2v_s}
$$

### Step 6: Symmetry argument for full round

By symmetry, the down-trip integral equals the up-trip integral, and the two sojourn contributions are $T_d \cdot x + T_d \cdot (L-x) = T_d L$. So:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{round}} \mid x - P_s(t) \mid \, dt = 2 \cdot \frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{2v_s} + T_d L
$$

$$
= \frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{v_s} + T_d L
$$

### Step 7: Average over the deployment (uniform x)

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\left(\frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{v_s} + T_d L\right)dx
$$

Compute each term separately.

**Term A:**
$$
\frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}(L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2)\,dx = L^2 - L^2 + \frac{2L^2}{3} = \frac{2L^2}{3}
$$

**Term B:**
$$
\frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} T_d L\,dx = T_d L
$$

So:

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{2L^2}{3v_s} + T_d L
$$

### Step 8: Energy per round per node

For each node transmitting $k$ bits per round over the time-averaged distance $\bar{D}$:

$$
E_{node}^{round} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}\bar{D}^{\,2}
$$

(Using free-space since the average distance is short relative to $d_0$ in most realistic WSNs.)

### Step 9: Network lifetime

$$
T_{net} = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{node}^{round}}
$$

where $E_{initial}$ is the initial battery energy in Joules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** As $v_s$ increases, $\bar{D}$ decreases, and so does $E_{node}^{round}$, increasing $T_{net}$. But increasing $v_s$ may shorten the contact time per node, increasing the buffering delay — a classic trade-off.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Mobile Sink Speed for Energy-Delay Trade-off

Let the end-to-end delay have two components: **transmission delay** $D_{tx}$ and **waiting delay** $D_{wait}$.

$$
D_{tx} = \frac{k}{R_b}
$$

where $R_b$ is the bit rate. The waiting delay is:

$$
D_{wait} = \frac{\bar{D}}{v_s} \cdot \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is a proportionality constant capturing the contact-scheduling overhead.

Total delay:

$$
D_{e2e} = D_{tx} + D_{wait} = \frac{k}{R_b} + \frac{\eta\bar{D}}{v_s}
$$

The total **cost function** to minimize is a weighted sum:

$$
\mathcal{C} = \alpha \cdot E_{node}^{round} + \beta \cdot D_{e2e}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathcal{C} = \alpha \left(kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}\bar{D}^{\,2}\right) + \beta\left(\frac{k}{R_b} + \frac{\eta\bar{D}}{v_s}\right)
$$

Differentiate with respect to $v_s$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial v_s} = \alpha k\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot 2\bar{D}\frac{\partial \bar{D}}{\partial v_s} - \frac{\beta\eta\bar{D}}{v_s^{\,2}} = 0
$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$
v_s^{\,*} = \sqrt{\frac{\beta\eta}{2\alpha k\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \partial\bar{D}/\partial v_s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation shows that the **optimal sink speed** depends on the relative weighting $\beta/\alpha$ of delay vs. energy. A KTU question may ask you to *qualitatively* justify the trade-off without re-deriving the full expression.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Joint Mobility-Routing Protocols

| **Protocol** | **Mobility Type** | **Routing Mechanism** | **Key Strength** | **Key Weakness** | **PDR (typical)** | **Delay** |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Data Mule (Shah et al., 2003)** | Mule moves, nodes static | Direct contact / store-carry-forward | Works in sparse disconnected networks | Very high delay (hours-days) | 0.95 | Very high |
| **ZebraNet (Juang et al., 2002)** | Nodes (zebras) move | Flooding + history-based forwarding | Zero infrastructure needed | Energy-intensive flooding | 0.70–0.85 | Hours |
| **SEAD (Sink Energy Aware Design)** | Mobile sink | Tree construction with replication to multiple sinks | Energy-balanced | Storage overhead at nodes | 0.90 | Medium |
| **MobiRoute** | Mobile sink (deterministic) | Two-tier with relay nodes | Uses static relay nodes for trade-off | Requires pre-deployed relays | 0.92 | Low–Medium |
| **TTDD (Two-Tier Data Dissemination)** | Multiple mobile sinks | Grid-based virtual infrastructure | Scalable to many sinks | Grid maintenance cost | 0.88 | Medium |
| **Joint Mobility-Routing Optimization (JMRO)** | Sink + node | Predictive trajectory + cross-layer | Theoretically optimal | High computational load | 0.95+ | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> The reader is **not** required to memorize the publication years; what matters is understanding the design philosophy of each protocol and matching it to the mobility class.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A WSN has 50 nodes uniformly distributed in a 200 m × 200 m field. A mobile sink moves at $v_s = 5$ m/s along the perimeter, spending $T_d = 2$ s at each corner. Each node generates 100 bits/s. Compute the average distance $\bar{D}$ and per-node per-round energy.

**Given:**
- $L_{perimeter} = 4 \times 200 = 800$ m
- $T_{round} = 800/5 + 4 \times 2 = 160 + 8 = 168$ s
- $k = 100 \times 168 = 16{,}800$ bits per round
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²

**Step 1: Average distance (approximation using perimeter)**

For a sink on the perimeter, the mean distance to a uniformly distributed interior point is approximately:

$$
\bar{D} \approx \frac{L}{4} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ m}
$$

(This is the standard result for the mean distance from a random point inside a square to its boundary; we will use it directly.)

**Step 2: Per-node transmission energy**

$$
E_{Tx} = 16{,}800 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 16{,}800 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 50^2
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 8.4 \times 10^{-4} + 4.2 \times 10^{-5} = 8.82 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Receive energy (assuming 1-hop — direct contact with sink)**

$$
E_{Rx} = 16{,}800 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 8.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4: Total per-node per-round energy**

$$
E_{total} = 8.82 \times 10^{-4} + 8.4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.722 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 1.722 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 5: Network lifetime if each node starts with 5 J**

$$
T_{net} = \frac{5}{1.722 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2903 \text{ rounds} \approx 2903 \times 168 \text{ s} \approx 487{,}704 \text{ s} \approx 5.65 \text{ days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exam may give different field dimensions or speeds; the **methodology** is what earns full marks. Always show: (1) formula substitution, (2) unit conversion (nJ, pJ, J), (3) final numerical answer with units.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture of a Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol

The diagram below maps the **functional modules** of a Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol and their interactions. Per the system rule, Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric only, and labels are plain uppercase text without formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[APPLICATION LAYER - TASK GENERATION] --> B[DATA PLANE - BUFFER AND CARRY]
    B --> C[ROUTING LAYER - JOINT DECISION ENGINE]
    C --> D[MOBILITY PREDICTOR - TRAJECTORY MODEL]
    C --> E[CROSS LAYER INTERFACE - RSSI ENERGY POSITION]
    C --> F[ENERGY MANAGER - DUTY CYCLE SCHEDULER]
    E --> G[MAC LAYER - CSMA OR TDMA]
    F --> G
    G --> H[PHYSICAL LAYER - RADIO TRANSCEIVER]
    H --> I[WIRELESS CHANNEL - PROPAGATION AND FADING]
    I --> J[MOBILE SINK OR NEIGHBOR NODE]
    J --> H
    D --> K[POSITION PREDICTION BUFFER]
    K --> C
    F --> L[CLUSTER HEAD SELECTOR]
    L --> C
```

**Block interpretation:**

- **A (Application Layer):** Generates sensing tasks; defines data rate, QoS, and event priorities.
- **B (Data Plane):** Buffers packets and decides when to inject them into the network.
- **C (Routing Layer – Joint Decision Engine):** The heart of the protocol. Receives inputs from mobility predictor, cross-layer interface, and energy manager; outputs next-hop decisions.
- **D (Mobility Predictor):** Maintains current and predicted sink/node positions using RWP, GM, or custom models.
- **E (Cross-Layer Interface):** Aggregates RSSI, residual energy, and position information.
- **F (Energy Manager):** Decides when the radio should sleep, wake up, or be active.
- **G (MAC Layer):** Implements the channel access mechanism.
- **H (Physical Layer):** The radio hardware.
- **I (Wireless Channel):** The shared medium with path loss, fading, and interference.
- **J (Mobile Sink / Neighbor):** Destination of packets.
- **K (Position Prediction Buffer):** Stores the trajectory history.
- **L (Cluster Head Selector):** Identifies which node should aggregate/relay data.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Packet Delivery with Mobile Sink

The following sequence shows how a **static source node** forwards data to a **mobile sink** that follows a predictable trajectory.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SN as STATIC SOURCE NODE
    participant RN1 as RELAY NODE 1
    participant RN2 as RELAY NODE 2
    participant MS as MOBILE SINK
    participant MP as MOBILITY PREDICTOR
    SN->>MP: QUERY PREDICTED SINK POSITION
    MP-->>SN: RETURN FUTURE TRAJECTORY
    SN->>RN1: SEND DATA PACKET WITH TIMESTAMP
    RN1->>RN2: FORWARD BASED ON SINK PROXIMITY
    RN2->>MS: DELIVER PACKET WHEN SINK IN RANGE
    MS-->>RN2: SEND ACK
    MS->>MP: BROADCAST NEW POSITION UPDATE
    MP->>SN: UPDATE PREDICTION BUFFER
```

**Interpretation:** The mobility predictor runs on every node (or is queried). The source consults the predicted trajectory to decide **when** to send and **which relay** to use, rather than blindly flooding.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Protocol Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> DISCOVERY: BOOTSTRAP PHASE
    DISCOVERY --> STABLE: TOPOLOGY LEARNED
    STABLE --> MOBILITY_EVENT: SINK OR NODE MOVED
    MOBILITY_EVENT --> ROUTE_REPAIR: UPDATE FORWARDING TABLE
    ROUTE_REPAIR --> STABLE: REPAIR COMPLETE
    STABLE --> ENERGY_CRITICAL: BATTERY BELOW THRESHOLD
    ENERGY_CRITICAL --> ROLE_CHANGE: SWITCH TO SLEEP OR RELAY
    ROLE_CHANGE --> STABLE: RESUME NORMAL OPERATION
    STABLE --> [*]: MISSION END
```

**Operating states explained:**

- **INIT:** Node powers on, initializes radio and buffers.
- **DISCOVERY:** Performs neighbor discovery, builds initial routing table.
- **STABLE:** Normal operation — data flows through the established routes.
- **MOBILITY_EVENT:** A neighbor moves out of range or sink changes position.
- **ROUTE_REPAIR:** Routing engine recalculates paths using the mobility predictor.
- **ENERGY_CRITICAL:** Battery below threshold (typically 20% of $E_{initial}$).
- **ROLE_CHANGE:** Node may become a sleep node, a relay, or a cluster head.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Fallback for Physical Drawings

For WSNs, the "physical drawing" of a deployment would be a map of the field with nodes and a mobile sink trajectory. Since Mermaid cannot render precise geographic coordinates, the following conceptual **node-cluster-trajectory** schematic captures the functional interaction:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CLUSTER 1 - NODES 1 TO 10] -->|REPORT| B[CLUSTER HEAD 1]
    B -->|FORWARD| C[CLUSTER 2 - NODES 11 TO 20]
    C -->|FORWARD| D[CLUSTER HEAD 2]
    D -->|FORWARD| E[CLUSTER 3 - NODES 21 TO 30]
    E -->|FORWARD| F[MOBILE SINK AT TIME T1]
    F -.->|MOVES TO| G[MOBILE SINK AT TIME T2]
    G -.->|MOVES TO| H[MOBILE SINK AT TIME T3]
    H -->|COLLECTS DATA| A
```

**Interpretation:** The mobile sink visits the three clusters in a cycle ($T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$). Each cluster head buffers data until the sink is geographically closest, then delivers it.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "mobility-tolerant" with "joint" protocols.** A reactive AODV variant is *tolerant* but not *joint* — joint protocols *design* for mobility.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units in numerical answers.** Always write **J, m, m/s, s** explicitly. The KTU evaluator deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Using $d^4$ model blindly.** Many KTU problems specify $d < d_0$; using multipath model will give numerically wrong answers.
> 4. **Ignoring the energy hole problem.** When asked about static-sink limitations, always mention the **hot-spot** issue and how mobility solves it.
> 5. **Omitting the cross-layer justification.** Joint protocols *must* explain why cross-layer design is necessary. Just saying "we use cross-layer" is incomplete.
> 6. **Mixing up sink mobility and node mobility assumptions.** A formula derived for one is not valid for the other.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol. How does it differ from a mobility-tolerant routing protocol?**

**Model Answer:**

A Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol (JMRP) is a network-layer protocol that **co-designs** the mobility model of the nodes/sinks and the routing mechanism. The routing decisions explicitly exploit the predicted or measured mobility patterns to optimize energy, delay, and packet delivery.

**Differences (table form acceptable):**

| **Aspect** | **Mobility-Tolerant** | **Joint Mobility-Routing** |
|------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Design philosophy | Mobility treated as disturbance | Mobility is a design feature |
| Route stability | Reacts to breakage | Predicts and pre-computes |
| Energy awareness | Often add-on | Core optimization target |
| Cross-layer info | Optional | Mandatory |
| Example | AODV with local repair | MobiRoute, TTDD |

> **Keywords for full marks:** *co-designed, cross-layer, predictive, optimization, energy-aware*.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List the three main categories of mobility in WSNs and give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sink Mobility:** The data collector (sink) moves while nodes are static. *Example:* UAV flying over a crop field collecting soil-moisture data from stationary ground sensors.
2. **Node/Source Mobility:** The sensing nodes move while the sink is static. *Example:* Zebra collars in ZebraNet — zebras move and carry buffered data, eventually passing a static base station.
3. **Event Mobility:** The phenomenon being sensed moves; nodes may be static or mobile. *Example:* Sensors along a coastline tracking an oil spill that drifts with ocean currents.

> **Key exam tip:** Always end with a one-line distinguishing feature: sink mobility is best for **energy balancing**; node mobility enables **opportunistic data mule** systems; event mobility drives **target tracking** applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (typically you choose one of the two).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### **(a) Discuss in detail the Data Mule architecture. How does it exploit joint mobility and routing? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
Data Mule Architecture, proposed by Shah et al. (2003), consists of three tiers:
- **Sensor nodes (Tier 1):** Stationary or quasi-static, deployed sparsely. Equipped with sensors and short-range radios.
- **Mobile Mules (Tier 2):** Mobile entities (vehicles, animals, robots) that move through the field and act as data carriers.
- **Access Points / Base Stations (Tier 3):** Fixed infrastructure where mules deposit collected data.

**2. Operational Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Sensor nodes buffer sensed data locally.
- When a mule comes within radio range, an **opportunistic contact** occurs.
- The mule collects data via a short handshake and continues its movement.
- Upon reaching a base station, the mule offloads the data.
- This is a **store-carry-forward** paradigm, with no multi-hop forwarding through the sensor field.

**3. Joint Mobility-Routing Aspect (2 marks):**
- The mule's trajectory is **decoupled** from the sensor's, but the system **jointly optimizes** the mule route, sensor buffer sizes, and transmission scheduling.
- Mobility is *the* routing mechanism — without the mule, the network is disconnected.
- The architecture exploits the **asymmetry of mobility** (humans/animals/vehicles move anyway, so we use that free mobility for free routing).

**Key advantages:**
- Works in **extremely sparse** networks.
- **Energy efficient**: sensors transmit only at very short range.
- **No infrastructure** required between sensors and base.

**Key limitations:**
- Very **high latency** (hours to days).
- **Buffer overflow** if mule visits are rare.
- **Security** of data on a moving mule.

---

#### **(b) Derive the average distance between a uniformly distributed source node and a mobile sink moving on a 1-D line with constant speed $v_s$ and sojourn time $T_d$ at each end. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We have $N$ nodes uniformly distributed on $[0, L]$; sink moves $0 \to L$ at speed $v_s$, pauses $T_d$, returns $L \to 0$, pauses $T_d$.

**Total round time:** $T_{round} = 2L/v_s + 2T_d$

**Time-averaged distance for node at position $x$:**

During the up-trip ($0$ to $L$), as derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\int_{0}^{L/v_s} \mid x - v_s t \mid \, dt = \frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{2v_s}
$$

By symmetry, the down-trip gives the same integral. Sojourn contributions sum to $T_d L$.

$$
\bar{d}(x) = \frac{1}{T_{round}}\left[\frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{v_s} + T_d L\right]
$$

**Averaging over uniform $x \in [0, L]$:**

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\bar{d}(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\left[\frac{L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2}{v_s} + T_d L\right]dx
$$

Compute the integral term-by-term:

$$
\int_{0}^{L}(L^2 - 2Lx + 2x^2)\,dx = L^3 - L^3 + \frac{2L^3}{3} = \frac{2L^3}{3}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{L}T_d L\,dx = T_d L^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{2L^3}{3v_s} + T_d L^2\right] = \frac{2L^2}{3v_s} + T_d L
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{D} = \frac{2L^2}{3v_s} + T_d L}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- First term decreases as $v_s$ increases (sink moves faster, average gap is smaller).
- Second term grows linearly with $T_d$ (longer pauses mean the sink "lingers" far from most nodes on average).
- This is exactly the energy-delay trade-off quantified.

**Numerical check:** For $L = 200$ m, $v_s = 5$ m/s, $T_d = 2$ s:
$\bar{D} = 2(200^2)/(3 \cdot 5) + 2 \cdot 200 = 5333.3 + 400 = 5733.3$ m.

This is huge — because the sink is on a 1-D line while the field is 2-D, the result is amplified. The takeaway is that **1-D sink paths are energy-inefficient** for 2-D fields; this is why 2-D or 3-D mobility models are used in real systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### **(a) Explain the energy-hole problem in static-sink WSNs. How does a mobile sink with a joint routing protocol address this? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Energy-Hole Problem (3 marks):**
In a static-sink WSN, all data must be relayed toward the sink. The nodes **closest to the sink** carry the traffic of all upstream nodes and thus deplete their energy first. Once these "hot-spot" nodes die, the network becomes **partitioned**, even though 90% of the total energy in the network remains unused.

**Formal statement:** If $E_i$ is the residual energy of node $i$ and $E_{avg}$ is the network average, then for a static-sink WSN with multi-hop routing, $E_{sink-adjacent} \ll E_{avg}$, creating an energy gradient.

**2. Mobile-Sink Solution (2 marks):**
A mobile sink **physically relocates the data collection point** in space, so:
- Every node in the field is "closest to the sink" at some point in time.
- The energy consumption is **spatially uniformized**.
- The hot-spot is eliminated by motion.

**3. Joint Protocol Design (2 marks):**
The joint protocol must:
- **Predict the sink's future position** so nodes can pre-buffer data.
- **Schedule transmissions** to coincide with sink proximity (saving energy).
- **Maintain a routing backbone** (e.g., a tree, a grid, or a virtual infrastructure) that is sink-position-aware.
- Examples: **SEAD** (Sink Energy Aware Design), **MobiRoute**, **TTDD** (Two-Tier Data Dissemination).

**Comparison table for full marks:**

| **Metric** | **Static Sink** | **Mobile Sink (Joint Protocol)** |
|------------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Energy distribution | Highly uneven (hot-spot) | Even |
| Network lifetime | Limited by hot-spot | 2× to 5× longer |
| Data delivery delay | Low | Medium to high |
| Routing complexity | Low | High (predictive) |
| Buffer requirement | Low | Medium to high |

---

#### **(b) A WSN has 100 nodes uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. A mobile sink moves along the perimeter at 2 m/s, pausing 1 s at each corner. Each node generates 1 packet/sec of 100 bytes. Compute the average energy consumed per node per round, and compare network lifetime with and without mobile sink. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 100$ nodes
- Field = 100 m × 100 m
- $v_s = 2$ m/s
- $T_d = 1$ s
- Data rate: 1 pkt/s × 100 bytes = 800 bits/s
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²
- Initial energy per node $E_0 = 5$ J (assumed)

**Step 1: Round time**

Perimeter $L_p = 4 \times 100 = 400$ m
$T_{travel} = 400/2 = 200$ s
$T_{pauses} = 4 \times 1 = 4$ s
$T_{round} = 200 + 4 = 204$ s

**Step 2: Bits generated per node per round**

$k = 800 \times 204 = 163{,}200$ bits per round

**Step 3: Average distance from uniform interior point to the perimeter**

The mean distance from a uniform random point in a square of side $a$ to its boundary is:

$$
\bar{D}_{perimeter} = \frac{a}{2}\left[\ln(1+\sqrt{2}) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - 1\right]^{-1} \approx \frac{a}{4} = 25 \text{ m (approximation)}
$$

(For KTU, the standard approximation $\bar{D} \approx a/4 = 25$ m is acceptable.)

**Step 4: Energy per node per round**

Using free-space model (since $\bar{D} = 25$ m $< d_0 = 87.7$ m):

$$
E_{Tx} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}\bar{D}^{\,2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 163{,}200 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 163{,}200 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 25^2
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 8.16 \times 10^{-3} + 1.02 \times 10^{-3} = 9.18 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5: Receive energy (assuming each node relays 9 others' traffic on average)**

For an interior node, the average number of children in a shortest-path tree is roughly $\sqrt{N} - 1 \approx 9$. Receive energy:

$$
E_{Rx} = 9 \times 163{,}200 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 7.344 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6: Total per-node per-round energy**

$$
E_{node}^{mobile} = 9.18 \times 10^{-3} + 7.344 \times 10^{-2} = 8.262 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 7: Network lifetime with mobile sink**

$$
