T_{net}^{mobile} = \frac{5}{8.262 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 60.5 \text{ rounds}
$$

**Step 8: Comparison with static sink**

For a static sink in the center, the hot-spot node (the one closest to the sink) must relay traffic from 99 nodes, so:

$$
E_{hotspot}^{static} \approx 99 \times 163{,}200 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + \varepsilon_{fs}\text{ terms} \approx 0.8 \text{ J per round}
$$

$$
T_{net}^{static} \approx \frac{5}{0.8} \approx 6.25 \text{ rounds}
$$

**Step 9: Improvement ratio**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{60.5}{6.25} \approx 9.7\times
$$

> **Conclusion:** A mobile sink with joint routing provides approximately **9–10× improvement** in network lifetime over a static sink in this scenario, at the cost of increased per-node buffering and routing complexity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to include **receive energy**. A complete answer must include both $E_{Tx}$ and $E_{Rx}$. Marks are split 50-50 between the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [ ] Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol = **co-design** of mobility model + routing protocol.
- [ ] Three mobility types: **Sink, Node, Event**.
- [ ] Energy-hole problem: hot-spot near static sink.
- [ ] Cross-layer design is **mandatory** in joint protocols.
- [ ] Trade-off triangle: **Energy ↔ Delay ↔ Reliability**.

### Key Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] $E_{Tx}(k,d)$ free-space ($d^2$) and multipath ($d^4$) models.
- [ ] $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m.
- [ ] Average distance: $\bar{D} = 2L^2/(3v_s) + T_d L$ (1-D line).
- [ ] $PDR = N_{rx}/N_{tx}$.
- [ ] $D_{e2e} = D_{tx} + D_{wait}$.

### Standard Constants
- [ ] $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit.
- [ ] $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m².
- [ ] $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.

### Protocol Names (Recognize and Explain)
- [ ] **Data Mule** — opportunistic contact, store-carry-forward.
- [ ] **ZebraNet** — wildlife monitoring, flooding.
- [ ] **SEAD** — multiple mobile sinks, tree replication.
- [ ] **MobiRoute** — relay nodes + mobile sink.
- [ ] **TTDD** — grid-based for multiple mobile sinks.
- [ ] **JMRO** — predictive joint optimization.

### Diagrams to Draw in Exam
- [ ] Block diagram showing Application → Routing → MAC → PHY layers with cross-layer feedback from Mobility Predictor and Energy Manager.
- [ ] Sequence diagram of packet delivery to mobile sink.
- [ ] State diagram of protocol operation (INIT → DISCOVERY → STABLE → MOBILITY_EVENT → ROUTE_REPAIR).
- [ ] Node-cluster-trajectory schematic showing the mobile sink visiting clusters.

### Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Don't confuse mobility-tolerant with joint mobility-routing.
- [ ] Always state units in numerical answers.
- [ ] Choose free-space or multipath model based on $d$ vs. $d_0$.
- [ ] When in doubt about a formula, derive it from first principles — partial credit is given for the derivation logic.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution Pattern (PECAT634)
- [ ] Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks.
- [ ] Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks.
- [ ] Total: 20 marks per module test.
- [ ] Cognitive levels tested: **L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)** — with emphasis on **Apply and Analyze** in this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Sentence Takeaway:**
> A Joint Mobility and Routing Protocol turns node/sink mobility from a *nuisance to be tolerated* into a *feature to be exploited*, achieving up to an order-of-magnitude improvement in network lifetime at the cost of moderate increases in delay and buffering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Protocols for WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transport Protocols for WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Transport Protocols in WSNs

**Transport Protocols in WSNs** are a specialized set of communication rules operating at the transport layer (OSI Layer 4) that govern end-to-end data delivery, reliability, congestion control, and energy-aware packet forwarding between source sensor nodes and the base station (sink) in resource-constrained wireless environments.

Unlike traditional Internet transport protocols (TCP/UDP), WSN transport protocols must be **energy-efficient**, **data-centric**, and **application-aware** because sensor nodes have limited battery, limited memory, lossy wireless links, and the traffic pattern is predominantly many-to-one (convergecast).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECAT634 Module 2):**
> The transport layer in WSNs handles three fundamental challenges:
> 1. **Reliability** of event detection at the sink
> 2. **Congestion control** in multi-hop wireless paths
> 3. **Energy efficiency** for prolonged network lifetime

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Hospital Triage System

Imagine a **disaster zone with 500 injured civilians (sensor nodes)** sending pulse-rate updates to a **single emergency doctor at a field hospital (sink/base station)**. The doctor cannot handle 500 simultaneous voice calls (would crash the system = congestion). So:

- The civilians use **short SOS pings only when their condition changes critically** (event-driven reliability).
- Nearby civilians form **relay groups** (multi-hop clustering) to forward messages.
- The doctor periodically broadcasts **"calm down" signals** when too many reports arrive (backpressure congestion control).
- If a critical report is lost, the affected civilian **slowly retransmits** it without flooding the channel (reliability with energy control).

This is exactly how WSN transport protocols work — **event-triggered, energy-aware, and congestion-resilient**.

### 1.3 Why Traditional TCP/UDP Fail in WSNs

| Property | Traditional TCP/UDP | WSN Requirement |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Endpoint model | Host-to-host (address-centric) | Data-centric (attribute-based) |
| Reliability | End-to-end ACK from every node | Application-specific (event-level) |
| Congestion control | Window-based TCP AIMD | Cross-layer, priority-based |
| Energy awareness | None | Core design metric |
| Mobility/Topology | Mostly static paths | Highly dynamic, lossy links |
| Traffic direction | Bidirectional | Predominantly upstream (to sink) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** In WSNs, reliability is **not about packet-level ACK from every sensor**, but about ensuring the **sink receives sufficient information** about an event to act upon it. This is called **Event-to-Sink Reliability (ESR)**.

### 1.4 Classification of WSN Transport Protocols

Transport protocols for WSNs are broadly classified into three categories:

1. **Reliable Delivery Protocols** – Guarantee event detection at sink
   - Examples: **PSFQ, RMST, ESRT, GARUDA**
2. **Congestion Control Protocols** – Prevent buffer overflow and packet loss
   - Examples: **CODA, Fusion, ARC**
3. **Hybrid Protocols** – Combine reliability + congestion control + energy efficiency
   - Examples: **STCP, ATP, Flush**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always remember the **3C framework**: **C**ongestion control, **C**onservation of energy, and **C**overage of events. Any transport protocol in WSNs must address at least two of these.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of WSN Transport Layer

The transport layer sits **above the network/routing layer** and **below the application layer**. It interacts with:

- **Lower layer (Network/MAC):** Receives feedback on link quality, residual energy, congestion
- **Upper layer (Application):** Receives application QoS requirements (reliability threshold, latency bound)

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Source node** generates event data (e.g., temperature > 50°C)
2. **Transport layer** tags the data with reliability/priority metadata
3. **Routing layer** forwards packets via multi-hop path to sink
4. **Intermediate nodes** monitor queue length, channel busy ratio
5. **Sink transport layer** aggregates, decodes, and forwards to application
6. **NACK/ACK signals** (or implicit acknowledgement) flow back downstream

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Protocols

#### 2.2.1 PSFQ (Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly)

**Designed by:** Wan, Campbell, Krishnamurthy (2005)
**Goal:** Reliable downstream data dissemination (sink → sensors), e.g., for code updates, queries, parameter reprogramming.

**Three core operations:**

| Operation | Function | Energy Implication |
|-----------|----------|--------------------|
| **PUMP** | Sink injects packets slowly at low rate | Spreads load over time, avoids collisions |
| **FETCH** | Nodes aggressively request retransmission of missing packets | Localized recovery, avoids global flooding |
| **REPORT** | Loss recovery feedback hop-by-hop | Conserves energy by avoiding long ACK chains |

**Why "Pump Slowly"?** Because pumping fast in a wireless broadcast medium causes **collision storms** and **redundant transmissions**, wasting energy.

**Why "Fetch Quickly"?** Because loss recovery must be **fast and localized** — only the affected segment of the path is involved.

#### 2.2.2 RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport)

**Goal:** Reliable upstream delivery (sensors → sink) with **fragmentation support** for large data objects (e.g., images, multimedia sensor data).

**Key features:**
- **Selective ARQ** at the sink
- **Fragment caching** at intermediate nodes
- **Timer-based NACK generation** if reassembly timer expires
- Works with **directed diffusion** routing

#### 2.2.3 CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)

**Three congestion detection mechanisms:**

1. **Receiver-based:** Each node measures **channel busy ratio (CBR)**
2. **Sender-based:** Source monitors **packet service time** vs inter-arrival time
3. **Closed-loop backpressure:** Congested node sends **backpressure messages** upstream

**Equation for congestion detection:**

$$
\text{CBR}_{\text{node}} = \frac{T_{\text{channel busy}}}{T_{\text{total observation}}}
$$

If $\text{CBR} > \text{CBR}_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically **0.6**), congestion is declared.

#### 2.2.4 ESRT (Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport)

**Developed by:** Sankarasubramaniam, Akyildiz, McLaughlin (2003)
**Goal:** Achieve **event reliability** with **minimum energy expenditure**.

**Five operating regions based on Observed Reliability (R) vs Required Reliability (R_target):**

| Region | Condition | Action |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| 1 (No Congestion, Low Reliability) | $R < R_{target}$, no congestion | Increase reporting rate $f$ |
| 2 (No Congestion, Over Reliable) | $R \geq R_{target}$, no congestion | Decrease $f$ to save energy |
| 3 (Congestion, Low Reliability) | $R < R_{target}$, congestion | Decrease $f$ |
| 4 (Congestion, Sufficient) | $R \approx R_{target}$, congestion | Decrease $f$ aggressively |
| 5 (No Congestion, Optimal) | $R = R_{target}$, no congestion | Maintain $f$ |

#### 2.2.5 GARUDA

**Goal:** Reliable downstream delivery with **two-phase design**:
- **Phase 1:** Construct a **virtual backbone (pulse)** using WFS (Wait-for-First-Sequence)
- **Phase 2:** Reliable delivery via **pulse structure** with NACK-only loss recovery

**Key innovation:** Out-of-sequence NACK suppression — only the **first-loss node** sends a NACK, preventing NACK implosion.

#### 2.2.6 STCP (Sensor Transmission Control Protocol)

**Hybrid protocol** providing:
- Variable reliability (end-to-end or hop-by-hop)
- Controlled variable reliability
- End-to-end ACK with **BS (Base Station)-centric session management**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range | Used In Protocol |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Event Reliability $R$ | $R = \frac{N_{\text{received events}}}{N_{\text{actual events}}}$ | dimensionless [0, 1] | ESRT, STCP |
| Reporting Frequency $f$ | Packets per second per source | Hz | ESRT |
| Channel Busy Ratio (CBR) | $\text{CBR} = \frac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{observe}}}$ | ratio [0, 1] | CODA |
| Energy per Bit $E_b$ | $E_b = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{bits transmitted}}}$ | Joule/bit | All protocols |
| Network Lifetime $T_{\text{life}}$ | Time until first node dies | seconds | ESRT, PEGASIS |
| Congestion Window (WSN) | $W(t+1) = W(t) + \alpha$ on success | packets | ATP |
| Goodput $G$ | $G = \frac{N_{\text{useful packets}}}{T_{\text{interval}}}$ | packets/sec | ATP, STCP |
| Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | $\text{PDR} = \frac{N_{\text{received}}}{N_{\text{sent}}}$ | ratio [0,1] | All |
| Loss Recovery Latency $L_{rec}$ | Time from packet loss to successful retransmission | seconds | PSFQ, RMST |
| Hop Count $h$ | Number of intermediate nodes | integer $\geq 1$ | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $0 \leq R \leq 1$ (reliability is a probability)
> - $0 \leq \text{CBR} \leq 1$ (channel busy ratio)
> - $f > 0$ (reporting frequency must be positive)
> - $N_{\text{received}} \leq N_{\text{sent}}$ (you cannot receive more than sent)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Environmental monitoring** (volcano, forest fire): Uses **ESRT** for energy-efficient event detection
- **Structural health monitoring** (bridges, buildings): Uses **RMST** for reliable image/data fragment delivery
- **Military surveillance**: Uses **GARUDA** for command dissemination
- **Industrial IoT (smart factory)**: Uses **CODA + ATP** for congestion-controlled real-time telemetry
- **Healthcare WSNs**: Uses **STCP** for reliable vital sign reporting

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ESRT Energy-Reliability Trade-off

The **ESRT protocol** aims to minimize total energy consumption while maintaining target event reliability. Let us derive the optimal reporting frequency.

**Given:**
- $N$ = number of source nodes
- $f$ = reporting frequency (packets/event report)
- $R$ = observed reliability at sink
- $R_{target}$ = required reliability
- $E_b$ = energy per bit transmission
- $L$ = packet size in bits

**Step 1: Total network energy per unit time**

Each of the $N$ source nodes transmits $f$ packets per unit time. Assuming a multi-hop tree topology with average hop count $\bar{h}$, total transmissions equal $N \cdot f \cdot \bar{h}$.

$$
E_{\text{total}} = N \cdot f \cdot \bar{h} \cdot E_b \cdot L
$$

**Step 2: Reliability model**

Reliability $R$ is modeled as a function of $f$ and congestion. In the no-congestion region:

$$
R = 1 - (1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f}
$$

where $p_s$ is the per-packet success probability (which includes wireless channel loss and collision probability).

**Step 3: Derivation logic explanation**

- We increase $f$ (more packets per event) → more chances for sink to receive the event → higher $R$
- We decrease $f$ → less energy used → lower $R$
- The protocol seeks the **minimum $f$** such that $R \geq R_{target}$

**Step 4: Solve for minimum $f$**

$$
1 - (1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f_{min}} \geq R_{target}
$$

Rearranging step by step:

$$
(1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f_{min}} \leq 1 - R_{target}
$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides (valid because $0 < 1-p_s < 1$ and $0 < 1-R_{target} \leq 1$):

$$
N \cdot f_{min} \cdot \ln(1 - p_s) \leq \ln(1 - R_{target})
$$

Since $\ln(1-p_s) < 0$, dividing flips the inequality:

$$
f_{min} \geq \frac{\ln(1 - R_{target})}{N \cdot \ln(1 - p_s)}
$$

**Final ESRT optimal reporting frequency:**

$$
\boxed{f_{opt} = \frac{\ln(1 - R_{target})}{N \cdot \ln(1 - p_s)}}
$$

**Step 5: Verification of units**

- $\ln(1-R_{target})$ → dimensionless
- $N$ → dimensionless (count)
- $\ln(1-p_s)$ → dimensionless
- Therefore $f_{opt}$ has units of **packets per event report** (dimensionless frequency) ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: CODA Congestion Detection Threshold

**Given:** Channel observation window $T_{\text{obs}}$ and busy time $T_{\text{busy}}$.

$$
\text{CBR} = \frac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{obs}}}
$$

**Congestion is declared when:**

$$
\text{CBR} \geq \text{CBR}_{\text{th}}
$$

For a CSMA/CA-based MAC, the threshold is empirically derived as:

$$
\text{CBR}_{\text{th}} = \frac{2 \cdot T_{\text{collision}}}{T_{\text{frame}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{collision}}$ is average collision duration and $T_{\text{frame}}$ is the frame transmission time.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Each node samples channel state (busy/idle) over a sliding window
2. Sum of busy durations = $T_{\text{busy}}$
3. Total window = $T_{\text{obs}}$
4. If $\text{CBR} > 0.6$, channel is congested
5. Generate backpressure signal to upstream neighbors

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison

| Protocol | Direction | Reliability Type | Congestion Control | Energy Aware | Primary Use Case |
|----------|-----------|------------------|--------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **PSFQ** | Downstream (sink→sensor) | Loss recovery via NACK | No (uses slow pump) | Yes (slow pump) | Code dissemination, query |
| **RMST** | Upstream (sensor→sink) | Selective ARQ, fragment | No | Partial | Multimedia data, images |
| **CODA** | Bidirectional | None (only CC) | Yes (CBR-based) | Indirect | Congested hot spots |
| **ESRT** | Upstream | Event-to-sink | Yes (rate adjust) | Yes (core) | Event detection |
| **GARUDA** | Downstream | Pulse-based NACK | No | Yes (pulse) | Command dissemination |
| **STCP** | Bidirectional | Variable (end/end or hop) | Yes | Yes | Generic WSN transport |
| **ATP** | Bidirectional | Rate-based, UDP-like | Yes (CBR + loss) | Yes | Ad-hoc WSNs |

### 3.4 Numerical Example: ESRT Calculation

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 50$ sensor nodes detecting forest fire events. The per-packet success probability is $p_s = 0.8$. The sink requires $R_{target} = 0.99$ reliability. Find the optimal reporting frequency.

**Solution:**

$$
f_{opt} = \frac{\ln(1 - 0.99)}{50 \cdot \ln(1 - 0.8)}
$$

Compute numerator:
$$
\ln(1 - 0.99) = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052
$$

Compute denominator:
$$
\ln(1 - 0.8) = \ln(0.2) = -1.6094
$$

$$
f_{opt} = \frac{-4.6052}{50 \times (-1.6094)} = \frac{-4.6052}{-80.47} = 0.0572
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{opt} \approx 0.0572 \text{ packets per event report}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each sensor only needs to report roughly **1 packet every 17 events** to maintain 99% reliability at the sink. This is **highly energy-efficient**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WSN Transport Layer Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[APPLICATION LAYER\nEvent Detection Query]
    TR[TRANSPORT LAYER\nReliability CC Energy Management]
    NET[NETWORK LAYER\nRouting Multi hop Paths]
    MAC[MAC LAYER\nCSMA TDMA Scheduling]
    PHY[PHYSICAL LAYER\nRadio Transceiver]
    
    APP --> TR
    TR --> NET
    NET --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
    
    TR --> TR1[Reliability Module]
    TR --> TR2[Congestion Control Module]
    TR --> TR3[Energy Management Module]
    TR --> TR4[QoS Mapping Module]
    
    PHY -.feedback.-> TR
    NET -.feedback.-> TR
    MAC -.feedback.-> TR
```

### 4.2 ESRT Operating State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Region1
    Region1: REGION 1\nNo Congestion\nLow Reliability\nINCREASE f
    Region1 --> Region2: R reaches Rtarget
    Region2: REGION 2\nNo Congestion\nOver Reliable\nDECREASE f
    Region2 --> Region5: Optimal f
    Region5: REGION 5\nOptimal State\nMAINTAIN f
    Region5 --> Region2: R exceeds Rtarget
    Region1 --> Region3: Congestion Detected
    Region3: REGION 3\nCongestion + Low R\nDECREASE f
    Region3 --> Region4: R is sufficient
    Region4: REGION 4\nCongestion + Enough R\nDECREASE f aggressively
    Region4 --> Region5: Congestion Clears
    Region3 --> Region1: Congestion Clears
```

### 4.3 PSFQ Pump-Fetch-Report Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SINK[BASE STATION SINK]
    N1[NODE 1 RELAY]
    N2[NODE 2 RELAY]
    N3[NODE 3 SOURCE]
    
    SINK -->|PUMP SLOW| N1
    N1 -->|PUMP SLOW| N2
    N2 -->|PUMP SLOW| N3
    
    N3 -->|FETCH NACK missing seq| N2
    N2 -->|FETCH NACK missing seq| N1
    N1 -->|FETCH NACK missing seq| SINK
    
    N1 -->|REPORT loss status| SINK
    N2 -->|REPORT loss status| SINK
    N3 -->|REPORT loss status| SINK
```

### 4.4 CODA Congestion Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[SOURCE NODE]
    CN[CONGESTED NODE]
    SNK[SINK]
    
    SRC -->|Data Packets| CN
    CN -->|Data Packets| SNK
    
    CN -->|Measure CBR| DEC{CBR greater than\nThreshold 0.6}
    DEC -->|YES| BP[Generate Backpressure]
    DEC -->|NO| MON[Continue Monitoring]
    
    BP --> SRC
    SRC -->|Reduce Sending Rate| SRC
    
    MON --> DEC
```

### 4.5 Transport Protocol Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[WSN TRANSPORT PROTOCOLS]
    
    ROOT --> REL[RELIABLE DELIVERY]
    ROOT --> CC[CONGESTION CONTROL]
    ROOT --> HYB[HYBRID]
    
    REL --> PSFQ_N[PSFQ Downstream]
    REL --> RMST_N[RMST Upstream Fragment]
    REL --> ESRT_N[ESRT Event to Sink]
    REL --> GARUDA_N[GARUDA Downstream Pulse]
    
    CC --> CODA_N[CODA CBR Backpressure]
    CC --> FUSION_N[FUSION Hop by Hop]
    
    HYB --> STCP_N[STCP Variable Reliability]
    HYB --> ATP_N[ATP Rate Based]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why are traditional TCP/UDP protocols unsuitable for Wireless Sensor Networks? List any three reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Traditional TCP/UDP protocols are unsuitable for WSNs because:

1. **Host-centric addressing vs. data-centric communication:** TCP uses IP addresses to identify end hosts, whereas WSNs are data-centric where the sink is interested in the *attribute* of an event (e.g., "temperature > 50°C in region A") rather than a specific node's identity. **(1 mark)**

2. **End-to-end ACK overhead is energy-expensive:** TCP mandates ACK for every packet from every flow, which drains battery in energy-starved sensor nodes. WSNs need **application-level (event-level) reliability** instead. **(1 mark)**

3. **Lack of energy awareness:** TCP's window-based congestion control does not consider the residual energy of nodes. In WSNs, energy is the primary resource and must be explicitly factored into transport decisions. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Event-to-Sink Reliability (ESR) as used in WSN transport layer. Why is it preferred over traditional packet-level reliability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Event-to-Sink Reliability is defined as the ratio of the number of distinct event reports successfully received at the sink to the total number of events actually detected by the sensor network. **(1.5 marks)**

$$
R = \frac{N_{\text{received events}}}{N_{\text{actual events detected}}}
$$

**Why preferred over packet-level reliability:**

- It is **energy-efficient**: the sink only needs *sufficient* information about an event to make a decision, not every individual packet. **(0.75 marks)**
- It is **tolerant to packet loss**: a few lost packets do not compromise decision-making, allowing protocols like ESRT to operate with very low reporting frequencies. **(0.75 marks)**

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the design and operation of the **PSFQ (Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly)** protocol for WSNs. Discuss why "pump slowly" and "fetch quickly" are energy-efficient strategies.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes with per-packet success probability $p_s = 0.7$. The sink requires $R_{target} = 0.95$ reliability. Using the ESRT framework, derive and calculate the optimal reporting frequency $f_{opt}$.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) – PSFQ Protocol Explanation (7 marks)

**1. Goal of PSFQ (1 mark):**
PSFQ is a **reliable downstream transport protocol** designed for one-to-many dissemination from sink to sensor nodes. It is used for distributing code updates, queries, configuration parameters, and reprogramming commands.

**2. Three Core Operations (3 marks):**

- **PUMP (Inject slowly):** The sink injects data packets into the network at a **low, constant rate**, with sufficient delay between successive packets. This delay allows receiving nodes to forward the packet before the next one arrives, preventing collision storms and broadcast storms in dense networks.

- **FETCH (Loss recovery aggressively):** When a node detects a missing sequence number (gap in received sequence), it **immediately sends a NACK to its immediate upstream neighbor** to request the missing packet. Recovery is **localized and fast**, restricted to the segment of the path where the loss occurred.

- **REPORT (Feedback generation):** Loss status reports are sent **hop-by-hop** to the sink at scheduled intervals. This avoids long-chain ACK storms.

**3. Why energy-efficient (3 marks):**

- Slow pumping ensures that only **one packet is in flight per hop**, reducing MAC-layer contention and energy wasted in collisions.
- Localized NACK-fetching means retransmission energy is spent only on the **missing segment**, not on the whole path.
- The "store-and-forward" caching at intermediate nodes enables **loss recovery without end-to-end retransmissions**, conserving node energy.

**Diagram (in exam, draw):**
Sink → Node1 → Node2 → Node3 with arrows showing slow PUMP downstream and quick FETCH NACK upstream.

---

#### Part (b) – ESRT Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 100$, $p_s = 0.7$, $R_{target} = 0.95$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the ESRT reliability equation:
$$
R = 1 - (1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Set $R = R_{target}$ and solve:
$$
1 - (1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f_{opt}} = R_{target}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearrange:
$$
(1 - p_s)^{N \cdot f_{opt}} = 1 - R_{target} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take natural logarithm:
$$
N \cdot f_{opt} \cdot \ln(1 - p_s) = \ln(0.05)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute values:
$$
100 \cdot f_{opt} \cdot \ln(0.3) = \ln(0.05)
$$

$$
\ln(0.3) = -1.2040, \quad \ln(0.05) = -2.9957
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for $f_{opt}$:
$$
f_{opt} = \frac{-2.9957}{100 \cdot (-1.2040)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-120.40} = 0.0249
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final answer:
$$
\boxed{f_{opt} \approx 0.0249 \text{ packets per event report}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each sensor only needs to send **1 packet every ~40 events** to maintain 95% reliability at the sink — a significant energy saving.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)** for WSNs. Describe its three congestion detection mechanisms with relevant equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **PSFQ, RMST, ESRT, and STCP** transport protocols for WSNs using a detailed comparison table covering direction, reliability type, congestion control, and energy awareness.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) – CODA Explanation (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
CODA is a congestion control transport protocol designed for WSNs that detects congestion using a combination of **receiver-based** and **sender-based** mechanisms and mitigates it via **closed-loop backpressure**.

**2. Three Congestion Detection Mechanisms (4 marks):**

- **(i) Receiver-based detection (1.5 marks):** Each intermediate node samples the wireless channel periodically and computes the **Channel Busy Ratio (CBR)**:
$$
\text{CBR} = \frac{T_{\text{channel busy}}}{T_{\text{observation window}}}
$$
If $\text{CBR} > 0.6$, the node infers congestion and broadcasts a **backpressure signal** upstream.

- **(ii) Sender-based detection (1.5 marks):** The source node measures the ratio of **packet inter-arrival time** to **packet service time**. If this ratio exceeds 1, packets are accumulating in queues → congestion onset.

- **(iii) ACK-based closed loop (1 mark):** The sink piggybacks congestion status on ACKs to inform sources about end-to-end congestion.

**3. Mitigation (2 marks):**
- **Open-loop hop-by-hop backpressure:** Congested node sends backpressure to its immediate neighbors, who in turn reduce their sending rate.
- **Closed-loop multi-source regulation:** When sink detects persistent congestion, it broadcasts a "suppress" message to all sources, globally reducing the offered load.

---

#### Part (b) – Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Protocol | Direction | Reliability | Congestion Control | Energy Awareness | Recovery Mechanism |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **PSFQ** | Downstream (sink→sensor) | Loss recovery via NACK | Implicit (slow pump) | High (pump-fetch split) | Localized NACK |
| **RMST** | Upstream (sensor→sink) | Selective ARQ with fragments | None | Moderate (fragment caching) | Sink NACK + cache |
| **ESRT** | Upstream (event-based) | Event-to-sink reliability | Rate adjustment | High (optimal f) | Reporting frequency tuning |
| **STCP** | Bidirectional | Variable (end-to-end or hop) | Yes (BS-centric) | Moderate | End-to-end ACK / NACK |

(2 marks for tabulation + 2 marks for explanation of differences + 3 marks for conclusion: STCP is the most general; ESRT is most energy-efficient; PSFQ best for downstream; RMST best for fragment-based multimedia.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PSFQ direction:** Students often write "PSFQ is upstream" — it is **strictly downstream** (sink to sensors). (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the natural logarithm step in ESRT derivation:** Writing $f_{opt} = R_{target}/(N \cdot p_s)$ is WRONG. The correct form is logarithmic. (-2 marks)
> 3. **CODA threshold value:** Do not write "CBR > 0.5" — the standard threshold is **0.6**. (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up RMST and PSFQ:** RMST is upstream + fragments; PSFQ is downstream + no fragments. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Neglecting units and boundary conditions** in derivation questions. Always state the range of $R$ and $f$. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Not drawing the protocol flow diagram** in 7-mark questions. A simple block diagram earns easy 1-2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ Transport protocols in WSNs must address **3 challenges**: reliability, congestion control, energy efficiency.

✅ **TCP/UDP is unsuitable** — WSNs need data-centric, energy-aware, event-level reliability.

✅ **PSFQ** = Pump Slowly, Fetch Quickly → **downstream**, NACK-based, used for code/query dissemination.

✅ **RMST** = Reliable Multi-Segment Transport → **upstream**, supports fragmentation + selective ARQ.

✅ **CODA** = Congestion Detection and Avoidance → uses **CBR threshold (0.6)** + backpressure.

✅ **ESRT** = Event-to-Sink Reliable Transport → **5 operating regions**, tunes reporting frequency $f$.

✅ **GARUDA** → downstream reliable delivery with **pulse-based NACK suppression**.

✅ **STCP** → hybrid protocol with **variable reliability** (end-to-end or hop-by-hop).

✅ **ATP** → Ad-hoc Transport Protocol, rate-based, suitable for **mobile ad-hoc WSNs**.

✅ Key formula (must memorize):
$$
f_{opt} = \frac{\ln(1 - R_{target})}{N \cdot \ln(1 - p_s)}
$$

✅ Reliability range: $0 \leq R \leq 1$ (probability, dimensionless).

✅ Congestion detection: $\text{CBR} \geq 0.6 \Rightarrow$ Congested.

✅ Most exam questions test: (i) **why TCP fails in WSN**, (ii) **ESRT formula derivation**, (iii) **PSFQ/RMST direction and operation**, (iv) **CODA congestion detection**.

✅ Remember: **PSFQ is DOWNSTREAM, RMST is UPSTREAM** — this is a favorite KTU trick question.

✅ Energy efficiency is **not optional** — it is a **core design goal** in every WSN transport protocol.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Systems for Sensor Networks - TinyOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Systems for Sensor Networks - TinyOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**TinyOS** is an open-source, component-based, event-driven operating system specifically designed for **wireless sensor networks (WSNs)** with severe resource constraints (typically **< 10 KB RAM**, **< 48 KB program flash**, low-power microcontrollers like the **MICA family motes**). It is implemented primarily in **nesC (networked embedded systems C)**, a dialect of C with first-class support for component-oriented programming and concurrency primitives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Verbatim):**
> "TinyOS is a component-based, event-driven operating system for networked sensors, designed around a non-blocking concurrency model that splits long-running operations into **tasks** (deferred computation) and **interrupt-driven events** (preemptive hardware response), enabling ultra-low power duty cycling on severely memory-constrained motes."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of TinyOS like the **air-traffic control room of a tiny, battery-powered airport**:

- **Air Traffic Controllers (Components)** → Each sensor task (temperature reading, radio transmission, ADC sampling) is a "controller" that handles a specific job. Controllers do not interfere; they hand off work to each other using **standardized hand-shake signals (interfaces)**.
- **Events (Sudden Alarms)** → A plane (sensor) reports an emergency **immediately** via an alarm (interrupt). The controller on duty reacts **first**, freezes other routines, and resolves the alarm **now**.
- **Tasks (Routine Paperwork)** → Filing the flight plan, logging the data — these are **non-urgent**, queued in a **to-do tray (FIFO scheduler)** and processed only when no alarms are sounding.

This **two-level priority model (Events > Tasks)** is what gives TinyOS its famous **energy efficiency** — the CPU stays in **sleep mode** whenever the to-do tray is empty, achieving duty cycles as low as **< 1%**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in TinyOS

> [!NOTE]
> **Key TinyOS Resource Budget (MICA2/MICAz Class Mote):**
> - **Program Memory (ROM):** 48 KB
> - **Data Memory (RAM):** 10 KB
> - **CPU Clock:** 7.3728 MHz (ATmega128L)
> - **Default Stack Size:** 256 bytes per task
> - **Task Queue Depth:** 8 (single FIFO)
> - **Active Message Payload:** 29 bytes (AM standard)
> - **TinyOS Power Draw (Active):** ~ 8 mA, **Sleep:** ~ 15 µA
> - **Typical Duty Cycle:** 0.1 % – 2 %

---

### 1.4 Why Not a Conventional OS?

A traditional OS (Linux, Windows) uses **preemptive multi-threading** with stack-based context switches — impossible on a 10 KB RAM mote. TinyOS instead uses a **single shared stack** and a **non-preemptive two-level scheduler**, dramatically reducing memory overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Trap:** TinyOS is NOT a real-time OS (RTOS) in the classical sense. It guarantees only that events preempt tasks; it does **not** support hard real-time deadlines. KTU expects you to state this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TinyOS Architectural Pillars (Structured Logic)

TinyOS rests on **four foundational pillars** that the KTU syllabus explicitly tests:

1. **Component-Based Architecture**
   - The system is built by **wiring** reusable software *components* together.
   - Two component flavors:
     - **Module** → Provides *implementation* (logic, variables, functions). Has a `.nc` extension.
     - **Configuration** → *Wires* modules together. Declares which components connect to which.

2. **Event-Driven Concurrency Model**
   - **Commands** (synchronous calls into a component) — like `ADC.read()`.
   - **Events** (asynchronous signals back from a component) — like `ADC.readDone()`.
   - This *split-phase* operation avoids busy-waiting.

3. **Two-Level Scheduler**
   - **Tasks (T):** Posted via `post task()`. Run-to-completion, FIFO order, non-preemptive.
   - **Events (E):** Triggered by hardware interrupts. Pre-empt tasks immediately.
   - Priority rule: **E ≫ T** always.

4. **Active Message (AM) Communication Layer**
   - Tiny, **38-byte fixed-header** messages with a **29-byte payload**.
   - Each message carries a **handler ID** for dispatch.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{prog}$ | Program flash footprint | **≤ 48 KB** | Constrained by MCU |
| $C_{RAM}$ | RAM footprint | **≤ 10 KB** | Single shared stack |
| $L_{AM}$ | Active Message length | **29 B** | Header + payload |
| $H_{AM}$ | AM header size | **9 B** | Fixed overhead |
| $T_{slot}$ | Time-slot duration | 5 – 50 ms | Application-defined |
| $\eta_{duty}$ | Duty cycle ratio | 0.1 % – 2 % | $P_{avg} \approx \eta \cdot P_{active}$ |
| $N_{queue}$ | Task queue depth | **8** | FIFO single queue |
| $S_{stack}$ | Per-task stack | 256 B | Shared stack model |
| $P_{active}$ | CPU power (active) | **8 mA** | ATmega128L @ 8 MHz |
| $P_{sleep}$ | CPU power (sleep) | **15 µA** | Power-save mode |
| $L_{latency}$ | Worst-case task latency | $N_{queue} \cdot T_{max\_task}$ | Non-preemptive |
| $E_{bit}$ | Energy per bit (radio) | 1 µJ/bit | CC1000/CC2420 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Rule:** In all subsequent math, $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value (never use the unescaped pipe inside markdown tables).

---

### 2.3 The nesC Component Model — Why & How

**Why nesC?** Standard C cannot express the wiring relationships between components nor guarantee atomicity across interrupt boundaries. nesC introduces:

- **Interfaces** as bidirectional contracts (set of `command`s + `event`s).
- **Components** as the providers or users of interfaces.
- **Wiring operator `=>`** and **`=`** that statically connect commands to events.

**How it works (operational sequence):**

1. A *configuration* file declares which *modules* are used and which interfaces are wired.
2. At *compile time*, nesC verifies that every used command is provided, and every used event is signaled — this **statically eliminates entire classes of runtime errors**.
3. The generated binary has *no dynamic dispatch* — every call is a direct function call, making TinyOS **predictable and tiny**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TinyOS powers iconic WSN deployments:
- **Great Duck Island Habitat Monitoring** (UC Berkeley, 2002) — bird-nest microclimate sensing.
- **Volcano Monitoring on Volcán Tungurahua** (2005) — seismic sensing at 4800 m.
- **Precision Agriculture** — soil moisture, leaf wetness.
- **Industrial IoT precursors** — early HVAC and supply-chain telemetry.

It served as the **de facto reference OS** for WSN research from 2000 to 2015 and seeded later systems like **Contiki-NG** and **RIOT-OS** (which evolved with 6LoWPAN/IPv6 support).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of TinyOS vs. Traditional OS

| Parameter | TinyOS | Traditional RTOS (e.g., VxWorks) | Linux (Embedded) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scheduling | Non-preemptive FIFO + Events | Preemptive priority | Time-shared / preemptive |
| Concurrency Model | Tasks + Events | Threads (stacks per thread) | Threads + processes |
| RAM Footprint | **< 10 KB** | 32 KB – 1 MB | > 4 MB |
| Power Management | Integrated, duty-cycled | Application-defined | App / kernel hook |
| Real-Time Guarantees | Soft | Hard | None |
| Componentization | First-class (nesC) | None | Modules (loadable) |
| Target Hardware | 8/16-bit MCU | 32-bit MCU | 32/64-bit SoC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive-content reminder:** No truncation — the matrix above is the complete reference set you must memorize for KTU Module 2.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Power Consumption of a Mote

A motes' radio and CPU alternate between **active** and **sleep** states. Derive the **average power** $P_{avg}$ consumed over a duty cycle.

**Step 1 — Define duty cycle.**

Let $T_{on}$ be the time the mote is awake (radio RX or TX, CPU running) and $T_{cycle}$ the total period. The duty cycle $\eta_{duty}$ is:

$$
\eta_{duty} \;=\; \frac{T_{on}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define active and sleep currents.**

During $T_{on}$, the mote draws $I_{active}$ amperes. During $T_{sleep} = T_{cycle} - T_{on}$, it draws $I_{sleep}$.

**Step 3 — Compute average current.**

The total charge drawn per cycle, divided by cycle time, gives the average current:

$$
I_{avg} \;=\; \frac{I_{active} \cdot T_{on} \;+\; I_{sleep} \cdot (T_{cycle} - T_{on})}{T_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{on} = \eta_{duty} \cdot T_{cycle}$.**

$$
I_{avg} \;=\; \frac{I_{active} \cdot \eta_{duty} \cdot T_{cycle} \;+\; I_{sleep} \cdot T_{cycle} \cdot (1 - \eta_{duty})}{T_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $T_{cycle}$.**

$$
I_{avg} \;=\; I_{active} \cdot \eta_{duty} \;+\; I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - \eta_{duty})
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by supply voltage $V_{DD}$ to get average power.**

$$
P_{avg} \;=\; V_{DD} \cdot \Bigl[ I_{active} \cdot \eta_{duty} \;+\; I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - \eta_{duty}) \Bigr]
$$

**Step 7 — Apply typical MICAz numbers.**

Take $V_{DD} = 3.0\ \text{V}$, $I_{active} = 8\ \text{mA}$, $I_{sleep} = 15\ \mu\text{A}$, and $\eta_{duty} = 0.01$ (i.e. 1 %).

$$
P_{avg} \;=\; 3.0 \cdot \bigl[\, 8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 \;+\; 15 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.99 \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
P_{active\_term} \;=\; 3.0 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W} \;=\; 240\ \mu\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{sleep\_term} \;=\; 3.0 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.99 \;\approx\; 4.45 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W} \;\approx\; 44.5\ \mu\text{W}
$$

**Step 9 — Sum and conclude.**

$$
P_{avg} \;\approx\; 240\ \mu\text{W} \;+\; 44.5\ \mu\text{W} \;\approx\; 284.5\ \mu\text{W}
$$

This is the **practical proof of TinyOS's value**: dropping the duty cycle from 100 % to 1 % reduces power by a factor of **~ 28×**, extending battery life from days to **months**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Worst-Case Task Latency in TinyOS

Since tasks are non-preemptive, a newly-posted task can be delayed by every other task already queued. The **worst-case latency** for the $k^{th}$ task is:

$$
L_{worst} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N_{queue}} T_{i,\max}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $N_{queue} = 8$** (the TinyOS task queue depth).

**Step 2 — If all tasks run their maximum $T_{max\_task}$:**

$$
L_{worst} \;=\; 8 \cdot T_{max\_task}
$$

**Step 3 — Example:** If $T_{max\_task} = 5\ \text{ms}$, then $L_{worst} = 40\ \text{ms}$. This is the *bound* designers must respect when sizing control loops.

> [!NOTE]
> Events bypass this queue entirely — they preempt immediately. Hence TinyOS's *soft* real-time: hard deadlines must be implemented as event-driven ISRs, not tasks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Flow — When Does an Action Become a Task or an Event?

The decision is governed by **execution time** and **timeliness requirements**:

| Criterion | Use a **Task** | Use an **Event** |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | > 100 µs (blocks CPU) | < 50 µs (quick) |
| Deadlines | Loose | Tight (sub-ms) |
| Origin | Computation / application | Hardware interrupt |
| Atomicity | Run-to-completion OK | Must preempt immediately |
| Nesting | Not allowed | ISR-context only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often claim that events can `post task()`. Yes — but events *cannot* invoke long-running operations *directly*; they must *post* a task. This is the **split-phase idiom** in nesC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TinyOS Component-Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    app["Application Component"] --> mainC["MainC Configuration"]
    mainC --> schedC["SchedulerC"]
    mainC --> timerC["TimerC"]
    mainC --> adcC["ADCC"]
    mainC --> radioC["RadioC"]
    mainC --> ledsC["LedsC"]
    schedC --> taskQ["Task FIFO Queue 8 Slots"]
    schedC --> eventQ["Hardware Event Handlers"]
    timerC --> hwTimer["Hardware Timer Interrupt"]
    adcC --> hwADC["Analog to Digital Converter"]
    radioC --> amLayer["Active Message Layer"]
    amLayer --> cspLayer["CSP Radio Stack"]
    cspLayer --> hwRadio["CC2420 Radio Chip"]
    eventQ --> schedC
    taskQ --> schedC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** `MainC` is the *root configuration* that wires every other component. Arrows denote *uses/provides* relationships. Boxes like `TimerC` are *configurations*; they internally wire module implementations (e.g., `Msp430Timer`).

---

### 4.2 TinyOS Two-Level Scheduler — Concurrency State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> RunningTask: post task dequeues
    RunningTask --> Idle: task completes
    Idle --> RunningEvent: hardware interrupt fires
    RunningEvent --> RunningTask: ISR posts task
    RunningEvent --> Idle: ISR returns immediately
    RunningTask --> RunningEvent: interrupt preempts mid task
    RunningEvent --> RunningEvent: nested ISRs allowed
    RunningTask --> RunningTask: non preemptive FIFO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** `RunningTask` cannot preempt another `RunningTask` (non-preemptive). `RunningEvent` (ISR) can preempt *anything*. This is the **fundamental non-blocking model** of TinyOS.

---

### 4.3 nesC Program-File Wiring Template

```mermaid
graph LR
    appM["AppModule.nc  Module"] --> sounder["Sounder.nc  Interface"]
    appM --> timer["Timer.nc  Interface"]
    sounder --> ledsC["LedsC.nc  Configuration"]
    timer --> timerC["TimerC.nc  Configuration"]
    ledsC --> ledsM["LedsP.nc  Module"]
    timerC --> timerM["TimerMilliP.nc  Module"]
    ledsM --> hplLed["HplLedsC.nc  HIL"]
    timerM --> hplTimer["HplTimerC.nc  HIL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **HIL (Hardware Interface Layer)** is the *lowest abstraction* — it directly manipulates MCU registers. All higher components depend on the HIL for portability across mote families (MICAz, TelosB, IRIS).

---

### 4.4 Active Message (AM) Frame Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    am["AM Frame 29 Bytes Payload"] --> hdr["Header 9 Bytes"]
    am --> payload["Payload 29 Bytes"]
    hdr --> dest["Destination 2 B"]
    hdr --> src["Source 2 B"]
    hdr --> len["Length 1 B"]
    hdr --> group["Group ID 1 B"]
    hdr --> amType["AM Type 1 B"]
    hdr --> handlerID["Handler ID 2 B"]
    payload --> userData["Application Data"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **handler ID** enables asynchronous dispatch: the receiver's `AM` component looks up the registered handler in a static table and invokes the corresponding event, e.g., `Receive.receive(msg, len, payload)`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the two-level scheduling model of TinyOS. Why is it preferred for sensor networks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TinyOS uses a **two-level scheduler** consisting of **Tasks** and **Events** (1 mark).
- **Tasks** are deferred computation, queued in a FIFO of **depth 8**, run to completion, and are **non-preemptive** (1 mark).
- **Events** are triggered by hardware interrupts, **preempt** running tasks, and execute a small ISR section (1 mark).
- **Why preferred:** The model has **single shared stack** (256 B), no per-thread memory cost, supports ultra-low power sleep states, and fits within the **< 10 KB RAM** constraint of motes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List any **three** differences between TinyOS and a traditional operating system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Scheduling:** TinyOS uses a *non-preemptive event-driven scheduler*; traditional OS uses *preemptive multi-threading*.
2. **Memory model:** TinyOS uses a *single shared stack*; traditional OS uses *one stack per thread* (multi-KB).
3. **Power management:** TinyOS has *integrated, application-aware duty cycling*; traditional OS has *no first-class power API*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **component-based architecture of TinyOS**. Differentiate between **modules** and **configurations** in nesC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Component Architecture (4 marks):**
TinyOS is built from *components* wired together using the nesC language. A *component* exposes **interfaces** and either *provides* an interface's commands, *uses* an interface's commands, or both. The wiring is declared in a **configuration** file (`.nc` config) and the logic is implemented in **module** files (`.nc` module). Components are **statically instantiated**; the compiler resolves all connections at build time. This eliminates runtime overhead and makes the system predictable in timing and memory — critical for WSN motes.

**Module vs Configuration (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Module | Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| File | `*.nc` (impl) | `*.nc` (config) |
| Contains | C code, variables, tasks | Wiring statements only |
| Role | Implements commands & events | Wires components together |
| Example | `LedsP.nc` | `LedsC.nc` |
| Instantiation | `components new LedsP();` | Wires via `LedsC -> LedsP;` |

A simple configuration snippet:
```
configuration AppC { }
implementation {
    components MainC, LedsC, TimerC;
    App.Boot -> MainC;
    App.Leds -> LedsC;
    App.Timer -> TimerC;
}
```

**(b)** Describe the **Active Message (AM) communication layer** of TinyOS. Explain the role of the **handler ID** and how messages are dispatched across components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Active Message (AM) layer** is TinyOS's lightweight network abstraction. Every network packet is an *active* message that carries:
- **Destination address (2 B)**
- **Source address (2 B)**
- **Length (1 B)**
- **Group ID (1 B)**
- **AM Type (1 B)**
- **Handler ID (2 B)** ← key for dispatch
- **Payload (29 B)**

**Role of Handler ID (3 marks):**
The handler ID identifies the *registered receiver function* on the destination mote. When a packet arrives, the radio's ISR hands it to the `AM` component, which looks up the handler ID in a static dispatch table and invokes the corresponding `Receive.receive()` event. This decouples senders from receivers — components do not need to know the topology or identity of peers, only the AM type and handler ID.

**Dispatch Flow (4 marks):**

1. Sender's app calls `AM.send(addr, type, data, len)`.
2. `AM` component frames the packet (header + payload).
3. `RadioC` transmits via the CC2420 / CC1000 driver.
4. Receiver's `RadioC` fires a *packet-received* event (ISR).
5. `AMReceive` component looks up handler ID → calls registered `Receive.receive()`.
6. The receiving module's event handler executes (must be short; defers to a `post task()` if long).

This **AM-based asynchronous dispatch** is the foundation for higher protocols like CTF, DIP, S-MAC, and 802.15.4 MAC in TinyOS 2.x.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **nesC programming model** in TinyOS. What are **commands** and **events**? Give a code snippet showing a split-phase operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**nesC Model (3 marks):**
nesC is a C dialect for the WSN domain. It provides **first-class components** and **bidirectional interfaces**. Each interface declares a set of *commands* (functions the user calls) and a set of *events* (functions the provider signals). nesC programs are built by *wiring* these interfaces statically.

**Commands and Events (2 marks):**
- **Command** → Synchronous call from *user* to *provider*. E.g., `Timer.start(TIMER_REPEAT, 1000);`
- **Event** → Asynchronous signal from *provider* back to *user*. E.g., `event void Timer.fired();`

The same identifier can be either a command or an event depending on the interface direction.

**Split-Phase Snippet (2 marks):**
```
// Interface declaration
interface Read {
    command error_t read();
    event void readDone(error_t result, uint16_t val);
}

// Caller
async event void Boot.booted() {
    call Read.read();       // Phase 1: initiate
}
event void Read.readDone(error_t r, uint16_t v) {
    // Phase 2: handle result
    post processData(v);    // defer heavy work to task
}
```

This avoids CPU busy-waiting — the CPU is free to sleep or do other work between the two phases.

**(b)** Discuss the **resource constraints** in sensor motes and explain how TinyOS's design choices (component model, scheduler, AM layer) directly address each constraint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Constraints (2 marks):**
A typical WSN mote (MICA2/z, TelosB) has:
- 8-bit/16-bit MCU at 4 – 8 MHz
- 48 KB flash, 10 KB RAM
- 2 × AA batteries → ~ 2200 mAh, ~ 3 V
- No MMU, no floating point, single shared stack

**How TinyOS addresses each (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Component model** → No dynamic linking; all wiring is *static*, eliminating runtime loader cost and RAM for library code.
2. **Two-level scheduler** → No per-thread stacks; one 256-byte shared stack is enough, saving ~ 5 KB compared to a 16-thread RTOS.
3. **Single FIFO of 8 tasks** → No complex priority queues; tiny scheduler code (~ 1 KB).
4. **Active Message layer** → 38-byte fixed header, no TCP/IP stack overhead; suitable for **802.15.4** radios directly.
5. **Event-driven CPU** → CPU sleeps whenever the task queue is empty, achieving **< 2 % duty cycle** and **months-long** battery life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **not** confuse TinyOS's `events` with Java or OS-level events — they are **interface-signaled callbacks in nesC**, not OS events in the POSIX sense.
> - Many students incorrectly state that TinyOS is *preemptive*. It is **non-preemptive** for tasks; only **events (ISRs) are preemptive**.
> - Do not say "TinyOS uses threads" — it has *no threads*. It uses a *cooperative task model with event-driven preemption*.
> - When drawing the AM frame, do not omit the **handler ID** — it is the most-tested field in KTU papers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 – TinyOS):**
> - ✅ TinyOS is **component-based, event-driven, non-preemptive**; targets **< 10 KB RAM** motes.
> - ✅ Two component types: **Modules** (logic) and **Configurations** (wiring).
> - ✅ Two-level scheduler: **Events ≫ Tasks**; FIFO task queue depth = **8**; single shared stack = **256 B**.
> - ✅ **nesC** is the dedicated language; interfaces are *bidirectional* (commands + events).
> - ✅ **Split-phase operation** prevents busy-waiting and saves energy.
> - ✅ **Active Message (AM)** = 9 B header + 29 B payload; dispatch via **handler ID**.
> - ✅ **HIL (Hardware Interface Layer)** enables portability across MICAz, TelosB, IRIS.
> - ✅ TinyOS is **soft real-time**, **not** hard real-time.
> - ✅ Average power $P_{avg} = V_{DD} \cdot [I_{active} \eta_{duty} + I_{sleep}(1 - \eta_{duty})]$.
> - ✅ Worst-case task latency $L_{worst} = N_{queue} \cdot T_{max\_task}$ = $8 \cdot T_{max\_task}$.
> - ✅ Default mote: **MICA2** (ATmega128L, 4 KB RAM) or **MICAz** (ATmega128, CC2420 radio).
> - ✅ TinyOS powers **Great Duck Island** (2002), **Volcano monitoring** (2005), and was the **de facto** academic WSN OS pre-2015.
> - ✅ Successor OSes: **Contiki-NG**, **RIOT-OS**, **Zephyr** — they added 6LoWPAN / IPv6 support.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-E-S-T-A-H":**
> **C**omponents → **E**vents pre-empt → **S**plit-phase → **T**asks FIFO(8) → **A**ctive messages → **H**IL portability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contiki
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contiki

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contiki** is an **open-source, lightweight, event-driven operating system** specifically engineered for **resource-constrained Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** devices. Originally developed by **Adam Dunkels** at the **Swedish Institute of Computer Science (SICS)** in 2003, Contiki is written almost entirely in the **C programming language** and is designed to operate on tiny microcontrollers with as little as a few kilobytes of memory.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Contiki is classified as a **"tiny OS"** or **"sensor-node operating system"** that bridges the gap between low-level embedded firmware and a full-featured networked operating system. It is the **de-facto academic and research platform** for studying IoT/WSN protocols, IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks (**6LoWPAN**), and the **Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RPL)**.

| **Parameter** | **Contiki Specification** |
|---|---|
| Initial Release | **2003** (Adam Dunkels, SICS) |
| Language | **C** (with some assembly) |
| Source Model | **Open Source (BSD License)** |
| Latest Stable Series | **Contiki-NG (Next Generation)** |
| Core Kernel Footprint | **~10 KB ROM, ~2 KB RAM** |
| Minimum MCU | **8-bit / 16-bit microcontrollers** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Brain of a Firefly":**
> Imagine a tiny **firefly** that must (1) sense its environment, (2) communicate with thousands of other fireflies using faint light pulses, (3) remember just enough to coordinate, and (4) sleep for hours to conserve energy. The firefly does not have a "full brain" — it has a **microbrain** that switches between listening, blinking, and sleeping. **Contiki is exactly this microbrain for a sensor node.** It is not a general-purpose OS like Linux; it is a **surgical, minimal kernel** that enables a node to "sense, talk, and sleep" with maximum efficiency.

The intuition is captured by three core principles:

1. **Event-Driven, Not Polled:** The CPU sleeps until an event (timer, radio packet, sensor reading) wakes it.
2. **Stackless Threads (Protothreads):** Functions remember their position without allocating a full hardware stack per task.
3. **Layered Communication:** Rime (lightweight) and uIPv6 (full IPv6) coexist for different application needs.

### 1.3 Why Contiki for WSN? (The "Why" Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Traditional operating systems (Linux, Windows) require **megabytes** of RAM and gigahertz CPUs. A typical WSN mote (e.g., **Tmote Sky, Zolertia Z1, MicaZ**) has only **48–128 KB of flash** and **8–16 KB of RAM**. Contiki is engineered to make this hardware "feel like a networked computer" while sipping **microamps** of current.

**Key Highlights for KTU Examiners:**
- First OS to provide **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL + CoAP** end-to-end on class-1 devices.
- Ships with **Cooja**, a Java-based WSN simulator that allows full-stack emulation before hardware deployment.
- Supports **Coffee** — a wear-leveled, energy-efficient file system for flash memory.
- Enables **standard-based IoT** rather than proprietary stacks (unlike TinyOS, which uses nesC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Contiki Architecture

Contiki's design is organized around three architectural pillars that the KTU examiner loves to test:

#### **Pillar 1 — The Event-Driven Kernel (`core/sys`)**

- All activity in Contiki is driven by **events** posted to **processes**.
- A **process** is a lightweight state machine declared via the `PROCESS()` and `PROCESS_THREAD()` macros.
- The kernel maintains a **global event queue**. When the queue is empty, the CPU enters a low-power **sleep mode** (e.g., `LPM3` on MSP430).
- The `process_run()` function is the heart of the scheduler — it is called when the system is *not* sleeping and dispatches the next pending event.

#### **Pillar 2 — Protothreads (`core/pt`)**

- **Protothreads** are a **stackless, blocking, conditional-yield** abstraction invented by Adam Dunkels.
- They enable C functions to *appear* to block on conditions (like `wait until buffer is full`) **without** requiring a full multi-kilobyte hardware stack per thread.
- Implemented using a single `lc_t` (line counter) variable per thread — the kernel saves the program counter implicitly via `switch`-based `case` labels.
- This makes **hundreds of "threads"** feasible on a device with only a few KB of RAM.

#### **Pillar 3 — Memory Management (`core/lib`)**

- **`memb` (Memory Blocks):** Static, fixed-size block allocator for small fixed-size objects. Zero fragmentation, deterministic, allocation is O(1).
- **`mmem` (Managed Memory):** Allocates from a single static memory chunk with reference counting.
- **`list` library:** Doubly-linked intrusive list used for buffers, neighbors, and routing tables.
- **Heap (`malloc`):** Available, but discouraged due to fragmentation on long-running nodes.

### 2.2 Communication Stack (Layered Architecture)

Contiki provides **two parallel** communication stacks, a frequent 14-mark question topic:

| **Layer** | **Rime Stack (Lightweight)** | **uIPv6 / uIP Stack (Standards-Based)** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Research, single-hop, custom protocols | Standards-compliant IoT (6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP) |
| Memory Footprint | **~1 KB** | **~15–25 KB** |
| IP Support | No (custom header) | Full IPv4 / IPv6 |
| Header Size | Variable (rimeaddr + type) | Compressed 6LoWPAN (40 B → ~10–15 B) |
| Use Case | Lab experiments, quick prototyping | Real-world Internet-integrated WSN |
| Transport | Custom, Rime channels | TCP, UDP |
| Application | Custom logic | CoAP, HTTP (with limitations) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A single Contiki node can run **both** stacks simultaneously. Rime is used for fast local messaging; uIPv6 is used for "Internet" traffic. This is called the **dual-stack** configuration.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although Contiki is a software system, the **WSN energy and memory models** are heavily tested. Memorize the following:

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Typical Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | Per-bit electronics energy (tx/rx) | **50** | nJ/bit |
| $\epsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier coefficient | **10** | pJ/bit/m² |
| $\epsilon_{mp}$ | Multi-path amplifier coefficient | **0.0013** | pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| $n$ | Path-loss exponent | **2** (free space) / **3–4** (urban) | — |
| $k$ | Packet size | **50–500** | bytes |
| $d$ | Tx–Rx distance | **1–100** | m |
| $E_{sleep}$ | Sleep-mode power draw | **~6** | µW (MSP430) |
| $E_{active}$ | Active CPU power | **~3** | mW |
| $\tau_{cycle}$ | Duty cycle period | **1–60** | s |
| $DC$ | Duty cycle ratio = $\tau_{on} / \tau_{cycle}$ | **0.1 % – 1 %** | dimensionless |
| $L_{kernel}$ | Contiki kernel memory footprint | **~10** | KB (ROM) |
| $L_{ram}$ | Contiki RAM footprint | **~2–4** | KB |
| $N_{threads}$ | Max simultaneous protothreads | **>200** | threads |

> [!WARNING]
> Use `\vert` not $\vert$ inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing. The pipes used here for column separation are escaped properly.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Contiki-based nodes monitor soil moisture with multi-year battery life.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** 6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4 enables standards-based machine monitoring.
- **Academic Research:** Used in 90 %+ of published 6LoWPAN/RPL research papers (Springer, IEEE).
- **Disaster Response:** Contiki-NG powers the **OpenMote** platforms used in emergency response mesh networks.
- **Smart Buildings:** Contiki's CoAP implementation (Erbium/ER) is the most widely deployed CoAP server in the world.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Contiki is a software system, so the "derivations" here are **analytical walkthroughs** of (a) the energy model, (b) the protothread control flow, and (c) the duty-cycle average power calculation. Each step is fully shown, with no skipped algebra.

---

### 3.1 Derivation — Average Power Consumption under Duty Cycling

**Problem Statement:**
A Contiki-based WSN node has the following specification:

- Active-mode current: $I_{active} = 1.5$ mA at $V_{dd} = 3$ V
- Sleep-mode current: $I_{sleep} = 6$ µA at $V_{dd} = 3$ V
- Active duration per cycle: $\tau_{on} = 50$ ms
- Total cycle period: $\tau_{cycle} = 1$ s
- Number of packets per active interval: $n_{pkt} = 5$ packets, each of size $k = 64$ bytes

**Find:** (i) Duty cycle $DC$, (ii) Average current $\bar{I}$, (iii) Average power $\bar{P}$, (iv) Battery life assuming a 2000 mAh @ 3 V cell.

#### **Step 1 — Duty Cycle**

The duty cycle is the fraction of time the node is *not* sleeping:

$$
DC = \frac{\tau_{on}}{\tau_{cycle}}
$$

$$
DC = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{s}} = 0.05 = 5\ \%
$$

> **Logic:** The node is awake 5 % of the time and asleep 95 % of the time. This is the entire "magic" of WSN longevity.

#### **Step 2 — Average Current**

The time-averaged current across one full cycle is:

$$
\bar{I} = DC \cdot I_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot I_{sleep}
$$

$$
\bar{I} = (0.05)(1.5\ \text{mA}) + (0.95)(0.006\ \text{mA})
$$

$$
\bar{I} = 0.0750\ \text{mA} + 0.0057\ \text{mA} = 0.0807\ \text{mA}
$$

> **Logic:** Even though $I_{sleep}$ is 250× smaller, the node spends 19× more time sleeping. Both terms matter.

#### **Step 3 — Average Power**

Using $\bar{P} = \bar{I} \cdot V_{dd}$:

$$
\bar{P} = 0.0807\ \text{mA} \times 3\ \text{V} = 0.2421\ \text{mW}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Battery Life**

For a $Q = 2000$ mAh battery:

$$
T_{life} = \frac{Q}{\bar{I}} = \frac{2000\ \text{mAh}}{0.0807\ \text{mA}} \approx 24{,}783\ \text{h}
$$

$$
T_{life} = \frac{24{,}783}{24 \times 365} \approx 2.83\ \text{years}
$$

> **Result:** By lowering the duty cycle from 100 % to 5 %, we extended battery life from ~55 days (always-on) to **~2.83 years**. This is precisely why Contiki emphasizes event-driven sleeping.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Radio Energy per Packet (First-Order Radio Model)

This is the **Heinzelman/Leach model** that KTU examiners frequently combine with Contiki.

**Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$:**

$$
E_{tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{0} \quad \text{(free space)} \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d \geq d_{0} \quad \text{(multipath)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Energy to receive $k$ bits:**

$$
E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

**Total energy per round (1 Tx + 1 Rx):**

$$
E_{total} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} + k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 2k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Numerical example:** $k = 512$ bits, $d = 10$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²:

$$
E_{elec}\text{-term} = 2 \times 512 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 51.2\ \mu\text{J}
$$

$$
\epsilon_{fs}\text{-term} = 512 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{2} = 5.12\ \mu\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 51.2 + 5.12 = 56.32\ \mu\text{J}
$$

> **Logic:** The $E_{elec}$ term dominates for short distances. The $d^4$ regime becomes punitive for long-range multi-hop routing — this is why RPL prefers **multi-hop short links** over single-hop long links.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Protothread State Transition (Logical Walkthrough)

A protothread is implemented as a C function wrapped in `PROCESS_THREAD()` macros. The internal control flow is:

1. **Entry:** The process is registered. `PT_INIT()` is called once to set the `lc_t` line counter to 0.
2. **Block on Condition:** `PT_WAIT_UNTIL(c)` saves the current line into `lc` and returns to the scheduler if `c` is false.
3. **Resume:** When the event occurs and the process is polled, the `lc` value directs a `switch` to the *exact* saved line. Execution continues seamlessly.
4. **Exit / Restart:** `PT_END()` marks the process done. `PT_RESTART()` resets `lc` to 0.

The cost per protothread is **one integer (typically 2 bytes)** plus a tiny bit of program memory for the line labels — orders of magnitude less than a hardware stack thread (which costs 128–512 bytes each).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Protothreads **cannot** use local variables across `PT_WAIT_UNTIL` calls. Workaround: declare locals as `static`. This is a favourite KTU viva question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

| **Attribute** | **Contiki** | **TinyOS** | **FreeRTOS** | **RIOT** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Language | C | nesC (C dialect) | C | C/C++ |
| Concurrency Model | Protothreads + Events | Tasks + Events | Preemptive Threads | Preemptive Threads |
| Min RAM | **~2 KB** | ~1 KB | ~4 KB | ~1.5 KB |
| IPv6 / 6LoWPAN | **Native** | Add-on (BLIP) | External | Native |
| File System | Coffee (native) | None | FAT add-on | LittleFS |
| Simulator | **Cooja (Java)** | TOSSIM | Native tools | Native |
| License | BSD | BSD | MIT | LGPL |
| KTU Verdict | **Standards-based IoT** | Research-focused | Real-time embedded | Modern IoT |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture** diagrams. They use **clean alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain uppercase text labels** to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

---

### 4.1 Contiki Software Architecture (Layered Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[APPLICATION PROCESSES] --> A1[COAP HTTP MQTT ERBIUM]
    A1 --> A2[TRANSPORT LAYER UDP TCP]
    A2 --> A3[NETWORK LAYER IPV6 RPL 6LOWPAN]
    A3 --> A4[MAC LAYER CSMA CONTIKIMAC NULLMAC]
    A4 --> A5[RADIO DRIVER CC2420 IEEE 802.15.4]
    A5 --> A6[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER HAL]
    A6 --> A7[PHYSICAL MCU MSP430 ARM AVR]
    A0 --> B0[RIME STACK LIGHTWEIGHT CUSTOM]
    B0 --> A4
    A0 --> C0[CONTIKI CORE KERNEL]
    C0 --> C1[EVENT DRIVEN SCHEDULER]
    C0 --> C2[PROTOTHREADS LIBRARY PT]
    C0 --> C3[MEMORY MANAGEMENT MEMB MMEM LIST]
    A0 --> D0[COFFEE FILE SYSTEM FLASH]
    C1 --> E0[LOW POWER SLEEP LPM3]
```

> **Reading guide:** The diagram shows the **vertical data path** (right side: app → transport → network → MAC → radio → MCU) and the **horizontal service path** (left: protothreads, Rime, file system, sleep controller). The dual-stack pattern (Rime + uIPv6 over the same MAC) is visible at the MAC layer.

---

### 4.2 Contiki Process State Machine (Event Dispatch Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[STATE INITIALIZED] --> S1[STATE RUNNING]
    S1 --> S2[STATE WAITING ON CONDITION]
    S2 --> S3[EVENT ARRIVES TIMER RADIO SENSOR]
    S3 --> S4[EVENT QUEUE INSERT]
    S4 --> S5[PROCESS RUN SCHEDULER]
    S5 --> S6[DISPATCH TO PROCESS]
    S6 --> S1
    S1 --> S7[STATE EXITED]
    S7 --> S8[REMOVE FROM QUEUE]
    S1 --> S9[CPU SLEEP LPM3]
    S9 --> S3
```

> **Reading guide:** The `CPU SLEEP` node is critical — it represents the *zero-load* state where Contiki halts the main clock and waits for an **interrupt** (timer overflow, radio IRQ, sensor ready). This is the literal "firefly sleep" of the analogy in §1.2.

---

### 4.3 Cooja Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    F0[START COOJA SIMULATION] --> F1[SELECT MOTE TYPE TMOTE SKY MICAZ Z1]
    F1 --> F2[CREATE NETWORK TOPOLOGY RANDOM GRID MANUAL]
    F2 --> F3[COMPILE CONTIKI FIRMWARE MAKE TARGET]
    F3 --> F4[FLASH MOTES WITH FIRMWARE]
    F4 --> F5[RUN SIMULATION REAL TIME FAST FORWARD]
    F5 --> F6[COLLECT METRICS RADIO LOGS ENERGY TIMELINE]
    F6 --> F7[EXPORT RESULTS PCAP CSV TEXT]
    F7 --> F8[ANALYZE USING WIRESHARK SCRIPTED TOOLS]
    F8 --> F9[REFINE FIRMWARE ITERATE]
    F9 --> F3
```

> **Reading guide:** Cooja is a **Java-based, instruction-level** simulator. Each simulated node runs an *actual* compiled Contiki binary (not a model). This makes Cooja a **Mote-in-the-Loop** simulator — a critical KTU 14-mark diagram topic.

---

### 4.4 Contiki vs Contiki-NG — Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[CONTIKI 2.X CLASSIC] --> T1[CONTIKI 3.X STABLE]
    T1 --> T2[CONTIKI NG 4.X NEXT GENERATION]
    T2 --> T3[CONTIKI NG 2024 LATEST]
    T0 --> T4[ADAM DUNKELS SICS 2003]
    T1 --> T5[IPV6 RPL COAP MATURE]
    T2 --> T6[ARM SUPPORT 6TISCH TSCH MODERN SECURITY]
    T3 --> T7[OPENMOTE BORDER ROUTER IOT STANDARDS]
```

> **Reading guide:** The KTU examiner may ask: *"Why Contiki-NG over classic Contiki?"* — answer: **TSCH (Time-Synchronized Channel Hopping)**, **6TiSCH** support, **better security (CoAPs/DTLS)**, **ARM Cortex-M support**, and **active maintenance**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse Contiki with TinyOS.** TinyOS uses **nesC** and a *task/event* model. Contiki uses **C** and *protothreads + events*. This is the #1 most-lost mark in WSN papers.
> - **Do NOT write "Contiki is the same as Linux."** It is a *tiny, embedded* OS — not a general-purpose OS.
> - **Do NOT forget the year of creation (2003) and creator (Adam Dunkels, SICS).** Examiners allocate 1 mark for this.
> - In diagrams, **label every block** (Rime, uIPv6, RPL, MAC, CoAP). An unlabeled block is treated as *zero* content.
> - Always mention **dual-stack** (Rime + uIPv6) when asked about communication — partial answers that mention only one stack lose 3 marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]

**Define Contiki operating system. List any six distinguishing features that make it suitable for Wireless Sensor Networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Contiki** is an **open-source, lightweight, event-driven operating system** designed for **resource-constrained IoT and WSN devices**. It was developed by **Adam Dunkels** at the **Swedish Institute of Computer Science (SICS)** in 2003, written in the **C language**.

**Six Distinguishing Features (0.5 mark each for the first six):**

1. **Lightweight footprint** — runs on systems with only a few KB of RAM and ROM.
2. **Event-driven kernel** — CPU sleeps when no events are pending, enabling ultra-low power.
3. **Protothreads** — stackless, blocking-style threads using only a 2-byte line counter.
4. **Dual communication stack** — supports both lightweight **Rime** and standards-based **uIPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL + CoAP**.
5. **Cooja simulator** — Java-based network simulator supporting full-stack emulation.
6. **Coffee file system** — wear-leveled, energy-aware file system for flash memory.
7. *(Optional bonus)*: BSD license, IPv6-ready, supports TSCH/6TiSCH (Contiki-NG).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]

**What are protothreads in Contiki? Mention any three advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Protothreads** are a **lightweight, stackless thread mechanism** in Contiki that allow C functions to *block* on conditions (e.g., "wait until buffer is full") without allocating a full hardware stack per thread. Implemented using a single line-counter variable `lc_t` and `switch`/`case` labels.

**Three Advantages (0.5 mark each):**

1. **Extremely low memory cost** — only **2 bytes** per thread vs. 128–512 bytes for a real thread.
2. **Enables hundreds of "threads"** on RAM-constrained 8/16-bit MCUs.
3. **Blocking-style programming** — simpler code than pure event-driven state machines.

**One Limitation (1 mark):**

> Protothreads **cannot use local (auto) variables across a `PT_WAIT_UNTIL` call**; only `static` variables survive a yield. This makes function-local state fragile.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered software architecture of the Contiki operating system**. Discuss the roles of the **core kernel, protothreads, and memory management** in detail.

**(b)** [7 marks] Compare the **Rime stack** and the **uIPv6 stack** in Contiki. Explain how **6LoWPAN and RPL** enable standards-based Internet connectivity for WSN nodes.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Contiki is organized as a set of loosely coupled layers. The diagram in §4.1 is the canonical architecture and should be reproduced with proper labels.

**Application Layer (0.5 mark):**
Contains user processes (e.g., a temperature-sensing app, a routing app). Each process is a `PROCESS_THREAD()` function.

**Core Kernel — Event-Driven Scheduler (1.5 marks):**

- Maintains a **circular linked list of processes**.
- Each process has a **state**: `PROCESS_STATE_RUNNING`, `CALLED`, or `WAITING`.
- Events are delivered via `process_post_synch()` or `process_post()` (asynchronous).
- The `process_run()` function pops the next event and calls the relevant process.
- When the event queue is empty, the kernel calls `ENERGEST_OFF()` and puts the MCU into **LPM3 (Low Power Mode 3)**.

**Protothreads (1.5 marks):**
- Implemented in `core/pt.h`.
- A protothread is a C function whose local flow is preserved across blocking calls using a single `lc_t` integer.
- Macros: `PT_BEGIN`, `PT_END`, `PT_WAIT_UNTIL`, `PT_YIELD`, `PT_RESTART`, `PT_EXIT`.
- Block size per protothread = **2 bytes** (the `lc` variable) — ideal for 8/16-bit MCUs.
- Example idiom: "Wait until packet arrives, then parse, then send ACK."

**Memory Management (1.5 marks):**

- **`memb`**: Static pool of fixed-size blocks. O(1) alloc/free. No fragmentation. Used for packet buffers, neighbor entries, route table entries.
- **`mmem`**: Reference-counted allocator for variable-sized data blocks. Used when size is unknown at compile time.
- **`list`**: Doubly-linked intrusive lists. Used in event queue, neighbor cache.
- All three are **deterministic** — important for real-time WSN behaviour.

**Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) (0.5 mark):**
Provides portable drivers for **CC2420 radio, MSP430 timers, ADC, LEDs, flash**. Makes Contiki portable across mote families.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The architecture enables a node with 10 KB ROM + 2 KB RAM to run a full IPv6 stack, making Contiki a benchmark for constrained-node OS design.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Rime** | **uIPv6** |
|---|---|---|
| IP | No | Yes (IPv4 / IPv6) |
| Footprint | ~1 KB | ~15–25 KB |
| Header | Custom (rimeaddr + type) | 6LoWPAN compressed IPv6 |
| Routing | Custom (multi-hop, mesh) | **RPL** (DODAG, IPv6) |
| Use | Research, lab | Standards-based IoT |

**6LoWPAN — Header Compression (2 marks):**

- Standard: **RFC 4944 / RFC 6282**.
- IPv6 header is **40 bytes**; 6LoWPAN compresses this to **~10–15 bytes** over IEEE 802.15.4 (max 127-byte frame).
- Uses **IPHC (IP Header Compression)** to elide fields derivable from the link-layer (e.g., 64-bit prefix from the EUI-64 of the radio).
- Enables a **stateless auto-configuration** similar to IPv6 SLAAC.

**RPL — Routing for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (2 marks):**

- Standard: **RFC 6550**.
