- Builds a **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at a **sink / border router**.
- Uses **DIO (DODAG Information Object)**, **DAO (Destination Advertisement Object)**, and **DIS (DODAG Information Solicitation)** ICMPv6 messages.
- Objective function (e.g., **MRHOF — Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function**) minimizes **ETX (Expected Transmission Count)**.
- Two modes: **Storing** (full route table at every node) and **Non-Storing** (only at the root).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **protothread mechanism in Contiki** with a neat diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** over traditional preemptive threads.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **Cooja network simulator**. List the steps to **simulate a WSN application using Cooja** and discuss its **advantages**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A protothread is a **stackless, blocking-style control abstraction** in Contiki that allows C functions to suspend and resume at arbitrary points.

**Internal Mechanism (2 marks):**
- A protothread is implemented using a single `lc_t` variable (line counter) and the `switch` statement.
- When `PT_WAIT_UNTIL(c)` is called and `c` is *false*, the current `case` label value is saved into `lc` and the function returns.
- When the process is rescheduled, the `switch (lc)` jumps to the saved label — execution resumes *exactly* where it stopped.
- This makes the thread *cooperative* (not preemptive) and *stackless* (no per-thread stack).

**Diagram (1.5 marks):**
Use the state machine in §4.2.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**

1. **Memory cost is just 2 bytes per thread** — supports hundreds of threads on 8 KB RAM.
2. **Blocking-style code is readable** — like a `pthread` but with a fraction of the cost.
3. **Zero context-switch overhead** — only a function call.
4. **Deterministic** — no preemption surprises, easier to verify.

**Limitations (1 mark):**

1. **No preemption** — a long-running computation blocks all other threads.
2. **No local variables across `PT_WAIT_UNTIL`** — must use `static`.
3. **Difficult to debug** — call stack does not reflect protothread nesting.
4. **Cannot block on multiple events** simultaneously without manual multiplexing.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Cooja** is a **Java-based, cross-level WSN simulator** bundled with Contiki. It supports **instruction-level emulation** — each simulated mote runs an actual compiled Contiki binary, not a behavioural model.

**Key Features (1.5 marks):**

- **Mote-in-the-Loop:** runs the real firmware, so what you simulate is what you deploy.
- **Multiple mote types:** Tmote Sky, MicaZ, Z1, Cooja's own "Java mote", and even CoAP/UDP "net motes" for pure network simulation.
- **Radio models:** Unit Disk Graph (UDGM), Directed Graph Radio Medium (DGRM), and real-radio loss models.
- **Plugins:** Radio logger, power tracker, timeline, mote output, script editor (in JavaScript or Python).

**Steps to Simulate (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Open Cooja** via `ant run` or by clicking the Cooja icon from the Contiki toolchain.
2. **Create a new simulation** and select the **mote type** (e.g., Tmote Sky).
3. **Compile the Contiki firmware** with `make TARGET=sky` for the chosen application.
4. **Add motes** to the simulation area (random, grid, or manual placement).
5. **Start the simulation** in real time or fast-forward.
6. **Collect metrics** using plugins (Radio Messages, Power Consumption, Timeline).
7. **Export logs** as `.pcap` for Wireshark analysis, or `.csv` for scripting.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**

1. **High fidelity** — runs real Contiki binaries.
2. **Rapid iteration** — no need to flash hardware during development.
3. **Reproducibility** — simulations are deterministic with seeded RNG.
4. **Visual debugging** — radio traffic and topology are visible in real time.
5. **Cost-effective** — no physical motes required for initial testing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Contiki (Module 2)**

- [ ] Contiki = **open-source, event-driven, C-based, tiny OS for WSN/IoT**.
- [ ] Created by **Adam Dunkels at SICS in 2003**; latest series is **Contiki-NG**.
- [ ] **Three pillars:** Event kernel, Protothreads, Memory management.
- [ ] **Protothreads** are stackless, blocking-style threads costing **only 2 bytes** each.
- [ ] **Limitation:** no local variables across `PT_WAIT_UNTIL`; use `static`.
- [ ] **Dual communication stack:** Rime (lightweight) + uIPv6 (standards-based).
- [ ] **6LoWPAN** compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to ~10–15 bytes for IEEE 802.15.4.
- [ ] **RPL** (RFC 6550) builds a DODAG; uses DIO/DAO/DIS; objective function like MRHOF.
- [ ] **Cooja** simulator = Java-based, mote-in-the-loop, runs real Contiki binaries.
- [ ] **Coffee file system** = wear-leveled, designed for flash memory.
- [ ] **Duty cycling** is the key to multi-year battery life (e.g., 5 % DC → ~2.8 years on 2000 mAh).
- [ ] **Energy model:** $E_{tx} = kE_{elec} + k\epsilon_{fs}d^2$ (free) or $k\epsilon_{mp}d^4$ (multipath).
- [ ] **Differentiator from TinyOS:** Contiki is C; TinyOS is nesC.
- [ ] **Differentiator from FreeRTOS:** Contiki is cooperative + event-driven; FreeRTOS is preemptive.
- [ ] Contiki is the **de-facto standard** for 6LoWPAN/RPL academic research.
- [ ] **Sleep mode = LPM3** on MSP430; triggered when event queue is empty.
- [ ] **Standard mention for KTU:** RFC 4944 (6LoWPAN), RFC 6282 (IPHC), RFC 6550 (RPL), RFC 7252 (CoAP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Requirements in WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Requirements in WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, *Security Requirements* refer to the comprehensive set of cryptographic, protocol-level, and architectural guarantees that must be enforced to ensure the **confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, availability, and freshness** of sensed data and control messages exchanged among resource-constrained sensor motes operating over an inherently insecure, broadcast wireless medium.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECAT634 – Module 3)** syllabus, security in WSNs is not a single feature but an **end-to-end design constraint** spanning all five layers of the protocol stack, dictated by the unique constraints of motes: limited energy (~2–3 AA batteries), limited memory (~4–10 KB RAM), limited computation (4–8 MHz MCUs), and open, unattended deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Dec 2024 onwards):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly categorizes WSN security requirements into **Primary (CIA triad adapted)**, **Secondary (Freshness, Self-Organization, Time Synchronization)**, and **Tertiary (Secure Localization, Data Aggregation Privacy)** requirements. Examiners expect students to enumerate and explain at least *six* security requirements with one-line justifications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **team of 100 volunteer doctors stationed across a war-torn city**, each carrying a walkie-talkie, a notepad, and a basic first-aid kit. They have no central headquarters, no identity cards, and the enemy is listening to every frequency. Every evening, they radio patient reports back to a base hospital.

Now ask:

- What stops the enemy from *intercepting* patient names? → **Confidentiality**
- What stops the enemy from *altering* a report from "minor wound" to "critical, ignore"? → **Integrity**
- How does the base hospital know the voice on the radio is *really* Dr. Asha and not an impersonator? → **Authentication**
- What if the enemy floods all walkie-talkies with noise so no real report gets through? → **Availability**
- What if the enemy replays yesterday's report *today* to confuse the hospital? → **Freshness**
- What if Dr. Asha denies she ever said "send the ambulance here"? → **Non-Repudiation**

That is *exactly* the WSN scenario — a network of unattended, resource-starved, battery-powered motes sensing critical data (battlefield, volcano, patient vitals) over a hostile wireless channel. **Security requirements in WSNs are the cryptographic, protocol, and architectural rules that keep these "doctor radios" trustworthy.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition Point:** Traditional Internet security (TLS, PKI) assumes *abundant power, CPU, and bandwidth*. WSN security must deliver the *same guarantees* with **1/1000th** of the resources — that is the central design tension.

### 1.3 Why Traditional Security Fails in WSNs

| Traditional Internet Security | WSN Reality | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Asymmetric crypto (RSA-2048) feasible | Public-key too slow on 8-bit motes | Need **symmetric** or **elliptic curve** alternatives |
| Trusted infrastructure (PKI, DNS) | No fixed infrastructure, ad-hoc | Need **distributed key management** |
| Unlimited power | **2 AA batteries**, lifetime ~ 1 year | Crypto must be **energy-efficient** |
| Physical security of devices | **Unattended outdoor deployment** | Hardware tamper-resistance required |
| High bandwidth (Mbps+) | **< 250 kbps** at 2.4 GHz | Lightweight protocols only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - **Mica2 mote:** 8-bit ATmega128 @ **8 MHz**, **4 KB RAM**, **128 KB Flash**
> - **Public-key RSA-1024:** ~ **0.5 J** per operation
> - **Symmetric AES-128:** ~ **1.5 µJ** per operation
> - Energy ratio: **Public-key is ~300,000× more expensive** than symmetric crypto on motes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Core Security Requirements of WSNs

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a clear, structured understanding of **ten security requirements**, grouped into three tiers. Below is the exhaustive treatment.

#### Tier 1 — Primary Requirements (CIA Triad + Authenticity)

1. **Confidentiality**
   - **Why:** Sensed data (e.g., enemy position, patient vitals) must be hidden from eavesdroppers.
   - **How:** Encryption (AES-128 in CTR/CBC mode, RC5, Skipjack). Link-layer or application-layer encryption.
   - **Real-world:** Military battlefield surveillance; industrial trade secrets.

2. **Integrity**
   - **Why:** An adversary could flip bits in transit, e.g., alter "Temperature = 25 °C" to "Temperature = 250 °C".
   - **How:** Message Authentication Codes (MAC — keyed hash) using **CBC-MAC**, **CMAC**, or **HMAC-SHA1**.
   - **Real-world:** Critical infrastructure (nuclear plant sensor readings).

3. **Authentication**
   - **Why:** Receiver must verify the sender is a legitimate network member, not an injected node.
   - **How:** Shared symmetric keys per pair, or group keys, or digital signatures (rare, expensive).
   - **Real-world:** Anti-node-spoofing in IoT smart-meter networks.

4. **Availability**
   - **Why:** WSN must continue functioning despite DoS (Denial of Service) attacks — jamming, flooding, exhaustion.
   - **How:** Spread-spectrum, frequency hopping, rate limiting, redundant paths, energy-aware protocols.
   - **Real-world:** Forest fire detection network must report even during intentional RF interference.

#### Tier 2 — Secondary Requirements

5. **Data Freshness**
   - **Why:** Adversary may *replay* old messages; receiver must ensure message is *current*, not stale.
   - **How:** Two subtypes:
     - *Weak freshness:* Partial timing info — message is recent but no strict order.
     - *Strong freshness:* Total ordering — message is the *latest* and ordered.
   - **Implementation:** Monotonic counters, timestamps + nonce, sliding-window protocols.

6. **Self-Organization**
   - **Why:** No fixed infrastructure; motes must form keys, detect intruders, and reconfigure **autonomously**.
   - **How:** Distributed key pre-distribution (e.g., **EG scheme**, **Blundo's polynomial**), local trust decisions.

7. **Time Synchronization**
   - **Why:** Many WSN protocols (TDMA, freshness, localization) require synchronized clocks.
   - **How:** Secure time-sync protocols (e.g., **TinySeRSync**) resistant to pulse-delay attacks.

8. **Secure Localization**
   - **Why:** Adversary can spoof GPS signals or beacon positions, causing wrong sensor-location mapping.
   - **How:** Verifiable multilateration, authenticated beacon nodes, range-bound checks.

#### Tier 3 — Operational & Tertiary Requirements

9. **Authorization**
   - **Why:** Some motes should only access certain services (e.g., aggregator vs. leaf).
   - **How:** Capability-based access control, role-based keys.

10. **Non-Repudiation**
    - **Why:** Sender cannot deny having sent a critical alarm.
    - **How:** Digital signatures (rare in pure WSN, used in sink-to-base-station leg).

### 2.2 The WSN Threat Model

A **threat model** formalizes the *adversary's capabilities* and *goals*. KTU examiners expect this classification:

| Adversary Class | Capability | Example Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Outside (Mote-class)** | Limited CPU, but access to crypto | Eavesdropping, simple DoS |
| **Inside (Compromised node)** | Holds all legitimate keys | Selective forwarding, false data injection |
| **Laptop-class** | Powerful, multiple radios | Jamming, wormhole, Sybil |
| **Mote-class insider** | One legitimate node compromised | Sinkhole attack |

**Dolev–Yao Model (1983):** Standard formal adversary model — adversary controls the network (can read, inject, modify, replay, drop messages) but **cannot break crypto** without the key. All WSN security proofs assume Dolev–Yao.

### 2.3 Layer-wise Attack Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield)

| Layer | Attack | Defense Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Jamming, Tampering, Node destruction | Tamper-proofing, spread-spectrum |
| **Link (MAC)** | Collision, Exhaustion, Unfairness | Error-correcting codes, rate limits |
| **Network / Routing** | Selective forwarding, Sinkhole, Wormhole, Sybil, Hello flood | Authenticated routing, geographic constraints |
| **Transport** | Flooding, De-synchronization | Authenticated handshake, limit connection requests |
| **Application** | False data injection, Clock skewing | Data aggregation verification, secure time-sync |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities and metrics below are **verifiable** from standard WSN security literature (Perrig–Stankovic–Wagner 2004 survey, Wang–Attebury–Ramamurthy 2006 survey).

| Symbol / Term | Meaning / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{AES}$ | Energy for one AES-128 encryption | ~ **1.5 µJ** per block |
| $E_{RSA}$ | Energy for one RSA-1024 sign | ~ **0.5 J** per op |
| $E_{ECDSA}$ | Energy for one ECDSA-163 sign | ~ **20 mJ** per op |
| $N$ | Number of motes in network | dimensionless |
| $k$ | Key pool size (in EG scheme) | dimensionless |
| $p(q)$ | Probability of direct key sharing | $1 - \frac{((N-k-1)!)^2}{(N-1)! \cdot (N-k-1-(k-1))!}$ |
| $P_{c}$ | Network compromise probability (single key compromise) | $p(q) \approx \frac{k}{N}$ |
| $\tau$ | Time-to-live of a nonce / counter | seconds |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash output length | bits (e.g., 128 for MD5) |
| $MAC_{len}$ | MAC tag length | bits, typically **64–96** |
| $T_{jam}$ | Time for successful jamming | seconds, must be $>$ T_deploy |
| $\Delta t$ | Maximum allowable clock drift | seconds, typically $<$ 1 µs |
| $R$ | Radio range | meters, **10–100 m** typical |
| $E_{tx}(l,d)$ | Energy to transmit $l$ bits over distance $d$ | $E_{elec} \cdot l + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot l \cdot d^{\alpha}$ |

> **Energy model (Heinzelman):**
> $$E_{tx}(l,d) \;=\; l \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; l \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{\alpha}$$
> where $\alpha \in [2, 4]$ is the **path-loss exponent**.

> **Identity-bound keying (key probability for EG scheme):**
> $$P_{direct} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\binom{N - k - 1}{k}}{\binom{N - 1}{k}} \;\approx\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{N}\right)^{k}$$

> **Verifier-impersonation resistance** (authentication):
> $$\Pr[\text{forge} \mid q_v \text{queries}] \;\leq\; \frac{q_v}{2^{n-1}}$$
> where $n$ is the MAC output bit-length.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Key Connectivity Probability in the Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) Scheme

The **Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) key pre-distribution scheme (2002)** is the foundational probabilistic key management scheme for WSNs. It is part of the **KTU Module 3 syllabus** and frequently appears in 14-mark questions.

#### Step 1: Setup
Each mote $M_i$ is loaded with a **random subset** of $k$ keys drawn from a large key pool $S$ of size $\vert S \vert$. Let:

- $N$ = total number of motes in deployment
- $k$ = size of key ring per mote
- $\vert S \vert$ = key pool size (large, e.g., 100,000)

#### Step 2: Probability that two motes share at least one key
Let $A$ = event "mote $M_i$ and mote $M_j$ share at least one key".

The probability that a *specific* key (out of $\vert S \vert$) is **not** in $M_i$'s ring is:

$$
p_{miss} \;=\; 1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}
$$

The probability that the same key is also not in $M_j$'s ring (independent draws) is:

$$
p_{miss}^2 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}\right)^{2}
$$

For $\vert S \vert$ keys, the probability that **none** of them is shared is:

$$
P(\bar{A}) \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}\right)^{k}
$$

Therefore, the probability of **at least one shared key** is:

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}\right)^{k}
$$

#### Step 3: Approximation for Large $\vert S \vert$

Using the limit $\lim_{x \to \infty}(1 - \frac{a}{x})^{x} = e^{-a}$:

$$
P(A) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-k^{2}/\vert S \vert}
$$

#### Step 4: Network-wide connectivity

If every pair must share a key with probability $P(A)$, the **expected number of secure links** in the network is:

$$
E[L_{sec}] \;=\; \binom{N}{2} \cdot P(A) \;\approx\; \frac{N^{2}}{2} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k^{2}/\vert S \vert}\right)
$$

#### Step 5: Security — probability of compromise when one mote is captured

If an adversary captures one mote, it obtains $k$ keys. The probability that a *non-compromised* link (between two un-captured motes) is broken is the fraction of pool compromised:

$$
P_{break} \;=\; \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}
$$

For a network where $x$ motes are captured:

$$
P_{break}(x) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}\right)^{x}
$$

#### Step 6: Numerical Example (typical KTU exam)

> Let $N = 1000$, $\vert S \vert = 100{,}000$, $k = 50$.

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{50}{100{,}000}\right)^{50}
$$

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - (0.9995)^{50}
$$

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - e^{-50 \times 0.0005} \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-0.025}
$$

$$
P(A) \;\approx\; 1 - 0.9753 \;=\; 0.0247 \quad (\text{only } 2.47\%)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With $k = 50$ and $\vert S \vert = 100{,}000$, only **~2.5 %** of pairs share a key — far too low. We must **increase $k$** (e.g., to $k = 200$) to raise $P(A)$ to a useful level.

Recomputing with $k = 200$:

$$
P(A) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-(200)^{2}/100{,}000} \;=\; 1 - e^{-0.4} \;\approx\; 0.3297
$$

Now ~33 % of pairs share a key — a realistic value. Increasing $k$ **trades** memory for connectivity.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Requirement → Countermeasure

| Threat / Attack | Security Requirement Violated | Countermeasure | Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | Confidentiality | AES-128 encryption (CTR mode) | All |
| **Packet modification** | Integrity | CBC-MAC, CMAC | Network |
| **Node impersonation** | Authentication | Shared symmetric keys, µTESLA | Network |
| **Replay attack** | Freshness | Monotonic counters, nonce, timestamps | All |
| **Jamming** | Availability | Spread-spectrum, frequency hopping | Physical |
| **Selective forwarding** | Availability + Integrity | Multi-path routing, watchdog | Network |
| **Sybil attack** | Authentication + Integrity | Identity certification, resource test | Network |
| **Sinkhole** | Availability | Geographic routing, authenticated beacons | Network |
| **Wormhole** | Authentication | Packet leashes (geographic / temporal) | Network |
| **HELLO flood** | Authentication | Bidirectional link verification | Network |
| **False data injection** | Integrity + Authentication | Commit-and-attest, statistical en-route filtering | App |
| **Node capture** | Confidentiality + Auth | Tamper-proofing, key revocation, EG/q-composite | All |

### 3.3 Verifier's Authenticity Bound — Step-by-Step

For a MAC of $n$-bit tag length, the probability of an adversary forging a valid tag after $q_v$ verifier queries is bounded by the **birthday paradox / guessing bound**:

$$
\Pr[\text{forge}] \;\leq\; \frac{q_v}{2^{n}} \;+\; \varepsilon
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the cryptographic advantage (negligible for sound MACs). For $n = 64$ and $q_v = 10^{6}$:

$$
\Pr[\text{forge}] \;\leq\; \frac{10^{6}}{2^{64}} \;\approx\; 5.4 \times 10^{-14}
$$

This is the **negligible** advantage demanded by the Dolev–Yao model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WSN Security Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Layer Sensors] --> B[Link Layer MAC]
    B --> C[Network Layer Routing]
    C --> D[Transport Layer]
    D --> E[Application Layer Data Aggregation]
    E --> F[Base Station Sink Node]

    P1[Jamming Attack] -.-> A
    P2[Collision Exhaustion] -.-> B
    P3[Sybil Wormhole Sinkhole] -.-> C
    P4[Flooding DeSync] -.-> D
    P5[False Data Injection] -.-> E

    S1[Tamper Proofing Spread Spectrum] -.-> A
    S2[Authenticated MAC] -.-> B
    S3[Secure Routing Packet Leash] -.-> C
    S4[Limit Conn Rate] -.-> D
    S5[Commit and Attest Filter] -.-> E

    K[Symmetric Key Ring EG Scheme] -.-> B
    K -.-> C
    K -.-> E
    T[Secure Time Sync] -.-> C
    L[Secure Localization] -.-> C
    F --> G[End User Application]
```

### 4.2 WSN Security Requirements Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[WSN Security Requirements]
    R --> P[Primary CIA Plus]
    R --> S[Secondary Operational]
    R --> T[Tertiary Advanced]

    P --> P1[Confidentiality]
    P --> P2[Integrity]
    P --> P3[Authentication]
    P --> P4[Availability]

    S --> S1[Data Freshness]
    S --> S2[Self Organization]
    S --> S3[Time Synchronization]
    S --> S4[Secure Localization]

    T --> T1[Authorization]
    T --> T2[Non Repudiation]
    T --> T3[Privacy Preserving Aggregation]
```

### 4.3 Attack–Defense Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Eavesdropping] -->|violates| R1[Confidentiality]
    A2[Bit Flipping] -->|violates| R2[Integrity]
    A3[Node Spoofing] -->|violates| R3[Authentication]
    A4[Replay] -->|violates| R4[Freshness]
    A5[Jamming] -->|violates| R5[Availability]

    R1 --> D1[AES 128 CTR]
    R2 --> D2[CBC MAC CMAC]
    R3 --> D3[Shared Key]
    R4 --> D4[Nonce Counter]
    R5 --> D5[Spread Spectrum FHSS]
```

### 4.4 Key Pre-Distribution in EG Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KPool[Large Key Pool S of 100000 keys]
    KPool -->|random draw k| M1[Mote M1 Key Ring]
    KPool -->|random draw k| M2[Mote M2 Key Ring]
    KPool -->|random draw k| M3[Mote M3 Key Ring]
    M1 -->|shared key K12| M2
    M2 -->|shared key K23| M3
    M1 -.->|with prob PA| M3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any four primary security requirements of a Wireless Sensor Network.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — each requirement = 0.5 m for statement + 0.25 m for example):**

The four primary security requirements of a WSN are:

1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures that sensed data and control messages are not disclosed to unauthorized parties. Achieved via symmetric encryption such as AES-128. *Example:* Military battlefield sensor data must not be readable by the enemy.

2. **Integrity:** Guarantees that data has not been modified during transit. Implemented using Message Authentication Codes (MACs) such as CBC-MAC. *Example:* A temperature reading of 25 °C must not be altered to 250 °C by a man-in-the-middle.

3. **Authentication:** Verifies that a message indeed originates from a legitimate node and not an impersonator. Realized via shared symmetric keys or digital signatures. *Example:* The sink must reject messages from an injected rogue node.

4. **Availability:** Ensures the WSN continues to deliver its sensing service even under DoS attacks (jamming, flooding). Provided via spread-spectrum, redundant paths, and energy-aware protocols. *Example:* A forest fire sensor must report despite intentional RF jamming.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-1]**
*Why are traditional public-key cryptography schemes like RSA not directly suitable for WSN motes? Justify with two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RSA is not directly suitable for WSN motes for the following reasons:

1. **Computational cost:** RSA-1024 requires modular exponentiation of 1024-bit numbers. On an 8 MHz ATmega128 mote, one RSA signature takes ~10 seconds, while a single AES-128 operation takes ~0.1 ms — a **100,000× difference**.

2. **Energy cost:** A single RSA-1024 signature consumes ~0.5 J, while AES-128 consumes ~1.5 µJ. Mote batteries (2 × AA, ~2.5 Ah at 3 V ≈ 27 kJ total) can support only **~50,000 RSA signatures** vs. **~18 billion AES operations**.

3. (Bonus point) **Memory cost:** RSA keys and signatures are 128–1024 bytes; mote RAM is only 4–10 KB, leaving little room for application code.

Hence WSNs use **symmetric crypto (AES)** or **elliptic curve crypto (ECC)** with **~163-bit keys** that offer comparable security at lower cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ten security requirements of a Wireless Sensor Network**, classifying them into Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary tiers. For each, give one countermeasure. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) key pre-distribution scheme** in detail. Derive the probability that two motes share at least one key when $N = 500$, key pool size $\vert S \vert = 50{,}000$, and key ring size $k = 100$. Comment on the security of the scheme. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks — 0.5 m per requirement + 0.2 m for countermeasure]**

| Tier | Requirement | Threat Addressed | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary | **Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping | AES-128 (CTR mode) |
| Primary | **Integrity** | Packet modification | CBC-MAC / CMAC |
| Primary | **Authentication** | Node impersonation, Sybil | Shared symmetric key per pair, µTESLA |
| Primary | **Availability** | Jamming, DoS | Spread-spectrum, multi-path routing |
| Secondary | **Data Freshness** | Replay attack | Nonces, monotonic counters, timestamps |
| Secondary | **Self-Organization** | Node capture, ad-hoc failure | Distributed key mgmt (EG, q-Composite) |
| Secondary | **Time Synchronization** | Pulse-delay, desync | TinySeRSync, authenticated beacons |
| Secondary | **Secure Localization** | GPS spoofing, wormhole | Verifiable multilateration |
| Tertiary | **Authorization** | Privilege escalation | Capability tokens, role-based keys |
| Tertiary | **Non-Repudiation** | Sender denial | Digital signatures (sink-to-base leg) |

**Explanation (brief, high-yield):**
- *Confidentiality* uses **AES** with a **per-link or global key**; CTR mode is preferred over CBC for resilience to bit-flipping.
- *Integrity* via **MAC** is mandatory because encryption alone does not prevent tampering.
- *Authentication* is hardest because PKI is too expensive; **µTESLA** (micro-Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication) uses symmetric MAC with delayed key disclosure to simulate asymmetric authentication.
- *Availability* needs cross-layer solutions since jamming affects the physical layer but routing protocols can mitigate by re-routing.
- *Freshness* has two flavors: **weak** (partial timing — "this message is recent") and **strong** (total ordering — "this is the *latest* message").
- *Self-organization* is required because motes cannot rely on a central KDC.
- *Time synchronization* prevents replay and is essential for TDMA-based MAC protocols.
- *Secure localization* prevents adversaries from claiming a false position; essential for geographic routing.
- *Authorization* restricts which services a mote can invoke (e.g., only aggregators may forward data to sink).
- *Non-repudiation* is rarely enforced inside the WSN but is required at the sink-to-base boundary for legal/medical evidence.

**(b) [7 marks — 2 m for description, 3 m for derivation, 1 m for numerical, 1 m for security comment]**

**Description of EG Scheme:**
- Proposed by **Eschenauer and Gligor in 2002**, it is the **first probabilistic key management scheme** for WSNs.
- A **large key pool $S$** of size $\vert S \vert$ is generated offline.
- Each mote $M_i$ is loaded with a **random subset (key ring)** of $k$ keys from $S$.
- After deployment, two motes $M_i, M_j$ exchange their key-ring IDs and discover a **shared key** (if any) to establish a secure link.
- If no shared key exists, a **path key** is established through intermediaries via the **shared-key graph**.

**Derivation of $P(A)$:**

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{\vert S \vert}\right)^{k}
$$

**Step-by-step:** (already derived in Section 3.1).

**Numerical computation:**

Given $N = 500$, $\vert S \vert = 50{,}000$, $k = 100$:

$$
P(A) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{100}{50{,}000}\right)^{100}
$$

$$
= \; 1 - (1 - 0.002)^{100}
$$

$$
= \; 1 - (0.998)^{100}
$$

Using $\ln(0.998) = -0.002002$, hence $(0.998)^{100} = e^{-0.2002} \approx 0.8187$.

$$
P(A) \;\approx\; 1 - 0.8187 \;=\; 0.1813
$$

**So ~18.13 % of pairs share a key directly.** This is a moderate value; to increase connectivity, we could raise $k$ to ~200 (which would give $P(A) \approx 0.55$).

**Security comment:**
The EG scheme is **resilient to small-scale capture** because a captured mote reveals only $k$ out of $\vert S \vert$ keys, compromising only $\frac{k}{\vert S \vert} = 0.2\%$ of all secure links. However, it is **vulnerable to large-scale capture** and **node-replication attacks** since identical key rings could be duplicated. The **q-Composite scheme** (Chan–Perrig–Song 2003) improves on EG by requiring $q \geq 1$ shared keys, but reduces the connectivity probability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Draw the **layer-wise attack taxonomy** of WSNs (Physical, Link, Network, Transport, Application layers). For each layer, list two attacks and their defenses. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Dolev–Yao adversary model** as applied to WSNs. Why is this model insufficient for *insider* attackers, and what extensions are used in modern WSN threat models? (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) Layer-wise Attack Taxonomy [7 marks — 1 m for diagram + 1 m per layer × 2 attacks + 0.5 m per defense]**

**Physical Layer Attacks:**
1. **Jamming** — Adversary transmits continuous RF noise, preventing communication. *Defense:* Spread-spectrum (DSSS/FHSS), region-based mapping, channel surfing.
2. **Tampering / Node Capture** — Adversary physically opens a mote and extracts crypto keys. *Defense:* Tamper-proof hardware, self-destruct on intrusion, key revocation.

**Link Layer (MAC) Attacks:**
1. **Collision** — Attacker sends a packet at the same time, causing CRC failure. *Defense:* Error-Correcting Codes (ECC), time/frequency diversity.
2. **Exhaustion** — Repeated RTS/CTS causes battery drain. *Defense:* Rate limiting, MAC-layer authentication before request acceptance.

**Network / Routing Layer Attacks:**
1. **Selective Forwarding (Blackhole)** — Malicious node drops packets. *Defense:* Multi-path routing, watchdog monitoring, neighbor trust.
2. **Sinkhole / Wormhole / Sybil / Hello-Flood** — Sinkhole advertises fake high-quality route; wormhole relays packets through fast out-of-band link; Sybil creates many fake identities; Hello-flood exploits unsecured "hello" beacons. *Defense:* Geographic routing, packet leashes (geographic & temporal), identity certification, bidirectional link verification.

**Transport Layer Attacks:**
1. **Flooding** — Adversary opens many transport sessions, exhausting memory. *Defense:* Connection rate limits, client puzzles.
2. **Desynchronization** — Adversary spoofs sequence numbers, forcing retransmission. *Defense:* Authentication of all control packets.

**Application Layer Attacks:**
1. **False Data Injection / Aggregation Manipulation** — Forged sensor readings poison decisions. *Defense:* Commit-and-attest, statistical en-route filtering, witness-based aggregation.
2. **Clock Skewing** — Adversary modifies time-sync messages. *Defense:* Secure time-sync protocols (TinySeRSync), multiple time sources.

**(b) Dolev–Yao Model & Insider Extensions [7 marks]**

**Dolev–Yao Model (1983):**
- Formal adversary model originally for cryptographic protocol analysis.
- Assumes the adversary **controls the entire network**: can intercept, read, modify, delete, replay, inject, and reorder messages.
- The adversary **cannot break cryptography** without the key — i.e., encryption and MACs are considered *perfect* primitives.
- The adversary is **computationally unbounded** in symbolic logic (abstract algebraic reasoning).
- Standard tool: **BAN logic** (Burrows–Abadi–Needham) for protocol verification.

**Why Dolev–Yao is insufficient for WSNs:**

1. **Insider attackers (compromised motes)** *do* hold legitimate keys, so the "cannot break crypto" assumption is violated.
2. **Dolev–Yao assumes unbounded computation**, but mote CPUs are limited — this is both a weakness (insider can run expensive attacks) and a strength (computational hardness assumptions like factoring are reasonable).
3. **Node-capture attacks** are physical, not protocol-level; Dolev–Yao has no physical adversary.
4. **Dolev–Yao is symbolic**; WSNs need **computational** (game-based) proofs of security (e.g., IND-CCA2 for encryption, EUF-CMA for signatures).

**Modern Extensions for WSNs:**

- **CK (Canetti–Krawczyk) Model** — modular composable security with session keys.
- **BR (Bellare–Rogaway) Model** — game-based, with concrete adversary capabilities.
- **UC (Universal Composability)** Framework — guarantees security under protocol composition.
- **Adversary models for WSNs (Perrig–Stankovic–Wagner 2004):**
  - **Mote-class adversary** — limited resources.
  - **Laptop-class adversary** — powerful, fast, multiple radios.
  - **Insider adversary** — has all keys of captured motes.
- **Probabilistic security arguments** — e.g., EG scheme's $\frac{k}{\vert S \vert}$ capture-resilience.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Confidentiality and Integrity** — encryption alone does NOT provide integrity. Always state *both* a cipher and a MAC.
> 2. **Forgetting Freshness as a distinct requirement** — replay protection is *not* the same as authentication.
> 3. **Skipping numerical substitution in EG derivation** — examiners *require* substituting values and simplifying the exponent. Showing only the formula = 0 marks.
> 4. **Not classifying the threat model** — every security design must begin by stating the adversary (Mote-class / Insider / Dolev–Yao).
> 5. **Confusing Dolev–Yao with physical-layer attacks** — Dolev–Yao is a *logical* model; jamming is a *physical* threat.
> 6. **Writing "AES is secure" without justification** — must specify mode (CTR/CBC/GCM), key length (128/256), and tag length for MAC.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Security Requirements in WSNs**

- **10 Security Requirements** of WSNs:
  - **Primary (4):** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Availability.
  - **Secondary (4):** Data Freshness (weak + strong), Self-Organization, Time Synchronization, Secure Localization.
  - **Tertiary (2):** Authorization, Non-Repudiation.
- **Dolev–Yao Model** = adversary controls network, but cannot break crypto.
- **Threat classes:** Mote-class, Laptop-class, Insider (compromised node).
- **Energy costs (on Mica2):** AES-128 ≈ **1.5 µJ**; RSA-1024 ≈ **0.5 J**; ECDSA-163 ≈ **20 mJ**.
- **EG scheme:** $P(A) = 1 - (1 - k/\vert S \vert)^{k} \approx 1 - e^{-k^{2}/\vert S \vert}$.
- **q-Composite Scheme** (Chan et al. 2003) improves on EG by requiring $q$ shared keys.
- **µTESLA** = micro-Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication — emulates asymmetric auth using delayed symmetric key disclosure.
- **Layer-wise attacks:**
  - *Physical:* Jamming, Tampering.
  - *Link:* Collision, Exhaustion.
  - *Network:* Selective forwarding, Sinkhole, Wormhole, Sybil, Hello-flood.
  - *Transport:* Flooding, De-sync.
  - *App:* False data injection, Clock skewing.
- **Freshness Types:** *Weak* (partial timing) vs. *Strong* (total ordering).
- **Countermeasures (one-liners):**
  - Confidentiality → **AES-128 CTR**.
  - Integrity → **CBC-MAC / CMAC**.
  - Authentication → **µTESLA** (broadcast), **shared pair keys** (unicast).
  - Availability → **Spread-spectrum, multi-path**.
  - Freshness → **Nonces, counters, timestamps**.
  - Secure localization → **Verifiable multilateration**.
- **KTU must-mention keywords in 14-mark answers:** Dolev–Yao, Mica2, energy model $E_{tx} = lE_{elec} + l\epsilon_{amp}d^{\alpha}$, path-loss exponent $\alpha \in [2,4]$, key pool $\vert S \vert$, key ring $k$, $q$-composite, µTESLA, packet leashes (geographic and temporal).
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Don't equate "encryption = security". Always pair cipher + MAC.
  - Don't write generic "use strong encryption" — name the algorithm and mode.
  - Don't skip the *insider adversary* — it is the dominant threat in WSNs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Vulnerabilities in WSNs - DoS Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Security Vulnerabilities in WSNs - DoS Attacks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of DoS Attacks in WSNs A **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a malicious, coordinated attempt by an adversary to exhaust, disrupt, or disable the fundamental resources (battery energy, radio bandwidth, computational cycles, or memory) of sensor nodes, thereby preventing the legitimate WSN from delivering its intended sensing, communication, or actuation services with the expected **Quality of Service (QoS)** parameters. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (aligned with RFC 4949 and the classical WSN threat model of Wood & Stankovic, 2002), DoS attacks in WSNs are **multi-layer threats** that exploit the broadcast nature of the wireless medium, the resource-constrained nature of motes, and the multi-hop routing dependencies of the network. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Anchor:** A DoS attack is defined as *"any event that diminishes or eliminates a network's capacity to perform its expected function"*, causing severe degradation in **packet delivery ratio (PDR)**, **end-to-end latency**, and **network lifetime**. ## 1.2 Why WSNs Are Uniquely Vulnerable Wireless Sensor Networks have a fundamentally different threat surface than traditional IP networks because of four intrinsic limitations: | WSN Limitation | Impact on DoS Vulnerability | |---|---| | **Resource-constrained hardware** (typically 8-bit/16-bit MCU, 4–10 KB RAM) | Cannot run heavyweight cryptographic or intrusion detection algorithms | | **Battery-powered operation** (AA cells, 2.7–3.3 V, 2–3 Ah) | Energy-depletion attacks (sleep deprivation) are devastating | | **Unattended deployment** (forests, battlefields, oceans) | Physical tampering is easy; nodes cannot be guarded | | **Wireless broadcast medium** (ISM band, 2.4 GHz) | Jamming and eavesdropping are trivially launched with off-the-shelf SDRs | | **Multi-hop routing** with no central authority | Selective forwarding and sinkhole attacks cripple the entire network | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Village Letter Delivery System:** > Imagine a remote village where postmen (sensor nodes) deliver letters (data packets) by passing them from one person to the next (multi-hop routing) to reach a distant post office (sink/base station). Each postman has limited stamina (battery) and can carry only a small satchel (memory). > > A **DoS attack** in this village could be: > 1. A gang standing on the path yelling loudly (**Jamming at Physical Layer**) — the postmen cannot hear each other. > 2. A thief who steals every 5th letter from the satchel (**Blackhole Attack at Network Layer**) — letters vanish silently. > 3. A mischievous child who keeps shaking every postman's hand for hours (**Resource Exhaustion at MAC Layer**) — postmen collapse from exhaustion. > 4. A saboteur who replaces the village map with a fake one pointing to a dead-end (**Sinkhole/Wormhole Attack**) — all letters get lost in the wrong direction. > > In every case, the mail system is **denied its service** — not because the postmen are bad, but because the system was overwhelmed or misled by an adversary. ## 1.4 The WSN Security Threat Model The adversary in a WSN can be classified along two axes: - **Capability axis**: *Mote-class* attacker (controls few nodes, similar hardware) vs. *Laptop-class* attacker (powerful hardware, more energy). - **Location axis**: *External* attacker (outside cryptographic boundary) vs. *Internal* attacker (has compromised a legitimate node's key). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The most damaging DoS attacks on WSNs are launched by *internal, mote-class* attackers because they bypass perimeter defenses and abuse legitimate credentials. This is why **defense-in-depth** at every layer of the protocol stack is mandated in the KTU syllabus. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Protocol-Stack View of DoS Attacks WSNs follow a layered protocol stack (typically 5 layers matching the OSI/ISO model adapted for sensor motes). DoS attacks target every single layer. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to map attacks to layers. ### 2.1.1 Physical Layer DoS Attacks | Attack | Mechanism | Targeted Resource | |---|---|---| | **Jamming** | Adversary emits high-power RF noise in the 2.4 GHz ISM band | Radio spectrum | | **Tampering** | Physical destruction or extraction of cryptographic keys from captured motes | Hardware, key storage | | **Node Destruction / Capture** | Adversary physically destroys nodes or extracts firmware via JTAG | Network density, connectivity | **Jamming Categories (per KTU syllabus):** 1. **Constant Jamming** — Continuous emission of a radio signal. 2. **Deceptive Jamming** — Injection of semantically valid (but illegitimate) packets to keep the receiver busy. 3. **Random Jamming** — Alternates between sleep and jam modes to conserve attacker energy. 4. **Reactive Jamming** — Emits jamming signal only when it senses ongoing legitimate transmission (most energy-efficient attacker). ### 2.1.2 Data Link / MAC Layer DoS Attacks | Attack | Mechanism | Targeted Resource | |---|---|---| | **Collision** | Transmitting during another node's transmission to corrupt the frame (CRC fails) | Channel access | | **Exhaustion** | Repeated Request-To-Send (RTS) frames to force victims to stay in receive mode | Battery energy | | **Unfairness** | Capturing the channel intermittently, breaking MAC fairness protocols (e.g., S-MAC) | Time-slice allocation | ### 2.1.3 Network Layer DoS Attacks | Attack | Mechanism | Targeted Resource | |---|---|---| | **Blackhole** | Compromised node drops every packet it receives | Data delivery | | **Sinkhole** | Adversary advertises a high-quality (low-cost) route to the sink; all traffic reroutes to it | Topology integrity | | **Wormhole** | Two colluding adversaries create a low-latency out-of-band tunnel to replay packets far away | Routing paths | | **Selective Forwarding** | Compromised node forwards only some packets, drops others | Data integrity | | **Hello Flood** | Attacker with high-RF power broadcasts Hello packets to convince distant nodes it is a neighbor | Routing tables | | **Sybil** | A single node fabricates many fake identities to dominate routing or voting | Redundancy checks | | **Homing** | Attacker identifies and targets cluster heads or base-station-adjacent nodes | Critical nodes | ### 2.1.4 Transport Layer DoS Attacks | Attack | Mechanism | Targeted Resource | |---|---|---| | **Flooding** | Attacker initiates many transport-layer sessions (e.g., TCP-like in 6LoWPAN) that are never completed | Connection tables, buffers | | **Desynchronization** | Spoofed control frames (FIN/ACK in 6LoWPAN, FIN/RST) repeatedly tear down existing connections | Session state | ### 2.1.5 Application Layer DoS Attacks | Attack | Mechanism | Targeted Resource | |---|---|---| | **Clock Skewing** | Compromised nodes broadcast false time synchronization beacons | Time-triggered MAC/sensing | | **Data Aggregation Attack** | False readings injected during in-network aggregation | Data accuracy | | **Path-Based DoS** | Energy-draining path is repeatedly forced on a node | Battery life | ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---| | **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $PDR = \frac{P_{rx}}{P_{tx}}$ | Fraction of transmitted packets successfully received. KTU benchmark: PDR $\ge$ 0.9 for healthy WSN. | | **Energy Depletion per Jam** | $E_{jam} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{jam}$ | Energy consumed by an attacker node to keep a victim jammed for time $t_{jam}$. | | **Energy Depletion per Node** | $E_{total} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{idle} + E_{sleep}$ | Standard energy model (Crossbow MICAz). Typical: $E_{tx}$ = 50 nJ/bit, $E_{rx}$ = 50 nJ/bit. | | **Jammer Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR_{jam} = \frac{P_{jam}}{N_0 \cdot B}$ | Ratio of jammer power to thermal noise. Successful jamming typically requires $SNR_{jam} \ge 1$. | | **Network Lifetime under Attack** | $L = \frac{E_{initial} - E_{attack}}{E_{round}}$ | Rounds until first node death; $E_{attack}$ is energy drained by the DoS attack per round. | | **Attack Detection Probability** | $P_d = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | Probability of detecting at least one attack event in $n$ monitoring windows, each with single-window detection probability $p$. | | **Sybil Impact on Voting** | $V_{compromised} = \frac{k}{n+k}$ | Voting power of $k$ Sybil identities out of $n$ legitimate + $k$ fake nodes. | | **Blackhole Drop Rate** | $D_{bh} = 1 - \alpha$ | Where $\alpha$ is the forwarding probability of the compromised node ($\alpha = 0$ for full blackhole). | | **Wormhole Latency Condition** | $t_{tunnel} < t_{legitimate}$ | Wormhole wins route selection when its out-of-band tunnel beats any legitimate multi-hop path. | | **Channel Saturation Threshold** | $\rho \ge \rho_{critical}$ | When offered load $\rho$ exceeds the CSMA/CA backoff capacity, the MAC becomes saturated. | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Military battlefield surveillance** (e.g., BAE Systems REMBASS-II): DoS-resistant WSNs must survive 72 hours under coordinated jamming. - **Smart grid SCADA substation monitoring**: A sinkhole attack on a substation WSN could mask load fluctuations leading to grid instability. - **Healthcare patient monitoring**: A desynchronization attack on a body-area network (BAN) could disconnect an ICU patient's vital-sign stream. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Homing attacks on cluster heads in a pipeline monitoring WSN can blind the operator to leakage events. ## 2.4 Defensive Countermeasures by Layer > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examination Focus:** Students must pair each attack with at least one named defense. The 2024 scheme specifically tests the **attack-defense mapping** competency under Course Outcome CO3. | Layer | Attack | Countermeasure | |---|---|---| | Physical | Jamming | Spread spectrum (FHSS/DSSS), region-based mapping, frequency hopping | | Physical | Tampering | Tamper-proof hardware, self-destruction, key erasure on tamper detection | | MAC | Collision | Error-correcting codes (FEC), time-diversity retransmission | | MAC | Exhaustion | Rate-limiting RTS/CTS, MAC-layer authentication | | Network | Sinkhole/Wormhole | Geographic routing (GPSR), authentication of route metrics | | Network | Blackhole | Multi-path routing, watchdog monitors, reputation systems (e.g., CONFIDANT) | | Network | Sybil | Radio resource testing, registration at base station, random key predistribution | | Transport | Flooding | Client puzzles, SYN cookies adapted for 6LoWPAN | | Transport | Desynchronization | Authenticated headers (using $\mu$TESLA / IEEE 802.15.4 security suites) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Depletion Rate of a Sleep Deprivation Attack The **sleep deprivation** attack (a specific form of MAC-layer exhaustion DoS) keeps a victim sensor node awake by repeatedly forcing it to handle spurious traffic. We derive the time-to-death of the victim node. ### Step 1: Define the standard mota energy budget Let the node be powered by **2 AA alkaline cells** with initial energy: $$E_{initial} = V \cdot Q \cdot 3600 = 3.0 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 3600 = 27000 \text{ J}$$ (KTU reference: standard MICAz mote at 3.0 V, 2.5 Ah capacity.) ### Step 2: Define the per-state current draw The mote (Crossbow MICAz datasheet) consumes: - $I_{tx}$ = 17.4 mA (transmit) - $I_{rx}$ = 19.7 mA (receive) - $I_{idle}$ = 8.0 mA (CPU active, radio idle) - $I_{sleep}$ = 20 µA (deep sleep) ### Step 3: Compute per-state power $$P_{tx} = 3.0 \cdot 17.4 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.0522 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{rx} = 3.0 \cdot 19.7 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.0591 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{idle} = 3.0 \cdot 8.0 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.0240 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{sleep} = 3.0 \cdot 20 \cdot 10^{-6} = 0.00006 \text{ W}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the attack-induced energy drain Under a sleep deprivation attack, the victim is forced to remain in the $rx$ state for fraction $f_{rx}$ of the time. The attack energy drain per unit time is: $$P_{attack} = f_{rx} \cdot P_{rx} + f_{idle} \cdot P_{idle}$$ If the attacker forces 100% reception ($f_{rx} = 1.0$, $f_{idle} = 0$): $$P_{attack} = 1.0 \cdot 0.0591 + 0 \cdot 0.0240 = 0.0591 \text{ W}$$ ### Step 5: Compute the time to energy depletion $$t_{death} = \frac{E_{initial}}{P_{attack}} = \frac{27000}{0.0591} = 456852 \text{ seconds} \approx 5.29 \text{ days}$$ ### Step 6: Compute the time to depletion under normal duty cycling Normal duty cycle: 1% active ($f_{rx} = 0.005$, $f_{tx} = 0.005$, $f_{sleep} = 0.99$): $$P_{normal} = 0.005 \cdot 0.0591 + 0.005 \cdot 0.0522 + 0.99 \cdot 0.00006$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{normal} &= 0.0002955 + 0.000261 + 0.0000594 \\ &= 0.0006159 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ $$t_{normal} = \frac{27000}{0.0006159} = 43838310 \text{ s} \approx 507.4 \text{ days}$$ ### Step 7: Compute the lifetime reduction ratio $$\eta = \frac{t_{normal} - t_{death}}{t_{normal}} = \frac{507.4 - 5.29}{507.4} = 0.9896 \approx 98.96\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Insight:** A sleep-deprivation DoS attack reduces the WSN node's operational lifetime by **~99%**, taking the network from ~17 months of operation down to ~5 days. This is the most cited KTU numerical example for DoS attacks. ## 3.2 Derivation: Jammer Coverage Radius vs. Jammer Power For a constant jammer, the required transmit power to cover a radius $R$ follows the **log-distance path loss model**: $$P_{rx} = P_{tx} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^n$$ Where: - $P_{rx}$ = minimum signal strength required at the receiver to cause a DoS (typically $-85$ dBm for IEEE 802.15.4) - $P_{tx}$ = jammer transmit power - $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains (assume 1, i.e., 0 dBi) - $\lambda$ = wavelength (for 2.4 GHz: $\lambda \approx 0.125$ m) - $n$ = path loss exponent (free space: $n = 2$; outdoor foliage: $n \approx 3$) - $d$ = distance from jammer to victim ### Step 1: Solve for $P_{tx}$ $$P_{tx} = \frac{P_{rx}}{G_t \cdot G_r} \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^n$$ ### Step 2: Substitute $P_{rx} = -85$ dBm $= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11}$ W $$P_{tx} = 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi d}{0.125}\right)^2$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate at $d = 50$ m (typical outdoor WSN range), $n = 2$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{tx} &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 50}{0.125}\right)^2 \\ &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot (5026.5)^2 \\ &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot 2.527 \cdot 10^{7} \\ &= 7.98 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ W} \approx 0.8 \text{ mW} = -1 \text{ dBm} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate at $d = 500$ m, $n = 3$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{tx} &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 500}{0.125}\right)^3 \\ &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot (50265)^3 \\ &= 3.16 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot 1.27 \cdot 10^{14} \\ &= 4.01 \text{ W} \approx 36 \text{ dBm} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Insight:** A low-power jammer (1 mW) can disable a WSN at 50 m range. To jam at 500 m, the attacker must emit **4 W** continuously — feasible with a small SDR (e.g., USRP B200) but easily detected by spectrum analyzers. KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is constant jamming inefficient for the attacker?"* — Answer: because the attacker's high power consumption makes them locatable via direction-finding RF triangulation. ## 3.3 Derivation: Blackhole Impact on End-to-End PDR Consider a linear WSN: $N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to N_4 \to$ Sink. Suppose $N_2$ is a blackhole that drops all packets ($\alpha = 0$). ### Step 1: Per-hop success probability (no attack) $$P_{hop} = 1 - BER \approx 0.99 \text{ (for short-range IEEE 802.15.4 in clean spectrum)}$$ ### Step 2: Per-hop success probability with blackhole at $N_2$ $$P'_{hop,N_2} = 0 \text{ (full drop)}$$ ### Step 3: End-to-end PDR without attack $$PDR_{no\_attack} = P_{hop}^4 = 0.99^4 \approx 0.961$$ ### Step 4: End-to-end PDR with blackhole $$PDR_{bh} = P_{hop} \cdot 0 \cdot P_{hop} \cdot P_{hop} = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Insight:** A single blackhole in a 4-hop path causes **100% packet loss** at the sink, even though the attack only touches one node. This demonstrates the **outsized leverage** an internal attacker has in a multi-hop WSN. ## 3.4 Detection Threshold for Wormhole Attacks Wormholes are detected by measuring the round-trip time (RTT) of a packet and comparing to a maximum legitimate RTT bound. ### Step 1: Legitimate maximum RTT $$RTT_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{c} + 2 \cdot t_{proc}$$ For a WSN with $d_{max} = 100$ m, $t_{proc} = 0.1$ ms: $$RTT_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot 100}{3 \cdot 10^8} + 2 \cdot 0.0001 = 6.67 \cdot 10^{-7} + 2 \cdot 10^{-4} \approx 2 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$ ### Step 2: Wormhole RTT A wormhole uses an out-of-band link (e.g., wired Ethernet) of length $L_{tunnel}$. If $L_{tunnel} = 50$ m: $$RTT_{wh} = \frac{2 \cdot L_{tunnel}}{c \cdot v_{rel}} \text{ where } v_{rel} \text{ is relative propagation velocity}$$ For a wired link ($v_{rel} \approx 0.67c$ in copper): $$RTT_{wh} = \frac{2 \cdot 50}{0.67 \cdot 3 \cdot 10^8} = 4.97 \cdot 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$ ### Step 3: Detection condition Since $RTT_{wh} \ll RTT_{max}$, the wormhole succeeds in **bypassing distance checks** unless the detector uses geographic / position verification. This is why **geographic routing (GPSR)** is the canonical KTU defense. ## 3.5 The KTU Attack-Defense Analytical Matrix | Attack Layer | Attack | Defense | Quantitative Effect | |---|---|---|---| | Physical | Constant Jamming | Frequency Hopping (FHSS, 16 channels) | Reduces effective jammer power by factor of 16 | | Physical | Tampering | Tamper-evident enclosure | Adds **0.5 s** tamper-detection latency | | MAC | Collision | FEC (Hamming code rate 7,4) | Reduces BER from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ | | MAC | Exhaustion | Rate-limiting RTS | Caps DoS drain at **5%** of battery per day | | Network | Blackhole | Multi-path routing with 3 disjoint paths | PDR improves from **0% to $\approx 1 - 0.5^3$ = 87.5%** | | Network | Sinkhole | Authentication of route advertisements | Eliminates **100%** of spoofed sink advertisements | | Network | Wormhole | Geographic packet leashes | Catches wormholes exceeding **200 m** tunnel length | | Network | Sybil | Random key pre-distribution | Reduces Sybil voting power by **factor of $n/k$** | | Transport | Flooding | Client puzzles (Hashcash-style) | Adds **1 s** CPU cost per SYN; attacker can sustain only **1 SYN/s** | | Transport | Desynchronization | Authenticated sequence numbers | Eliminates **100%** of spoofed FIN/RST frames | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. List any three DoS attacks targeting the network layer.** **Model Answer:** A Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack in a Wireless Sensor Network is a deliberate attempt by an adversary to disrupt, exhaust, or render unavailable the resources of the WSN — including battery energy, radio bandwidth, computation, and memory — thereby preventing the network from delivering its expected sensing and communication services with the desired Quality of Service (QoS). **Three network-layer DoS attacks:** 1. **Blackhole attack** — A compromised node drops every data packet it is supposed to forward. 2. **Sinkhole attack** — The adversary advertises a high-quality (low-cost, high-bandwidth) route to the sink, attracting all traffic through itself, then drops or tampers with it. 3. **Wormhole attack** — Two colluding adversaries create a low-latency out-of-band tunnel that replays packets at a distant location, subverting routing. *(Valid alternatives: Hello Flood, Sybil, Selective Forwarding, Homing)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: What is Jamming? Differentiate between constant jamming and reactive jamming.** **Model Answer:** **Jamming** is a Physical-layer DoS attack in which an adversary deliberately transmits radio frequency (RF) energy in the WSN's operating band (typically the 2.4 GHz ISM band for IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee) to disrupt legitimate wireless communication. | Aspect | Constant Jamming | Reactive Jamming | |---|---|---| | **Transmission Pattern** | Emits RF signal continuously, regardless of whether legitimate traffic exists | Emits jamming signal **only** when it senses an ongoing legitimate transmission on the channel | | **Attacker Energy Cost** | High (always-on) | Low (transmits selectively) | | **Detection Probability** | High (always-visible RF signature) | Low (silent most of the time) | | **Efficiency** | Low (wastes attacker power) | High (precise targeting) | | **Countermeasure Difficulty** | Easy to detect via spectrum sensing | Hard to detect — appears only briefly | --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module 3 Internal Choice):** Students must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Each choice has two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain in detail the various Denial-of-Service attacks possible at the Physical and MAC layers of a WSN. For each attack, mention one specific countermeasure.** **Model Solution:** ### A1. Physical Layer DoS Attacks **(i) Jamming** - The attacker transmits a high-power RF signal in the WSN's frequency band (2.4 GHz for IEEE 802.15.4). - Categories: *Constant, Deceptive, Random, Reactive* jammers. - Effect: Legitimate nodes cannot decode incoming packets because $SNR_{jam} \ge 1$. - **Countermeasure:** Spread spectrum (FHSS with 16 channels in IEEE 802.15.4) makes the jammer have to cover a wider bandwidth, reducing its effective power per channel by a factor of 16. **(ii) Tampering / Node Capture** - The attacker physically accesses a deployed node, extracts cryptographic keys from its memory (via JTAG or hardware probing), and may even replace the node with a malicious one. - Effect: Compromises all cryptographic security downstream of that node. - **Countermeasure:** Tamper-proof hardware enclosures with self-erasing memory on tamper detection. **(iii) Node Jamming via Eavesdropping** - Attacker records ongoing transmissions and replays them with destructive interference. - **Countermeasure:** Time-varying channel hopping and message authentication codes (MACs). ### A2. MAC Layer DoS Attacks **(i) Collision** - The attacker transmits a packet at the exact moment a legitimate node is transmitting, causing the CRC to fail and forcing retransmission. - **Countermeasure:** Forward Error Correction (FEC) codes (e.g., Hamming 7,4) recover corrupted bits; time diversity (retransmit at random backoff). **(ii) Exhaustion** - The attacker sends repeated Request-To-Send (RTS) frames, forcing victims to remain in continuous receive mode. - Effect: Battery drains rapidly; legitimate nodes cannot sleep. - **Countermeasure:** Rate-limit RTS frames per sender; require MAC-layer authentication before accepting RTS. **(iii) Unfairness** - Attacker intermittently captures the channel by abusing MAC fairness protocols (e.g., S-MAC's time-slot allocation). - **Countermeasure:** Slot rotation enforced by cryptographic tokens; small, fair contention windows. **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** - Physical layer attacks with 3 attack types & defenses: **3.5 marks** - MAC layer attacks with 3 attack types & defenses: **3.5 marks** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A WSN is deployed for forest fire monitoring. Each node is powered by 2 AA batteries (3.0 V, 2.5 Ah). A reactive jammer is targeting the network, forcing nodes to remain in receive mode 80% of the time. Compute the network lifetime under attack and compare it with the normal duty-cycled lifetime. Use the standard MICAz energy model: $I_{tx} = 17.4$ mA, $I_{rx} = 19.7$ mA, $I_{idle} = 8.0$ mA, $I_{sleep} = 20$ µA. Assume normal duty cycle: 1% active, 99% sleep.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Initial Energy** $$E_{initial} = V \cdot Q = 3.0 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 3600 = 27000 \text{ J}$$ **Step 2: Power under Attack** Attack forces $f_{rx} = 0.8$, $f_{idle} = 0.2$ (rest): $$P_{attack} = 0.8 \cdot (3.0 \cdot 0.0197) + 0.2 \cdot (3.0 \cdot 0.008)$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{attack} &= 0.8 \cdot 0.0591 + 0.2 \cdot 0.024 \\ &= 0.04728 + 0.0048 \\ &= 0.05208 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Lifetime under Attack** $$t_{attack} = \frac{27000}{0.05208} = 518434 \text{ s} \approx 6.0 \text{ days}$$ **Step 4: Power under Normal Duty Cycle** Normal: $f_{rx} = 0.005$, $f_{tx} = 0.005$, $f_{sleep} = 0.99$: $$P_{normal} = 0.005 \cdot 0.0591 + 0.005 \cdot 0.0522 + 0.99 \cdot 0.00006$$ $$P_{normal} = 0.0002955 + 0.000261 + 0.0000594 = 0.0006159 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5: Normal Lifetime** $$t_{normal} = \frac{27000}{0.0006159} = 43838310 \text{ s} \approx 507 \text{ days}$$ **Step 6: Comparison** $$\frac{t_{normal}}{t_{attack}} = \frac{507}{6.0} \approx 84.5 \times$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The reactive jammer reduces the WSN's lifetime by a factor of **~85**, making the deployment economically unviable for a long-term forest monitoring application. This justifies the use of **reactive jamming countermeasures** like *channel hopping, rate limiting, and authentication of frames*. --- ### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Blackhole, Sinkhole, and Wormhole attacks on the network layer of a WSN. How do multi-path routing and geographic routing defend against these attacks?** **Model Solution:** ### B1. Blackhole Attack - A compromised node advertises itself as having the shortest path to the sink and drops every packet it receives. - **Diagram: Node A → Sink** routed through a malicious node B, which silently drops packets. - **Effect:** End-to-end PDR = 0% on paths through the blackhole. ### B2. Sinkhole Attack - The adversary (Node M) advertises a high-quality, fictitious route (e.g., zero-cost) to the sink. - All neighboring nodes, deceived, route their data through M. - M can then launch a *combined* attack — drop packets (blackhole) or selectively forward, inject false data, or partition the network. - **Effect:** Total traffic capture by one node. ### B3. Wormhole Attack - Two adversarial nodes (W1 and W2) collaborate via a low-latency out-of-band link (e.g., Ethernet, high-power RF). - W1 receives a Route Request (RREQ) from a source, tunnels it to W2, and W2 rebroadcasts it near the sink. - The tunneled RREQ appears to be a *very short path*, so the source picks the wormhole as the best route. - **Effect:** All traffic is funneled through the adversaries' out-of-band link, which they can monitor, modify, or drop. ### B4. Defenses **(i) Multi-Path Routing Defense (against Blackhole):** - The source sends each packet along **$k$ disjoint paths** simultaneously. - If any path has a blackhole, the others succeed. - Recovery: $PDR_{recovered} = 1 - (1 - PDR_{single})^k$. For $k=3$ and $PDR_{single} = 0.5$: $PDR_{recovered} = 0.875$. **(ii) Geographic Routing (against Sinkhole & Wormhole):** - **GPSR (Greedy Perimeter Stateless Routing):** Each packet header contains the destination's geographic coordinates. Forwarders only relay to neighbors that are *strictly closer* to the destination. - A wormhole that tunnels packets across a long distance cannot beat geographic distance checks, and is rejected. - Sinkholes fail because neighbors verify the *actual* geographic progress of advertised routes. **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** - Blackhole explanation with effect: **2 marks** - Sinkhole explanation with effect: **2 marks** - Wormhole explanation with effect: **2 marks** - Defenses: **1 mark** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: What is a Sybil attack? A WSN has 10 legitimate nodes and an attacker injects 5 fake Sybil identities. What fraction of the voting power does the attacker control? Propose two defenses.** **Model Solution:** ### B5. Sybil Attack Definition A **Sybil attack** is a network-layer DoS attack in which a single malicious node forges multiple fake identities (Sybil nodes) to gain disproportionate influence in distributed operations such as voting, routing, data aggregation, or reputation systems. ### B6. Voting Power Calculation With $n = 10$ legitimate nodes and $k = 5$ Sybil identities: $$V_{compromised} = \frac{k}{n + k} = \frac{5}{10 + 5} = \frac{5}{15} = 0.333 \text{ or } 33.3\%$$ The attacker controls **one-third** of the total voting power from a single physical device. In some systems (e.g., Byzantine fault tolerance requires $< 33\%$ malicious), this is *just below* the critical threshold — adding even one more Sybil identity would breach it. ### B7. Two Defenses **(i) Radio Resource Testing:** - Each node is assigned a unique radio channel or time-slot. Sybil identities would need to transmit on multiple channels simultaneously, which is impossible with one physical radio. - Limitation: Requires deterministic channel assignment (not random MAC). **(ii) Random Key Pre-Distribution:** - Each legitimate node is pre-loaded with $m$ keys out of a pool of $P$ keys (Eschenauer-Gligor scheme). - A Sybil node would need to share a key with every other node, which becomes statistically impossible as $P$ grows large relative to $m$. - Detection: A Sybil node will fail pairwise key validation with most legitimate nodes. **Mark Distribution:** - Sybil definition: **1.5 marks** - Voting power calculation: **2.5 marks** - Defense 1: **1.5 marks** - Defense 2: **1.5 marks** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on DoS Attack questions:** > > 1. **Confusing DoS attacks with active eavesdropping** — DoS attacks *disrupt* service; eavesdropping *passively violates* confidentiality. They are different threat classes. (Lose 1–2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting to specify the layer** of the attack in the answer — KTU examiners award marks for *layer-classified* explanations. (Lose 1 mark) > 3. **Not pairing attacks with defenses** — A common 2024 scheme instruction is *"For each attack, mention a countermeasure."* Omitting this costs 50% of the sub-question marks. > 4. **Numerical errors in energy calculations** — Watch unit conversions: $mAh \rightarrow Ah \rightarrow C \rightarrow J$ must be done explicitly. KTU examiners will not award partial credit for missing the **3600** conversion factor. > 5. **Writing "Jamming" without classifying its type** — Always specify *constant / deceptive / random / reactive* in the answer. > 6. **Confusing Wormhole with Sinkhole** — Wormholes use *out-of-band tunnels*; sinkholes use *fabricated route advertisements*. Examiners deduct marks if these are conflated. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):** - ☐ DoS = *any event that diminishes or eliminates a network's capacity to perform its expected function* (Wood & Stankovic). - ☐ WSN vulnerabilities stem from: **wireless broadcast medium + resource-constrained motes + unattended deployment + multi-hop dependency**. - ☐ **Physical layer DoS:** Jamming (4 types), Tampering, Node Capture. - ☐ **MAC layer DoS:** Collision, Exhaustion, Unfairness. - ☐ **Network layer DoS:** Blackhole, Sinkhole, Wormhole, Selective Forwarding, Hello Flood, Sybil, Homing. - ☐ **Transport layer DoS:** Flooding, Desynchronization. - ☐ **Application layer DoS:** Clock Skewing, Path-based, Data Corruption. - ☐ **Sleep deprivation attack** can reduce node lifetime from ~507 days to ~5–6 days (~99% reduction). - ☐ **Jammer SNR $\ge 1$** is the condition for successful jamming. - ☐ **Blackhole in a 4-hop path** → end-to-end PDR = 0%. - ☐ **Sybil voting power** = $\frac{k}{n+k}$. - ☐ **Multi-path routing** with $k$ disjoint paths gives $PDR_{recovered} = 1 - (1-PDR_{single})^k$. - ☐ **Geographic routing (GPSR)** is the canonical defense against sinkhole and wormhole. - ☐ **Random key pre-distribution (Eschenauer-Gligor)** is the canonical defense against Sybil. - ☐ **Spread spectrum (FHSS, 16 channels in IEEE 802.15.4)** is the canonical defense against narrowband jamming. - ☐ **Multi-path + Geographic + Authenticated routing + Random key predistribution** = the four pillars of KTU 2024 WSN DoS defense. - ☐ MICAz reference mote currents: $I_{tx}=17.4$ mA, $I_{rx}=19.7$ mA, $I_{idle}=8.0$ mA, $I_{sleep}=20$ µA at 3.0 V. - ☐ Standard cell: 2 AA alkaline, 3.0 V, 2.5 Ah → $E_{initial} = 27000$ J. - ☐ Always cite the **attack layer** and pair **attack-defense** in KTU 2024 answers for full marks.

topics.title: Physical Layer Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, the **Physical Layer** corresponds to Layer-1 of the OSI reference model. It is responsible for the actual **transmission and reception of raw bit-streams** over a wireless medium using radio frequency (RF), optical, or acoustic signals. Sensor motes employ low-power transceivers (e.g., **CC2420** operating in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**) and are typically constrained by limited battery energy and an open, broadcast air-interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Layer Attacks** are malicious actions that target the hardware, the wireless transmission medium, or the physical signal properties (frequency, amplitude, phase) of a WSN, with the intent of disrupting, degrading, intercepting, or completely destroying the communication capability of the network.

Because the wireless medium is inherently **shared, open, and accessible to anyone within radio range**, the physical layer is the **most exposed layer** of the entire WSN protocol stack. An adversary does not require logical credentials to launch a physical-layer attack — mere proximity and inexpensive hardware (e.g., a **USRP software-defined radio** or even a **jamming-capable Arduino + RF module**) are sufficient.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Whispering in a Crowded Hall" Model**
>
> Imagine a large, open hall where many people are whispering secret messages to one another using walkie-talkies. There is **no wall, no door, no lock** between them and the outside world. Now consider a few malicious individuals entering the hall:
>
> 1. A person with a **loud noise generator (jamming)** — they don't need to know the secret; they just shout so loudly that no one can hear the whispers anymore.
> 2. A **sneaky listener with a sensitive microphone (eavesdropping)** — they quietly record every whisper without the speakers ever knowing.
> 3. A person who **physically picks up a walkie-talkie, opens it, and tampers with its circuit board (tampering / node capture)** — they steal the device's identity and keys.
> 4. A person who **floods the room with fake whispers mimicking legitimate users (replay / signal injection)**.
>
> All these attacks happen at the *physical whisper level* — the messages never even reach the "language rules" (MAC) or "meaning" (application) levels. This is exactly what happens in WSNs at the physical layer.

## 1.3 Why the Physical Layer is Uniquely Vulnerable in WSNs

| WSN Characteristic | Consequence for Physical Layer |
|---|---|
| **Unattended deployment** (forests, battlefields, oceans) | Adversary has **physical access** to motes for tampering. |
| **Battery-powered motes** | Limited energy budget makes **jamming extremely effective** (defender drains battery responding). |
| **Broadcast wireless medium** | Any RF-capable device in range is a potential **eavesdropper or jammer**. |
| **Harsh environments** | Hard to detect **physical tampering** or replacement of nodes. |
| **Low-cost hardware** | No tamper-proof chips, no shielding, no **hardware cryptographic accelerators**. |

## 1.4 Standard Physical-Layer Threat Metrics

The following metrics are universally used in KTU/WSN literature to quantify physical-layer attacks:

- **Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR)** — expressed in **dB**, indicates how strong the jammer's power is relative to the legitimate signal.
- **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** — fraction of packets successfully received, drops sharply under attack.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** — increases under jamming, interference, and noise injection.
- **Energy Consumption per Bit (E_b)** — measured in **Joules/bit (J/bit)**, rises under DoS conditions.
- **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** — used to detect anomalies like constant high-power noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):**
> Physical layer attacks are a *foundational security topic* in WSNs. Examiners frequently test: (a) classification of attacks, (b) jamming model mathematics, and (c) countermeasures such as **spread spectrum, FHSS/DSSS, and node tampering detection**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Physical Layer Attacks

Physical layer attacks in WSNs can be organized into **four primary categories** based on the adversary's objective and mode of action:

### Category A — **Interference / Jamming Attacks**
The adversary radiates RF energy into the wireless medium to **degrade or block** legitimate communication.

1. **Constant Jammer** — emits a continuous radio signal (Gaussian noise) on the channel.
2. **Deceptive Jammer** — constantly injects *legitimate-looking* packets (valid headers, valid preambles) so that the victim nodes remain in "receive mode" and never notice the attack.
3. **Random Jammer** — alternates between sleeping and jamming in a probabilistic pattern (energy-saving attack strategy).
4. **Reactive Jammer** — stays silent until it senses legitimate traffic, then jams the packet *as soon as it detects the preamble*. This is the **most energy-efficient and hardest-to-detect** jammer.

### Category B — **Eavesdropping / Passive Interception**
- The adversary uses a sensitive receiver to **silently listen** to all RF traffic in the area.
- WSNs are particularly vulnerable because encryption is often disabled to save energy.

### Category C — **Tampering and Node Capture**
- The adversary **physically accesses** a sensor node, removes it, extracts cryptographic keys from memory, reprograms it, and reintroduces it (or a clone) into the network.
- Once captured, the node becomes an **insider adversary** capable of launching higher-layer attacks.

### Category D — **Physical-Layer Signal Manipulation**
- **Node Destruction / Wipeout** — physically damaging the mote (e.g., destroying a forest-deployed node with the intent of losing forest-fire data).
- **Battery Exhaustion Attack** — forcing the mote to drain its battery (often via continuous retransmissions triggered by the jammer).

## 2.2 The Wireless Propagation Model (Foundation for Attack Analysis)

To understand the impact of jamming, the received signal power at any legitimate receiver follows the **Log-Distance Path Loss Model**:

$$P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

Where:
- $P_r(d)$ = received power (in **dBm**) at distance $d$
- $P_t$ = transmit power of the legitimate sender (**dBm**)
- $PL(d_0)$ = path loss at the reference distance $d_0$ (typically **d_0 = 1 m**)
- $n$ = **path-loss exponent** (free-space: $n=2$, urban: $n=3$ to $4$)
- $X_\sigma$ = shadow fading, a **zero-mean Gaussian random variable** with standard deviation $\sigma$ in **dB**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for attacks:** A jammer placed closer to a receiver (smaller $d$) achieves a *disproportionately large* $P_r$ because of the $10n\log_{10}(d)$ term. This is why a low-power **proximity jammer** is more dangerous than a high-power distant one.

## 2.3 Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR) and Bit Error Probability

The **Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR)** at the receiver is given by:

$$\text{SINR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{jammer}} + P_{\text{noise}}}$$

In **dB** form:

$$\text{SINR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{jammer}} + P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

The **Bit Error Rate (BER)** for non-coherent BFSK modulation under AWGN + jamming is approximately:

$$P_e \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-\frac{E_b}{2 \cdot (N_0 + J_0)}}$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = energy per bit (**Joules**)
- $N_0$ = noise power spectral density (**W/Hz**)
- $J_0$ = jammer power spectral density (**W/Hz**)

The **Jamming Margin (JM)** — the additional power a jammer needs to disrupt communication — is:

$$\text{JM}_{\text{dB}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{legitimate}} - \text{SNR}_{\text{required}} + \text{Processing Gain (PG)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
