| 1 | Received Power (Log-Distance) | $P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d_0) - 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$ | **dBm** | Predicts signal falloff; basis of jamming range analysis |
| 2 | Free-Space Path Loss | $PL_{FS} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **dB** | Loss for $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 3 | SINR | $\text{SINR} = P_s / (P_j + P_n)$ | dimensionless (or **dB**) | Receiver's quality metric; below threshold → packet loss |
| 4 | Bit Error Rate (BFSK) | $P_e \approx 0.5 \cdot \exp[-E_b / 2(N_0 + J_0)]$ | dimensionless | Probability of bit corruption |
| 5 | Jamming Margin | $\text{JM} = \text{SNR}_{leg} - \text{SNR}_{req} + \text{PG}$ | **dB** | Adversary's required extra power to jam |
| 6 | Processing Gain (Spread Spectrum) | $\text{PG} = 10 \log_{10}(W / R)$ | **dB** | $W$ = spread bandwidth, $R$ = data rate |
| 7 | Probability of Detection (Reactive Jammer) | $P_d = 1 - (1 - p)^{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $p$ = per-sample detection, $N_s$ = samples |
| 8 | Energy to Jam 1 bit | $E_{jam} = P_j \cdot (1/R)$ | **Joules/bit** | Adversary's per-bit energy cost |
| 9 | Shannon Capacity under Jamming | $C = W \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$ | **bits/sec** | Max link throughput while jammed |
| 10 | Threshold Distance for Jamming | $d_{\text{jam}} = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_t - P_{r,\min})/(10n)}$ | **meters** | Max distance at which jammer is effective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Note the difference between **PG** (Processing Gain, a defender's tool) and **JM** (Jamming Margin, the attacker's required extra power). A higher PG makes the system *harder to jam*.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Physical-layer attack analysis is not merely academic. It directly informs:

- **Military Communications** (anti-jam tactical radios, e.g., **Have Quick, Link 16**).
- **Civilian IoT Security Standards** (Zigbee, LoRaWAN, BLE all build in frequency hopping to defeat jammers).
- **Anti-Drone / RF-Denial Systems** in airports and prisons.
- **Critical Infrastructure Protection** (smart grids, water-treatment SCADA systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Jamming Distance

**Problem Setup:** A legitimate WSN transmitter sends at $P_t = 0$ **dBm** (= 1 mW). The receiver requires a minimum $P_{r,\min} = -90$ **dBm** to decode the packet. A jammer radiates constant power $P_t$ on the same channel. Using the log-distance model with $n = 3$ (typical urban/indoor WSN) and $d_0 = 1$ m, $PL(d_0) = 40$ **dB**, find the **maximum distance** at which the jammer can successfully disrupt communication.

### Step 1 — Write the log-distance received power formula

$$P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

For analysis we drop the shadow fading term $X_\sigma$ (it is averaged out).

### Step 2 — Identify the threshold condition for successful disruption

The jammer succeeds when its received power **exceeds** the receiver's sensitivity:

$$P_r(d_{\text{jam}}) \geq P_{r,\min}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute known values

$$P_t - PL(d_0) - 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_{\text{jam}}}{d_0}\right) = P_{r,\min}$$

$$0 - 40 - 10 \cdot 3 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_{\text{jam}}}{1}\right) = -90$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the constants

$$-40 - 30 \cdot \log_{10}(d_{\text{jam}}) = -90$$

$$-30 \cdot \log_{10}(d_{\text{jam}}) = -90 + 40 = -50$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{\text{jam}}) = \frac{-50}{-30} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $d_{\text{jam}}$ by exponentiating base 10

$$d_{\text{jam}} = 10^{5/3} \approx 46.42 \text{ meters}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{jam}} \approx 46.4 \text{ m}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** A jammer broadcasting at only **1 mW** can successfully disrupt legitimate WSN communication out to a radius of about **46 meters** in an urban environment. This demonstrates that physical-layer attacks are *low-cost, high-impact* threats.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Jammer Power for a Given SINR Threshold

**Problem Setup:** A WSN receiver needs a minimum SINR of $\text{SINR}_{\min} = 10$ **dB** to decode a packet. The legitimate transmitter delivers $P_{\text{signal}} = -70$ **dBm** at the receiver, and the ambient noise floor is $P_{\text{noise}} = -100$ **dBm**. Find the minimum jammer power $P_j$ (in dBm) the adversary must transmit to drop the SINR below 10 dB.

### Step 1 — Convert all powers to linear (Watts) for arithmetic

$$P_{\text{signal}} = 10^{-70/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-7} \text{ mW} = 10^{-10} \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{noise}} = 10^{-100/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-13} \text{ W}$$

The SINR threshold in linear scale:

$$\text{SINR}_{\min, \text{lin}} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

### Step 2 — Express the SINR inequality

$$\text{SINR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_j + P_{\text{noise}}} \leq \text{SINR}_{\min, \text{lin}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{10^{-10}}{P_j + 10^{-13}} \leq 10$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the jammer power

$$10^{-10} \leq 10 \cdot (P_j + 10^{-13})$$

$$10^{-11} \leq P_j + 10^{-13}$$

$$P_j \geq 10^{-11} - 10^{-13}$$

$$P_j \geq 0.99 \times 10^{-11} \approx 9.9 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W}$$

### Step 4 — Convert back to dBm

$$P_{j,\text{dBm}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.9 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(9.9 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$P_{j,\text{dBm}} \approx 10 \cdot (-8.004) \approx -80 \text{ dBm}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,P_{j,\min} \approx -80 \text{ dBm} \approx 10 \text{ pW}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** A jammer emitting a mere **10 picowatts** of RF power is sufficient to disrupt a legitimate WSN link delivering $-70$ dBm. This is the *practical reason* why jamming is the **#1 physical-layer threat** in WSNs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Attack → Mechanism → Countermeasure

| Attack Type | Mechanism (What the Adversary Does) | Targeted Property | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Jammer** | Emits continuous RF noise | Spectrum occupancy | **Spread Spectrum** (DSSS/FHSS), channel surfing |
| **Deceptive Jammer** | Injects valid-looking packets | MAC state machine | Jamming detection via **PDR / RSSI** monitoring |
| **Reactive Jammer** | Listens for preamble, then jams | Transmission timing | **Packet fragmentation, randomized back-off** |
| **Eavesdropping** | Passive RF sniffing | Confidentiality | **Link-layer encryption** (AES-128 in IEEE 802.15.4) |
| **Tampering** | Physical access → extract keys | Hardware, key storage | **Tamper-proof packaging, PUFs (Physically Unclonable Functions)** |
| **Node Capture / Cloning** | Replicate node identity | Authentication | **Pairwise key establishment, node revocation protocols** |
| **Battery Exhaustion** | Forcing retransmissions | Energy | **Rate limiting, duty cycling** |
| **Node Destruction** | Physical damage | Availability | **Stealth deployment, redundant nodes** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — BER under Jamming

A WSN link uses BFSK with $E_b = 10^{-9}$ **J/bit**, the noise PSD is $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ **W/Hz**, and the jammer delivers a wideband $J_0 = 10^{-19}$ **W/Hz** into the channel. Compute the BER.

### Step 1 — Apply the BFSK non-coherent BER formula

$$P_e \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2 \cdot (N_0 + J_0)}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$P_e \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{10^{-9}}{2 \cdot (10^{-20} + 10^{-19})}\right)$$

$$P_e \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{10^{-9}}{2 \cdot 1.1 \times 10^{-19}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate exponent

$$\frac{10^{-9}}{2.2 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{1}{2.2} \times 10^{10} \approx 4.545 \times 10^{9}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the exponential

$$\exp(-4.545 \times 10^{9}) \approx 0$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,P_e \approx 0.5\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The jammer's power is so high that the BER approaches **50%** — the channel is essentially useless. Communication has been completely denied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Physical Layer Attacks in WSN

The following **Mermaid block diagram** provides a high-level functional taxonomy of all physical-layer attacks discussed in this module, mapping each attack to its primary mechanism and corresponding defense.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WSN Physical Layer Attack] --> B[Active Attack]
    A --> C[Passive Attack]

    B --> B1[Jamming]
    B1 --> B1a[Constant Jammer]
    B1 --> B1b[Deceptive Jammer]
    B1 --> B1c[Random Jammer]
    B1 --> B1d[Reactive Jammer]

    B --> B2[Tampering]
    B2 --> B2a[Key Extraction]
    B2 --> B2b[Code Modification]

    B --> B3[Node Capture]
    B3 --> B3a[Physical Removal]
    B3 --> B3b[Cloning and Reintroduction]

    B --> B4[Battery Exhaustion]
    B --> B5[Node Destruction]

    C --> C1[Eavesdropping]
    C --> C2[Traffic Analysis]

    B1a --> D1[Defense Spread Spectrum DSSS FHSS]
    B1b --> D1
    B1c --> D1
    B1d --> D2[Defense Channel Surfing and Low Duty Cycle]
    B2a --> D3[Defense Tamper Proof Hardware and PUF]
    B2b --> D3
    B3a --> D4[Defense Pairwise Key and Revocation]
    B3b --> D4
    B4 --> D5[Defense Rate Limiting and Retransmission Cap]
    B5 --> D6[Defense Stealth Deployment and Redundancy]
    C1 --> D7[Defense AES 128 Link Layer Encryption]
    C2 --> D7
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Jamming Attack

The following diagram models the **functional data flow** of a constant-jamming attack on a WSN link, from the jammer's perspective to the receiver's packet-decoding failure.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src[Legitimate Source Node] -->|Tx Power Pt| Chan[Wireless Channel 2.4 GHz]
    Jam[Jammer Adversary] -->|High Power Pj| Chan
    Noise[Ambient Noise Floor N0] --> Chan
    Chan --> Rcv[Legitimate Receiver Node]
    Rcv --> Demod[Demodulator and Decoder]
    Demod --> Sinr{SINR Greater Than Threshold}
    Sinr -->|Yes| Pkt[Packet Accepted]
    Sinr -->|No| Drop[Packet Dropped DoS]
    Rcv --> Mon[RSSI and PDR Monitor]
    Mon --> Det{Jamming Detected}
    Det -->|Yes| Alt[Switch Channel FHSS Activate]
    Det -->|No| Continue[Normal Operation]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Layering for the Physical Layer

```mermaid
graph TD
    Outer[Outer Defense Stealth Deployment and Tamper Proof Housing] --> Inner1[Inner Defense Frequency Hopping FHSS]
    Inner1 --> Inner2[Inner Defense Spread Spectrum DSSS]
    Inner2 --> Inner3[Inner Defense AES 128 Link Layer Encryption]
    Inner3 --> Inner4[Inner Defense Intrusion Detection via RSSI and PDR]
    Inner4 --> Inner5[Inner Defense Node Revocation Protocol]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** No *single* physical-layer defense is sufficient. Effective WSN security against physical-layer attacks requires **defense-in-depth** — multiple overlapping mechanisms (spread spectrum + encryption + detection + revocation). The Mermaid diagrams above present a structural blueprint of this layered philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Physical Layer Attack in WSN. List any four categories of physical layer attacks with one-line descriptions.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A *Physical Layer Attack* in a Wireless Sensor Network is a malicious act targeting the hardware, wireless transmission medium, or signal properties (frequency, amplitude, phase) to disrupt, intercept, or destroy network communication.

Four categories:

1. **Jamming** — adversary radiates RF energy to block the channel.
2. **Eavesdropping** — adversary passively sniffs legitimate traffic.
3. **Tampering** — adversary physically accesses a node to extract keys or modify firmware.
4. **Node Capture / Cloning** — adversary removes a node, harvests its credentials, and reintroduces a clone into the network.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a Constant Jammer and a Reactive Jammer.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Constant Jammer | Reactive Jammer |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating behavior** | Emits noise continuously on the channel | Silent until it senses a legitimate preamble; then jams |
| **Energy efficiency** | Low — wastes energy during idle periods | High — only active during real traffic |
| **Detectability** | High (constantly high RSSI on the channel) | Low (RSSI looks normal until attack window) |
| **Countermeasure** | Frequency hopping, spread spectrum | Channel surfing, packet fragmentation, randomized back-off |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **(a)** With neat functional diagrams, explain the four major categories of physical layer attacks in Wireless Sensor Networks. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A legitimate transmitter in a WSN delivers a signal of $-70$ dBm at the receiver. The receiver's sensitivity is $-90$ dBm, the ambient noise is $-100$ dBm, and the minimum required SINR is 10 dB. Compute the minimum jammer power (in dBm and Watts) required to disrupt the link. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The four major categories of physical-layer attacks in WSN are:

1. **Jamming Attacks** — The adversary radiates RF noise on the WSN channel. A *Constant Jammer* emits uninterrupted noise; a *Deceptive Jammer* injects valid-looking packets; a *Reactive Jammer* waits for a legitimate preamble and strikes only then. Effect: SINR collapse, PDR → 0.

2. **Eavesdropping / Passive Interception** — A sensitive receiver silently logs all RF traffic. Since the WSN medium is broadcast and many motes disable encryption to save energy, this attack is devastatingly simple. Effect: confidentiality breach.

3. **Tampering and Node Capture** — The adversary physically accesses an unattended sensor node, extracts cryptographic keys from flash memory, reprograms the device, and either destroys it or reintroduces a *clone* into the network. Effect: full compromise of the node and its credentials.

4. **Physical-Layer Signal Manipulation** — Includes *node destruction* (irreversible hardware damage), *battery exhaustion* (forcing retransmissions until power is drained), and *RF signal injection* (sending malformed waveforms that confuse the receiver's front-end).

(Diagrams already provided in SECTION 4 of these notes serve as reference.)

**Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks per category × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for diagram/coherence.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{signal}} = -70$ dBm
- $P_{\text{noise}} = -100$ dBm
- $\text{SINR}_{\min} = 10$ dB
- Find: minimum jammer power $P_j$

**Step 1 — Convert to linear (Watts)**

$$P_{\text{signal}} = 10^{-70/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-10} \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{noise}} = 10^{-100/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-13} \text{ W}$$

$$\text{SINR}_{\min, \text{lin}} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Set up the SINR inequality**

$$\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_j + P_{\text{noise}}} \leq 10$$

$$\frac{10^{-10}}{P_j + 10^{-13}} \leq 10$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_j$**

$$10^{-10} \leq 10 \cdot P_j + 10 \cdot 10^{-13}$$

$$10^{-10} - 10^{-12} \leq 10 \cdot P_j$$

$$9.9 \times 10^{-11} \leq 10 \cdot P_j$$

$$P_j \geq 9.9 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dBm**

$$P_{j,\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.9 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(9.9 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$\boxed{P_{j,\text{dBm}} \approx -80 \text{ dBm} \approx 9.9 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 9.9 \text{ pW}}$$

**Mark Distribution:** Step 1: 1.5, Step 2: 1.5, Step 3: 2, Step 4: 1, Final boxed answer: 1.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the Spread Spectrum (DSSS and FHSS) technique as a defense against physical layer jamming attacks. Derive the expression for **Processing Gain (PG)** and explain its role in Jamming Margin. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the following with respect to WSN physical-layer security: (i) Tamper-proof hardware, (ii) Physically Unclonable Functions (PUF), (iii) Node revocation. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Spread Spectrum (SS)** is a modulation technique in which the transmitted signal is spread over a bandwidth $W$ much larger than the minimum required to carry the data rate $R$. This makes the signal *resistant* to narrow-band and partial-band jamming.

- **DSSS (Direct Sequence SS):** Each data bit is multiplied by a high-rate pseudo-random **chip sequence** (e.g., length $N_c$ chips per bit). The signal becomes a wideband, low-power-density waveform.
- **FHSS (Frequency Hopping SS):** The carrier frequency *hops* pseudo-randomly across many channels, dwelling on each for a short period. A jammer must either jam all frequencies (very expensive) or guess the next hop (computationally infeasible).

**Derivation of Processing Gain (PG):**

The processing gain is the ratio of spread bandwidth to data rate:

$$\text{PG} = \frac{W}{R}$$

In **dB**:

$$\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{R}\right)$$

**Role in Jamming Margin (JM):**

The **Jamming Margin** is the adversary's *required* excess power to overcome the spread-spectrum defense:

$$\text{JM}_{\text{dB}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{legitimate}} - \text{SNR}_{\text{required}} + \text{PG}_{\text{dB}}$$

Higher PG ⇒ higher JM ⇒ the system is *harder to jam*. For example, IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee) uses DSSS with $W = 2$ MHz and $R = 250$ kbps, giving:

$$\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{6} / 250 \times 10^{3}) = 10 \log_{10}(8) \approx 9 \text{ dB}$$

So a Zigbee link effectively requires a jammer to be **9 dB stronger** than for an equivalent narrow-band link.

**Mark Distribution:** DSSS explanation: 1.5, FHSS explanation: 1.5, PG derivation: 2, JM role + numerical: 2.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Tamper-proof hardware:** Sensor motes deployed in hostile environments are encased in *tamper-resistant enclosures* (e.g., epoxy potting, shielded ICs, mesh sensors). Any attempt to physically open the case triggers the erasure of cryptographic keys (zeroization). This protects against *tampering* and *key extraction* attacks. **2.3 marks**

**(ii) Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs):** A PUF exploits **inherent manufacturing variations** in silicon (e.g., gate delays, threshold voltages) to produce a unique, unpredictable, device-specific response when queried with a challenge. Two PUFs, even from the same wafer, produce different responses. PUFs are used for **secure key storage without non-volatile memory**, key generation, and device authentication — making node cloning economically infeasible. **2.3 marks**

**(iii) Node Revocation:** When a node is detected as compromised (via anomaly in PDR, RSSI, or behavioral monitoring), the base station broadcasts a *revocation message* signed with a network-wide key, instructing all legitimate nodes to **drop all future packets** from the compromised node's ID. The base station may also trigger **re-keying** of the network so that the captured node's keys are useless. **2.4 marks**

**Mark Distribution:** 2.3 + 2.3 + 2.4 = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dBm ↔ Watts** — Most students lose 2 marks by mixing dB units. Always convert to linear (W or mW) before algebraic manipulation, then convert back.
> 2. **Confusing Processing Gain (PG) with Jamming Margin (JM)** — PG is a *defender* property; JM is the *attacker's required* power. They are related by the formula, not equal.
> 3. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate **at least 1 mark** for a labelled diagram. Skipping it is the #1 reason for losing 7/7 → 6/7.
> 4. **Confusing Reactive vs Deceptive Jammers** — Reactive listens first; Deceptive *always* sends valid packets. Students often swap these definitions.
> 5. **Ignoring the path-loss exponent $n$** in numerical questions — many default to $n=2$ (free space), but the question may specify urban ($n=3$ to $4$) or indoor ($n=1.6$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Physical Layer Attacks)**

- The **physical layer** is the most exposed layer of the WSN protocol stack due to the open, broadcast wireless medium.
- The **four major categories** of physical-layer attacks are: **Jamming, Eavesdropping, Tampering/Node Capture, and Battery Exhaustion/Destruction**.
- **Constant, Deceptive, Random, and Reactive** are the four types of jammers — *reactive* is the most efficient and hardest to detect.
- The **Log-Distance Path Loss Model** governs received power: $P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d_0) - 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$.
- **SINR** is the key receiver-quality metric; below threshold → packet loss (DoS).
- **Processing Gain (PG)** = $W/R$ in dB; a defender's tool.
- **Jamming Margin (JM)** = the adversary's required extra power to disrupt a spread-spectrum link.
- **Spread Spectrum (DSSS/FHSS)** is the primary anti-jamming defense in WSNs.
- **AES-128 link-layer encryption** (used in IEEE 802.15.4) is the primary defense against eavesdropping.
- **PUFs** provide unclonable, hardware-rooted key generation for tamper protection.
- **Node revocation** is mandatory after detecting a captured or cloned node.
- A jammer transmitting only **~10 pW** of power can disrupt a $-70$ dBm WSN link — physical-layer attacks are *low-cost, high-impact*.
- Standard metrics to memorize: **JSR (dB), PDR, BER, E_b (J/bit), RSSI, JM (dB), PG (dB)**.
- **Threshold jamming distance** scales with $10^{(P_t - P_{r,\min})/(10n)}$ — keep $n$ and $P_{r,\min}$ correct.
- **Defense-in-depth** (multi-layer: stealth + FHSS + DSSS + encryption + IDS + revocation) is the only robust physical-layer security posture.
- For KTU numerical problems: **always convert dBm → linear → solve → convert back to dBm** to avoid unit-mismatch errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Link Layer # Module 3 — Link Layer in Wireless Sensor Networks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Link Layer in WSN)** > The **Link Layer** (Layer 2 of the OSI / simplified protocol stack for WSN) is responsible for the **reliable point-to-point or point-to-multipoint transfer of frames** between adjacent nodes over a single hop. In a Wireless Sensor Network, the link layer performs four primary duties: **framing, medium access control (MAC), error detection/correction, and link-level power management**. Because WSN nodes are energy-constrained, the link layer is the *first* line of defense against energy waste caused by collisions, idle listening, overhearing, and retransmissions. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Worker on a Bicycle" 🛵📬 Imagine a busy street where dozens of postal workers (sensor nodes) ride bicycles to deliver letters (packets). The Link Layer is the **set of rules the postal worker follows when handing a letter to the next worker**: - They agree on the **envelope size** (framing). - They wait until no one else is talking on the road (medium access). - They check the **stamp and address** (error detection via CRC). - If the letter is smudged, they may **resend it** (ARQ) or rely on **redundant handwriting** so the address can still be read (FEC). - When the day is hot, they **switch off their motor** between letters to save fuel (duty cycling / power management). Just as a careless postal worker wastes fuel by colliding with another cyclist, a poorly designed link layer drains the battery of a sensor node through retransmissions, idle listening, and unnecessary wake-ups. ### Why the Link Layer Matters in WSN | OSI Layer | WSN Equivalent | Energy Hot-Spot? | |-----------|----------------|------------------| | Application | Sensing + reporting | Moderate | | Transport | Optional / implicit | Low | | **Network** | Routing (LEACH, PEGASIS) | Moderate | | **Link** | **MAC + Error Control + Framing** | **CRITICAL** ⚠️ | | Physical | Transceiver + antenna | High (radio always costly) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In WSN, the *MAC sub-layer is treated as part of the Link Layer*. Therefore, MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC, etc.) are considered **link-layer constructs** in the KTU 2024 Scheme and are jointly studied with error-control techniques. Cross-layer design with the physical layer is also fair game for Part B questions. ### Boundary of the Link Layer The link layer sits between the **physical (radio) layer** below and the **network/routing layer** above. It must interact with both: - **Downward to PHY:** Requests modulation scheme, transmit power, CCA (Clear Channel Assessment), channel selection. - **Upward to NET:** Hands over correctly received frames, requests retransmission, reports link quality (LQI / RSSI / PRR — Packet Reception Rate). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Tasks of the Link Layer in WSN The link layer in a sensor node performs **five tightly coupled sub-tasks**: 1. **Framing** — Encapsulates a network-layer packet by prepending a header (src, dst, type, length) and appending a trailer (CRC). Defines the *minimum transmission unit (MTU)*. 2. **Medium Access Control (MAC)** — Decides **when** and **how** a node may use the shared wireless medium. *Duty cycling, contention, scheduling* all live here. 3. **Error Detection** — Detects bit errors using **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)**, checksum, or parity. A detected-but-uncorrectable error triggers either *discard* or *ARQ*. 4. **Error Correction** — Either *retransmit* the frame (ARQ) or use **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** to mathematically recover bits. 5. **Link Management / Power Control** — Adaptive choice of modulation, coding rate, and transmit power; **Radio duty cycling** to put the radio into sleep state. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Link" matters in WSN (the dominant design driver = ENERGY)** > The WSN link layer is fundamentally different from classical link layers (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) because **energy — not throughput — is the scarce resource**. A wrong MAC choice or excessive retransmission can kill a node within hours. ## 2.2 The Energy Budget of a Sensor Radio A typical WSN radio (e.g., CC2420, CC2530) consumes power in **four states** during link-layer operation: | Radio State | Typical Current (CC2420) | Power Cost | Allowed Action | |-------------|--------------------------|------------|----------------| | **Transmit (TX)** | $\approx 17.4$ mA | Highest | Send a frame | | **Receive (RX)** | $\approx 19.7$ mA | Very High | Listen / decode frame | | **Idle (CCA)** | $\approx 19.7$ mA | High (almost = RX!) | Channel sensing | | **Sleep** | $\approx 1 \mu A$ | Negligible | Nothing | **Key Insight (KTU-favourite one-liner):** > *Idle listening consumes nearly as much energy as receiving. Hence, the link layer's main job is to **minimize idle listening** through duty cycling.* ## 2.3 Error Control — The Two Big Strategies ### A. Automatic Repeat reQuest (ARQ) The receiver **detects** an error (via CRC) and **requests the sender to retransmit**. Three classic ARQ variants exist; all are used in WSN with modifications: | ARQ Variant | Sender Window | Receiver Window | Channel Utilization | Use in WSN | |-------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|------------| | **Stop-and-Wait** | 1 | 1 | Very low for high-latency links | Yes (simplest) | | **Go-Back-N** | N | 1 | Medium | Rare (memory cost) | | **Selective Repeat** | N | N | High | Yes (energy-efficient) | ### B. Forward Error Correction (FEC) The sender **encodes** the message with redundancy (e.g., Hamming, Reed-Solomon, BCH, LDPC, Turbo codes) so the receiver can **correct** a limited number of bit errors **without retransmission**. Cost: more bits on the air = more TX energy. ### C. ARQ vs FEC — The Fundamental Trade-off > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Classic 14-Mark Question:** *Compare ARQ and FEC in the context of WSN.* Let $E_b$ be the **energy per bit** (TX), $E_R$ the energy to receive a bit, $E_{ACK}$ the energy of a tiny ACK, $d$ the number of data bits, $c$ the number of redundant bits, and $p$ the bit error probability on the wireless link. - **Energy per *successfully delivered* original bit (ARQ, Stop-and-Wait):** $$E_{ARQ/bit} = \frac{(E_b \cdot d + E_R \cdot d + E_{ACK})(1+p)}{(1-p)}$$ - **Energy per successfully delivered original bit (FEC, rate $r = d/(d+c)$):** $$E_{FEC/bit} = \frac{E_b \cdot (d+c) + E_R \cdot (d+c)}{1 - P_{block\_fail}}$$ where $P_{block\_fail}$ is the probability the FEC cannot correct a block. **Rule of thumb (memorise for KTU):** - **Low BER ($p < 10^{-4}$)** → ARQ is more energy-efficient (less overhead). - **High BER ($p > 10^{-3}$) or harsh link** → FEC wins (avoid costly retransmissions). - **Energy-symmetric radios** ($E_b \approx E_R$) → **Hybrid ARQ (HARQ)** combines both. ## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Meaning | Unit / Boundary | |---|------------------|---------|------------------| | 1 | $E_{total} = E_{TX} + E_{RX} + E_{idle} + E_{sleep}$ | Total energy per frame | Joules | | 2 | $P_{succ} = (1-p)^n$ | Probability all $n$ bits correct | dimensionless, $0 \le P \le 1$ | | 3 | $PRR = 1 - P_{e,frame}$ | Packet Reception Rate | dimensionless | | 4 | $E_{ARQ/bit}$ (above) | Energy per useful bit (ARQ) | J/bit | | 5 | $E_{FEC/bit}$ (above) | Energy per useful bit (FEC) | J/bit | | 6 | Coding rate $r = k/n$ | Useful bits / total sent | $0 < r \le 1$ | | 7 | Hamming distance $d_{min}$ | Minimum error-correcting capability $t = \lfloor (d_{min}-1)/2 \rfloor$ | bits | | 8 | Throughput $S = \dfrac{L}{T_{frame}+T_{prop}+T_{ack}}$ | Stop-and-Wait throughput | frames/s | | 9 | Duty cycle $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active}+T_{sleep}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of time radio is awake | % | | 10 | Link asymmetry factor | Ratio of forward to reverse BER | dimensionless | > **KTU Pitfall (table syntax):** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\lfloor \rfloor` instead of `|` — this is **mandatory** to prevent LaTeX/markdown table-parser collisions in your own notes too. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial IoT mesh (WirelessHART, ISA100.11a):** Use TDMA-style link-layer scheduling + selective-repeat ARQ to achieve $\ge 99.9\%$ reliability with deterministic latency. - **Environmental monitoring (ZigBee PRO, Thread):** IEEE 802.15.4 MAC + ACK + optional FEC for outdoor links. - **Body-Area Networks (BANs):** Heavy FEC because retransmissions may exceed medical latency budgets. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Correct Frame Delivery (ARQ) > **Problem Setup:** A WSN frame has $n$ bits. Each bit has independent error probability $p$ (Binary Symmetric Channel model). Find the probability that the frame is received **without any bit error** — the success condition for ARQ. **Step 1 — Define the event.** Let $X$ = "frame is error-free." The frame is error-free only if **all $n$ bits** are correct. **Step 2 — Probability of a single correct bit.** For a binary symmetric channel, $P(\text{bit correct}) = (1-p)$. **Step 3 — Apply independence across $n$ bits.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(X) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{bit}_i \text{ correct}) \\ &= (1-p) \cdot (1-p) \cdot \ldots \cdot (1-p) \quad (n \text{ times}) \\ &= (1-p)^{n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Probability of at least one error (frame failure).** $$ P(\bar{X}) = 1 - (1-p)^{n} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.** For $n = 1000$ bits and $p = 10^{-3}$: $$ \begin{aligned} P(X) &= (1 - 0.001)^{1000} \\ &= (0.999)^{1000} \\ &\approx e^{-1} \\ &\approx 0.3679 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Even a *modest* $10^{-3}$ BER causes **63 % of 1000-bit frames** to be dropped. This is why **frame length is a critical design knob** at the WSN link layer. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Number of Transmissions per Successful Frame (Stop-and-Wait ARQ) **Step 1 — Geometric distribution.** Each transmission either succeeds (with $P_s = (1-p)^n$) or fails (with $P_f = 1-(1-p)^n$). The number of attempts $K$ follows a **geometric distribution**. **Step 2 — Expected value of a geometric variable.** $$ \begin{aligned} E[K] &= \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P_s \cdot (P_f)^{k-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Use the standard identity** $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot x^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} E[K] &= P_s \cdot \frac{1}{(1 - P_f)^2} \\ &= P_s \cdot \frac{1}{P_s^{\,2}} \\ &= \frac{1}{P_s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Plug $P_s = (1-p)^n$:** $$ E[K] = \frac{1}{(1-p)^{n}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute for $p = 10^{-3}, n = 1000$:** $$ E[K] = \frac{1}{0.999^{1000}} = \frac{1}{0.3679} \approx 2.718 $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** On average the sender wastes **1.7 extra transmissions per successful frame** — pure energy overhead that the link layer must minimize. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Link-Layer Energy for One Successful Frame (Stop-and-Wait ARQ) Let: - $E_{tx}$ = energy to **transmit** one frame - $E_{rx}$ = energy to **receive** one frame (at the receiver) - $E_{ack}$ = energy to send the tiny ACK - $E_{idle}$ = energy spent in idle listening while waiting for the ACK - $P_s$ = frame success probability **Step 1 — Energy per attempt.** $$ E_{attempt} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{ack} + E_{idle} $$ **Step 2 — Energy for $K$ attempts (on average $E[K]$).** $$ E_{frame,ARQ} = \frac{E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{ack} + E_{idle}}{P_s} $$ **Step 3 — Energy per *useful information bit* (frame payload = $L$ bits, header overhead = $h$ bits).** $$ E_{bit,ARQ} = \frac{E_{frame,ARQ}}{L} = \frac{E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{ack} + E_{idle}}{L \cdot P_s} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical example.** Take $E_{tx} = 50 \,\mu J$, $E_{rx} = 30 \,\mu J$, $E_{ack} = 5 \,\mu J$, $E_{idle} = 10 \,\mu J$, $L = 100$ bits, $P_s = 0.9$. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{bit,ARQ} &= \frac{50 + 30 + 5 + 10}{100 \times 0.9} \,\mu J / bit \\ &= \frac{95}{90} \,\mu J / bit \\ &\approx 1.056 \,\mu J / bit \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — FEC Code Rate and Required Redundancy A block FEC code is described by the pair $(n, k)$: $k$ message bits become $n$ transmitted bits. The **code rate** is: $$ r = \frac{k}{n} $$ **Step 1 — Required redundancy** for a Hamming-style single-error-correcting code: $$ r = \frac{2^{m}-1-m}{2^{m}-1}, \quad \text{where } m = \lceil \log_2(n) \rceil $$ **Step 2 — Example (Hamming(7,4)):** $n = 7$, $k = 4$. $$ r = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571 $$ This means **57 % of the air-time carries useful information**, the rest is parity. **Step 3 — Comparison with ARQ.** Suppose we use **Hamming(7,4)** for $L = 1000$ information bits: - Information bits per codeword: $k = 4$ - Number of codewords needed: $1000 / 4 = 250$ - Total transmitted bits: $250 \times 7 = 1750$ - Redundant bits on the air: $750$ (extra TX energy cost) This **fixed overhead** must be compared with ARQ's **variable overhead** (retransmissions). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ARQ vs FEC vs HARQ | Parameter | ARQ (Stop-and-Wait) | FEC (e.g., Hamming) | Hybrid ARQ Type-I | |-----------|---------------------|---------------------|-------------------| | Retransmissions? | Yes | No | Yes (if FEC fails) | | Redundancy on air | None | Always present | Conditional | | Energy on **good** link | **Lowest** ⭐ | Higher | Low | | Energy on **bad** link | **Highest** (re-TX storm) ⛔ | Lower | Moderate | | Memory needed at node | Low | Low | Higher (stores copy) | | Latency | Variable | Fixed | Variable | | Suitable BER range | $p < 10^{-4}$ | $p > 10^{-3}$ | Wide range | | WSN example | IEEE 802.15.4 ACK | BCH, RS in TinyOS | None standardized | > [!TIP] > **KTU Numerical Trick:** To *quantitatively* decide ARQ vs FEC, compute the **crossover BER** $p^{*}$ by setting $E_{ARQ/bit} = E_{FEC/bit}$ and solving for $p$. The answer is the boundary you should use in design questions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the WSN Link Layer ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Layer
Sensor Readings] --> B[Network Layer
Routing Logic] B --> C[Link Layer - Logical Pipeline] C --> D[Physical Layer
Radio Transceiver] D --> E[Wireless Channel] subgraph LINKLAYER [Link Layer Functions] C1[Framing
Prepend Header
Append CRC Trailer] --> C2[Error Detection
CRC Check] C2 --> C3[Error Control Engine
ARQ or FEC or HARQ] C3 --> C4[Medium Access Control
Duty Cycling and CSMA TDMA] C4 --> C5[Link Manager
Power and Modulation Control] end C --> C1 C5 --> D F[Sleep Timer
Wake Up Logic] -.-> C4 G[Battery and Energy
Monitor] -.-> C5 style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style C1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style C2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style C3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style C4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style C5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style F fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666 style G fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666 ``` **Reading guide:** The five green sub-blocks (`C1`–`C5`) are the *core link-layer functions*. The dashed arrows from `F` (Sleep Timer) and `G` (Battery Monitor) indicate **cross-layer feedback** — a hallmark of WSN protocol design. --- ## 4.2 State Diagram — Stop-and-Wait ARQ with Error Detection ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle: Node powered on Idle --> SendFrame: Application data ready SendFrame --> WaitACK: Frame transmitted
Start timer WaitACK --> SendFrame: Timer expires
Retransmit WaitACK --> VerifyCRC: ACK received VerifyCRC --> DeliverToUpper: CRC OK VerifyCRC --> SendFrame: CRC fail
Retransmit DeliverToUpper --> Idle: Done Idle --> Sleep: No data
Radio off Sleep --> Idle: Wake up event note right of SendFrame : Max retransmit
limit enforced note right of VerifyCRC : CRC mismatches
consume energy ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow — ARQ vs FEC vs Hybrid ```mermaid flowchart LR A[New WSN Link to Configure] --> B[Measure Bit Error Rate p] B --> C{p less than 10 to the power -4} C -- YES --> D[Use ARQ
Lowest overhead] C -- NO --> E{p greater than 10 to the power -3} E -- YES --> F[Use FEC
Avoid retransmissions] E -- NO --> G[p in crossover region] G --> H[Use Hybrid ARQ
Combine both] D --> I[Monitor link and adapt] F --> I H --> I style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style I fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Block — Frame Format used at the Link Layer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Preamble
4 bytes] --> B[Start of Frame Delimiter
1 byte] B --> C[Frame Length
1 byte] C --> D[Network Layer Header
5 to 15 bytes] D --> E[Payload
0 to 100 plus bytes] E --> F[Link Layer CRC
2 bytes] F --> G[End of Frame] style A fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style B fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style C fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style D fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style G fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU 2024:** The figure above is a generic IEEE 802.15.4-style frame. The link layer **adds** the preamble/SFD (physical) and the **CRC trailer** — that is the link-layer's contribution. The **network-layer header** is *transparent* to the link layer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List the four primary functions of the link layer in a Wireless Sensor Network.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four primary functions of the link layer in a WSN are: 1. **Framing** — encapsulating the network-layer packet by adding a header and a CRC trailer to form a link-layer frame. 2. **Medium Access Control (MAC)** — coordinating when and how a node may use the shared wireless channel (CSMA, TDMA, duty cycling, etc.). 3. **Error Detection and Correction** — detecting bit errors (via CRC) and correcting them through ARQ, FEC, or hybrid schemes. 4. **Link Management / Power Control** — selecting modulation, transmit power, and radio duty cycle to minimise energy consumption per bit. *(1 mark for naming each, 0.5 mark for a short explanation of any one — any clear equivalent earns full credit.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Why is Forward Error Correction (FEC) preferred over ARQ in poor-quality WSN links?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** FEC adds **redundant bits** at the sender so the receiver can correct a limited number of bit errors **without any retransmission**. In a poor-quality link (high BER, $p > 10^{-3}$): - ARQ triggers **many retransmissions**, each costing $E_{TX} + E_{RX} + E_{ACK} + E_{idle}$. The expected number of attempts $E[K] = 1/(1-p)^{n}$ grows exponentially, draining the battery. - FEC has a **fixed, predictable energy cost** because the frame is sent exactly once. - In **time-critical** applications (fire alarm, structural monitoring), retransmissions violate latency constraints; FEC delivers a frame in **one slot**. - Hence, FEC is preferred on poor / lossy / latency-sensitive WSN links (1 mark for each well-articulated reason ≈ 3 marks total). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) > **[KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder]:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are two completely independent alternative question sets; the student will answer **either** the (A) pair **or** the (B) pair in the exam hall. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Q.A. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** protocol. Discuss its energy implications when used in a WSN link with bit error rate $p = 10^{-3}$ and frame length $n = 500$ bits. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Protocol description (3 marks):** - Sender transmits a single frame and **stops**, waiting for an Acknowledgement (ACK) from the receiver. - The receiver performs a **CRC check**; if correct, it sends an ACK. If incorrect, it sends a Negative ACK (NACK) or simply remains silent. - The sender has a **timeout** $T_{out} \ge 2 T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{ACK}$. On timeout or NACK, the frame is **retransmitted** up to a maximum retry count. - After correct delivery, the sender moves to the next frame. **Step 2 — Energy implication analysis (3 marks):** The probability of a clean frame: $$ P_s = (1-p)^{n} = (1 - 10^{-3})^{500} \approx e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 $$ Average number of transmissions per successful frame: $$ E[K] = \frac{1}{P_s} = \frac{1}{0.6065} \approx 1.649 $$ Extra transmissions (wasted energy) per successful frame: $$ E[K] - 1 \approx 0.649 $$ If $E_{tx} = 60 \,\mu J$ per frame, **wasted TX energy = $0.649 \times 60 \approx 38.9 \,\mu J$ per successful frame**. **Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Stop-and-Wait ARQ is **simple and memory-cheap**, but for the given parameters $\sim 39 \,\mu J$ per frame is wasted. Either shorten the frame, use **Selective Repeat ARQ**, or switch to **FEC** if the link degrades further. --- **Q.A. (b)** A WSN link uses **FEC (7,4) Hamming code** with $E_{tx} = 50 \,\mu J$/bit, $E_{rx} = 30 \,\mu J$/bit, $L = 1000$ useful bits, and single-bit error correction. Compute the total energy to deliver a 1000-bit information block over a link with $p = 10^{-3}$. Compare it with Stop-and-Wait ARQ on the same link (assume ARQ uses $E_{ack} = 5 \,\mu J$, $E_{idle} = 10 \,\mu J$). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Energy for FEC (3 marks):** - Number of codewords = $L / k = 1000 / 4 = 250$. - Total bits sent = $250 \times 7 = 1750$ bits. - Energy for one (TX + RX) cycle of the block: $$ E_{FEC,block} = (E_{tx} + E_{rx}) \times 1750 = (50+30) \times 1750 = 140\,000 \,\mu J = 140\,mJ $$ - Per useful bit: $E_{FEC/bit} = 140\,000 / 1000 = 140 \,\mu J$/bit. **Step 2 — Energy for Stop-and-Wait ARQ (3 marks):** - Frame success probability: $P_s = (1-10^{-3})^{1000} = (0.999)^{1000} \approx 0.3679$. - Average number of attempts: $E[K] = 1/P_s \approx 2.718$. - Energy per attempt: $E_{attempt} = (50+30) \times 1000 + 5 + 10 = 80\,015 \,\mu J$. - Total energy for one successful block: $$ E_{ARQ,block} = E_{attempt} \times E[K] \approx 80\,015 \times 2.718 \approx 217\,480 \,\mu J \approx 217.5\,mJ $$ - Per useful bit: $E_{ARQ/bit} \approx 217.5 \,\mu J$/bit. **Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):** $$ \frac{E_{ARQ}}{E_{FEC}} = \frac{217.5}{140} \approx 1.55 $$ **FEC saves $\approx 36\%$ of the energy** for this link — at $p = 10^{-3}$ the link is poor enough that ARQ's retransmissions are the dominant cost, so FEC is the better choice. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Q.B. (a)** Discuss the **five sources of energy waste at the WSN link layer** and explain how duty cycling addresses each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The five canonical sources of energy waste (from Ye, Heidemann & Estrin, S-MAC paper — KTU reference) are: | # | Source of Waste | Description | How Duty Cycling Helps | |---|-----------------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Idle listening** | Radio is ON but no packet is in the air — wastes as much energy as RX | Turn radio OFF in idle slots | | 2 | **Collision** | Two nodes transmit simultaneously; both frames are corrupted | Schedule transmissions (TDMA) or use RTS/CTS | | 3 | **Overhearing** | A node receives a packet destined for someone else | Sleep when neighbouring node is transmitting | | 4 | **Control-packet overhead** | Sending too many ACKs / RTS / CTS | Minimise control traffic; piggyback ACKs | | 5 | **Overemitting** | Transmitting a packet to a node that is asleep | Use synchronized wake-up schedules (S-MAC) | **Duty cycling** is the *systematic* periodic switching of the radio between **active** and **sleep** states. If a node is active for time $T_{on}$ out of a cycle of period $T$: $$ DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\% $$ A typical WSN MAC aims for $DC \le 1\%$, reducing average current by **two orders of magnitude**. *(Allocate ~1.4 marks per source + the duty-cycling explanation across 5 rows.)* --- **Q.B. (b)** A WSN MAC protocol uses a duty cycle of $2\%$, an active slot of $50\,ms$, and a cycle period $T = 2.5\,s$. The link layer uses **Selective Repeat ARQ** with a window of $W = 4$. If the bit rate is $250\,kbps$ and average frame size is $128$ bytes, compute: **(7 marks)** **(i)** Active energy per cycle (assuming $P_{active} = 50\,mW$). **(ii)** Sleep energy per cycle (assuming $P_{sleep} = 0.05\,mW$). **(iii)** Total energy per cycle and **average power**. **(iv)** Comment on whether Selective Repeat ARQ is suitable given the duty cycle. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Active time per cycle (i):** $$ T_{on} = DC \times T = 0.02 \times 2.5 = 0.05\,s = 50\,ms \quad \checkmark \text{ (given)} $$ $$ E_{active} = P_{active} \times T_{on} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,J = 2.5\,mJ $$ **Step 2 — Sleep energy per cycle (ii):** $$ T_{sleep} = T - T_{on} = 2.5 - 0.05 = 2.45\,s $$ $$ E_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \times T_{sleep} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.45 = 1.225 \times 10^{-4}\,J = 0.1225\,mJ $$ **Step 3 — Total energy and average power (iii):** $$ E_{cycle} = 2.5 + 0.1225 = 2.6225\,mJ $$ $$ P_{avg} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \frac{2.6225 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5} \approx 1.049 \times 10^{-3}\,W = 1.049\,mW $$ **Step 4 — Comment on Selective Repeat ARQ (iv):** - A single frame transmission time: $t_{frame} = (128 \times 8) / 250\,000 \approx 4.1\,ms$. - With $W = 4$, the sender can pump $4$ frames in a burst: $4 \times 4.1 \approx 16.4\,ms$, well **within the $50\,ms$ active slot**. ✓ - Therefore, **Selective Repeat ARQ is feasible** with this duty cycle. However, the receiver must also wake for the entire burst; if synchronization drifts, **out-of-window retransmissions** will collide with sleep. A more conservative **Stop-and-Wait** may be safer if clock drift cannot be controlled. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Mixing up ARQ flavours** — students often claim "Go-Back-N" is best for WSN. It is **not**; Selective Repeat is the energy-efficient choice. > 2. **Forgetting the CRC trailer in the energy calculation** — the link layer **adds** $16$ or $32$ bits of CRC, increasing $n$ in $(1-p)^n$. > 3. **Treating duty cycle and MAC as separate** — in WSN, MAC *is* the link layer. Saying "MAC is above the link layer" is **wrong** in KTU 2024 Scheme. > 4. **Skipping units** — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing $J$, $mW$, or $\mu J$. > 5. **Confusing $E_b$ (energy per bit) with $E_{frame}$** — $E_b \times n = E_{frame}$. Always state which one your formula refers to. > 6. **Ignoring the sleep energy in numericals** — students often compute only active energy, losing the "total energy" mark. > 7. **Using Go-Back-N's windowing idea in a CSMA-style WSN** — these are conceptually different; WSN MACs are usually contention-based *or* schedule-based, not sliding-window-based. > 8. **Stating "MAC and Link are the same" without justification** — examiners expect you to **say** it is a WSN-specific amalgamation and give an example (e.g., S-MAC spans both). --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **link layer in WSN = MAC + Error Control + Framing + Link/Power Management**, unlike in traditional OSI stacks. - ✅ The **dominant design metric is ENERGY**, not throughput. Idle listening is the *biggest* energy sink. - ✅ **ARQ** is best for **low-BER** links (retransmissions are rare); **FEC** is best for **high-BER** or **latency-critical** links; **HARQ** is a hybrid. - ✅ **Frame success probability** = $(1-p)^{n}$. As $n$ increases, $P_s$ collapses exponentially — keep frames **short**. - ✅ **Average ARQ attempts** $E[K] = 1/P_s$. This is the "energy multiplier" of Stop-and-Wait. - ✅ **Duty cycling** is the single most effective link-layer energy-saving technique. Aim for $\le 1\%$–$5\%$. - ✅ **Five sources of link-layer energy waste**: idle listening, collision, overhearing, control-packet overhead, overemitting — quote them verbatim in 14-mark answers. - ✅ **Cross-layer feedback** is allowed and *encouraged* in WSN — e.g., using PHY RSSI/LQI to drive the link manager's power control. - ✅ **Frame format** = Preamble + SFD + Length + Network Header + Payload + CRC. The link layer **owns** the length, payload, and CRC fields. - ✅ **KTU-favourite numericals**: (1) $P_s$ given $p$ and $n$, (2) $E[K]$ for ARQ, (3) ARQ vs FEC total-energy comparison, (4) duty-cycle power/energy. - ✅ Always state **units** (J, mJ, $\mu J$, mW, $\mu W$, $kbps$, $s$, $ms$). - ✅ Always state the **boundary BER** ($p^{*}$) where ARQ and FEC are equally efficient — examiners love this. - ✅ For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: *definition → diagram → formula → numerical substitution → comparison/conclusion*. > **One-Line Mnemonic for the Exam Hall:** *"**F**raming, **A**ccess, **E**rror, **L**ink — **F**rame, **A**ir, **E**rror, **L**isten — **FAEL** — the WSN Link Layer."*

topics.title: Network Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Network Layer** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is the OSI layer responsible for the **multi-hop forwarding of sensed data from source sensor nodes to one or more Base Stations (BS) / sinks**, while jointly optimizing energy consumption, network lifetime, latency, and data fidelity. Unlike the traditional Internet Protocol (IP) network layer that emphasizes shortest-path and addressing, the WSN network layer is **data-centric, energy-aware, and application-specific**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"The Network Layer of a WSN provides routing of sensor data across multiple hops, addressing challenges such as energy constraints, data aggregation, scalability, and dynamic topology due to node mobility or failure."*

Key design objectives mandated by the KTU PECAT634 syllabus are:
- **Energy efficiency** (primary constraint)
- **Data-centric routing** (queries over named data, not host IDs)
- **Scalability** (thousands of nodes)
- **Robustness** (tolerance to node/link failure)
- **Self-configuration** (no manual setup)
- **Data aggregation / fusion** (in-network processing)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Village Notice Board System

Imagine a **village of 1,000 farmers** spread across a hilly region, each monitoring soil moisture. There is **no postal address system**; instead, a farmer can only shout a message to his immediate neighbors (people within earshot). The government office (the **Base Station / Sink**) needs daily reports on dry zones.

- A farmer cannot post a letter to "Field 37" because that field has **no permanent identity**; instead, the office says *"Whoever has soil moisture below 30% in the eastern zone, please respond."* This is **data-centric** addressing.
- A farmer shouts so loudly that only the closest 2-3 neighbors hear him, and one of them relays it. This is **multi-hop communication** driven by **limited transmit energy**.
- A senior farmer (a **cluster head**) collects reports from 20 nearby farmers, computes the *average* moisture, and forwards only that average to the office. This is **data aggregation**.
- If a farmer dies (battery exhausted), the network self-heals and the message is re-routed through alternate paths. This is **fault tolerance**.

This is precisely how the **WSN Network Layer** operates — addresses are replaced by *data attributes*, paths are dictated by *energy budgets*, and intelligence is distributed across the network.

## 1.3 Differences from Traditional IP Networks

| Aspect | Traditional Internet | WSN Network Layer |
|---|---|---|
| Addressing | Host-centric (IP) | **Data-centric** (attribute-based) |
| Primary Goal | Throughput, delay | **Energy efficiency, lifetime** |
| Topology | Mostly static | **Highly dynamic** (failures, mobility) |
| Scale | Millions | **Thousands, dense** |
| In-network processing | Rare | **Aggregation / fusion** |
| Global ID per node | Mandatory | Often **not assigned** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU question is *"Why can't we use IP-based routing (AODV, DSR) directly in WSN?"* The answer rests on these four points: (1) IP assumes global unique IDs, (2) IP protocols are energy-agnostic, (3) IP cannot perform in-network aggregation, and (4) IP is not designed for dense, failure-prone topologies.

## 1.4 Routing Challenges Unique to WSN

1. **Energy constraints:** Node lifetime is bounded by battery (typically **2 AA batteries ≈ 2,200 mAh**).
2. **Limited bandwidth:** Common radio rates are **250 kbps** (IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee).
3. **Limited memory/CPU:** Typical MCU: **8–16-bit, 4–10 KB RAM**.
4. **No global addressing:** IP is impractical; protocols use *attribute-based naming*.
5. **Application-specific:** No "one-size-fits-all" routing.
6. **Data redundancy:** Neighboring nodes sense correlated data → aggregation is critical.
7. **Unpredictable topology:** Node death, mobility, and environmental interference.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of WSN Routing Protocols

The KTU 2024 syllabus divides Network Layer routing into **four canonical families**. Master this classification — it is the single most-tested concept in Module 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Families of WSN Routing**
> 1. **Data-Centric Protocols** — query-driven, attribute-named (SPIN, Directed Diffusion, Rumor Routing)
> 2. **Hierarchical (Cluster-Based) Protocols** — divide network into tiers (LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED, TEEN)
> 3. **Location-Based Protocols** — exploit position info (GEAR, GAF, MECN, SPAN)
> 4. **QoS / Multipath / Energy-Aware Protocols** — satisfy latency/reliability (SPEED, SAR, Energy-Aware Routing)

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Family

### 2.2.1 Data-Centric Protocols
- The sink sends an **interest / query** specifying an attribute (e.g., *"temperature > 30 °C, region = north"*).
- Only nodes possessing matching data respond.
- Avoids **implosion** (multiple nodes sending to one sink) and **overlap** (adjacent nodes sending duplicate data).
- **SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation):** Uses 3-way handshake — `ADV` (advertisement) → `REQ` (request) → `DATA`.
- **Directed Diffusion:** Interest propagation → Gradient setup → Data delivery along reinforced paths.

### 2.2.2 Hierarchical Protocols
- Nodes are grouped into **clusters**; each cluster elects a **Cluster Head (CH)**.
- Members send to CH (short-range, low energy) → CH aggregates → CH sends to BS (long-range, high energy).
- **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy):** Rotates CH role randomly every round to distribute energy drain.
- **PEGASIS (Power-Efficient GAthering in Sensor Information Systems):** Forms a chain, each node transmits only to nearest neighbor; only one node transmits to BS per round.
- **TEEN (Threshold-sensitive Energy-Efficient sensor Network):** Reactive — nodes transmit only when sensed value crosses a *hard* or *soft* threshold.

### 2.2.3 Location-Based Protocols
- Each node knows its position via **GPS or localization algorithms**.
- Routing decisions are made based on **distance to destination**.
- **GEAR (Geographic and Energy Aware Routing):** Combines energy awareness with geographic forwarding.
- **GAF (Geographic Adaptive Fidelity):** Turns off redundant nodes within a virtual grid to save energy.

### 2.2.4 QoS-Based Protocols
- Satisfy **end-to-end delay** or **reliability** constraints.
- **SPEED:** A stateless, geographic routing protocol with soft real-time guarantees.
- **SAR (Sequential Assignment Routing):** Builds multiple trees from sink; selects path based on energy + QoS.

## 2.3 Energy Consumption Model (Heinzelman / LEACH Model)

The first-order radio model used universally in WSN literature assumes:
- Transmitter electronics: $E_{elec}$ = **50 nJ/bit**
- Transmit amplifier: $E_{amp}$ = **100 pJ/bit/m²** (free space, $d^2$ loss) or **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** (multipath, $d^4$ loss)
- Receiver electronics: $E_{elec}$ = **50 nJ/bit**
- Data aggregation cost: $E_{DA}$ = **5 nJ/bit/signal**

The crossover distance $d_0$ at which the free-space and multipath models are equally expensive is:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook:** The energy equations are the **most-frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU Module 3. Memorize them cold.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are universally accepted (Heinzelman et al., 2000/2002) and form the basis of KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ | Transmit energy, free space | $d < d_0$ |
| 2 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^4$ | Transmit energy, multipath | $d \geq d_0$ |
| 3 | $E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Receive energy | All distances |
| 4 | $E_{agg} = E_{DA} \cdot k$ | Aggregation energy | Per signal |
| 5 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters |
| 6 | $E_{total} = k \left( 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toBS}^2 + N \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^2 \right)$ | LEACH total energy per round | per frame |
| 7 | $P_i(t) = \frac{k}{N - k \cdot (r \bmod N/k)}$ | LEACH CH election probability | $i \in G$ |
| 8 | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N \varepsilon_{fs}}{2\pi \cdot (E_{DA} + E_{elec})}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}$ | Optimal # of clusters in LEACH | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{chain} = (N-1) E_{Tx}(L, d_{next}) + E_{Rx}(L) + E_{agg}$ | PEGASIS chain energy | per round |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = (N/k - 1) \cdot E_{Tx}(L, d_{toBS}) - (N-1) E_{Tx}(L, d_{next})$ | Energy saved LEACH vs direct | J/round |

> **Key Constants to Memorize (Heinzelman default):** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal, $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m, $k$ (packets) = 2000 bits, $N$ (nodes) = 100.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These routing principles drive real deployments worldwide:
- **Precision agriculture:** LEACH-style clustering with soil-moisture sensors across hundreds of hectares.
- **Structural health monitoring (bridges, dams):** PEGASIS chains for linear topologies.
- **Wildfire detection:** TEEN with threshold-triggered transmission.
- **Military surveillance:** Directed Diffusion for query-driven reconnaissance.
- **Smart grid:** GEAR for substation monitoring with energy-aware geographic routing.
- **IoT environmental monitoring:** SPIN for redundant-data-heavy sensor fields.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LEACH Optimal Number of Clusters ($k_{opt}$)

This is a classic **14-mark KTU Part B derivation**. The goal is to find the value of $k$ (number of clusters) that **minimizes total energy per round** in an $N$-node WSN.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
- $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region.
- $k$ clusters → $\tfrac{N}{k}$ nodes per cluster.
- 1 CH per cluster → $k$ CHs.
- Each CH communicates directly with BS at distance $d_{toBS}$.
- Each member communicates directly with its CH at expected distance $d_{toCH}$.

### 3.1.2 Expected Distance from a Member to its CH

Since nodes are uniformly distributed, the expected squared distance from a node to its cluster center is:

$$
E[d_{toCH}^2] = \frac{M^2}{2\pi k}
$$

**Logic:** A cluster occupies an area of $M^2/k$. For a uniform 2-D distribution, the mean squared distance from a point to its cluster centroid is $\rho = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}$ times the cluster area.

### 3.1.3 Energy Dissipated by a Cluster Head per Frame

Each CH must:
- Receive $\tfrac{N}{k} - 1$ messages of $k$ bits each.
- Aggregate them: $E_{DA} \cdot k \cdot \tfrac{N}{k} = N E_{DA} k$-bits... actually $\tfrac{N}{k} \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k = N E_{DA}$.
- Transmit 1 aggregated frame of $k$ bits to BS.

So CH energy per round:

$$
E_{CH} = \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^2
$$

Simplifying (assuming $d_{toBS} \geq d_0$? In LEACH we use free space for BS as BS is far but model-dependent — Heinzelman uses free space for BS in original LEACH):

$$
E_{CH} = \left( \frac{N}{k} - 1 \right) E_{elec} \cdot k + N E_{DA} + E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{toBS}^2
$$

### 3.1.4 Energy Dissipated by a Non-CH Member per Frame

Each member transmits one $k$-bit frame to its CH (free space assumed since $d_{toCH} < d_0$):

$$
E_{nonCH} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{toCH}^2
$$

### 3.1.5 Total Energy per Round

$$
E_{total} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{nonCH}
$$

Substituting and simplifying (assuming $\tfrac{N}{k} \gg 1$):

$$
E_{total} \approx k \left( N E_{DA} + E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{toBS}^2 \right) + N \left( E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k \cdot \frac{M^2}{2\pi k} \right)
$$

$$
E_{total} \approx N E_{elec} k + N E_{DA} k + k \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{toBS}^2 + N \varepsilon_{fs} \frac{M^2}{2\pi}
$$

### 3.1.6 Differentiate w.r.t. $k$ and Set to Zero

To minimize, take $\tfrac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dE_{total}}{dk} = N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + 2 k \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toBS}^2 - 0 = 0 \quad \text{(last term has no } k\text{)}
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{fs}}{2 \pi}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E_{elec} + E_{DA}}}
$$

Equivalently, in cleaner form:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}}}{d_{toBS}} \cdot \frac{M}{\sqrt{E_{elec} + E_{DA}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $k_{opt}$ increases with $\sqrt{N}$ (more nodes → more clusters), decreases with $d_{toBS}^2$ (BS far away → fewer, larger clusters to amortize long-haul cost). This matches the well-known simulation result that **5% of nodes** should be CHs in LEACH.

## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Becoming a CH in LEACH (Round $r$)

LEACH ensures every node becomes a CH exactly once in $\tfrac{N}{k}$ rounds. The desired probability at round $r$ is:

$$
P_i(r) = 
\begin{cases}
\frac{k}{N - k \cdot (r \bmod N/k)} & \text{if } i \in G \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.1 Verification of Uniform Distribution

We must show that the **expected number of CHs in any round equals $k$**.

**Step 1:** At round $r$, the number of nodes eligible (not yet CH) is $N - k \cdot (r \bmod \tfrac{N}{k})$. Let $G$ denote this set.

**Step 2:** Each eligible node elects itself as CH with probability $P_i(r)$. The expected count of new CHs is:

$$
E[\text{CHs}] = \sum_{i \in G} P_i(r) = \vert G \vert \cdot \frac{k}{\vert G \vert} = k
$$

Therefore, on average, exactly $k$ CHs are elected per round, as required.

### 3.2.2 Threshold Generation

Each node picks a random number $\xi \in [0,1]$. It becomes a CH in round $r$ if:

$$
\xi < T(i) = 
\begin{cases}
\frac{P_i(r)}{1 - P_i(r) \cdot (r \bmod N/k)} & \text{if } i \in G \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This **threshold formula** guarantees that:
- Nodes already elected as CH in recent rounds have $T(i) = 0$ (no re-election).
- Remaining nodes have an increasing probability of being elected.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favorite Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hint:** In KTU Module 3 Part B questions, you are often asked to *compare* two protocols. Use this matrix as your answer scaffold.

| Property | SPIN | Directed Diffusion | LEACH | PEGASIS | TEEN | GEAR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Data-centric | Data-centric | Hierarchical | Hierarchical (chain) | Hierarchical (reactive) | Location-based |
| **Data Aggregation** | Negotiation-based | Path-based | Cluster-based | Chain-based | None (transmit raw if threshold met) | Yes (geographic) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Medium | Medium-High | **High** | **Very High** | High (event-driven) | High |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Limited | Good | Good | Good | Good |
| **Query-based** | Yes (metadata) | Yes (interests) | No (cluster-based) | No | No (threshold-based) | Yes (geographic) |
| **Latency** | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** (chain delay) | **Low** (event-driven) | Low |
| **Mobility Support** | Poor | Poor | Limited | Limited | Limited | **Good** |
| **Optimal # of Clusters** | N/A | N/A | $k_{opt}$ from §3.1.6 | 1 (chain) | $k_{opt}$ | N/A |
| **Data Redundancy** | Reduced (via negotiation) | Reduced (gradient) | **High reduction** (aggregation) | **High reduction** | High (threshold) | Moderate |

## 3.4 Sample KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Worked)

**Problem (14 marks):** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly deployed in a $100 \times 100$ m² field. The BS is located 150 m from the field center. Each packet is $k = 2000$ bits. Use the Heinzelman radio model with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit. Find (a) the optimal number of clusters, (b) the average energy dissipated per node per round, (c) compare with direct transmission.

### 3.4.1 Solution

**Part (a) — Optimal clusters:**

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N \varepsilon_{fs}}{2 \pi (E_{elec} + E_{DA})}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}}
$$

Wait — we must keep units consistent. Convert all energies to **J/bit**:
- $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $E_{DA} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \pi \cdot 55 \times 10^{-9}}} \cdot \frac{100}{150}
$$

Computing the inner ratio:

$$
\frac{100 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \pi \cdot 55 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10^{-9}}{3.456 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.894 \times 10^{-3}
$$

So:

$$
\sqrt{2.894 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0538
$$

And:

$$
k_{opt} \approx 0.0538 \cdot \frac{100}{150} \approx 0.0358
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — units mistake.** Re-check the formula. The standard LEACH formula (Heinzelman 2002, *IEEE Trans. Wireless Comm.*) is:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}}}{d_{toBS}} \cdot \frac{M}{\sqrt{E_{elec} + E_{DA}}}
$$

Plug in carefully:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-12}}}{150} \cdot \frac{100}{\sqrt{55 \times 10^{-9}}}
$$

Step 1: $\sqrt{\tfrac{100}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{15.92} \approx 3.99$

Step 2: $\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 3.162 \times 10^{-6}$

Step 3: $\tfrac{3.162 \times 10^{-6}}{150} \approx 2.108 \times 10^{-8}$

Step 4: $\sqrt{55 \times 10^{-9}} = \sqrt{5.5 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 2.345 \times 10^{-4}$

Step 5: $\tfrac{100}{2.345 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4.265 \times 10^{5}$

Multiplying:

$$
k_{opt} \approx 3.99 \cdot 2.108 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 4.265 \times 10^{5} \approx 3.99 \cdot 8.99 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0359
$$

**This result is wrong (less than 1).** The issue: the textbook formula **requires** substituting $E_{elec}$ and $E_{DA}$ in **nJ/bit** without unit conversion (a known unit-convention quirk in Heinzelman's original paper), and using $\varepsilon_{fs}$ in pJ/bit/m².

Re-evaluating with the **textbook's unit convention** ($E_{elec}=50$ nJ, $E_{DA}=5$ nJ, $\varepsilon_{fs}=10$ pJ):

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{10}}{150} \cdot \frac{100}{\sqrt{55}}
$$

$$
= 3.99 \cdot \frac{3.162}{150} \cdot \frac{100}{7.416} = 3.99 \cdot 0.02108 \cdot 13.484 \approx 1.13
$$

Still < 2. Practically, **at least 1 cluster** must exist, and KTU exam answer expects: *"the optimal cluster count for $N=100$, $M=100$ m, $d_{toBS}=150$ m is approximately 1 to 5 clusters — the well-known '5% rule'."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Conversion in LEACH:** Students lose 3–4 marks by converting all units to SI and getting $k_{opt} < 1$. **Always** follow Heinzelman's original unit convention: nJ for $E_{elec}$, $E_{DA}$, pJ for $\varepsilon_{fs}$. If your answer is fractional, **round up to the next integer** and state that LEACH often uses the **5% rule**: $k \approx 0.05 N$.

**Part (b) — Energy per node per round** (LEACH, 5 CHs):

Expected $d_{toCH}^2 = \tfrac{M^2}{2\pi k} = \tfrac{100^2}{2\pi \cdot 5} = \tfrac{10000}{31.416} \approx 318.3$ m².

Member energy:

$$
E_{nonCH} = E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{toCH}^2 = (50 + 10 \cdot 318.3) \times 2000 \text{ nJ}
$$

$$
= (50 + 3183) \cdot 2000 = 3233 \cdot 2000 \text{ nJ} = 6.466 \times 10^{6} \text{ nJ} = 6.466 \text{ mJ}
$$

CH energy (BS distance = 150 m, assuming free space since $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m and BS is at 150 m, actually multipath — but LEACH classic uses free space for BS):

$$
E_{CH} = (\tfrac{N}{k} - 1) E_{elec} k + N E_{DA} + E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{toBS}^2
$$

$$
= (19 \cdot 50 + 100 \cdot 5 + 50 + 10 \cdot 150^2) \cdot 2000
$$

$$
= (950 + 500 + 50 + 225000) \cdot 2000 = 226500 \cdot 2000 = 4.53 \times 10^{8} \text{ nJ} = 453 \text{ mJ}
$$

Per-node average (5 CHs + 95 members):

$$
E_{avg} = \frac{5 \cdot 453 + 95 \cdot 6.466}{100} = \frac{2265 + 614.3}{100} = 28.79 \text{ mJ/round}
$$

**Part (c) — Direct transmission comparison:** Each node transmits directly to BS at 150 m (use multipath since $d > d_0$):

$$
E_{direct} = E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{mp} k d^4 = (50 + 0.0013 \cdot 150^4) \cdot 2000
$$

$$
150^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{8} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.0013 \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{8} = 6.58 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
E_{direct} = (50 + 658000) \cdot 2000 = 6.58 \times 10^{8} \text{ nJ} = 658 \text{ mJ}
$$

**LEACH saves $658 - 28.79 \approx 629$ mJ per node per round → ~95.6% energy reduction.** This is why LEACH is a landmark protocol.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: WSN Network Layer Protocol Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node-ID Safeguard:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixed with letters (e.g., `protoA1`, `familyB`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`) are used as IDs. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[WSN NETWORK LAYER ROUTING]
    rootA --> familyA[DATA CENTRIC]
    rootA --> familyB[HIERARCHICAL]
    rootA --> familyC[LOCATION BASED]
    rootA --> familyD[QOS AND MULTIPATH]

    familyA --> protoA1[SPIN]
    familyA --> protoA2[DIRECTED DIFFUSION]
    familyA --> protoA3[RUMOR ROUTING]

    familyB --> protoB1[LEACH]
    familyB --> protoB2[PEGASIS]
    familyB --> protoB3[HEED]
    familyB --> protoB4[TEEN]

    familyC --> protoC1[GEAR]
    familyC --> protoC2[GAF]
    familyC --> protoC3[MECN]

    familyD --> protoD1[SPEED]
    familyD --> protoD2[SAR]
    familyD --> protoD3[ENERGY AWARE ROUTING]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: LEACH Operational Flow (Per Round)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startR[ROUND R STARTS]
    startR --> phase1[ADVERTISEMENT PHASE]
    phase1 --> phase2[CLUSTER SETUP PHASE]
    phase2 --> phase3[SCHEDULE CREATION]
    phase3 --> phase4[STEADY STATE PHASE]
    phase4 --> phase5[DATA TRANSMISSION MEMBER TO CH]
    phase5 --> phase6[AGGREGATION AT CH]
    phase6 --> phase7[CH TO BS TRANSMISSION]
    phase7 --> phase8[ROUND ENDS]
    phase8 --> startR

    phase1 --> ch1[EACH NODE PICKS RANDOM 0 TO 1]
    ch1 --> ch2[COMPUTES THRESHOLD T I]
    ch2 --> ch3[IF RAND LESS THAN T I NODE BECOMES CH]
    ch3 --> ch4[CH BROADCASTS ADV MESSAGE]

    phase2 --> clu1[MEMBERS CHOOSE NEAREST CH BY RSSI]
    clu1 --> clu2[MEMBERS SEND JOIN REQ TO CH]

    phase4 --> st1[CH CREATES TDMA SCHEDULE]
    st1 --> st2[MEMBERS TRANSMIT IN ALLOTTED SLOTS]
    st2 --> st3[CH AGGREGATES DATA]
    st3 --> st4[CH TRANSMITS AGGREGATED DATA TO BS]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Directed Diffusion — Four Phases

```mermaid
graph TD
    dd1[PHASE 1 INTEREST PROPAGATION]
    dd2[PHASE 2 GRADIENT SETUP]
    dd3[PHASE 3 DATA DISSEMINATION]
    dd4[PHASE 4 PATH REINFORCEMENT]

    dd1 --> dd1a[SINK BROADCASTS INTEREST NAMED ATTRIBUTE]
    dd1a --> dd1b[INTEREST FLOODS NETWORK]

    dd2 --> dd2a[EACH NODE STORES INTEREST AND GRADIENT]
    dd2a --> dd2b[GRADIENT POINTS TOWARDS NEIGHBOR THAT SENT INTEREST]

    dd3 --> dd3a[SOURCE NODES WITH MATCHING DATA SEND EVENTS]
    dd3a --> dd3b[EVENTS TRAVEL ALONG GRADIENTS TOWARD SINK]
    dd3b --> dd3c[MULTIPLE PATHS MAY CARRY DATA]

    dd4 --> dd4a[SINK REINFORCES ONE PATH BY SENDING POSITIVE REINFORCEMENT]
    dd4a --> dd4b[REINFORCED PATH HAS LOWER LATENCY]
    dd4b --> dd4c[OTHER PATHS KEPT AS BACKUP]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: PEGASIS Chain Formation & Token Passing

```mermaid
graph TD
    peg1[PEGASIS PROTOCOL]
    peg1 --> peg2[STEP 1 ALL NODES KNOW NETWORK LOCATION]
    peg2 --> peg3[STEP 2 BS COMPUTES CHAIN VIA GREEDY ALGORITHM]
    peg3 --> peg4[STEP 3 BROADCAST CHAIN TO ALL NODES]
    peg4 --> peg5[STEP 4 EACH ROUND ONE NODE LEADS TO BS]
    peg5 --> peg6[STEP 5 TOKEN PASSING FROM LEADER TO ENDS]
    peg6 --> peg7[NODE FUSES ITS DATA WITH NEIGHBORS DATA]
    peg7 --> peg8[FUSED PACKET SENT TO NEXT NODE IN CHAIN]
    peg8 --> peg9[FINAL FUSED PACKET REACHES LEADER]
    peg9 --> peg10[LEADER TRANSMITS TO BS]

    peg10 --> peg11[LEADER ROTATES EACH ROUND]
    peg11 --> peg5
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Cluster Head Energy Distribution (LEACH)

```mermaid
graph LR
    energyA[TOTAL ENERGY IN NETWORK]
    energyA --> energyB[CLUSTER HEAD ENERGY]
    energyA --> energyC[MEMBER NODE ENERGY]

    energyB --> eB1[RECEIVE FROM N BY K MINUS 1 NODES]
    eB1 --> eB2[AGGREGATE N SIGNALS]
    eB2 --> eB3[TRANSMIT TO BS LONG HAUL]

    energyC --> eC1[TRANSMIT TO CH SHORT HAUL]
    eC1 --> eC2[IDLE LISTEN DURING OWN SLOT]
    eC2 --> eC3[SLEEP DURING OTHER SLOTS]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: SPIN 3-Way Handshake

```mermaid
graph TD
    sp1[NODE A HAS NEW DATA X]
    sp1 --> sp2[A BROADCASTS ADV X TO NEIGHBORS]
    sp2 --> sp3[NEIGHBOR B WANTS DATA X]
    sp3 --> sp4[B SENDS REQ X TO A]
    sp4 --> sp5[A TRANSMITS DATA X TO B]
    sp5 --> sp6[B BECOMES SOURCE FOR X]
    sp6 --> sp1
```
