## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **KTU-style analytical problems** that frequently appear in Part B questions. Work through each step.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — AES Round Structure (FIPS-197)

The AES encryption algorithm with a 128-bit key consists of $N_r = 10$ rounds. Derive the round breakdown.

**Given:**
- Block size: $b = 128$ bits = 16 bytes.
- Key size: $k = 128$ bits.

**Step 1: Initial Round (Round 0)**

$$
\text{State}_{0} = \text{Plaintext} \oplus K_0
$$

where $K_0$ is the **initial round key** derived from the original key.

**Step 2: General Round (Rounds 1 to $N_r - 1$)**

For each round $i = 1, 2, \ldots, 9$:

$$
\text{State}_i = \text{MixColumns}(\text{ShiftRows}(\text{SubBytes}(\text{State}_{i-1} \oplus K_i)))
$$

**Step 3: Final Round (Round 10)**

The MixColumns step is **omitted** in the final round:

$$
\text{Ciphertext} = \text{ShiftRows}(\text{SubBytes}(\text{State}_9 \oplus K_{10}))
$$

**Step 4: Round Key Expansion**

Each $K_i$ is 128 bits and is derived from $K_{i-1}$ via the AES key schedule:
- **RotWord**: cyclic 1-byte left rotation.
- **SubWord**: apply S-box to each byte.
- **XOR with Rcon[i]**: round constant $Rcon[i] = (2^{i-1}, 0, 0, 0)$ in $GF(2^8)$.

$$
K_i = K_{i-1} \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(K_{i-1}[0:4])) \oplus Rcon[i]
$$

**Step 5: Total Computational Cost**

For a 128-bit key:
- **Total rounds:** $N_r = 10$.
- **Total S-box lookups:** $10 \times 16 = 160$.
- **Total XOR operations per round:** ~$(4 \times 4) + (4 \times 4) = 32$ per AddRoundKey and MixColumns.
- **Energy on ATmega128:** ~**9 µJ per block encryption** (TinySec benchmark).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Why is AES preferred over DES in WSNs?", answer with: **(1) Larger key (128 vs 56 bits)** → resists brute force; **(2) Larger block (128 vs 64 bits)** → less padding; **(3) Hardware support on modern motes**; **(4) No known practical cryptanalytic attack** unlike DES (broken by exhaustive search in 1998 for $2^{56}$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eschenauer–Gligor Key Pre-distribution Connectivity

**Problem (classic KTU question):**
> A WSN has $|S| = 100{,}000$ keys in the pool. Each node is pre-loaded with $k = 75$ random keys. What is the probability that two nodes share at least one common key?

**Step 1: Define the sample space.**

Total ways to choose $k$ keys from $|S|$:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \binom{|S|}{k} = \binom{100{,}000}{75}
$$

**Step 2: Define the favorable-but-failing event.**

The probability that **node B's key set is disjoint from node A's key set** is:

$$
P_{\text{disjoint}} = \frac{\binom{|S| - k}{k}}{\binom{|S|}{k}}
$$

This is because node B must choose all $k$ of its keys from the $|S| - k$ keys **not chosen by node A**.

**Step 3: Compute the connectivity probability.**

$$
P_c = 1 - P_{\text{disjoint}} = 1 - \frac{\binom{|S| - k}{k}}{\binom{|S|}{k}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$
P_c = 1 - \frac{\binom{100{,}000 - 75}{75}}{\binom{100{,}000}{75}} = 1 - \frac{\binom{99{,}925}{75}}{\binom{100{,}000}{75}}
$$

**Step 5: Approximate using the ratio.**

For large $|S|$ and small $k$:

$$
\frac{\binom{|S| - k}{k}}{\binom{|S|}{k}} \approx \left(1 - \frac{k}{|S|}\right)^k
$$

**Step 6: Numerical evaluation.**

$$
P_c \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{75}{100{,}000}\right)^{75} = 1 - (0.99925)^{75}
$$

**Step 7: Final calculation.**

$$
(0.99925)^{75} \approx e^{-75 \times 0.00075} = e^{-0.05625} \approx 0.9453
$$

$$
P_c \approx 1 - 0.9453 = 0.0547 \approx 5.47\%
$$

**Step 8: Increase $k$ for better connectivity.**

If $k = 200$:

$$
P_c \approx 1 - (1 - 0.002)^{200} = 1 - e^{-0.4} \approx 1 - 0.6703 = 32.97\%
$$

If $k = 500$:

$$
P_c \approx 1 - (1 - 0.005)^{500} = 1 - e^{-2.5} \approx 1 - 0.0821 = 91.79\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Larger $k$ → higher connectivity BUT larger memory cost and weaker resilience (more key overlap for an attacker to exploit). Typical sweet spot: $k \approx 200$ for $|S| = 100{,}000$ giving ~33% connectivity with reasonable resilience.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — RSA Public-Key Cryptosystem for WSNs

**Problem:** Show why RSA-1024 is impractical on a TelosB mote and how ECC-160 solves the problem.

**Step 1: RSA-1024 energy cost.**

A single **modular exponentiation** $C = P^e \bmod n$ with 1024-bit modulus and $e = 2^{16} + 1$ on an 8-bit ATmega128:

$$
E_{RSA-1024} \approx 0.5 \text{ J per decryption}
$$

**Step 2: WSN node energy budget.**

A TelosB mote with two AA batteries (each ~$2.85 \text{ kJ}$) running at 3V × 1.8 mA average draw:

$$
E_{\text{total}} \approx 2 \times 2.85 \text{ kJ} = 5.7 \text{ kJ} = 5700 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Number of RSA operations per node lifetime.**

$$
N_{ops} = \frac{5700 \text{ J}}{0.5 \text{ J/operation}} = 11{,}400 \text{ operations}
$$

**Step 4: Time per operation.**

At 8 MHz and ~1000 clock cycles per modular multiply, one RSA-1024 decryption takes:

$$
t = \frac{2^{20} \times 1000}{8 \times 10^6} \approx 0.131 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: ECC-160 alternative energy cost.**

A single **point multiplication** $Q = kG$ on a 160-bit elliptic curve:

$$
E_{ECC-160} \approx 6.1 \text{ mJ}
$$

That's **~82× faster** and **~82× more energy-efficient** than RSA-1024, while providing **equivalent cryptographic strength**.

**Step 6: Equivalent security strengths (NIST SP 800-57).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RSA-1024} &\longleftrightarrow \text{ECC-160} \\
\text{RSA-2048} &\longleftrightarrow \text{ECC-224} \\
\text{RSA-3072} &\longleftrightarrow \text{ECC-256}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: WSN conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** For WSNs, **ECC** is the asymmetric primitive of choice because it provides the same security as RSA with **~10× smaller key sizes and ~80× lower energy cost**. Protocols like **TinyPK** (Watro et al. 2004) and **TinyECC** (Liu & Ning 2008) bring ECC to motes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Comparison

| Protocol | Year | Layer | Cryptography | Energy | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SPINS** (SNEP + µTESLA) | 2002 | App/Transport | RC5 + CBC-MAC + µTESLA | Low | Strong | KDC dependency, stateful counters |
| **TinySec** | 2004 | Link | Skipjack/XTEA + CBC-MAC | Very Low | Moderate | No replay protection |
| **MiniSec** | 2007 | Link | AES-CCM (OCB mode) | Low | Strong | Patent restrictions on OCB |
| **TinyPK** | 2004 | App | RSA + Diffie-Hellman | High | Strong | High energy, not for data |
| **LEAP** (Localized Encryption) | 2003 | App | Multiple key types (pairwise, cluster, group) | Moderate | Strong | Per-node key storage |
| **TinyECC** | 2008 | App | ECC over $GF(p)$ or $GF(2^m)$ | Moderate | Strong | Slower than symmetric |

> [!NOTE]
> **TinySec is THE most cited WSN link-layer security protocol.** It was integrated into TinyOS and used skipjack/XTEA block ciphers with CBC-MAC. KTU questions on "WSN link layer security" almost always mean TinySec.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — µTESLA Authentication Delay

**Problem:** A WSN uses µTESLA with a 1-second interval $\delta$ and 4 clock-bits. If the maximum clock skew between sender and receiver is 2 seconds, what is the minimum disclosure delay $d$?

**Step 1: µTESLA equation.**

$$
d \cdot \delta \geq t_{max}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$
d \geq \frac{t_{max}}{\delta} = \frac{2 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ s}} = 2 \text{ intervals}
$$

**Step 3: Conclusion.**

The sender must wait **at least 2 time intervals** (i.e., 2 seconds) before disclosing the MAC key for time slot $i$. The receiver buffers all packets from interval $i$ and authenticates them only at interval $i + d$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this work?** Because the receiver can verify that at time $i$, the sender **does not yet know** the MAC key for interval $i$ (it only learns the key at $i + d$). An attacker cannot forge packets from interval $i$ in real time because it doesn't have the key yet — by the time it does (at $i + d$), the interval is already past.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1 — June 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **symmetric-key** and **asymmetric-key** cryptography. Why is symmetric-key cryptography preferred in WSN data communication?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Symmetric-Key | Asymmetric-Key |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys used** | Same secret key for encryption & decryption | Public + private key pair |
| **Speed** | **Fast** (hardware support, ~µs/operation) | **Slow** (modular exponentiation) |
| **Energy** | **~10 µJ** per operation (AES) | **~500 mJ** per RSA-1024 op |
| **Key count** | $O(n^2)$ pairwise / $O(n)$ with KDC | $O(n)$ public keys |
| **Best suited for** | Bulk data encryption (sensor readings) | Key establishment, signatures |

**Why SKC is preferred in WSNs:** Sensor data is **bulk, repetitive, and time-critical**. SKC's energy cost is **~10,000× lower** than RSA. Hence SKC (e.g., AES) is used for data confidentiality, while AKC is reserved for **key establishment** (e.g., TinyPK, ECDH) or authentication (e.g., ECDSA).

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2 — December 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the **Dolev-Yao adversary model**? How does it differ from the **lone outsider** model?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Dolev-Yao Model:** The adversary has **full control over the communication channel** — can **eavesdrop, intercept, inject, modify, replay, drop, and reorder** messages. The **only limitation** is that it cannot break the cryptographic primitives (cannot decrypt without the key or forge a MAC without the MAC key).
- **Lone Outsider Model:** A more restricted adversary that can **only eavesdrop passively**, but cannot inject or modify traffic.
- **Difference:** Dolev-Yao is **stronger and realistic** for WSNs (where the wireless medium is open). It forms the basis for **TinySec, SPINS, and MiniSec** security proofs.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **SPINS** security protocol suite for WSNs. With a neat diagram, describe **SNEP** and **µTESLA**. Discuss their cryptographic primitives, message formats, and security properties. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **challenges of implementing public-key cryptography in WSNs**. Compare **RSA** and **ECC** in terms of key size, energy, and security strength. Show that **160-bit ECC** provides equivalent security to **1024-bit RSA**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — SPINS Architecture (7 marks)**

**SPINS (Security Protocols for Sensor Networks)** was proposed by **Perrig, Szewczyk, Wen, Culler, and Tygar (2002)**. It has two components:

**1. SNEP — Secure Network Encryption Protocol**

- **Goal:** Provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and freshness** for point-to-point communication between a sensor node and the base station.
- **Cryptographic primitives:**
  - **Confidentiality:** Encryption using a block cipher in **CTR (counter) mode**. The counter is part of the message and incremented by both parties (stateless).
  - **Integrity & Authentication:** **CBC-MAC** computed over the encrypted data.
  - **Freshness:** **Counter-based** (each node maintains a counter synchronized with the base station).
- **Message format:**
  $$M = \{E_{K_{enc}}(D \vert \vert C), \text{MAC}_{K_{mac}}(C \vert \vert E_{K_{enc}}(D \vert \vert C))\}$$
  where $D$ = data, $C$ = counter, $K_{enc}$ = encryption key, $K_{mac}$ = MAC key.

- **Security properties:**
  - **Semantic security** (different ciphertext for same plaintext due to counter).
  - **Data authentication** (MAC verifies sender and integrity).
  - **Replay protection** (counter is monotonically increasing).
  - **Low communication overhead** — only a small counter added per message.

**2. µTESLA — Micro Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication**

- **Goal:** Authenticate **broadcast** messages from base station to all nodes.
- **Key idea:** Use **symmetric MAC keys** but disclose them with a **time delay**, creating an **asymmetric effect** without public-key cryptography.
- **Phases:**
  1. **Sender setup:** Base station computes a **one-way key chain** $K_0, K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_n$ where $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ for a one-way function $F$ (e.g., SHA-1).
  2. **Sending:** At interval $i$, base station sends $\{P_i \vert \vert \text{MAC}_{K_i}(P_i)\}$. The key $K_i$ is **not sent yet**.
  3. **Receiving:** Nodes buffer packets from interval $i$.
  4. **Disclosure:** At interval $i + d$, the base station discloses $K_i$. Nodes verify $K_i$ by checking $F(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ (the previously disclosed key), confirming the chain integrity.
  5. **Authentication:** Once $K_i$ is verified, the buffered MACs from interval $i$ can be checked.
- **Loose time synchronization** is required between sender and receiver.
- **Disclosure delay equation:** $d \cdot \delta \geq t_{max}$ where $\delta$ = interval duration, $t_{max}$ = max clock skew.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** See Section 4.5.

**Why SPINS is energy-efficient:** SNEP's CTR mode + CBC-MAC is ~10 µJ per packet, and µTESLA's broadcast authentication avoids per-receiver signatures.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — RSA vs ECC in WSNs (7 marks)**

**Challenges of PKC in WSNs:**

| Challenge | Detail |
|---|---|
| **CPU** | Modular exponentiation is **10⁴–10⁶× slower** than symmetric encryption. An 8-bit ATmega128 needs **0.13 s per RSA-1024 op**. |
| **Memory** | RSA-1024 needs **1024-bit buffers** (~128 bytes). ECC-160 needs **160-bit buffers** (~20 bytes). |
| **Energy** | RSA-1024 decryption: **~500 mJ**. AES-128: **~10 µJ**. Ratio: **~50,000×**. |
| **Code size** | Standard RSA library: **>50 KB**. TinyOS image: **<128 KB** total flash. |
| **Bandwidth** | RSA-1024 signature: **128 bytes per packet**. TinyOS packet: **≤127 bytes** total. |

**RSA vs ECC Comparison Table:**

| Property | RSA-1024 | ECC-160 |
|---|---|---|
| **Key size** | 1024 bits (128 bytes) | 160 bits (20 bytes) |
| **Equivalent security** | ~80 bits | ~80 bits |
| **Energy per op** | ~500 mJ | ~6 mJ |
| **Speed** | ~0.13 s | ~1.6 ms |
| **Storage** | 128 B | 20 B |
| **Operations** | Modular exponentiation | Point multiplication |

**Proof of Equivalence (NIST SP 800-57, 2016):**

> The best known attack on RSA-1024 is the **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)** with complexity:
> $$\exp\left((1.923 + o(1)) \cdot (\ln 2^1024)^{1/3} \cdot (\ln \ln 2^1024)^{2/3}\right) \approx 2^{80}$$

> The best known attack on ECC-160 is **Pollard's rho** with complexity:
> $$\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n}) = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{2^{160}}) = \mathcal{O}(2^{80})$$

**Both require ~$2^{80}$ operations to break**, hence they are **cryptographically equivalent**. However, ECC achieves this with **6.4× smaller keys** and **~80× lower energy** — a winning trade-off for WSNs.

**Conclusion:** For WSNs, **ECC-160 (or higher)** is the recommended PKC primitive. Real-world implementations: **TinyPK** (Watro et al. 2004), **TinyECC** (Liu & Ning 2008).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Eschenauer–Gligor key pre-distribution scheme** for WSNs. Derive the connectivity probability formula. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **AES encryption algorithm** for WSNs. Show the round structure for a 128-bit key. Compare **AES** with **DES** and justify why AES is preferred in resource-constrained WSNs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Eschenauer–Gligor KPS (7 marks)**

**Overview:**
The **Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) scheme (2002)** is a **probabilistic key pre-distribution scheme** that solves the key distribution problem for WSNs by leveraging random key pre-loading.

**Three Phases:**

**Phase 1: Key Generation (Offline)**
- A **Key Distribution Server (KDS)** generates a large **master key pool** $S$ of size $|S|$.
- Each key in $S$ is assigned a unique ID.

**Phase 2: Key Pre-distribution (Offline)**
- For each sensor node, the KDS **randomly selects $k$ keys** (without replacement) from the pool $S$.
- These $k$ keys (with their IDs) are stored in the node's memory before deployment.
- The KDS also stores the mapping of which keys each node received.

**Phase 3: Shared-Key Discovery (Online, Post-deployment)**
- After deployment, each node **broadcasts** the list of key IDs it carries.
- A neighbor node $B$ that hears node $A$'s list checks for **overlap**.
- If they share at least one common key, they can establish a **secure link**.

**Key-Graph Establishment:**
- The shared keys form a **key graph** $G(V, E)$ where $V$ = nodes, $E$ = secure links.
- Multi-hop paths can be established across nodes sharing keys.

**Path Key Establishment:**
- If two nodes $A$ and $D$ don't share a key directly, but both share keys with an intermediate node $B$, a **path key** $K_{AD}$ can be established via $B$.

**Connectivity Probability (already derived in §3.2):**

$$
P_c = 1 - \frac{\binom{|S| - k}{k}}{\binom{|S|}{k}} \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{|S|}\right)^k
$$

**Example (recap):** For $|S| = 100{,}000$ and $k = 500$, $P_c \approx 91.79\%$.

**Advantages:**

| # | Advantage |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Low memory per node** — only $k$ keys (e.g., 200 keys × 64 bits = 1.6 KB) |
| 2 | **Probabilistic resilience** — capturing $x$ nodes compromises only a fraction of the network |
| 3 | **No base station dependency** at runtime (unlike SPINS) |
| 4 | **Scalable** to thousands of nodes |
| 5 | **Localized** — only neighbors need to share keys |

**Limitations:**

| # | Limitation |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Probabilistic, not deterministic** — some nodes may be **isolated** (no shared key with any neighbor) |
| 2 | **Selective node capture** — an attacker who captures a node gets $k$ keys and can attack $k(k-1)/2$ secure links |
| 3 | **No authentication of public key IDs** — vulnerable to **key-predistribution attacks** unless IDs are authenticated |
| 4 | **No node revocation** mechanism in the basic scheme |
| 5 | **Limited scalability of security** as network grows |

**Improvements on EG:**

- **q-Composite Scheme** (Chan et al. 2003) — requires $q \geq 1$ shared keys for higher resilience.
- **Polynomial Pool-Based** (Liu & Ning 2003) — uses bivariate polynomials $f(x, y)$ with threshold $t$ for $t$-collusion resistance.
- **Blom's Scheme** (1985) — uses a **symmetric matrix** $K = A \cdot G \cdot A^T$ for perfect $t$-secure key distribution.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — AES Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram of AES-128:** See Section 4.4.

**AES Specifications (FIPS-197):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Block size** | 128 bits (16 bytes) |
| **Key size** | 128 / 192 / 256 bits |
| **Rounds** | 10 / 12 / 14 |
| **S-box** | 8-bit substitution (invertible, GF(2⁸)) |
| **Operations** | SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey |

**Round Structure (128-bit key, 10 rounds):**

- **Round 0:** AddRoundKey ($K_0$)
- **Rounds 1–9:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey ($K_i$)
- **Round 10:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey ($K_{10}$) (no MixColumns)

**Four Transformations:**

1. **SubBytes:** Each byte replaced via an invertible S-box.
   $$b_{ij}' = S[b_{ij}]$$
2. **ShiftRows:** Row $r$ is cyclically shifted left by $r$ bytes.
3. **MixColumns:** Each column is multiplied by a fixed $4 \times 4$ matrix over $GF(2^8)$.
4. **AddRoundKey:** XOR with the round key.
   $$\text{State} = \text{State} \oplus K_i$$

**AES vs DES Comparison:**

| Property | DES | AES |
|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1977 (NIST) | 2001 (NIST) |
| **Block size** | 64 bits | **128 bits** |
| **Key size** | 56 bits (effective) | **128/192/256 bits** |
| **Rounds** | 16 (Feistel) | **10/12/14 (SPN)** |
| **Structure** | Feistel network | **Substitution-Permutation Network** |
| **Brute-force time** | $2^{56}$ ≈ hours (1998) | $2^{128}$ ≈ **age of universe** |
| **Status** | **Broken** (1998 EFF Deep Crack) | **Secure** (as of 2024) |
| **Energy per block (TinyOS)** | ~30 µJ | **~9 µJ** |
| **Code size** | Smaller | Moderate |
| **Hardware support** | Legacy | **Modern motes (CC2420 radio, ATmega128 crypto co-processor)** |

**Why AES is preferred in WSNs:**

1. **Security:** 128-bit key is **beyond brute force** ($2^{128}$ ops). DES is broken.
2. **Efficiency on modern hardware:** AES-NI instructions on newer motes achieve < 1 µJ per block.
3. **Block size 128 bits:** Reduces padding overhead for typical 16-byte sensor packets.
4. **Standardization:** FIPS-197, ISO/IEC 18033-3 — guaranteed interoperability.
5. **Open source implementations:** TinyAES, AES-CCM readily available for TinyOS/Contiki.
6. **Authenticated encryption modes (GCM, OCB, CCM)** are natively supported.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ECB and CBC** — students often claim ECB is "more secure" because of parallelism. Actually, **ECB is insecure** for WSNs because identical plaintexts produce identical ciphertexts (vulnerability to traffic analysis).
> 2. **Forgetting freshness** — encryption alone does NOT prevent **replay attacks**. You must also use **counter, nonce, or timestamp** + **MAC**.
> 3. **Saying "RSA is used in WSNs"** — wrong. RSA is **not used in WSNs** for data encryption. Use **ECC** if PKC is needed.
> 4. **Not distinguishing link-layer vs application-layer security** — TinySec/MiniSec = link layer; SPINS/TinyECC = application layer. KTU marks are split for this.
> 5. **Forgetting the energy cost of MAC verification** — CBC-MAC is sequential; OCB/CCM are parallel and ~2× faster.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — A neat diagram of SPINS or AES rounds is worth **2 marks** on its own in Part B.
> 7. **Mixing up µTESLA and SPINS** — µTESLA is a **sub-component** of SPINS, not a standalone protocol.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam.**

#### ✅ **Core Concepts**
- [ ] WSN security must work within **< 1 J** energy budget per node.
- [ ] **Dolev-Yao adversary** = worst-case (eavesdrop + inject + replay + drop).
- [ ] Security services: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Freshness, Authorization**.
- [ ] **SKC** for data; **PKC (ECC)** for key establishment only.
- [ ] AES-128 is the **de facto** WSN symmetric primitive.

#### ✅ **Key Numbers to Memorize**
- [ ] AES block size: **128 bits**.
- [ ] AES rounds for 128-bit key: **10**.
- [ ] AES energy: **~9–10 µJ per block** (ATmega128).
- [ ] RSA-1024 energy: **~500 mJ** (≈ 50,000× AES).
- [ ] **160-bit ECC ≈ 1024-bit RSA** in security.
- [ ] SHA-256 output: **256 bits**.
- [ ] MD5 output: **128 bits** (broken, don't use).
- [ ] IEEE 802.15.4 packet: **≤127 bytes**.

#### ✅ **Protocols to Know Cold**
- [ ] **SPINS** (SNEP + µTESLA) — Perrig et al. 2002.
- [ ] **TinySec** — link-layer, Skipjack/XTEA + CBC-MAC.
- [ ] **MiniSec** — link-layer, AES-OCB (authenticated encryption).
- [ ] **LEAP** — multi-key (pairwise, cluster, group, individual).
- [ ] **TinyPK** — RSA-based key establishment.
- [ ] **TinyECC** — efficient ECC library for motes.

#### ✅ **Key Management Schemes**
- [ ] Single global key: O(1) memory, O(n) compromise.
- [ ] Pairwise keys: O(n) memory, perfect resilience.
- [ ] KDC / base station: O(1) memory, KDC = single point of failure.
- [ ] **Eschenauer–Gligor** (probabilistic, O(k) memory).
- [ ] **q-Composite** (requires q shared keys).
- [ ] **Polynomial / Blom's** (threshold $t$-secure).
- [ ] **Connectivity formula:** $P_c \approx 1 - (1 - k/|S|)^k$.

#### ✅ **Cryptographic Modes**
- [ ] **ECB** ❌ never use.
- [ ] **CBC** ✅ (TinySec).
- [ ] **CTR** ✅✅ (best for WSNs).
- [ ] **OCB** ✅✅ (MiniSec — authenticated encryption).
- [ ] **CCM** ✅✅ (AES-CCM, used in IEEE 802.15.4 security suite).

#### ✅ **Attack-Defense Pairs (Memorize)**
- [ ] **Eavesdropping** → Encryption (AES-CTR).
- [ ] **Packet injection** → MAC authentication.
- [ ] **Replay** → Freshness (counter / timestamp).
- [ ] **Sybil** → Identity authentication + ECC signatures.
- [ ] **Wormhole** → Geographic + time-of-flight checks.
- [ ] **Node capture** → Tamper-resistant hardware + key refresh.
- [ ] **DoS / Jamming** → Spread spectrum, frequency hopping (not crypto).

#### ✅ **Final Exam Strategy**
- [ ] Always draw a **diagram** in Part B (SPINS, AES, KPS).
- [ ] Use **bullet points and tables** — KTU examiners value clarity.
- [ ] When asked "Why X over Y?", always quantify: **energy (µJ/mJ/J), memory (bytes), security (bits)**.
- [ ] Mention **Dolev-Yao** explicitly when discussing threat models.
- [ ] Distinguish **link-layer** (TinySec, MiniSec) from **application-layer** (SPINS, TinyECC).
- [ ] If asked about ECC vs RSA, **always show the energy calculation** (it impresses examiners).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic are typically framed as: *"Explain the role of cryptography in securing WSNs. Discuss the protocols, key management schemes, and challenges."* — Structure your answer with the three-pillar framework (SKC, PKC, Hash) + two protocol layers (link + application) + key management (KPS families) + challenges. **10/10 guaranteed.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Management Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Key Management Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Key Management** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, and procedures used to generate, distribute, store, update, and revoke cryptographic keys among sensor nodes to enable secure communication. Due to severe resource constraints in sensor nodes (limited energy, memory, computation, and bandwidth), traditional key management schemes used in wired networks are infeasible. A **Key Management Protocol (KMP)** is therefore a lightweight, energy-efficient, and scalable cryptographic key lifecycle management framework specifically engineered for resource-constrained WSN environments.

The fundamental objective of any key management protocol is to establish a **shared secret key** between communicating nodes such that confidentiality, integrity, and authentication of sensor data is preserved across the multi-hop wireless medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Key Management is the process of handling cryptographic keys in a sensor network including key generation, key distribution, key storage, key update, and key revocation, while minimizing memory overhead, communication cost, and energy consumption.

### 1.2 Key Challenges in WSN Key Management

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Resource Scarcity** | Sensor motes have typically **4–10 KB RAM** and **48–128 KB Flash** (e.g., Mica2, TelosB) |
| **Energy Budget** | Each radio transmission consumes ~**50–100 mW**; asymmetric crypto is expensive |
| **No Fixed Infrastructure** | Ad-hoc deployment; no centralized KDC always available |
| **Node Capture / Physical Tampering** | Adversary can extract all secrets from a captured node |
| **Scale (Density)** | Networks can scale to **thousands of nodes** per cubic meter |
| **Unreliable Links** | Packet loss up to **30–70%** in hostile RF environments |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Neighborhood Lockbox" Analogy**
>
> Imagine moving into a new apartment complex with **1000 strangers** and no central locksmith. You cannot personally meet every neighbor to exchange house keys (impractical). So instead, before you move in, the post office randomly mails you **50 small lockboxes**, each opening with a unique key, AND a random subset of **50 keys** belonging to other residents. When you meet a neighbor, you try your keys on their lockbox. If a match exists, you two are now "cryptographically bonded" — you share a secret and can communicate securely.
>
> This is exactly how **Random Key Predistribution (RKP)** works. The "lockboxes and keys" are preloaded before deployment, and probabilistic matching establishes secure links **after** deployment.

### 1.4 Classification of Key Management Protocols

```
Key Management Protocols
        │
        ├─── Symmetric-Key Based
        │       ├── Random Key Predistribution (Eschenauer-Gligor)
        │       ├── q-Composite Key Predistribution
        │       ├── Random Pairwise Key Scheme
        │       └── LEAP (Localized Encryption & Auth Protocol)
        │
        ├─── Asymmetric-Key Based
        │       ├── Public Key Cryptography in WSN
        │       ├── Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) for WSN
        │       └── TinyPK / TinyECC
        │
        ├─── Hybrid / Hierarchical
        │       ├── SPINS (SNEP + µTESLA)
        │       ├── BROSK / EBS (Exclusion Basis System)
        │       └── Cluster-based Schemes
        │
        └─── Key Management for Heterogeneous / Mobile WSN
                ├── PANEL (Position-based)
                ├── SHELL
                └── Asymmetric pre-distribution
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Design Goals of a KMP
A robust key management protocol for WSN must satisfy the following design criteria:

1. **Confidentiality** – Eavesdroppers cannot decode intercepted traffic.
2. **Authentication** – Both endpoints verify identity of the other.
3. **Integrity** – Tampered packets are detectable.
4. **Forward & Backward Secrecy** – Compromise of node *X* must not reveal past or future keys of non-compromised nodes.
5. **Resilience** – Capturing *x* nodes should not collapse the entire network's security.
6. **Scalability** – Performance should not degrade as node count grows.
7. **Low Energy Footprint** – Minimal additional transmissions/computations.

### 2.2 Symmetric Key Predistribution Schemes

#### 2.2.1 Basic Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) Scheme
**Operational Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Key Pool Generation:** Off-line server generates a large pool **P** of random symmetric keys, each of length **k** bits (typically **k = 64 or 128**).
- **Step 2 — Key Ring Assignment:** Each node *i* is pre-loaded with a randomly chosen subset of **m** keys drawn from **P**. This subset is called the **key ring** of the node.
- **Step 3 — Shared Key Discovery:** After deployment, every node broadcasts identifiers (or hashed values) of its key ring. Nodes sharing ≥ 1 common key can establish a **link key**.
- **Step 4 — Path Key Establishment:** If two neighbors share no common key, they negotiate a path key through intermediate nodes that share keys with both endpoints.

**The Critical Probability:** For two nodes with key rings of size **m** drawn from a pool of size **|P|**, the probability that they share at least one common key is:

$$p_{shared} = 1 - \frac{\binom{|P|}{m}\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}^2} = 1 - \frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}}$$

This simplifies to a well-known approximation for large **|P|**:

$$p_{shared} \approx 1 - \frac{((|P|-m)!)^2}{|P|!(|P|-2m)!}$$

#### 2.2.2 q-Composite Key Predistribution
Improvement over EG requiring **q ≥ 1** common keys between two nodes.

- Each shared key $K_i$ is hashed to form the link key: $K_{link} = \text{H}(K_1 \Vert K_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert K_q)$.
- The probability of sharing **at least q** keys is computed via the hypergeometric distribution:

$$P_{q\text{-comp}} = \sum_{i=q}^{m} \frac{\binom{m}{i}\binom{|P|-m}{m-i}}{\binom{|P|}{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Higher *q* improves resilience against small-scale node capture but reduces connectivity (fewer pairs reach threshold *q*). Trade-off must be balanced.

#### 2.2.3 Random Pairwise Key Scheme (Chan-Perrig-Song)
- Server pre-generates **n(n-1)/2** unique pairwise keys for *n* nodes.
- Each node stores exactly **n-1** keys (one per other node).
- **Probability of link establishment = 1** (deterministic).
- **Memory cost** = **O(n)** — does not scale to large networks.

#### 2.2.4 LEAP (Localized Encryption and Authentication Protocol)
Four key types per node:
1. **Individual Key** $K_I^u$ — shared with the base station.
2. **Pairwise Key** $K_{AB}$ — shared with immediate neighbors.
3. **Cluster Key** $K_{C}^u$ — shared with all neighbors of *u*.
4. **Group Key** $K_{G}$ — shared by all nodes in the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight of LEAP:** Every node is pre-loaded with an **initial master key** $K_{IN}$. After a short time $T_{min}$ (e.g., **few seconds**), $K_{IN}$ is **erased** from memory. This makes passive key disclosure attacks via single-node capture significantly harder.

### 2.3 Asymmetric Key Management in WSN

Despite traditional beliefs, modern work (e.g., **Gura et al., 2004**) has shown **ECC point multiplication** can be performed in **~0.8 seconds** on 8-bit processors. **RSA-1024** signature verification takes ~**0.79 s** on ATmega128.

- **Public Key Size:** ECC-160 ≈ 320 bits; RSA-1024 ≈ 1024 bits.
- **Key Agreement:** **ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman)** is preferred for WSN.
- **Key Transport:** **ECDSA / TinyPK** for signature-based authentication.

### 2.4 SPINS — Security Protocols for Sensor Networks
A two-component suite:
- **SNEP (Sensor Network Encryption Protocol)** — provides confidentiality, authentication, integrity, and freshness using a shared master secret.
- **µTESLA (micro Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication)** — provides **authenticated broadcast** using delayed symmetric key disclosure.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Formula / Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\|P\|$ | Key pool size | 10,000 – 100,000 | Fixed offline parameter |
| $m$ | Key ring size per node | 50 – 250 | $\|P\| \cdot p$ |
| $k$ | Key length (bits) | **64 / 128** | $k = \lceil \log_2 \|P\| \rceil$ |
| $n$ | Network size (nodes) | 100 – 10,000 | Deployment scale |
| $d$ | Average node degree | 5 – 50 | Expected neighbors |
| $p_{shared}$ | Prob. two nodes share key | 0.33 – 0.99 | $1 - \frac{((|P|-m)!)^2}{\vert P \vert !(\vert P \vert -2m)!}$ |
| $P_{q\text{-comp}}$ | Prob. of q common keys | 0.0 – 1.0 | $\sum_{i=q}^{m} \frac{\binom{m}{i}\binom{\vert P \vert -m}{m-i}}{\binom{\vert P \vert}{m}}$ |
| $E_{TX}$ | Energy for transmission | ~50 nJ/bit | Energy model |
| $E_{RX}$ | Energy for reception | ~50 nJ/bit | Energy model |
| $T_{min}$ | LEAP setup window | seconds | Time-to-erase $K_{IN}$ |
| $E_{comp}$ | ECC op energy | ~15 mJ | ECDH on Mica2 |

> **Pitfall:** When using $\|P\|$ in formulas, use `\vert P \vert` in LaTeX — never `|P|` inside a markdown table cell, as the pipe character breaks the table parser.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Military Battlefield Surveillance:** Key management ensures that intercepted enemy radio traffic stays unintelligible.
- **Smart Grid & SCADA:** Authenticated sensor readings prevent false billing and false demand signals.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Encrypted patient telemetry over WSN (e.g., CodeBlue, Alarm-Net) requires lightweight KMPs.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Data integrity is critical for scientific validity (e.g., volcanic monitoring, glacier telemetry).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Key Sharing in EG Scheme

We want to find the probability that two randomly chosen nodes, each holding a key ring of size **m** from a pool of size **|P|**, share **at least one common key**.

**Step 1 — Total Ways to Choose Two Key Rings of Size m**
The number of ways to choose two unordered rings of size *m* each from a pool of size *|P|* is:

$$N_{total} = \binom{|P|}{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Ways to Choose Two Key Rings with NO Common Element**
This is the classic "two independent samples with disjoint support" problem. The first ring can be chosen in $\binom{|P|}{m}$ ways. The second ring must be chosen from the remaining *|P| - m* keys, again of size *m*:

$$N_{disjoint} = \binom{|P|}{m} \cdot \binom{|P|-m}{m}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of No Common Key**

$$P(no\ common\ key) = \frac{N_{disjoint}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of at Least One Common Key**

$$p_{shared} = 1 - P(no\ common\ key) = 1 - \frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}}$$

**Step 5 — Stirling's Approximation for Large Pools**
For large *|P|* and small *m*, apply Stirling's approximation. Expanding $\binom{|P|-m}{m}/\binom{|P|}{m}$:

$$\frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}} = \frac{(|P|-m)! / (m!(|P|-2m)!)}{|P|! / (m!(|P|-m)!)} = \frac{((|P|-m)!)^2}{|P|!(|P|-2m)!}$$

Thus:

$$p_{shared} = 1 - \frac{((|P|-m)!)^2}{|P|!\,(|P|-2m)!}$$

**Step 6 — Approximation via $p = m / |P|$**
Let $p = m / |P|$. Then using the limit identity for small $p$:

$$\frac{((|P|-m)!)^2}{|P|!\,(|P|-2m)!} \approx (1-p)^{m} \approx (1 - m/|P|)^{m} \approx e^{-m^2/|P|}$$

So the **classic closed-form** is:

$$p_{shared} \approx 1 - e^{-m^2 / |P|}$$

This is the most quoted form in KTU/KTU-style exams.

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**
Let *|P| = 10,000* and *m = 200*. Then:

$$m^2 / |P| = 200^2 / 10{,}000 = 4$$

$$p_{shared} \approx 1 - e^{-4} = 1 - 0.0183 = 0.9817$$

So **≈ 98.17%** of any two nodes share at least one key — extremely high connectivity.

### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Number of Secure Links per Node (Node Degree)

**Step 1 — Expected Neighbors in Wireless Range**
Let $d$ be the average physical node degree (i.e., number of RF neighbors). Typical outdoor WSN deployments have $d \in [5, 50]$.

**Step 2 — Expected Secure Links**
Each RF neighbor is independently "secured" with probability $p_{shared}$. By linearity of expectation:

$$E[secure\ links] = d \cdot p_{shared}$$

For $d = 20$ and $p_{shared} = 0.9817$:

$$E[secure\ links] = 20 \times 0.9817 = 19.63$$

The expected probability of a node being **isolated** (no secure neighbor) is:

$$P_{isolated} = (1 - p_{shared})^d = (0.0183)^{20} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-32}$$

Essentially zero — guarantees connectivity.

### 3.3 Derivation: Resilience Against Node Capture

Let $x$ nodes be physically captured. The fraction of pool keys compromised is:

$$f_{comp} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{m}{|P|}\right)^x$$

The probability that a non-captured link is broken:

$$p_{break} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{x}\left(1 - \frac{m}{|P|}\right) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{m}{|P|}\right)^x$$

For *|P| = 10,000*, *m = 200*, *x = 50*:

$$p_{break} = 1 - (1 - 0.02)^{50} = 1 - 0.98^{50} = 1 - 0.364 = 0.636$$

**~63.6% of non-captured links** are broken when 50 nodes are compromised — EG scheme has **poor resilience at low capture thresholds**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favorite)

| Property | EG Scheme | q-Composite | Pairwise | LEAP | SPINS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory/node** | $O(m)$ | $O(m)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(4)$ | $O(2)$ |
| **Connectivity** | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Deterministic |
| **Resilience to Capture** | Moderate | High (large q) | Perfect | High (per-link) | High |
| **Computation Cost** | Hash only | Hash only | Hash only | MAC + Hash | MAC + Hash + PRF |
| **Communication Overhead** | Low | Low | High (n-1 IDs) | Low | Low |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Limited | Better | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (O(n)) | Good | Good |
| **Node Capture Resistance (x small)** | Poor | Better | Perfect | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Authentication** | Weak | Weak | Strong | Strong | Strong |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical: Path Key Establishment

**Problem:** A network has *|P| = 5000*, *m = 100*. Two neighbors A and B share **no common key**. They need a path key via an intermediate node C that shares a key with both.

**Step 1:** Probability A and C share a key:

$$p_{AC} = 1 - e^{-m^2/|P|} = 1 - e^{-100^2/5000} = 1 - e^{-2} = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647$$

**Step 2:** Similarly, $p_{BC} = 0.8647$.

**Step 3:** Probability of **at least one** such intermediary C existing among *d = 30* common neighbors:

$$p_{path} = 1 - (1 - p_{AC} \cdot p_{BC})^{d} = 1 - (1 - 0.8647^2)^{30} = 1 - (1 - 0.7477)^{30}$$

$$p_{path} = 1 - (0.2523)^{30} \approx 1.0$$

Thus path key establishment is essentially guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Key Management Protocol Lifecycle

### 4.3 State Diagram: Key Ring Discovery and Path Key Negotiation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialized
    Initialized --> Discovering: Boot-up, broadcast key IDs
    Discovering --> SharedKeyFound: At least 1 common key in ring
    Discovering --> NoSharedKey: Zero overlap in key rings
    SharedKeyFound --> SecureLinkActive: Compute link key via hash
    SecureLinkActive --> Rekeying: Periodic timer expires
    NoSharedKey --> PathKeyNegotiation: Find intermediary C
    PathKeyNegotiation --> SecureLinkActive: Path key received via C
    SecureLinkActive --> Compromised: Neighbor captured
    Compromised --> KeyRevocation: Trigger broadcast
    KeyRevocation --> SecureLinkActive: New keys distributed
    SecureLinkActive --> [*]: Node energy depletion
    Compromised --> [*]: Node physically lost
```

    N2 -->|Cluster Key K_c^v| N3

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define key management in WSN. List any four challenges specific to WSN key management.**

**Model Answer:**

Key management in Wireless Sensor Networks refers to the set of procedures used to securely generate, distribute, store, update, and revoke cryptographic keys among resource-constrained sensor nodes, with the goal of establishing pairwise or group secrets to enable secure communication.

**Four Challenges:**
1. **Severe resource constraints** — limited RAM (4–10 KB), flash (48–128 KB), CPU (8 MHz), and energy (~2 AA batteries).
2. **No fixed infrastructure** — no always-available Key Distribution Center (KDC) in ad-hoc deployments.
3. **Physical node capture vulnerability** — adversaries can extract all keys from a single captured mote.
4. **Unreliable wireless medium** — packet loss up to 30–70% hampers handshake-based protocols.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key management approaches in WSN.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| Key Type | Same secret key shared | Public-private keypair |
| Computation | Cheap (hash, XOR, MAC) | Expensive (modular exp, ECC) |
| Memory | Small key storage | Larger key sizes |
| Speed | Fast (suitable for WSN) | Slower |
| Examples in WSN | LEAP, EG, q-Composite | TinyPK, TinyECC, ECDH |
| Scalability | Better with predistribution | Better for authentication |
| Energy Cost | Low | High (but ECC feasible) |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

#### 🔹 QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Eschenauer–Gligor (EG) random key predistribution scheme** in detail. Derive the expression for the probability that two nodes share at least one common key. Assume a network with key pool size *|P| = 10,000* and key ring size *m = 150*. Compute the probability of key sharing.

**Model Solution:**

**EG Scheme Phases (4 phases):**
1. **Key Pool Generation:** Offline Key Distribution Server (KDS) creates a pool of **|P|** random symmetric keys, each *k* bits long.
2. **Key Ring Selection:** Each sensor node *i* is pre-loaded with **m** keys randomly drawn (without replacement) from *P*. This is the node's *key ring*.
3. **Key Discovery Phase:** After deployment, each node broadcasts an identifier list (or hash list) of its key ring. Two neighbors with at least one common identifier establish a **shared link key** $K_{AB} = H(K_{shared})$.
4. **Path Key Establishment:** If no common key exists between A and B, they use an intermediate node C that shares a key with both to relay a freshly generated path key.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1 above):**
Two rings of size *m* chosen from a pool of size *|P|*.

Total ways to choose two rings: $\binom{|P|}{m}^2$

Ways to choose two disjoint rings: $\binom{|P|}{m} \cdot \binom{|P|-m}{m}$

Probability of no overlap: $\frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}}$

Probability of at least one shared key:
$$p_{shared} = 1 - \frac{\binom{|P|-m}{m}}{\binom{|P|}{m}} \approx 1 - e^{-m^2/|P|}$$

**Numerical Computation:**
With *|P| = 10,000* and *m = 150*:

$$m^2 / |P| = 150^2 / 10{,}000 = 22{,}500 / 10{,}000 = 2.25$$

$$p_{shared} \approx 1 - e^{-2.25} = 1 - 0.1054 = 0.8946$$

**∴ Probability of key sharing ≈ 89.46%**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **LEAP (Localized Encryption and Authentication Protocol)**. Discuss how it provides **node-capture resilience** and why the **initial master key $K_{IN}$** is erased after time $T_{min}$.

**Model Solution:**

**LEAP — Four Key Types per node u:**

1. **Individual Key $K_I^u$:** Node *u* shares this only with the Base Station. Used for BS-to-node secure messaging.

2. **Pairwise Key $K_{uv}$:** Shared between node *u* and each neighbor *v*. Used for secure neighbor-to-neighbor communication.

3. **Cluster Key $K_C^u$:** Shared between *u* and all its neighbors. Used for local broadcast within a cluster.

4. **Group Key $K_G$:** Shared globally across the entire network. Used for network-wide broadcast by the BS.

**Establishment of Pairwise Key (Master-Key-Based):**

Each node *u* is pre-loaded with a unique initial master key $K_{IN}$ and its own ID. After deployment:
- Node *u* computes $K_{uv} = f_{K_{IN}}(v)$ for each neighbor *v* it discovers (one-way function).
- After time $T_{min}$, all nodes **erase $K_{IN}$** from memory.

**Why Erase $K_{IN}$?**
- $K_{IN}$ is a *universal key* — capturing any single node exposes the key derivation function and *f_{K_{IN}}(v)$* for all *v*, compromising the **entire network's pairwise keys**.
- Erasing $K_{IN}$ after $T_{min}$ (chosen to exceed the maximum neighbor discovery time) **closes this vulnerability window**.
- After erasure, the attacker cannot derive any new pairwise keys even if they capture a node, because the function is no longer computable.

**Node-Capture Resilience:**
- $K_{IN}$ is gone → attacker cannot compute future pairwise keys.
- Cluster key is dynamically updated via pairwise-secure messages.
- Group key updates use µTESLA-style delayed disclosure.
- Compromised node reveals only **its own keys**, not others'.

**LEAP Architecture (Refer to Section 4.4 diagram).**

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **SPINS (Security Protocols for Sensor Networks)** with its two components **SNEP and µTESLA**. How does µTESLA provide **authenticated broadcast** in WSN?

**Model Solution:**

**SPINS Architecture:**
SPINS is a suite of two security protocols designed by Perrig et al. (2002) for sensor networks:

**1. SNEP (Sensor Network Encryption Protocol):**
Provides point-to-point secure communication with:
- **Confidentiality** — using RC5 stream cipher with a counter (CTR mode).
- **Authentication & Integrity** — using OMAC (One-key CBC-MAC).
- **Semantic Security** — counter ensures same plaintext produces different ciphertext.
- **Replay Protection** — message counters at both sender and receiver.
- **Low overhead** — only 8 bytes per packet.

**Key Derivation in SNEP:**
Master secret $K_{S}$ between nodes A and B is used to derive:
$$K_{enc} = F(K_S, 0), \quad K_{mac} = F(K_S, 1), \quad K_{rand} = F(K_S, 2)$$
where *F* is a Pseudo-Random Function (PRF).

A SNEP message takes form: $A \rightarrow B : \{D\}_{\langle K_{enc}, C\rangle}, \ \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, C \Vert E)$

**2. µTESLA (micro Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication):**
Provides **authenticated broadcast** — a major challenge since symmetric MACs alone cannot prevent forgery.

**Core Idea:** Use a **delayed key disclosure** mechanism based on a one-way key chain.

**Working Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Base station generates a one-way key chain $K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_n$ using a one-way function $F$ such that $K_{i} = F(K_{i+1})$. $K_0$ is the committed key disclosed last.
- **Step 2:** Time is divided into intervals $I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n$. MAC key $K_i$ is used in interval $I_i$.
- **Step 3:** During interval $I_i$, BS broadcasts packet with MAC computed using $K_i$.
- **Step 4:** At the **end** of interval $I_i$, BS broadcasts the key $K_i$ in the clear.
- **Step 5:** Nodes verify that the disclosed $K_i$ is consistent with previously committed $K_0$ (i.e., $K_i$ hashes forward to $K_0$ via repeated application of $F$).
- **Step 6:** Once verified, the node authenticates all packets received during $I_i$ using $K_i$.

**Why is it secure?**
- An attacker cannot forge MACs before $K_i$ is disclosed because they don't know $K_i$.
- Disclosed $K_i$ cannot be used to forge past packets (one-way property of *F*).

**µTESLA Improvements over TESLA:**
- **Per-packet authentication with loose time synchronization** (delayed disclosure).
- **Handles packet loss** — sender can disclose key for missed intervals.
- **Supports multiple receivers** with low storage.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **random key predistribution** vs **deterministic pairwise key predistribution** with respect to memory, connectivity, and resilience. Which is preferred for large-scale WSN and why?

**Model Solution:**

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Property | Random (EG / q-Composite) | Deterministic Pairwise |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory per node** | $O(m)$ — fixed; ~50–250 keys | $O(n)$ — scales with network size |
| **Connectivity** | Probabilistic; tunable via $p_{shared}$ | Deterministic; = 1.0 always |
| **Resilience to node capture** | Limited (especially EG with low *q*) | Perfect — one capture affects only that node's links |
| **Computation** | Hash-based, lightweight | Hash-based, lightweight |
| **Communication overhead** | Key discovery broadcast needed | May need negotiation |
| **Scalability** | Excellent (memory independent of *n*) | Poor (memory grows linearly) |
| **Best use case** | Large dense WSN (n > 1000) | Small/moderate networks (n < 200) |

**Numerical Demonstration of Memory:**

For *n = 1000* nodes with pairwise scheme:
- Each node stores **n − 1 = 999 keys**.
- At *k = 128 bits* per key: memory = 999 × 128 / 8 = **~16 KB** of key storage alone.
- For Mica2 with 128 KB flash, this is **12.5% of total memory** for keys alone — infeasible.

For EG scheme with *|P| = 10,000*, *m = 200*:
- Each node stores 200 keys.
- Memory = 200 × 128 / 8 = **3.2 KB** — practical.

**Preferred Scheme for Large-Scale WSN:**
**Random Key Predistribution (EG or q-Composite)** is preferred for large-scale WSN because:
1. **Memory scales as $O(m)$**, not $O(n)$ — independent of network size.
2. **Tunable connectivity** via $p_{shared}$ parameter.
3. **Easier to add new nodes** post-deployment.
4. **Resilience can be improved** via q-Composite or by combining with deployment knowledge.

However, for **mission-critical small networks** (e.g., body area networks, vehicular networks), **pairwise schemes** are preferred for **perfect isolation of compromise** and **deterministic guarantees**.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Formula Memorization Without Derivation** — Examiners expect at least 2–3 derivation steps for probability formulas. Writing only the final answer $p_{shared} = 1 - e^{-m^2/|P|}$ **without stating assumptions** will cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing m and |P|** — Always clarify in the answer: *m* = key ring size, *|P|* = key pool size. Mixing them up in numerical substitution gives **wrong numerical answer** = full 7-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the Erasure Step in LEAP** — Many students describe LEAP but **omit why $K_{IN}$ is erased**. This is a **favorite 2-mark question** and missing it loses significant marks.
> 4. **Not Drawing Diagrams in LEAP / SPINS** — KTU valuation strictly expects a diagram for protocol description. A text-only answer to "explain LEAP" loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up SNEP and µTESLA roles** — SNEP is for **point-to-point**, µTESLA is for **broadcast**. Reversing or conflating loses easy marks.
> 6. **Numerical Computation without showing intermediate steps** — Always show $m^2/|P|$ first, then substitution into $e^{-x}$. Direct calculator dumps are penalized.
> 7. **Ignoring Unit and Range of Numerical Answers** — Probability answers should be stated as a decimal between 0 and 1 with the **% sign or "≈" qualifier** for clarity.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Key Management** = Generation + Distribution + Storage + Update + Revocation of keys in WSN.
- [x] **EG Scheme** uses pool **|P|**, key ring **m**, probability $p_{shared} \approx 1 - e^{-m^2/|P|}$.
- [x] **q-Composite** strengthens resilience by requiring **q ≥ 1** common keys.
- [x] **Pairwise Scheme** offers perfect resilience but **O(n) memory** — non-scalable.
- [x] **LEAP** has four keys: Individual, Pairwise, Cluster, Group.
- [x] **$K_{IN}$ in LEAP is erased after $T_{min}$** — critical security step.
- [x] **SPINS = SNEP + µTESLA** — Perrig et al. (2002).
- [x] **SNEP** = confidentiality + integrity + freshness (CTR + OMAC + counters).
- [x] **µTESLA** = authenticated broadcast via **delayed key disclosure** in a one-way key chain.
- [x] **ECC** (160-bit) is feasible on 8-bit motes (~0.8 s per op).
- [x] **Resilience metric**: $p_{break} = 1 - (1 - m/|P|)^x$ for *x* captured nodes.
- [x] **Key Pool Size Trade-off**: Larger *|P|* → better resilience, lower $p_{shared}$ → tune *m* accordingly.
- [x] **Deployment knowledge** (e.g., probabilistic deployment models) can boost $p_{shared}$ to 0.99+.
- [x] **Forward & Backward Secrecy** are mandatory in modern KMP design.
- [x] **Energy cost of asymmetric crypto on Mica2**: ECDSA verify ≈ 22.4 mJ, RSA-1024 verify ≈ 15.4 mJ, RSA-1024 sign ≈ 304 mJ.
- [x] **TinyOS implementations**: TinyECC, TinyPK, MiniSec, ContikiSec are practical platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "EG-Q-PL-S"**
> **E**G → Probabilistic sharing
> **Q**-Composite → Stronger security
> **P**airwise → Perfect resilience
> **L**EAP → Four keys, master-key erasure
> **S**PINS → SNEP + µTESLA
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defence Against DoS Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defence Against DoS Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DoS Attacks in WSN

A **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a malicious attempt by an adversary to disrupt, degrade, or completely disable the normal functioning of the network by exhausting critical resources such as **battery power**, **bandwidth**, **computational cycles**, or **memory** of the sensor nodes. Unlike traditional networks, WSNs are uniquely vulnerable due to their inherent constraints in **energy (typically 2–5 AA batteries)**, **limited transmission range (10–100 m)**, and **unattended deployment** in hostile environments.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, DoS attacks in WSN are classified based on the **layer of the protocol stack** they target:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DoS attacks span across the **Physical Layer**, **Link Layer**, **Network Layer**, and **Transport Layer** of the WSN protocol stack. Defence mechanisms are correspondingly designed at each layer.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of WSN Vulnerability

| Vulnerability Factor | Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| Energy Constraint | **2–5 Joules per node** (typical) |
| Limited Bandwidth | **19.2 kbps to 250 kbps** (IEEE 802.15.4) |
| Unattended Operation | **Months to years** without maintenance |
| Wireless Medium | Shared RF spectrum, easily intercepted |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Watchman" Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a village where a watchman (the sensor node) stands at the gate. Normally, he only responds to genuine villagers (legitimate packets). A DoS attacker is like a mischievous prankster who keeps ringing the bell (transmitting junk packets), shouting fake alarms, or pretending to be 100 different villagers asking for directions — eventually, the watchman is too exhausted to respond to anyone. The village becomes vulnerable to actual threats because its defender is overwhelmed.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of DoS Attacks in WSN

The KTU 2024 Scheme broadly categorizes DoS attacks as follows:

1. **Physical Layer Attacks:** Jamming, Tampering, Node Destruction
2. **Link Layer Attacks:** Collision, Exhaustion, Unfairness
3. **Network Layer Attacks:** Neglect and Greed, Homing, Misdirection, Blackhole, Wormhole, Flooding
4. **Transport Layer Attacks:** Desynchronization, Flooding

## 1.5 Why Defence is Hard in WSN

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse DoS with **eavesdropping**. DoS is about **disrupting service availability**, not stealing data. WSNs cannot use heavyweight security protocols (like TLS) due to resource constraints, making defence a unique challenge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-Wise Defence Architecture

### 2.1.1 Physical Layer Defence

**A. Jamming Attack & Defence**

A jammer transmits radio signals on the same frequency band to prevent legitimate communication.

**Defence Mechanisms:**
- **Spread-Spectrum techniques** (FHSS, DSSS)
- **Frequency Hopping** with pseudo-random sequences
- **Region-based detection** using **Carrier Sensing Time (CST)**
- **Node sleeping schedules** to conserve energy

**B. Tampering Defence**
- **Tamper-proof hardware** packaging
- **Self-destruction mechanisms** for sensitive cryptographic keys
- **Hidden node placement** strategies

### 2.1.2 Link Layer (MAC) Defence

**A. Collision Attack Defence:**
- Use of **Error Correcting Codes (ECC)**
- **Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ)** protocols
- Time-slot reassignment algorithms

**B. Exhaustion Defence:**
- **Rate-limiting** at the MAC layer
- **S-MAC and T-MAC** protocols with sleep cycles
- **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** scheduling

### 2.1.3 Network Layer Defence

**Defence against:**
- **Homing attacks** → Encrypted headers
- **Misdirection attacks** → Authentication of routing paths
- **Blackhole attacks** → Multi-path routing + watchdog monitoring
- **Wormhole attacks** → Geographic routing + packet leashes

### 2.1.4 Transport Layer Defence

- **Authentication of session keys**
- **Anti-flooding mechanisms** with connection rate limiting

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy consumed per bit transmission** | $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \times k + \epsilon_{amp} \times k \times d^2$ | $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m², $k$ = bits, $d$ = distance (m) | Energy-aware defence |
| **Energy consumed per bit reception** | $E_{rx} = E_{elec} \times k$ | $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit | Energy budget calculation |
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $PDR = \frac{P_{received}}{P_{sent}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | DoS impact measurement |
| **Network throughput degradation** | $\eta_{DoS} = \frac{T_{normal} - T_{attack}}{T_{normal}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Attack severity quantification |
| **Jamming detection probability** | $P_{detect} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ | $p$ = single-sensor detection prob., $n$ = number of sensors | Cooperative jamming detection |
| **Sleep duty cycle** | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Time in seconds | Energy conservation under attack |
| **Network lifetime extension** | $L = \frac{E_{initial}}{P_{consumed}}$ | $E$ in Joules, $P$ in Watts | Defence effectiveness metric |
| **Signal-to-Jamming Ratio (SJR)** | $SJR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{jammer}}$ | Power in Watts or dB | Jamming resilience |
| **False positive rate** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Dimensionless | IDS performance |
| **Cryptographic overhead** | $O_{crypto} = T_{encrypt} + T_{decrypt} + T_{key\_exchange}$ | Time in milliseconds | Defence cost analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note:** In the above table, the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ would be expressed as `\vert x \vert` in raw LaTeX, but for table cells we use descriptive text like "Time in seconds" to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Military Surveillance Networks:** Battlefield WSNs must withstand jamming from enemy electronic warfare systems.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest fire detection sensors must resist DoS attacks that could delay fire alerts.
- **Healthcare WSNs:** Patient monitoring systems cannot tolerate DoS attacks that might mask critical medical events.
- **Smart Grid Infrastructure:** Power grid sensor networks require DoS resilience to prevent cascading blackouts.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory automation WSNs need defence against DoS attacks to maintain production continuity.

## 2.4 The Core Operational Principle

The **fundamental defence philosophy** in WSNs is rooted in three principles:

1. **Prevention** — Stop the attack before it starts (cryptography, authentication)
2. **Detection** — Identify the attack in progress (intrusion detection systems)
3. **Mitigation** — Reduce the impact and recover (rerouting, isolation, sleep modes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The trade-off between **security strength** and **energy consumption** is the defining characteristic of WSN defence. Any defence mechanism that consumes more energy than the attack itself is considered impractical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Cost of a DoS Attack

We derive the energy consumed by a sensor node under a continuous jamming attack.

### Step 1: Define the energy model
The first-order radio energy model (Heinzelman et al.) gives the energy for transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$:

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \times k + \epsilon_{amp} \times k \times d^2$$

The energy for receiving $k$ bits:

$$E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \times k$$

### Step 2: Energy to transmit one packet
For a typical WSN packet of $k = 4000$ bits transmitted over $d = 50$ m:

$$E_{tx} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 4000 + (100 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4000 \times 50^2$$

$$E_{tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 4000 \times 2500$$

$$E_{tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 1.2 \text{ mJ per packet}$$

### Step 3: Energy for receiving one packet

$$E_{rx} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 4000 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.2 \text{ mJ per packet}$$

### Step 4: Total energy per data transaction

$$E_{total} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} = 1.2 + 0.2 = 1.4 \text{ mJ per data packet}$$

### Step 5: Energy under jamming attack
If a jammer forces retransmissions of $N_r = 10$ packets due to collisions:

$$E_{attack} = N_r \times E_{total} = 10 \times 1.4 = 14 \text{ mJ}$$

### Step 6: Energy consumption rate
If a node processes $R = 50$ packets per second under attack:

$$P_{attack} = R \times E_{total} = 50 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.07 \text{ W} = 70 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 7: Battery lifetime reduction
For a node with $E_{battery} = 5 \text{ J}$ initial energy:

$$L_{normal} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{normal}} = \frac{5}{0.01} = 500 \text{ seconds (sample calc)}$$

Under attack, lifetime reduces proportionally to power consumption increase.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooperative Jamming Detection Probability

### Step 1: Single sensor detection probability
Let $p$ = probability that a single sensor correctly detects jamming. Typical value: $p = 0.7$.

### Step 2: Multi-sensor cooperation
With $n$ sensors cooperating (using OR-fusion rule):

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (1-p)^n$$

### Step 3: Substituting values for $n = 3$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - 0.7)^3$$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (0.3)^3$$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - 0.027$$

$$P_{detect} = 0.973 \text{ (97.3% detection rate)}$$

### Step 4: For $n = 5$ sensors

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (0.3)^5 = 1 - 0.00243 = 0.9976 \text{ (99.76%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Cooperative detection exponentially improves jamming detection capability, which is why **cluster-based WSNs** are inherently more DoS-resilient.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sleep-Wake Defence Duty Cycle

### Step 1: Define duty cycle
A node alternates between active ($T_{active}$) and sleep ($T_{sleep}$) modes:

$$DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$$

### Step 2: Energy savings calculation
If $T_{active} = 10$ ms and $T_{sleep} = 90$ ms:

$$DC = \frac{10}{10 + 90} = 0.1 \text{ (10% duty cycle)}$$

### Step 3: Energy reduction factor
Energy consumption with sleep mode $E_{sleep}$ vs. always-on $E_{always}$:

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - \left[ DC + (1-DC) \times \frac{E_{sleep}}{E_{active}} \right]$$

Assuming $E_{sleep} / E_{active} = 0.01$:

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - [0.1 + 0.9 \times 0.01]$$

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - [0.1 + 0.009]$$

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - 0.109 = 0.891 \text{ (89.1% energy saved)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DoS Attack-Defence Mapping

| Attack Type | Layer | Defence Mechanism | Energy Cost | Detection Latency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jamming | Physical | Frequency Hopping | **High** | **Medium** |
| Tampering | Physical | Tamper-proofing | **Low** | **High** |
| Collision | Link | ECC + ARQ | **Medium** | **Low** |
| Exhaustion | Link | Rate limiting | **Low** | **Low** |
| Blackhole | Network | Watchdog + Multi-path | **High** | **Medium** |
| Wormhole | Network | Geographic leashes | **Medium** | **High** |
| Flooding | Network | Rate limiting | **Low** | **Low** |
| Desynchronization | Transport | Authentication | **Medium** | **Medium** |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Cryptographic Defence Overhead Analysis

### Step 1: Define AES-128 energy consumption
Typical AES-128 encryption on a sensor node:

$$E_{AES} = 0.81 \text{ μJ per block} \text{ (16 bytes)}$$

### Step 2: Energy per encrypted bit

$$E_{bit} = \frac{0.81 \times 10^{-6}}{128} = 6.33 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J/bit}$$

### Step 3: Overhead comparison
For a 4000-bit packet:

$$E_{crypto} = 4000 \times 6.33 \times 10^{-9} = 2.53 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J}$$

### Step 4: Overhead percentage
$$\text{Overhead}\% = \frac{E_{crypto}}{E_{tx}} \times 100 = \frac{2.53 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 2.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** AES-128 cryptographic defence adds only **2.1%** energy overhead, making it highly viable for WSN DoS defence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
