# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. List any four types of DoS attacks at different layers of the WSN protocol stack.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** in WSN is a deliberate attempt by a malicious entity to disrupt the normal operation of the sensor network by exhausting critical resources such as energy, bandwidth, computational capacity, or memory, thereby preventing legitimate users from accessing network services.

**Four Types of DoS Attacks:**

1. **Jamming (Physical Layer):** Continuous RF transmission to block legitimate communication.
2. **Collision (Link Layer):** Intentional packet collision by transmitting simultaneously with legitimate nodes.
3. **Blackhole Attack (Network Layer):** Malicious node drops all packets it receives, creating a communication void.
4. **Desynchronization (Transport Layer):** Attacker repeatedly sends forged synchronization messages to disrupt existing connections.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the concept of jamming attack at the physical layer. What are the two main defence mechanisms against jamming?**

**Model Answer:**

A **jamming attack** involves an adversary transmitting radio frequency signals on the same channel used by the WSN, thereby increasing the noise floor and corrupting legitimate packets. This forces sensor nodes to retransmit, rapidly draining their limited battery reserves.

**Two Main Defence Mechanisms:**

1. **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS):** Both sender and receiver rapidly switch between different frequency channels following a pseudo-random sequence known only to legitimate nodes, making it difficult for the jammer to follow.

2. **Region-Based Detection with Sleep Scheduling:** Sensors in the jammed region detect high Signal-to-Jamming Ratio degradation and enter sleep mode to conserve energy while neighbouring nodes take over communication responsibilities.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the various DoS attacks at the **physical, link, and network layers** of a WSN. Discuss at least **two attacks per layer** with their impact on network performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **defence mechanisms** against the attacks discussed in part (a). Compare the energy overhead of cryptographic defence versus intrusion detection-based defence using a suitable mathematical model. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Layer-wise DoS Attacks:**

**Physical Layer:**
- **Jamming:** Adversary transmits high-power RF noise on the operational frequency. Impact: Complete communication blackout in the jammed region.
- **Tampering:** Physical capture and reprogramming of sensor nodes. Impact: Loss of cryptographic keys and network compromise.

**Link Layer:**
- **Collision:** Malicious node transmits simultaneously with legitimate nodes, causing packet corruption. Impact: Increased retransmissions and energy drain.
- **Exhaustion:** Attacker exploits RTS/CTS handshake to keep nodes awake unnecessarily. Impact: Rapid battery depletion.

**Network Layer:**
- **Blackhole Attack:** Malicious node advertises itself as having the shortest path and drops all forwarded packets. Impact: Severe packet loss and routing disruption.
- **Wormhole Attack:** Two colluding attackers create a low-latency tunnel, misleading routing decisions. Impact: Network partitioning and topology distortion.

**Part (b) — Defence Mechanisms & Comparison:**

**Defence Mechanisms:**
- For Jamming: **Spread Spectrum** (FHSS/DSSS)
- For Tampering: **Tamper-proof hardware** with key destruction
- For Collision: **Error Correcting Codes (ECC)** with **ARQ**
- For Exhaustion: **Rate limiting** and **TDMA scheduling**
- For Blackhole: **Watchdog monitoring** + **Multi-path routing**
- For Wormhole: **Geographic packet leashes** with timing constraints

**Energy Overhead Comparison:**

For AES-128 encryption (per 4000-bit packet):

$$E_{crypto} = k \times E_{bit} = 4000 \times 6.33 \times 10^{-9} = 2.53 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J}$$

For IDS monitoring (continuous listening, ~0.5 mW for 1 second):

$$E_{IDS} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Overhead Ratio:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{IDS}}{E_{crypto}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.53 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 19.76$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Cryptographic defence is **~20 times more energy-efficient** than continuous IDS monitoring, making it preferable for resource-constrained WSN nodes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** What is a **wormhole attack**? Explain how it disrupts network-layer routing in WSNs. Discuss the **packet leash** technique as a defence mechanism with its mathematical formulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN cluster of **5 sensors** is monitoring for jamming attacks. If each sensor has an **independent detection probability of 0.75**, calculate the **cooperative detection probability** using the OR-fusion rule. Also, derive the energy consumption model for a node defending against a jamming attack that forces **15 retransmissions** of a **4000-bit packet** over **75 meters**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Wormhole Attack & Packet Leash Defence:**

A **wormhole attack** is a severe network-layer DoS attack in which two or more malicious nodes create a high-speed out-of-band tunnel (wormhole link) between distant parts of the network. One attacker records packets at one location and tunnels them to another colluding attacker, which replays them locally. This makes the two distant nodes appear as neighbours, corrupting the network topology and routing decisions.

**Packet Leash Defence:**

A **packet leash** is cryptographic information added to a packet to restrict its maximum allowed transmission distance.

**Geographic Leash Formula:**

$$d_{max} = c \times (T_{recv} - T_{send})$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $T_{recv}$ = packet reception timestamp
- $T_{send}$ = packet transmission timestamp

If $d_{max}$ exceeds the legitimate maximum distance, the packet is discarded as a wormhole attack.

**Temporal Leash Formula:**

$$t_{expire} = T_{send} + \frac{d_{max}}{c}$$

Any packet arriving after $t_{expire}$ is rejected.

**Part (b) — Mathematical Calculations:**

**Step 1: Cooperative Detection Probability**

Given $n = 5$ sensors, $p = 0.75$:

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - 0.75)^5$$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - (0.25)^5$$

$$P_{detect} = 1 - 0.0009766$$

$$\boxed{P_{detect} = 0.9990 \text{ or } 99.90\%}$$

**Step 2: Energy per Packet Transmission (d = 75 m, k = 4000 bits)**

$$E_{tx} = E_{elec} \times k + \epsilon_{amp} \times k \times d^2$$

$$E_{tx} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 4000 + (100 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4000 \times 75^2$$

$$E_{tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (100 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4000 \times 5625$$

$$E_{tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 2.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_{tx} = 2.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 2.45 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3: Energy per Packet Reception**

$$E_{rx} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 4000 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.2 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4: Energy per Single Data Transaction**

$$E_{total} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} = 2.45 + 0.2 = 2.65 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 5: Total Energy with 15 Retransmissions**

$$E_{attack} = 15 \times E_{total} = 15 \times 2.65 = 39.75 \text{ mJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{attack} = 39.75 \text{ mJ} = 3.975 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks in two areas:
> 1. **Confusing detection probability with false positive rate** — Remember, $P_{detect}$ is the probability of correctly identifying an attack, while $FPR$ measures false alarms.
> 2. **Forgetting to include both transmission AND reception energy** — The total energy cost per packet is $E_{tx} + E_{rx}$, not just $E_{tx}$. Omitting $E_{rx}$ typically costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up wormhole with blackhole** — Wormhole is a **tunnel-based routing disruption**, while blackhole is a **packet-dropping** attack. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DoS attacks** in WSN aim to exhaust **energy, bandwidth, memory, or CPU** resources, not steal data.
- The **four attack layers** are Physical (jamming, tampering), Link (collision, exhaustion), Network (blackhole, wormhole, flooding), and Transport (desynchronization).
- **Defence principles:** Prevention (cryptography), Detection (IDS), Mitigation (sleep/rerouting).
- **Key formula — Cooperative Detection:** $P_{detect} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ — improves exponentially with more sensors.
- **Energy model:** $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \times k + \epsilon_{amp} \times k \times d^2$ — the $d^2$ term dominates over long distances.
- **Packet leashes** (geographic and temporal) are the standard defence against wormhole attacks.
- **AES-128 cryptographic overhead** is only **~2.1%** of transmission energy — highly viable for WSNs.
- **Sleep-wake duty cycling** can save up to **~89%** of energy during DoS conditions.
- **Multi-path routing + Watchdog monitoring** is the standard defence against blackhole attacks.
- **Frequency Hopping (FHSS)** is the primary defence against physical-layer jamming.
- The **signal-to-jamming ratio (SJR)** quantifies network resilience to jamming attacks.
- Always remember: in WSN, **energy efficiency trumps security strength** — defence must be lightweight.
- KTU exam questions frequently test the **mathematical model of energy consumption** and **cooperative detection probability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer into: (1) Attack description, (2) Layer identification, (3) Mathematical model, (4) Defence mechanism, (5) Trade-off analysis. This 5-part structure guarantees full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defence Against Routing Attacks - TESLA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defence Against Routing Attacks - TESLA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TESLA (Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication)** is a lightweight broadcast/multicast authentication protocol designed for resource-constrained networks like Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs). It provides **asymmetric cryptographic properties** using only **symmetric primitives** (MACs and hash functions) by exploiting **loose time synchronization** and a **delayed key disclosure** mechanism.

TESLA was proposed by **Perrig, Canetti, Szewczyk, and Tygar** as part of the SPINS security suite, specifically engineered to overcome the limitations of WSNs where traditional asymmetric cryptography (RSA, ECC) is computationally prohibitive for tiny sensor motes.

In the context of **routing attack defence**, TESLA provides a means to **authenticate the source and integrity of routing control packets** (e.g., route requests, route replies, beacon messages, data reports) that travel across the multi-hop WSN, mitigating attacks such as **sybil attacks, wormhole attacks, sinkhole attacks, and selective forwarding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Innovation of TESLA:** It achieves *asymmetric authentication* without using asymmetric cryptography — instead it uses a **one-way hash key chain** and **time-delayed key disclosure**, a technique known as *delayed key disclosure cryptography*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of TESLA as a "Locked Letterbox" system:**
>
> Imagine a teacher wants to broadcast the same secret message to 100 students over a public square, but only legitimate students should be able to verify the message came from the teacher (and wasn't tampered with). The teacher cannot use expensive digital signatures.
>
> **The teacher's trick:** 
> 1. He prepares 100 sealed envelopes (one-way hash key chain) numbered 100, 99, 98… 
> 2. He tells everyone: *"Sealed envelope #1 will be the master key — I promise that if I unseal #1, you'll see what was inside #2 matches what I claimed."*
> 3. Each broadcast message in time slot *i* is signed using key *K_i* (an envelope not yet opened).
> 4. When time slot *i* ends, the teacher publicly opens envelope *K_i*. Because of the one-way chain, no one can forge a future key, and since the seal was made *before* anyone knew the contents, no one can cheat.
>
> **In WSNs:** Sensor nodes pre-agree on a key chain, time-synchronize loosely, and authenticate routing messages with delayed disclosure of MAC keys.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **One-Way Hash Chain** | A sequence of keys $K_n, K_{n-1}, \dots, K_0$ where $K_{i-1} = H(K_i)$ and $H(\cdot)$ is a one-way hash function. |
| **Key Commitment** | The sender broadcasts $K_0$ (the *commitment key*) to all receivers in advance. |
| **Time Interval** | The WSN time is divided into uniform intervals $I_0, I_1, \dots, I_n$ of duration $T_{\text{int}}$. |
| **MAC Key per Interval** | Each interval $I_j$ uses key $K_j$ from the hash chain. |
| **Delayed Disclosure** | The key $K_j$ used in $I_j$ is *only* disclosed at the end of $I_j$, ensuring no receiver can pre-compute and forge packets. |
| **Loose Time Synchronization** | Receivers need only know an upper bound on sender-receiver clock skew $\delta$, not precise sync. |
| **Loss-Tolerance** | Receivers can tolerate packet loss via buffering and a "key window" verification. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value:** The original TESLA paper uses **HMAC truncated to 4 or 8 bytes** and **MD5 / SHA-1** hash chains. For modern WSNs, **RC5** (used in TinySec) and **SHA-1** are common primitives.

---

## 1.4 Why TESLA for Routing Attack Defence?

Wireless sensor routing is highly vulnerable because attackers can:
- **Inject** malicious routing packets
- **Replay** old valid packets
- **Forge** identities of legitimate nodes (Sybil attack)
- **Manipulate** route metrics (sinkhole/blackhole attack)

TESLA defends against these by ensuring **every routing packet** carries a **fresh MAC computed with a key that the receiver can later verify** — providing **source authentication**, **integrity**, and **freshness**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of TESLA

TESLA operates in **three sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Sender Setup (Key Chain Generation)
The sender $S$ generates the last key $K_n$ randomly and derives the entire chain backwards using a one-way hash function $H(\cdot)$:

$$K_{n-1} = H(K_n)$$
$$K_{n-2} = H(K_{n-1}) = H^{(2)}(K_n)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$K_0 = H^{(n)}(K_n)$$

$K_0$ is the **commitment key** and is distributed to all receivers via an authenticated channel *before* the protocol begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The commitment is **one-way**: given $K_i$, computing $K_{i+1}$ is computationally infeasible. Thus, the sender **cannot lie** about its future keys.

---

### Phase 2: Packet Transmission (MAC Computation)
Time is divided into intervals $\{I_0, I_1, \dots, I_m\}$. For a packet $P_j$ sent during interval $I_i$:

$$MAC_j = \text{MAC}_{K_i}(P_j)$$

The packet structure is:

$$P_j = \{ \text{data}_j \;\Vert\; MAC_j \;\Vert\; i \;\Vert\; K_{i-d} \}$$

where $d$ is the **disclosure lag** (number of intervals after which the key is released).

**The Why:** Since the key $K_i$ is still secret at the time of transmission, no adversary can forge the MAC. By the time the key is revealed, all receivers who buffered the packet can verify it.

---

### Phase 3: Key Disclosure & Verification
At the end of interval $I_i$, the sender discloses key $K_i$ in the next packet. A receiver $R$ performs the following **3-step verification**:

1. **Key Authenticity Check:** Verify that $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ (a previously trusted key).
2. **Key Freshness Check:** Verify that the disclosed key is for an interval that has already ended.
3. **MAC Verification:** Compute $\text{MAC}_{K_i}(P_j)$ and compare with the received MAC.

If all three checks pass → the packet is **authentic and unaltered**.

---

## 2.2 TESLA Security Conditions (The "Triple Constraint")

> [!WARNING]
> For TESLA to be secure, **all three** of the following must hold simultaneously. Missing any one breaks authentication.

| Condition | Symbolic Form | Real Meaning |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Condition 1 (Time Lock-in)** | $T_{\text{recv}} < T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + \delta$ | Receiver must be sure the key $K_i$ was still secret when the packet arrived. |
| **Condition 2 (Disclosure Lag)** | $d \geq 1$ interval | Key must be released *after* the packet is sent. |
| **Condition 3 (Hash Chain Integrity)** | $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ | The chain must be unbroken; backtracking is infeasible. |

Where:
- $T_{\text{recv}}$ = packet reception time
- $T_{\text{start}}(I_i)$ = start time of interval $I_i$ at the sender
- $\delta$ = maximum clock skew between sender and receiver

---

## 2.3 Defence Against Specific Routing Attacks via TESLA

> [!TIP]
> **The "How" of Routing Defence with TESLA:**

| Attack Type | How TESLA Defends |
|-------------|-------------------|
| **Packet Injection (Bogus Routing Updates)** | Attacker cannot forge MAC without secret key $K_i$ → forged packets rejected. |
| **Replay Attack** | Interval number $i$ is included in MAC input → old packets fail freshness check. |
| **Wormhole Attack** | Time-locked disclosure + tight key window prevents accelerated replay. |
| **Sybil Attack** | Combined with node-ID commitment; each node has its own key chain. |
| **Sinkhole / Blackhole** | Authenticated route metrics prevent fabrication of attractive routes. |
| **Selective Forwarding** | End-to-end MAC chains (µTESLA extensions) verify forwarding integrity. |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| Hash Chain Derivation | $K_{i-1} = H(K_i)$ | Backward derivation of key chain | — |
| Commitment Key | $K_0 = H^{(n)}(K_n)$ | Initial public commitment | — |
| MAC Computation | $MAC_j = \text{MAC}_{K_i}(P_j)$ | Per-packet authentication tag | **bytes** |
| Time Interval Duration | $T_{\text{int}}$ | Duration of each interval $I_i$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Maximum Clock Skew | $\delta$ | Upper bound on sender-receiver time difference | **seconds (s)** |
| Security Condition (Time Lock) | $T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + \delta \leq T_{\text{recv}}$ | Receiver must receive packet before key disclosure window | **seconds (s)** |
| Disclosure Lag | $d \geq 1$ | Minimum intervals between MAC use and key release | **intervals** |
| Key Window Size | $w$ | Number of intervals a receiver trusts without fresh commitment | **intervals** |
| Storage per Receiver | $\mathcal{O}(w)$ keys | Memory for verifying a window of keys | **keys** |
| Energy per Packet (MAC) | $E_{\text{MAC}} = n \cdot E_{\text{hash}}$ | Where $n$ = block count | **Joules (J)** |
| Energy per Packet (Verify) | $E_{\text{ver}} = n \cdot E_{\text{hash}} + E_{\text{comp}}$ | Hash + comparison | **Joules (J)** |
| Packet Buffer Size | $B = R \cdot T_{\text{int}} \cdot d$ | Packets to buffer before key disclosure | **packets** |
| Throughput Loss Factor | $\eta = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{int}}}{T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}}$ | Effective bandwidth due to key overhead | **dimensionless** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy/Power Note (bold constants):** A typical WSN mote (MICAz) has $E_{\text{hash}} \approx 15 \; \mu J/\text{byte}$ (SHA-1) and $E_{\text{MAC}} \approx 0.6 \; \mu J/\text{bit}$. Total authentication overhead per packet is approximately **5-15%** of transmission energy.

---

## 2.5 Variants of TESLA (Board-Relevant Distinctions)

| Variant | Innovation | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **TESLA** | Baseline delayed disclosure protocol | Wired broadcast, strong nodes |
| **µTESLA** | TinyOS-compatible, lightweight for WSNs | Mica motes, sensor authentication |
| **Multi-level µTESLA** | Hierarchical key chains via base station | Large-scale WSNs |
| **D-TESLA** | Distributed key chain (no central trust) | Peer-to-peer WSNs |
| **RT-TESLA** | Real-time enhanced, periodic beacon commitment | Time-critical control loops |
| **TESLA++** | Includes ECDSA-like root commitment | Hybrid systems with capable gateways |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hash Chain Commitment

**Problem Setup:** The sender $S$ must pre-commit to a sequence of keys $K_0, K_1, \dots, K_n$ such that:
- $S$ knows all keys.
- Each receiver knows only $K_0$.
- No receiver can predict $K_j$ for $j \geq 1$ from $K_0$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Sender $S$ generates a high-entropy random secret $s \in \{0,1\}^{128}$ (128-bit for **MD5** compatibility or 160-bit for **SHA-1**).

**Step 2:** $S$ selects a one-way hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^{128}$ (or 160-bit). This function must be:
- **Pre-image resistant:** Given $y$, finding $x$ s.t. $H(x) = y$ is hard.
- **Collision resistant:** Finding $x_1 \neq x_2$ s.t. $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ is hard.

**Step 3:** $S$ constructs the chain by repeated application of $H$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_n &= s \\
K_{n-1} &= H(K_n) = H(s) \\
K_{n-2} &= H(K_{n-1}) = H^{(2)}(s) \\
&\vdots \\
K_0 &= H^{(n)}(K_n) = H^{(n)}(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** $S$ broadcasts the *commitment tuple*:

$$C = \{K_0,\; T_{\text{start}},\; T_{\text{int}},\; d\}$$

where $T_{\text{start}}$ = start time of $I_0$, $T_{\text{int}}$ = interval duration, $d$ = disclosure lag.

**Step 5:** Each receiver $R_i$ authenticates $C$ via an out-of-band channel (e.g., ECDSA signature from base station, or pre-shared master key).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Logic:** The one-way property ensures receivers *cannot* invert $H$ to obtain $K_1$ from $K_0$. Thus, the sender's commitment is binding — it cannot change $K_1$ after the fact because the chain would be inconsistent when verified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Security Bound on Clock Skew

**Problem Setup:** Determine the maximum tolerable clock skew $\delta_{\max}$ such that the time-lock condition holds for all packets.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a packet $P$ sent by sender at time $T_s$ during interval $I_i$, where:

$$T_{\text{start}}(I_i) \leq T_s < T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}}$$

**Step 2:** The key $K_i$ is disclosed by the sender at time:

$$T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i) = T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}$$

**Step 3:** The packet $P$ arrives at receiver $R$ at time:

$$T_{\text{recv}} = T_s + \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}}$$

where $\text{Delay}_{\text{prop}}$ is the propagation + transmission delay.

**Step 4:** For the receiver to be sure the key was *still secret* upon arrival, we need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{recv}} &< T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i) \\
T_s + \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} &< T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}} \\
\text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} &< (T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}) - T_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The minimum value of the right-hand side occurs when $T_s$ is maximum, i.e., $T_s \to T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} &< (T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}) - (T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}}) \\
\text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} &< d \cdot T_{\text{int}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** The maximum tolerable clock skew is thus:

$$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = d \cdot T_{\text{int}} - \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} - \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a safety margin.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A larger $d$ or $T_{\text{int}}$ increases robustness to clock skew but also increases packet buffering time and memory. Typical values: $T_{\text{int}} = 100$ ms, $d = 2$, giving $\delta_{\max} \approx 200$ ms — sufficient for most WSNs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Receiver Buffer Size

**Problem Setup:** How many packets must a receiver buffer before it can verify them?

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Data rate from sender to receiver = $R$ (packets/sec).
**Step 2:** Time from packet arrival to key disclosure = $d \cdot T_{\text{int}}$ seconds.
**Step 3:** Number of packets buffered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= R \times (d \cdot T_{\text{int}}) \\
\boxed{B &= R \cdot d \cdot T_{\text{int}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Memory in bytes:

$$M_{\text{buffer}} = B \times (L_{\text{data}} + L_{\text{MAC}} + L_{\text{header}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Example:** If $R = 10$ pkt/s, $d = 2$, $T_{\text{int}} = 0.1$ s, packet size = 50 bytes:
> $B = 10 \times 2 \times 0.1 = 2$ packets, $M_{\text{buffer}} = 100$ bytes — very small for typical motes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Verification Matrix (Per Routing Attack)

| Attack | Attacker Capability | TESLA Mechanism Engaged | Verification Result |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| **Bogus RREQ Injection** | Forge route request | MAC of $K_i$ on RREQ | Receiver checks $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ after disclosure → **Rejected** |
| **Replay of Old RREP** | Resend captured reply | Interval number $i$ in MAC | New $i$ ≠ old $i$ → MAC mismatch → **Rejected** |
| **Sybil (Fake ID)** | Impersonate node $X$ | Node $X$'s unique key chain | Adversary doesn't know $K_i^X$ → MAC fails → **Rejected** |
| **Wormhole (Fast Replay)** | Tunnel packet across network | Key not yet disclosed at receiver | Replay arrives but key $K_i$ still unknown → cannot verify → **Rejected** |
| **Sinkhole (Fake Metric)** | Advertise high-quality route | MAC on routing metric | MAC forgery impossible → **Rejected** |
| **Selective Forwarding** | Drop packets silently | End-to-end µTESLA chain | Detected via base station verification → **Detected** |
| **Hello Flood** | Broadcast high-power HELLO | MAC of HELLO packet | Attacker can't MAC without key → **Rejected** |

---

## 3.5 Energy Cost Analysis (Board-Level Calculation)

**Question:** Estimate the additional energy cost of TESLA authentication per packet.

**Given:**
- MAC tag length = 8 bytes = **64 bits**
- HMAC computation energy = $0.6 \; \mu J/\text{bit}$
- Hash chain verification = 1 hash op per packet = $15 \; \mu J$
- Packet transmission energy = $1 \; \mu J/\text{bit}$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{MAC,compute}} &= 64 \text{ bits} \times 0.6 \; \mu J/\text{bit} = 38.4 \; \mu J \\
E_{\text{hash,verify}} &= 15 \; \mu J \\
E_{\text{MAC,transmit}} &= 64 \text{ bits} \times 1 \; \mu J/\text{bit} = 64 \; \mu J \\
E_{\text{TESLA,total}} &= 38.4 + 15 + 64 = 117.4 \; \mu J \\
\text{Overhead} &= \frac{E_{\text{TESLA,total}}}{E_{\text{packet,total}}} \approx \frac{117.4}{2000} \approx \mathbf{5.87\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Takeaway:** TESLA adds only **~6%** energy overhead — vastly lower than digital signatures (which add **~300-500%** for ECDSA on MICAz).

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define TESLA. What is the role of a one-way hash chain in TESLA authentication?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** TESLA (Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication) is a broadcast authentication protocol that provides source authentication and integrity for messages in WSNs using only symmetric primitives. It works by exploiting loose time synchronization between sender and receivers.

**One-Way Hash Chain Role (2 marks):** The one-way hash chain provides the **asymmetric security property** in TESLA without using public-key cryptography. The sender generates a sequence of keys $K_n, K_{n-1}, \dots, K_0$ where each key is derived by applying a hash function to the next: $K_{i-1} = H(K_i)$. Only the commitment key $K_0$ is publicly distributed initially. Since the hash function is one-way, receivers cannot predict future keys $K_j$ ($j \geq 1$) from $K_0$. This ensures that **only the sender can compute the MAC for a given interval**, since only the sender knows the future keys. The delayed disclosure of each key then allows receivers to verify the authenticity of all previously received packets.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Explain why TESLA is preferred over digital signatures for securing routing in WSNs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TESLA is preferred over digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA, DSA) for WSN routing because of the following reasons:

1. **Computational Cost (1 mark):** TESLA uses only symmetric MACs and hash functions, which require **~10,000× less energy** than ECDSA on resource-constrained motes. A digital signature (1024-bit RSA) takes ~300 ms on a MICAz, draining the battery rapidly, while TESLA MAC computation takes <1 ms.

2. **Memory Footprint (1 mark):** TESLA requires storing only a few keys (e.g., 4-8 hash chain elements) plus a small window of buffered packets. Digital signatures need large public key storage (128-256 bytes for ECDSA, 1024 bits for RSA).

3. **Bandwidth Efficiency (1 mark):** TESLA adds only 4-8 bytes of MAC per packet, while digital signatures add 48-128 bytes per packet, which is significant overhead for low-bandwidth WSNs (typically 250 kbps for IEEE 802.15.4).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** from the module. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: Describe the complete architecture of the TESLA protocol. With a neat diagram, explain the key chain construction, packet structure, and the verification process at the receiver.**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):** TESLA consists of three main components:
- The **Sender**, which maintains a hash chain and authenticates packets
- The **Receivers**, which buffer packets and verify them after key disclosure
- The **Time Synchronization Module**, which provides loose clock sync

**Key Chain Construction (2 marks):** The sender generates a random secret $K_n$ and computes the chain in reverse:
- $K_{n-1} = H(K_n)$
- $K_{n-2} = H(K_{n-1})$
- ...
- $K_0 = H^{(n)}(K_n)$

The commitment key $K_0$ is distributed to all receivers out-of-band before protocol execution.

**Packet Structure (1 mark):** Each packet $P$ contains:
$$P = \{\text{Data} \;\Vert\; \text{MAC}_{K_i}(\text{Data}) \;\Vert\; i \;\Vert\; K_{i-d}\}$$
where $i$ is the interval index, $K_i$ is the key used for MAC, and $K_{i-d}$ is the previously disclosed key.

**Receiver Verification Process (2 marks):**
1. **Step 1:** Receiver buffers packet $P$ on arrival.
2. **Step 2:** When key $K_i$ is disclosed (at end of interval $i + d$), receiver checks $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ (key authenticity).
3. **Step 3:** Receiver verifies the time condition: key was secret when packet was received.
4. **Step 4:** Receiver computes $\text{MAC}_{K_i}(\text{Data})$ and compares with the received MAC.

```
[Diagram Reference: See Section 4.1]
```

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: How does TESLA defend against (i) packet injection and (ii) replay attacks in WSN routing? Explain with relevant security conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Defence Against Packet Injection (3.5 marks):**

An attacker tries to inject bogus routing control packets (e.g., fake RREQ/RREP) into the network. Without authentication, such packets would propagate and disrupt routing.

**TESLA's Defence Mechanism:**
- The attacker **does not know the secret key** $K_i$ that is currently being used to MAC legitimate packets.
- Since the key $K_i$ is in the secret phase (not yet disclosed), the attacker **cannot compute a valid MAC** for the injected packet.
- When the receiver eventually receives the disclosed key $K_i$, it will:
  - Verify $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$ (authenticity)
  - Verify the MAC of the received packet matches $\text{MAC}_{K_i}(\text{Data})$
- The injected packet will **fail MAC verification** and be discarded.

**Relevant Security Condition:** *Condition 2 (Disclosure Lag):* $d \geq 1$ interval ensures the key remains secret during packet transmission.

---

**(ii) Defence Against Replay Attacks (3.5 marks):**

An attacker captures a legitimate packet $P$ (with valid MAC) and replays it later, hoping to confuse the routing protocol or replay old routing state.

**TESLA's Defence Mechanism:**
- Each packet's MAC is computed over the **interval index** $i$ in addition to the data:
$$\text{MAC} = \text{MAC}_{K_i}(\text{Data} \;\Vert\; i)$$
- The interval index $i$ acts as a **nonce**, ensuring freshness.
- If the attacker replays the packet in a later interval $j > i$, the receiver expects MACs computed with $K_j$ (or later disclosed keys), not $K_i$.
- Since the attacker does not know $K_j$ (it is still secret), the replayed MAC fails verification.

**Relevant Security Condition:** *Condition 1 (Time Lock-in):* $T_{\text{recv}} < T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + \delta$ — a replayed packet, even if authentic for interval $i$, is rejected if it's outside the receiver's valid verification window.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "the key is verified after disclosure" with "packets are valid only after disclosure." **CORRECT understanding:** The packet is **accepted on arrival but verified only after key disclosure.** The receiver must buffer all packets until the corresponding key arrives. Failing to mention buffering loses 2-3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: Explain the three critical security conditions that must be satisfied for TESLA to be secure. Derive the maximum tolerable clock skew.**

**Model Answer:**

**The Three Security Conditions (4 marks):**

**Condition 1 — Time Lock-in Condition:**
$$T_{\text{recv}} < T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i) = T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}$$
The receiver must be **certain** that the key $K_i$ used to MAC a packet was still secret at the time of reception. This prevents an attacker from pre-computing the key and forging packets.

**Condition 2 — Disclosure Lag Condition:**
$$d \geq 1 \text{ interval}$$
The sender must delay the disclosure of $K_i$ by at least one full interval after using it for MAC. This guarantees that by the time $K_i$ is known, all packets MACed with $K_i$ should have already reached the receivers.

**Condition 3 — Hash Chain Integrity Condition:**
$$H(K_i) = K_{i-1}, \quad \forall i \in [1, n]$$
The disclosed key $K_i$ must correctly hash to the previously trusted key $K_{i-1}$. This ensures the chain is unbroken and the sender cannot lie about any future key (because the receiver can check it against the committed chain).

---

**Derivation of Maximum Clock Skew (3 marks):**

Given the time-lock condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{recv}} &= T_s + \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} \\
T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i) &= T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum tolerable clock skew $\delta_{\max}$ is the difference between these, after accounting for the latest possible send time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\max} &= T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i) - T_s - \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} - \epsilon \\
&= T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}} + d \cdot T_{\text{int}} - T_s - \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} - \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

For the worst case $T_s \to T_{\text{start}}(I_i) + T_{\text{int}}$:

$$
\boxed{\delta_{\max} = d \cdot T_{\text{int}} - \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} - \epsilon}
$$

**Practical Example:** With $d = 2$, $T_{\text{int}} = 100$ ms, $\text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} = 10$ ms, $\epsilon = 5$ ms:
$\delta_{\max} = 200 - 10 - 5 = 185$ ms — sufficient for most WSN synchronization.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain how TESLA protects against wormhole and sinkhole attacks in WSN routing. What is the role of loose time synchronization?**

**Model Answer:**

**Wormhole Attack Defence (3 marks):**

In a wormhole attack, the adversary tunnels packets across a long physical distance (or even out-of-band channel) to create a false low-latency path.

**TESLA's Counter:**
- The wormhole tunnel replays packets at a remote location, but the receiving node still relies on **time-locked key disclosure**.
- A packet MACed with $K_i$ and replayed via wormhole will arrive at the **correct receiver's time window**, but the key $K_i$ is still secret at that moment.
- When the receiver later checks the key via $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$, it succeeds — **but the packet was already authenticated only via time-lock**, not via tunnel distance.
- Crucially, **the wormhole adversary cannot MAC new packets** with current $K_i$ because they don't know the secret. They can only replay old ones, which fail the freshness (interval) check.
- **Result:** Wormhole replay is limited to old valid packets (which are not useful for active disruption), and active forgery is impossible.

**Sinkhole Attack Defence (2 marks):**

In a sinkhole attack, a malicious node advertises an attractive routing path (e.g., high-quality metrics) to attract traffic and then drops or tampers with it.

**TESLA's Counter:**
- The advertised routing metric must be **MAC-authenticated** with the sender's current key $K_i$.
- Since the sinkhole attacker does not know the legitimate sender's key chain, **they cannot MAC a fake route metric**.
- The receiver verifies the MAC after key disclosure → fake metrics are rejected → sinkhole is rendered ineffective.

**Role of Loose Time Synchronization (2 marks):**

Loose time synchronization (knowing $\delta$ but not exact times) is sufficient for TESLA because:
- The receiver only needs an **upper bound** $\delta$ on the clock skew to determine the safe verification window.
- The protocol does not require precision clock sync (which is expensive in WSNs via NTP-like protocols).
- This makes TESLA practical for low-cost sensor deployments where precise sync is infeasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Zone:** When explaining TESLA's defence against attacks, students often forget to mention that the **interval index is included in the MAC input**. The interval index serves as a **freshness nonce**, and mentioning this is critical for the replay-attack defence to score full marks. Omitting this loses 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for TESLA:**

✅ **TESLA = Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication** — provides broadcast authentication for WSNs using only symmetric primitives.

✅ **Three Pillars of TESLA:**
- **One-way hash chain** (asymmetric property from symmetric primitives)
- **Loose time synchronization** (only $\delta$ needed, not exact sync)
- **Delayed key disclosure** (key released after use)

✅ **Hash Chain:** $K_{i-1} = H(K_i)$, $K_0$ is the publicly committed key.

✅ **MAC Computation:** $MAC_j = \text{MAC}_{K_i}(P_j)$, where $i$ is the current interval.

✅ **Three Security Conditions (must all hold):**
- Time lock-in: $T_{\text{recv}} < T_{\text{disclose}}(K_i)$
- Disclosure lag: $d \geq 1$
- Hash chain integrity: $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$

✅ **Maximum Clock Skew:** $\delta_{\max} = d \cdot T_{\text{int}} - \text{Delay}_{\text{prop}} - \epsilon$

✅ **Buffer Size:** $B = R \cdot d \cdot T_{\text{int}}$ packets

✅ **Energy Overhead:** ~5-15% per packet (vs. 300-500% for digital signatures)

✅ **Defended Routing Attacks:**
- Packet injection (no MAC forgery)
- Replay (interval index in MAC acts as nonce)
- Sybil (per-node key chain)
- Wormhole (time-lock prevents forgery)
- Sinkhole (metric authentication)
- Selective forwarding (end-to-end verification)

✅ **Variants:** TESLA → µTESLA → Multi-level µTESLA → D-TESLA → RT-TESLA → TESLA++

✅ **Key Insight:** TESLA achieves *asymmetric authentication* via *temporal asymmetry* (the key is secret at send time, public at verify time).

✅ **Comparison with Signatures:** TESLA = ~5% overhead; ECDSA = ~300% overhead on MICAz.

✅ **Standard Hash Function:** SHA-1 (160-bit) or MD5 (128-bit); MAC truncated to 4-8 bytes for bandwidth efficiency.

✅ **Time Sync Bound:** Loose sync with upper-bound $\delta$ suffices — full NTP-grade precision NOT required.

✅ **Buffering Requirement:** Receiver MUST buffer packets until key disclosure — failing to mention loses marks.

✅ **Authenticity Check Order:** First verify $H(K_i) = K_{i-1}$, then verify MAC. Wrong order wastes computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SPINS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SPINS # 🔐 SPINS: Security Protocols for Sensor Networks ## 1. Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SPINS (Security Protocols for Sensor Networks)** is a suite of cryptographic security protocols designed by **Adrian Perrig, Robert Szewczyk, J. D. Tygar, Wen, and Culler (2002)** specifically for resource-constrained sensor networks. It is built upon two fundamental building blocks: > > 1. **SNEP (Secure Network Encryption Protocol)** — handles **point-to-point** secure communication (confidentiality, two-party data authentication, message integrity, and freshness). > 2. **μTESLA (micro Timed, Efficient, Streaming, Loss-tolerant Authentication)** — handles **authenticated broadcast** through delayed disclosure of symmetric keys. > [!NOTE] > **Why SPINS matters in KTU syllabus:** Sensor motes (e.g., Berkeley MICA, Mica2Dot) have only **8-bit processors, 4–10 KB RAM, and 100 Kbps bandwidth**. Heavy public-key cryptography (RSA) is impractical. SPINS uses **only symmetric-key cryptography (RC5, CBC-MAC, HMAC)** to achieve equivalent security guarantees. --- ## 2. Intuitive Analogy: The "Sealed Envelope with a Delayed Key" 📨 Imagine two people, **Alice** and **Bob**, communicating in a hostile room full of eavesdroppers. | Real-World Analogy | SPINS Equivalent | |---|---| | Alice writes a letter in a **locked diary** whose page number changes every time | **Counter-mode (CTR) encryption** with monotonically increasing counters | | Alice seals the letter with a **wax seal unique to this message** | **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** using a shared key | | Each letter has a **serial number** to prevent replay | **Counter-based replay protection** | | Bob announces "Tonight's prime-time show password is…" **after everyone has gone home** | **μTESLA delayed key disclosure** for broadcast authentication | > [!NOTE] > **The clever insight:** In SPINS, *every node shares a unique master secret key with the Base Station (BS)*. There are **no pairwise keys** between arbitrary sensor nodes — a major optimization for memory-constrained motes. --- ## 3. Threat Model & Assumptions SPINS assumes the following adversary model: - The attacker can **passively eavesdrop** on all radio transmissions. - The attacker can **actively inject, modify, replay, or jam** messages. - Each sensor node has a **trustworthy, tamper-resistant environment** (e.g., a secure coprocessor) only for the **master key K_master**. - The **Base Station (BS)** is the *trusted third party* and is assumed to be a laptop-class device with abundant resources. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse SPINS with full pairwise key distribution (like in traditional networks). Remember: **SPINS centralizes trust at the Base Station** — only $n$ master keys for $n$ nodes, not $O(n^2)$. # 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis: SNEP & μTESLA ## Part A: SNEP — Secure Network Encryption Protocol SNEP provides **five distinct security properties** for unicast communication between a sensor node $A$ and the base station $BS$ (or another node $B$). ### Property 1: Semantic Security (Confidentiality) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Semantic security** ensures that encrypting the *same plaintext twice* produces *different ciphertexts*. This is critical because sensor data is often **highly redundant** (e.g., periodic temperature readings). **Mechanism:** SNEP uses **Counter (CTR) mode encryption**. $$E = \{D\} \langle K_{enc}, C \rangle$$ Where: - $D$ = plaintext data - $K_{enc}$ = encryption key (derived from master key) - $C$ = counter value (incremented at both ends) - $E$ = ciphertext output **XOR Operation:** $$E = D \oplus F(K_{enc}, C)$$ Where $F(\cdot)$ is a **pseudorandom function (PRF)** — implemented using RC5 in the original SPINS paper. --- ### Property 2: Data Authentication & Integrity > [!NOTE] > Authentication is achieved via a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** computed using **CBC-MAC** with the authentication key $K_{mac}$. $$M = \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, C \mid E)$$ The MAC is appended to the encrypted message. The receiver recomputes the MAC and compares. --- ### Property 3: Replay Protection A **monotonically increasing counter** $C$ is included in the MAC computation: - If an attacker replays an old message, the **counter value will be stale**. - Both sender and receiver maintain synchronized counters. - If counters fall out of sync, an **anti-replay protocol** is used. > [!WARNING] > **Counter desynchronization** is a real issue. SPINS uses a counter-exchange mechanism: the receiver sends its current counter $C'$ to the sender whenever messages arrive with $C' < C_{expected}$. --- ### Property 4 & 5: Weak vs. Strong Freshness | Freshness Type | Guarantee | Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Weak Freshness** | Message was sent *after* the previous message from the same sender (partial ordering) | Counter value $C$ | | **Strong Freshness** | Message was generated *in response to a specific challenge* (total ordering) | **Nonce** $N_A$ or $N_B$ | **Strong freshness example (request-response):** $$A \rightarrow B : N_A$$ $$B \rightarrow A : \{D_B\} \langle K_{enc}, C_B \rangle, \ \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, N_A \mid C_B \mid E_B)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The nonce $N_A$ binds the response to the specific request, preventing an attacker from replaying an old response. --- ### 📊 SNEP Message Format | Field | Size (typical) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | $E$ (encrypted data) | Variable | Ciphertext (XOR with PRF output) | | $C$ (counter) | 32 bits | Replay protection | | $M$ (MAC) | 64 bits (8 bytes) | Authentication tag | **Total overhead:** Only **8 bytes per message** — extremely efficient! --- ## Part B: μTESLA — Authenticated Broadcast > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Broadcasting is needed for software updates, routing beacons, and query dissemination. But **asymmetric digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA) are too expensive** for sensor motes (1024-bit RSA takes ~10 seconds on a MICA mote). > > **Solution:** μTESLA uses **delayed key disclosure** of *symmetric* keys, leveraging the fact that **time synchronization** between senders and receivers is feasible in WSNs. ### Core Concept (Step by Step) > [!NOTE] > μTESLA is based on **TESLA** (used for Internet multicast) but adapted for **highly resource-constrained sensor nodes** through optimizations like *per-key computation* using a one-way function. **Step 1 — Time Division into Intervals:** The base station divides time into uniform intervals $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$. Each interval has a duration of one time slot. **Step 2 — Key Chain Generation:** The base station picks a **random master key** $K_n$ and generates a one-way key chain using a **one-way function** $F$ (e.g., SHA-1 or RC5): $$K_i = F(K_{i+1}) \quad \text{for } i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 0$$ **One-way property:** Knowing $K_i$ makes it **computationally infeasible** to compute $K_{i+1}$. **Step 3 — Key Assignment:** - Key $K_i$ is used to **MAC all broadcast messages** sent during interval $I_i$. - Key $K_i$ is **disclosed publicly at time $T_i + \delta$**, where $\delta$ is the disclosure delay (e.g., 2 time slots). **Step 4 — Verification:** - A receiver buffers all messages received in interval $I_i$. - When key $K_i$ is disclosed at time $T_i + \delta$, the receiver: 1. Verifies that $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ (using a previously authenticated key). 2. Verifies all MACs computed with $K_i$. --- ### μTESLA Optimizations (Tailored for WSNs) > [!NOTE] > μTESLA differs from TESLA in three key ways to fit sensor constraints: | Optimization | TESLA | μTESLA | |---|---|---| | **Key chain computation** | Sender stores all keys | Sender uses a one-way function and generates keys on demand | | **Authentication of first key** | Requires digital signature | Uses **SNEP unicast** to send initial key | | **Message overhead** | Larger | Reduced by inlining keys in subsequent packets | --- ## 🎯 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation / Concept | Formula | Purpose | |---|---|---| | CTR-mode encryption | $E = D \oplus F(K_{enc}, C)$ | Semantic security | | MAC computation | $M = \text{CBC-MAC}(K_{mac}, C \mid E)$ | Authentication & integrity | | Key chain | $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ | μTESLA one-way chain | | Disclosure condition | $K_i$ revealed at $T_i + \delta$ | Authentication delay | | Nonce-based freshness | MAC over nonce $N_A$ | Strong freshness | | Master key derivation | $K_{enc} = F(K_{master}, 2)$, $K_{mac} = F(K_{master}, 3)$ | Per-purpose subkeys | | Key overlap property | $K_i$ used in $I_i$, $K_j$ used in $I_j$, with $j < i$ | Strict temporal ordering | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus highlight:** Memorize the **MAC format for strong freshness requests** and the **key chain generation equation** — both are recurring 14-mark question topics in KTU ESE. --- ## 🌍 Real-World Utility - SPINS is foundational to **TinyOS security** and has influenced protocols like **TinySec**, **MiniSec**, and **ContikiSec**. - μTESLA's delayed-disclosure paradigm is the precursor to modern **time-based authentication schemes** in IoT. - Used as a reference architecture in **WSN textbooks** (Karl & Willig, Wang & Li). # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Security Properties of SNEP from First Principles We are given a master key $K_{master}$ shared between node $A$ and base station $BS$. We need to derive subkeys and verify security properties. ### Step 1 — Subkey Derivation > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** From a single master key $K_{master}$, derive three independent subkeys to ensure **cryptographic separation of concerns** (so compromising one key does not compromise all properties). $$K_{enc} = F^{(1)}(K_{master})$$ $$K_{mac} = F^{(2)}(K_{master})$$ $$K_{rand} = F^{(3)}(K_{master})$$ In practice (per Perrig et al.), the convention is: $$K_{enc} = F(K_{master}, 2)$$ $$K_{mac} = F(K_{master}, 3)$$ $$K_{rand} = F(K_{master}, 4)$$ where $F$ is the RC5-based pseudorandom function. --- ### Step 2 — Encryption and Authentication A message $D$ from $A$ to $BS$ is constructed as: $$\text{SNEP Message} = E \mid M$$ where $$E = \{D\}_{\langle K_{enc}, C \rangle} = D \oplus F(K_{enc}, C)$$ $$M = \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, C \mid E)$$ **Proof of Semantic Security (sketch):** Assume the PRF $F$ is indistinguishable from a truly random function. Then $F(K_{enc}, C)$ is a **uniform random pad**. XOR-ing $D$ with this pad yields a ciphertext that leaks **no information about $D$**, even across multiple encryptions of the same $D$ (since $C$ changes). $$\therefore \text{SNEP} \Rightarrow \text{Semantic Security} \blacksquare$$ **Proof of Authentication (sketch):** A passive/active attacker who does not know $K_{mac}$ cannot forge a valid MAC for a chosen ciphertext. By the **unforgeability of CBC-MAC**, the probability of forgery is at most $2^{-n}$ where $n$ is the MAC length (e.g., 64 bits). $$\therefore \text{SNEP} \Rightarrow \text{Data Authentication} \blacksquare$$ --- ### Step 3 — Counter Synchronization Anti-Replay > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** If $A$'s counter $C_A = 100$ and $BS$'s counter $C_{BS} = 105$ (due to lost messages), how does $A$ recover? **Anti-replay protocol:** $$A \rightarrow BS : N_A$$ $$BS \rightarrow A : C_{BS}, \ \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, N_A \mid C_{BS})$$ $A$ then updates: $$C_A \leftarrow C_{BS}$$ This **resyncs** the counters over an authenticated channel (the MAC provides authentication). --- ## Derivation 2: μTESLA Key Chain Authenticity > [!NOTE] > **Claim:** If $K_0$ is authenticated (e.g., via SNEP), and $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ for all $i$, then revealing $K_i$ does not compromise $K_{i+1}, K_{i+2}, \ldots$ **Proof sketch:** Let $F$ be a **one-way function** (preimage-resistant). Given $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$: $$\text{Pr}[\text{attacker computes } K_{i+1} \mid K_i] \leq \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon$ is a negligible function in the security parameter. $$\therefore \text{Key chain security} \blacksquare$$ **Authentication of subsequent keys:** Once $K_i$ is known, the receiver verifies: $$F(K_{i+1}) \stackrel{?}{=} K_i$$ If equal, $K_{i+1}$ is **authentic** (the attacker cannot have produced a collision due to one-wayness). --- ## Derivation 3: Overhead Comparison > [!NOTE] > **Sizing example for KTU numerical-style questions.** **SNEP packet overhead per message:** $$\text{Overhead}_{SNEP} = |C| + |M| = 32 \text{ bits} + 64 \text{ bits} = 96 \text{ bits} = 12 \text{ bytes}$$ **μTESLA broadcast overhead per message:** $$\text{Overhead}_{\mu TESLA} = |K_i| \text{ (inlined per } T \text{ messages)} + |MAC| \approx 8 \text{ bytes}$$ **RSA signature overhead for comparison (why SPINS is necessary):** $$\text{Overhead}_{RSA-1024} = 1024 \text{ bits} = 128 \text{ bytes}$$ **Efficiency ratio:** $$\frac{\text{Overhead}_{RSA}}{\text{Overhead}_{SNEP}} = \frac{128}{12} \approx 10.67 \times$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > SPINS achieves **10× lower communication overhead** than public-key alternatives — a critical win for bandwidth-starved WSNs. --- ## 🧠 Analytical Matrix: SNEP vs. μTESLA | Dimension | SNEP | μTESLA | |---|---|---| | **Communication type** | Unicast / point-to-point | Broadcast / multicast | | **Crypto primitive** | Symmetric (RC5, CBC-MAC) | Symmetric (MAC + one-way chain) | | **Freshness** | Counter + nonce | Time-based (interval $I_i$) | | **Authentication timing** | Immediate (with message) | Delayed (after $\delta$ intervals) | | **Key management** | Per-node master key with BS | Shared key chain per sender | | **Time synchronization** | Not required | **Required** | | **Buffer requirement** | Low | High (must buffer until key disclosure) | | **Failure mode** | Counter desync | Time desync / packet loss | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: SPINS Protocol Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD node1["Base Station BS
Trusted Third Party"] node2["Node A
Sensor Mote"] node3["Node B
Sensor Mote"] node4["SNEP Module
Unicast Security"] node5["muTESLA Module
Broadcast Security"] node6["Master Key K_AB
Shared with BS"] node7["Master Key K_BB
Shared with BS"] node8["Key Chain K_0 to K_n
One-way function F"] node9["Pseudorandom F RC5
Encrypts and Authenticates"] node10["CBC-MAC
Authentication Tag"] node11["Time-Synced
Intervals I_0 to I_n"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node8 node2 --> node6 node3 --> node7 node6 --> node4 node7 --> node4 node8 --> node5 node4 --> node9 node4 --> node10 node5 --> node11 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The Base Station is the *cryptographic anchor* for both unicast (via SNEP) and broadcast (via μTESLA) communications. All sensor nodes share a master key **only with the BS** — not with each other directly. --- ## Diagram 2: μTESLA Key Chain & Disclosure Timeline ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["K_n
Random Master"] nodeB["K_n-1 = F K_n"] nodeC["K_n-2 = F K_n-1"] nodeD["K_0 = F K_1"] nodeE["Interval I_0
MAC with K_0"] nodeF["Interval I_1
MAC with K_1"] nodeG["Interval I_2
MAC with K_2"] nodeH["Reveal K_0
At T_0 + delta"] nodeI["Reveal K_1
At T_1 + delta"] nodeJ["Reveal K_2
At T_2 + delta"] nodeK["Verify F K_1 = K_0"] nodeL["Verify F K_2 = K_1"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeE -.-> nodeH nodeF -.-> nodeI nodeG -.-> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeK nodeI --> nodeL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight:** The arrows go *backward in time*. The key $K_0$ (used in interval $I_0$) is **derived from** $K_1$, which is itself derived from $K_2$, and so on. When $K_0$ is finally disclosed, the receiver can verify all messages from $I_0$ by checking $F(K_1) = K_0$. --- ## Diagram 3: SNEP Strong Freshness Handshake ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Node A participant B as Node B A->>B: Nonce N_A Note over B: Increment counter C_B B->>A: E = D XOR F K_enc, C_B B->>A: MAC K_mac, N_A | C_B | E Note over A: Verify MAC matches N_A A->>A: Decrypt E using K_enc, C_B Note over A: Strong freshness guaranteed ``` > [!NOTE] > The **nonce $N_A$** is the critical element. It binds the response to the request, preventing an attacker from replaying an old response to a new request. --- ## Diagram 4: SPINS Threat Coverage Matrix ```mermaid graph TD nodeT1["Passive Eavesdropping"] nodeT2["Message Injection"] nodeT3["Message Modification"] nodeT4["Replay Attack"] nodeT5["Compromised Node"] nodeD1["SNEP CTR Mode
Semantic Security"] nodeD2["SNEP CBC-MAC
Integrity Check"] nodeD3["Counter and Nonce
Replay Defense"] nodeD4["muTESLA Key Chain
Broadcast Auth"] nodeD5["Out of Scope
Requires Tamper-proof HW"] nodeT1 --> nodeD1 nodeT2 --> nodeD2 nodeT3 --> nodeD2 nodeT4 --> nodeD3 nodeT4 --> nodeD4 nodeT5 --> nodeD5 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Compromised nodes are out of scope for SPINS.** If an attacker physically captures a node and extracts its master key, SPINS provides no defense. This requires **tamper-proof hardware** — a key KTU discussion point. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between weak freshness and strong freshness as used in the SNEP protocol.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Weak Freshness | Strong Freshness | |---|---|---| | **Guarantee** | Partial ordering — message is *newer* than the previous one | Total ordering — message is generated *in response* to a specific challenge | | **Implementation** | Monotonically increasing counter $C$ | Random nonce $N$ generated by the requester | | **Use case** | Periodic data reporting | Request-response, time-critical commands | | **Attack prevented** | Replay of very old messages | Replay of any old response (even recent) | (3 marks — full marks require both the conceptual distinction and an example of each.) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Why does SPINS use symmetric-key cryptography instead of public-key cryptography like RSA?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Resource constraints:** Sensor motes (e.g., MICA2) have 8-bit processors and ~10 KB RAM. A **1024-bit RSA signature takes 10–30 seconds** on such hardware, making it impractical for time-sensitive WSN applications. 2. **Energy:** Public-key operations consume **~100× more energy** than symmetric-key operations (RC5, CBC-MAC). 3. **Bandwidth:** RSA signatures are **1024 bits** per message, while SPINS SNEP adds only **96 bits** of overhead per message. 4. **Adequate security:** SPINS achieves **confidentiality, authentication, integrity, and freshness** using only symmetric primitives, by leveraging the Base Station as a trusted key distribution center. (3 marks — full marks require at least three concrete justifications with metrics.) --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both must be answered together. --- ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — SNEP Deep Dive #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the SNEP protocol with its security properties. Show the message format and explain how semantic security and replay protection are achieved.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1.5 marks):** SNEP is the unicast security component of SPINS. It provides **confidentiality, two-party data authentication, message integrity, replay protection, and freshness** using only symmetric cryptography between a sensor node and the Base Station. **Key Derivation (1 mark):** From a single master key $K_{master}$, three subkeys are derived using a pseudorandom function $F$: $$K_{enc} = F(K_{master}, 2), \quad K_{mac} = F(K_{master}, 3), \quad K_{rand} = F(K_{master}, 4)$$ **Message Format (1.5 marks):** $$A \rightarrow B : \{D\}_{\langle K_{enc}, C \rangle}, \ \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, C \mid E)$$ **Semantic Security (1.5 marks):** - Uses **CTR-mode encryption**: $E = D \oplus F(K_{enc}, C)$. - The counter $C$ changes with every message, so the *same plaintext produces different ciphertexts* on each encryption. - Since $F(K_{enc}, C)$ is a pseudorandom pad, the ciphertext leaks no information. **Replay Protection (1.5 marks):** - Each message carries the current counter $C$, which is part of the MAC. - Both ends maintain synchronized counters. - If counters desynchronize, an authenticated counter-resync protocol is invoked (using nonces and MACs). --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain strong freshness in SNEP with a suitable handshake diagram. Why is it needed for time-critical commands?** **Model Answer:** **Concept (2 marks):** Strong freshness ensures that the received message was **generated in direct response to a specific request**, not replayed from an earlier session. It provides **total ordering** rather than partial ordering. **Handshake (3 marks):** **Step 1 (Request):** $$A \rightarrow B : N_A$$ (where $N_A$ is a fresh random nonce chosen by $A$) **Step 2 (Response):** $$B \rightarrow A : \{D_B\}_{\langle K_{enc}, C_B \rangle}, \ \text{MAC}(K_{mac}, N_A \mid C_B \mid E_B)$$ **Step 3 (Verification by A):** - Verify MAC: recompute $\text{MAC}(K_{mac}, N_A \mid C_B \mid E_B)$ and compare. - Decrypt: $D_B = E_B \oplus F(K_{enc}, C_B)$. **Why needed (2 marks):** - For **actuation commands** (e.g., "open valve," "shut down motor"), a stale command could be catastrophic. - Weak freshness only guarantees "newer than last message," but an attacker could capture a recent valid command and replay it after the operator has changed their mind. - The nonce $N_A$ binds the response to the *current* request, making replay impossible. --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — μTESLA Deep Dive (Alternative Choice) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the μTESLA protocol. How does it achieve broadcast authentication in WSNs using only symmetric cryptography?** **Model Answer:** **Problem Statement (1 mark):** Broadcasts (e.g., software updates, routing beacons) are needed in WSNs, but public-key signatures are too expensive. Asymmetric digital signatures are the natural choice for broadcast authentication, but infeasible for 8-bit motes. **Core Idea (2 marks):** μTESLA achieves broadcast authentication by: 1. **Time-synchronizing** the sender and receivers. 2. Using a **symmetric MAC** for each packet. 3. **Delaying the disclosure** of the MAC key until *after* the packet was sent. 4. Receivers **buffer packets** and verify them once the key is disclosed. **Key Chain Construction (2 marks):** The sender generates a one-way key chain: $$K_0 = F(K_1), \quad K_1 = F(K_2), \quad \ldots, \quad K_{n-1} = F(K_n)$$ where $K_n$ is a random master and $F$ is a one-way function (e.g., SHA-1). **Protocol Flow (2 marks):** - Time is divided into intervals $I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n$. - In interval $I_i$, packets are MAC'd with $K_i$. - Key $K_i$ is disclosed at time $T_i + \delta$ (e.g., 2 slots later). - Receivers verify $F(K_i) \stackrel{?}{=} K_{i-1}$ (using the previously disclosed key). - The first key $K_0$ is bootstrapped via **SNEP unicast** (which uses the master key $K_{master}$ for authentication). --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **List and explain the three key optimizations of μTESLA over TESLA that make it suitable for sensor networks.** **Model Answer:** **Optimization 1: Efficient Key Chain Computation (2.5 marks):** - **In TESLA:** The sender must store all $n$ keys in the chain, requiring large memory. - **In μTESLA:** The sender stores only $K_n$ and computes $K_i = F^{(n-i)}(K_n)$ on demand, using a one-way function. This reduces memory to **$O(1)$ per node** — critical for RAM-constrained motes. **Optimization 2: Bootstrapping the First Key via SNEP (2.5 marks):** - **In TESLA:** The first key $K_0$ is authenticated using a **digital signature** (PKI infrastructure required). - **In μTESLA:** The first key $K_0$ is delivered to each node via a **SNEP-authenticated unicast** from the Base Station. This leverages the already-established master key, requiring no public-key infrastructure. **Optimization 3: Reduced Per-Packet Overhead (2 marks):** - **In TESLA:** Each packet carries the key disclosure, increasing per-packet overhead. - **In μTESLA:** The disclosed key is **inlined only once per multiple packets**, amortizing the cost. The MAC and disclosed key add only ~8 bytes per packet, fitting within typical WSN frame sizes. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common reasons for losing marks in SPINS questions:** > > 1. **Confusing SNEP with μTESLA's purpose:** SNEP is for *unicast*, μTESLA is for *broadcast*. Mixing up their properties costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the role of the Base Station:** Every node shares a master key *only with the BS*, not with peer nodes. Writing "nodes share keys with each other" is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Not mentioning time synchronization for μTESLA:** μTESLA is **fundamentally impossible** without time sync. Students who skip this lose 2 marks. > 4. **Writing the equation $E = D \oplus F(K, C)$** without explaining that $F$ is the **pseudorandom function**, not the encryption algorithm itself. Examiners expect the RC5-based PRF context. > 5. **Omitting the disclosure delay $\delta$:** The value of $\delta$ is critical — it must be **greater than the maximum packet loss recovery time** so receivers can buffer and verify. > 6. **Strong vs. weak freshness:** Conflating these is a classic 2-mark trap in SNEP questions. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — SPINS** > > - ⭐ **SPINS = SNEP + μTESLA** — two-component suite proposed by Perrig et al. (2002). > - ⭐ **SNEP properties:** Semantic security (CTR mode), authentication (CBC-MAC), replay protection (counter), weak/strong freshness (counter/nonce). > - ⭐ **SNEP overhead:** Only **8–12 bytes per message** — ultra-efficient. > - ⭐ **Key derivation:** $K_{enc}, K_{mac}, K_{rand}$ all derived from $K_{master}$ via a PRF $F$ (RC5). > - ⭐ **μTESLA = delayed key disclosure** of symmetric keys, leveraging time synchronization. > - ⭐ **Key chain equation:** $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ — one-way chain. > - ⭐ **Disclosure rule:** Key $K_i$ is revealed at time $T_i + \delta$ (after $\delta$ intervals). > - ⭐ **μTESLA optimizations over TESLA:** $O(1)$ key storage, SNEP bootstrapping, reduced overhead. > - ⭐ **Threat model:** SPINS defends against **eavesdropping, injection, modification, replay** — but **not physical node capture** (out of scope). > - ⭐ **Master key distribution:** Every node shares a master key **only with the BS** — centralizes trust and reduces key storage to $O(n)$. > - ⭐ **Why no public key?** Public-key operations are **100× slower and 10× larger** — infeasible for 8-bit motes. > - ⭐ **Time sync is mandatory** for μTESLA — without it, the entire authentication model collapses. > - ⭐ **Buffer requirement** for receivers: must store all packets from $I_i$ until $K_i$ is disclosed.

topics.title: Intrusion Detection in WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection in WSNs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Intrusion Detection System (IDS) in WSNs:** A security mechanism that monitors, collects, and analyzes audit data from monitored sensor nodes in order to detect malicious activities (intrusions) that compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of the network, while remaining resource-aware due to the inherent energy, computation, and memory constraints of sensor motes. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, an **Intrusion** is any unauthorized attempt to compromise the network's routing fabric, data integrity, or node availability. Because WSNs operate in hostile, unattended, and physically accessible environments, traditional cryptographic defenses alone are insufficient — the system requires a **second line of defense**, which is precisely the role of an IDS. ## 1.2 The CIA Triad in WSN Context | Security Property | WSN-Specific Meaning | Example Threat | |---|---|---| | **Confidentiality** | Sensor data (temperature, military info) must not leak to adversaries | Eavesdropping on RF channel | | **Integrity** | Aggregated data must be unaltered during multi-hop routing | False data injection, sinkhole | | **Availability** | Network must respond to queries despite attacks | Denial-of-Service, jamming | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Night Watchman in a Factory:** > Imagine a large, automated factory (the WSN) filled with hundreds of small, low-battery sensors monitoring machines. The factory has a basic lock on the door (cryptography = first line of defense). However, an intruder might still break in, disable sensors, or feed false readings. To protect against this, the factory hires a **night watchman** (the IDS) who periodically: > 1. **Walks around** (monitoring) checking each sensor's status. > 2. **Compares** what the sensor says to what is "normal" (anomaly detection). > 3. **Sounds an alarm** when a reading is suspiciously high or a sensor suddenly goes silent (alarm generation). > 4. **Uses minimal power**, so he does not drain the factory's electricity budget (energy-awareness). The watchman does not prevent intrusions (he is not a wall), but he **detects** them — which is exactly the role of an IDS. ## 1.4 Why WSNs Are Uniquely Vulnerable > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Reasons IDS is Non-Negotiable in WSNs (High-Yield KTU Point):** > - **Wireless Medium:** Open broadcast channel → trivial to eavesdrop and inject packets. > - **Resource Constraints:** Limited battery (typically **2 AA batteries = 2.4 V, ~2000 mAh**), limited RAM (often **4–10 KB**), and 8-bit microcontrollers → cannot run heavyweight crypto/IDS protocols. > - **Unattended Deployment:** Nodes are physically accessible → node capture and tampering are realistic threats. > - **Hostile Environments:** Battlefield, ocean floor, forest → no physical security. > - **Multi-hop Routing:** Each forwarding node is a potential insider threat. > - **Dynamic Topology:** Mobile or failed nodes complicate trust management. ## 1.5 The Three-Tier Security Model in WSNs | Tier | Mechanism | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Tier 1** | **Prevention** | Cryptography, key management, secure routing | | **Tier 2** | **Detection** | **IDS** (this topic) | | **Tier 3** | **Reaction / Recovery** | Revocation, re-routing, isolation of malicious nodes | The IDS occupies **Tier 2** — the critical middle layer that catches what prevention misses. ## 1.6 Insider vs. Outsider Attacks (KTU Classification) - **Outsider Attack:** Adversary has no cryptographic keys; launches DoS, jamming, traffic analysis. Partially mitigated by prevention. - **Insider Attack (Compromised Node):** A legitimate node's keys are extracted via physical tampering. It can now forge, drop, or alter packets — **prevention fails, IDS becomes essential.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Placement of IDS in WSN Protocol Stack The IDS hooks into multiple layers of the protocol stack simultaneously: > [!NOTE] > **Layer-wise IDS Responsibility:** > - **Physical Layer:** Detects jamming, node tampering, energy-depletion attacks. > - **Data Link (MAC) Layer:** Detects collision attacks, exhaustion attacks, unfairness. > - **Network (Routing) Layer:** Detects sinkhole, wormhole, blackhole, selective forwarding, Sybil, hello flood. > - **Application Layer:** Detects false data injection, clock skew, aggregation tampering. ## 2.2 Three Fundamental IDS Architectures (KTU High-Yield) ### A. Distributed / Cooperative IDS (Most Common in WSN) - **Architecture:** Every node (or a subset called **watchdog / monitor nodes**) runs a lightweight IDS agent. - **Workflow:** Local node collects audit data → runs lightweight detection → cooperates with neighbors via vote/aggregation. - **Pros:** No single point of failure; energy distributed. - **Cons:** Vulnerable to colluding adversaries; high communication overhead. - **KTU Use Case:** Most WSN IDS literature (e.g., watchdogs in routing). ### B. Centralized / Hierarchical IDS - **Architecture:** A powerful **base station (BS)** or cluster head collects all audit logs and runs the detection algorithm. - **Pros:** High accuracy, simpler algorithms. - **Cons:** Single point of failure; huge energy cost at BS-side; high latency. - **KTU Use Case:** Practical only in small or clustered WSNs. ### C. Hybrid IDS - **Architecture:** Combines distributed local detection with centralized global correlation. - **Pros:** Best trade-off between accuracy and overhead. - **Cons:** More complex protocol design. - **KTU Use Case:** Modern cluster-based WSN IDS papers. ## 2.3 Three Detection Methodologies (CORE KTU CONCEPT) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Detection Method Classification — Must Memorize for KTU:** | Method | Underlying Principle | Detects Known Attacks? | Detects Novel Attacks? | WSN Suitability | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Anomaly-Based** | Models "normal" behavior; flags deviations | Yes (false positives) | **Yes** ✓ | High (if lightweight model) | | **Misuse / Signature-Based** | Matches observed activity against a database of attack signatures | **Yes** (high accuracy) | No ✗ | Low (signature DB too large) | | **Specification-Based** | Uses a defined specification of correct behavior | Yes (low false positives) | Limited | **Highest for WSN** ✓ | ## 2.4 Detailed Mechanism of Anomaly Detection (KTU Favourite) **Step-by-step logical flow:** 1. **Training Phase:** The IDS collects baseline traffic features for a period $T_{\text{train}}$. Features include packet rate, packet size distribution, inter-arrival time, route diversity, and energy consumption rate. 2. **Statistical / ML Model Construction:** A model $\mathcal{M}$ is learned — examples: Gaussian distribution, Markov chain, SVM, one-class neural network. 3. **Detection Phase:** For each new observation $x_t$ at time $t$, the system computes an **anomaly score** $S(x_t)$. 4. **Decision Rule:** If $S(x_t) > \tau$ (threshold), raise an alarm. 5. **Feedback / Update:** Models may be periodically retrained to adapt to legitimate behavioral drift. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Memorize These Formulas — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU Numerical Questions.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_D = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | **Detection Probability** (Recall / True Positive Rate) | dimensionless | | 2 | $P_F = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | **False Positive Rate** | dimensionless | | 3 | $P_M = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = 1 - P_D$ | **Miss (False Negative) Rate** | dimensionless | | 4 | $A_c = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | **Detection Accuracy** | dimensionless | | 5 | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P_D \cdot \text{Prec}}{P_D + \text{Prec}}$ | **F1-Score** (harmonic mean) | dimensionless | | 6 | $E_{\text{IDS}} = N_m \cdot (e_s + e_r + e_c)$ | **Energy cost of distributed IDS** | Joules (J) | | 7 | $P_{\text{coll}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | **Probability of $n$ nodes collaborating to evade** | dimensionless | | 8 | $\tau = \mu + k\sigma$ | **Anomaly threshold** (statistical) | feature units | | 9 | $\text{SNR} = \frac{P_r}{P_n}$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** (jamming detector) | dimensionless | | 10 | $R_{\text{event}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{arr}}}$ | **Packet arrival rate** (anomaly input) | packets/s | > **Definitions of the confusion-matrix entries (used in formulas 1–5):** > - $TP$ = True Positives (correctly detected intrusions) > - $TN$ = True Negatives (correctly identified normal behavior) > - $FP$ = False Positives (false alarms) > - $FN$ = False Negatives (missed intrusions) > $N_m$ = number of monitor nodes, $e_s$ = sensing energy, $e_r$ = receiving energy, $e_c$ = computation energy, $p$ = per-node betrayal probability, $\mu$ = mean of normal feature, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $k$ = sensitivity factor (typically $2 \le k \le 3$). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where WSN IDS is deployed in practice:** > - **Military Battlefield Surveillance:** Detect compromised soldier-worn sensors. > - **Critical Infrastructure Monitoring:** Smart grid substations, nuclear plant perimeter. > - **Precision Agriculture:** Detect faulty/tampered soil sensors. > - **Healthcare WBANs (Wireless Body Area Networks):** Detect malicious pacemakers / insulin pumps. > - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Detect intrusion in factory-floor sensor meshes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Detection Probability under Multi-Node Collusion A common KTU-style numerical problem evaluates how detection probability degrades as multiple adversaries collude to evade the IDS. **Setup:** - The WSN deploys $N$ monitor nodes, each independently detecting an attack with probability $q$ (single-node detection probability). - An adversary controls $c$ compromised nodes. - The IDS raises an alarm only if **at least one** monitor detects the attack (distributed majority-vote style). - Each monitor independently fails to detect with probability $(1 - q)$. **Step 1: Probability a single monitor misses the intrusion.** $$P_{\text{miss, single}} = 1 - q$$ **Step 2: Probability that all $N$ monitors miss it (independent assumption).** $$P_{\text{miss, all}} = (1 - q)^N$$ **Step 3: Probability that at least one monitor catches it.** $$P_D = 1 - (1 - q)^N$$ **Step 4: Now factor in the adversary's effort.** If the attacker compromises $c$ monitor nodes, those nodes refuse to report detections. Effective honest monitors = $N - c$. $$P_D^{\text{collusion}} = 1 - (1 - q)^{N - c}$$ **Step 5: Limiting cases (useful for KTU conceptual questions).** $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{N \to \infty} P_D^{\text{collusion}} &= 1 \quad \text{(more monitors = better detection)} \\ \lim_{c \to N} P_D^{\text{collusion}} &= 0 \quad \text{(total compromise = total blindness)} \\ \lim_{q \to 1} P_D^{\text{collusion}} &= 1 \quad \text{(perfect single-node detector)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy-Aware IDS Lifetime Extension **Problem Setup (KTU standard form):** -
