Total initial energy in network: $E_0$ (Joules). - Without IDS, each node's average lifetime: $T_n$ (seconds). - Each node spends extra energy $e_{\text{IDS}}$ per unit time running the IDS agent. - We want to find the **network lifetime** $L_{\text{WSN}}$. **Step 1:** Per-node consumption without IDS. $$P_{\text{base}} = \frac{E_0}{T_n}$$ **Step 2:** Per-node consumption with IDS enabled. $$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}$$ **Step 3:** New per-node lifetime. $$T_n^{\text{new}} = \frac{E_0}{P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}}$$ **Step 4:** Normalized lifetime ratio (useful for comparing IDS schemes). $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{T_n^{\text{new}}}{T_n} = \frac{P_{\text{base}}}{P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}} \\ &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{e_{\text{IDS}}}{P_{\text{base}}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** - If $e_{\text{IDS}} \ll P_{\text{base}}$, then $\eta \to 1$ (IDS is "free"). - If $e_{\text{IDS}} \gg P_{\text{base}}$, then $\eta \to 0$ (IDS kills the network). **Design Rule (KTU favorite):** Keep $e_{\text{IDS}} \le 0.1 \cdot P_{\text{base}}$ to ensure $\eta \ge 0.91$. ## 3.3 Derivation: Bayes' Minimum-Error Threshold for Anomaly Detection **Problem Setup:** - Two hypotheses: $\mathcal{H}_0$ (normal) and $\mathcal{H}_1$ (intrusion). - Prior probabilities: $P(\mathcal{H}_0) = \pi_0$, $P(\mathcal{H}_1) = \pi_1$, with $\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1$. - Conditional PDFs of observation $x$: $f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_0)$, $f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_1)$. - Decision rule: choose $\mathcal{H}_1$ if $\Lambda(x) = \dfrac{f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_1)}{f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_0)} > \tau$. **Derivation of the minimum-error threshold (equal-cost Bayes):** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{error}) &= \pi_0 \cdot P(\text{decide } \mathcal{H}_1 \mid \mathcal{H}_0) + \pi_1 \cdot P(\text{decide } \mathcal{H}_0 \mid \mathcal{H}_1) \\ &= \pi_0 \int_{\{x: \Lambda(x) > \tau\}} f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_0) \, dx + \pi_1 \int_{\{x: \Lambda(x) \le \tau\}} f(x \mid \mathcal{H}_1) \, dx \end{aligned} $$ **Setting the derivative with respect to $\tau$ to zero** (variational argument with Lagrange multipliers gives): $$\tau^{*} = \frac{\pi_0 (C_{10} - C_{00})}{\pi_1 (C_{01} - C_{11})}$$ **Special case — equal costs ($C_{ij} = 1$ for $i \ne j$, $0$ otherwise):** $$\tau^{*} = \frac{\pi_0}{\pi_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Takeaway:** When intrusions are rare ($\pi_1 \ll \pi_0$), the threshold $\tau^{*}$ is large — i.e., the IDS must see very strong evidence before raising an alarm. This is the *mathematical reason* why anomaly-based IDS in WSNs suffer from false positives: legitimate behavior is statistically dominant. ## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style) **Question:** A WSN has $N = 10$ monitor nodes, each with single-node detection probability $q = 0.7$. An adversary compromises $c = 3$ monitors. Calculate the system-level detection probability $P_D$. **Solution (Step-by-step):** **Step 1:** Identify effective honest monitors. $$N - c = 10 - 3 = 7$$ **Step 2:** Probability a single honest monitor misses. $$1 - q = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3$$ **Step 3:** Probability all 7 honest monitors miss. $$(1 - q)^{N-c} = (0.3)^7$$ $$ \begin{aligned} (0.3)^2 &= 0.09 \\ (0.3)^4 &= (0.09)^2 = 0.0081 \\ (0.3)^7 &= (0.3)^4 \cdot (0.3)^2 \cdot (0.3)^1 \\ &= 0.0081 \cdot 0.09 \cdot 0.3 \\ &= 0.0002187 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** System-level detection probability. $$P_D = 1 - 0.0002187 = 0.9997813 \approx 0.9998$$ **Step 5: Interpret.** $$P_D \approx 99.98\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity Check:** Even with 3 compromised nodes, the redundant distributed IDS achieves near-perfect detection — this is precisely **why KTU 2024 emphasizes distributed architecture** for WSN IDS. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack-to-IDS-Method Mapping (Rapid Revision) | Attack | Layer | Anomaly Feature | Best Detection Method | |---|---|---|---| | **Jamming** | Physical | Abnormally high noise floor | Energy-based anomaly | | **Exhaustion** | MAC | Excessive retransmissions | Watchdog + rule-based | | **Selective Forwarding** | Network | Packet drop rate > threshold | Reputation-based | | **Sinkhole** | Network | Suspicious low-cost routes | Routing anomaly | | **Wormhole** | Network | Anomalously short RTT path | Statistical + neighbor verification | | **Sybil** | Network | One node, many identities | Resource testing / registration | | **Hello Flood** | Network | New node claims to be neighbor of all | Signal-strength check | | **False Data Injection** | App | Outlier sensor readings | Consistency / aggregation rules | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Placement of IDS in the WSN Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer[Application Layer
Data Aggregation / Queries] transLayer[Transport Layer
Reliable Delivery] netLayer[Network Layer
Routing / Multi Hop] macLayer[MAC Layer
Channel Access] phyLayer[Physical Layer
Radio Transmission] idsa[IDS Agent at App Layer
False Data Detection] idsb[IDS Agent at Network Layer
Routing Attack Detection] idsc[IDS Agent at MAC Layer
Collision and Exhaustion Detection] idsd[IDS Agent at Physical Layer
Jamming and Tamper Detection] alarm[Centralized Alarm Sink
or Cluster Head] appLayer --> idsa netLayer --> idsb macLayer --> idsc phyLayer --> idsd idsa --> alarm idsb --> alarm idsc --> alarm idsd --> alarm ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Distributed Cooperative IDS Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR n1[Sensor Node 1
with Watchdog Agent] n2[Sensor Node 2
with Watchdog Agent] n3[Sensor Node 3
with Watchdog Agent] n4[Sensor Node 4
Compromised Node] n5[Sensor Node 5
with Watchdog Agent] bs[Base Station
Global Correlation Engine] n1 -- Local Detection Vote --> n2 n2 -- Local Detection Vote --> n3 n3 -- Local Detection Vote --> n5 n5 -- Aggregated Alert --> bs n2 -- Forged Packet --> n4 n4 -- Drop or Alter --> bs ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Each honest node (1, 2, 3, 5) runs a **Watchdog Agent** (the "local IDS module") that overhears its neighbors' transmissions. - Node 4 is a **compromised insider** that selectively drops/forwards packets. - The other nodes exchange detection votes, and a consolidated alert is sent to the Base Station. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Anomaly-Based IDS Decision Flow (State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Training Training: Training Phase
Collect Baseline Features
Build Normal Profile Model M Training --> Detection: Model Ready Detection: Detection Phase
Observe Feature Vector x sub t Detection --> CompareScore: Compute Anomaly Score S sub x sub t CompareScore: Compare S with Threshold tau CompareScore --> Alarm: S greater than tau CompareScore --> NormalLog: S less or equal tau CompareScore --> Retrain: Periodic Timer Trigger Alarm: Raise Intrusion Alert
Send to BS or Cluster Head Alarm --> Mitigation: Trigger Response Module NormalLog: Log as Normal Event NormalLog --> Detection: Continue Monitoring Retrain: Update Model M
Adapt to Drift Retrain --> Detection: Resume Monitoring Mitigation: Isolate / Blacklist Node
Re Route Traffic Mitigation --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WSN IDS Node ```mermaid flowchart TB radio[Radio Module
Receive and Transmit] sniff[Packet Sniffer
Overhear Neighbor Traffic] feature[Feature Extractor
Packet Rate, RTT, Energy, Route Hops] profile[Normal Profile Database
Trained Statistics] engine[Detection Engine
Anomaly or Signature Matcher] decision[Decision Module
Threshold Comparison] alarm[Alarm Generator
Alert Message] radio --> sniff sniff --> feature profile --> engine feature --> engine engine --> decision decision --> alarm radio2[Local Packet Handling] --> radio energy[Energy Budget Manager] --> feature energy --> engine ``` **Reading the Block Diagram:** - The **Packet Sniffer** operates in promiscuous mode — critical for watchdogs. - The **Feature Extractor** compresses traffic into a small vector to save RAM. - The **Detection Engine** is the algorithm (e.g., one-class SVM, Markov chain). - The **Energy Budget Manager** dynamically throttles the IDS duty cycle to prevent battery drain — a key WSN-specific concern. ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Insider Attack Lifecycle as Detected by IDS ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Attacker Physically Captures Node] --> B[Extract Cryptographic Keys] B --> C[Reprogram Node as Insider] C --> D{Attack Type} D -->|Drop| E[Selective Forwarding] D -->|Inject| F[False Data Injection] D -->|Fake ID| G[Sybil Attack] D -->|Blackhole| H[Absorb All Packets] E --> I[IDS Detects Abnormal Drop Rate] F --> J[IDS Detects Statistical Outlier Reading] G --> K[IDS Detects Identity Duplication] H --> L[IDS Detects Zero Forwarding Rate] I --> M[Alert to Base Station] J --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N[Revocation and Re Keying] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define Intrusion Detection System in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. Why is it considered the "second line of defense"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) in a WSN is a security mechanism that monitors sensor network traffic and node behavior to detect malicious activities that bypass primary preventive measures such as cryptography and secure routing. It is called the "second line of defense" because prevention mechanisms (Tier-1 security) cannot stop insider attacks launched by compromised legitimate nodes — once cryptographic keys are extracted via physical tampering, the adversary behaves as a trusted insider. The IDS provides Tier-2 security by detecting such insider misbehavior through observation and analysis, triggering alarms and enabling response. **(3 marks)** > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** > **Compare anomaly-based and misuse-based intrusion detection techniques for WSNs.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Anomaly-Based | Misuse / Signature-Based | |---|---|---| | Detection principle | Detects deviation from learned normal behavior | Matches against known attack signatures | | Novel attack detection | **Yes** | **No** | | False alarm rate | Higher | Lower | | Storage requirement | Stores normal model | Stores full signature database | | Suitability for WSN | **High** (small model) | **Low** (DB too large for 4–10 KB RAM) | Anomaly-based IDS is preferred in WSNs because it can detect novel insider attacks and requires only a small statistical model. Misuse-based IDS, while highly accurate for known attacks, is impractical due to memory constraints. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three major IDS architectures used in Wireless Sensor Networks. Discuss their advantages and limitations in the WSN context. **Model Solution:** **1. Distributed / Cooperative IDS [2 marks]:** In a distributed IDS, every sensor node (or a selected subset called watchdogs) runs a lightweight IDS agent. Each agent monitors local behavior and exchanges detection votes with neighbors. The system raises a global alarm when a quorum of nodes confirms the attack. - *Advantage:* No single point of failure; energy distributed; scalable. - *Limitation:* Vulnerable to colluding attackers; high communication overhead. **2. Centralized IDS [2 marks]:** All audit logs are forwarded to a powerful base station, which runs the full detection algorithm. - *Advantage:* High detection accuracy; complex algorithms possible. - *Limitation:* Single point of failure; high energy cost at the BS; large communication overhead makes it impractical for large WSNs. **3. Hierarchical / Hybrid IDS [3 marks]:** The network is organized into clusters. Cluster Heads (CHs) aggregate local detection results from member nodes, perform regional correlation, and forward consolidated alerts to the BS. - *Advantage:* Balances accuracy and overhead; leverages cluster structure. - *Limitation:* CH itself becomes a high-value target; requires cluster maintenance. **Conclusion [Bonus point for completeness]:** The distributed and hierarchical architectures are best suited for WSNs due to their energy-awareness and fault tolerance. --- **(b) [7 marks]** A WSN deploys 12 monitor nodes in distributed cooperative mode. Each monitor has a single-node detection probability of 0.75. An adversary compromises 4 of these monitors. Compute the system-level detection probability. Comment on the result. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify parameters [1 mark].** - $N = 12$, $c = 4$, $q = 0.75$. - Effective honest monitors: $N - c = 12 - 4 = 8$. **Step 2: Single-monitor miss probability [1 mark].** $$1 - q = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$ **Step 3: Probability all 8 honest monitors miss [3 marks].** $$ \begin{aligned} (1 - q)^{N-c} &= (0.25)^8 \\ (0.25)^2 &= 0.0625 \\ (0.25)^4 &= (0.0625)^2 = 0.00390625 \\ (0.25)^8 &= (0.00390625)^2 = 0.0000152587 \\ &\approx 1.526 \times 10^{-5} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: System-level detection probability [1 mark].** $$P_D = 1 - (0.25)^8 = 1 - 1.526 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.9999847$$ $$\boxed{P_D \approx 99.998\%}$$ **Step 5: Comment [1 mark].** Even after compromising 33% of the monitors, the system still detects the attack with 99.998% probability. This demonstrates the **resilience of distributed cooperative IDS** through redundancy — a key reason this architecture is favored in WSNs. --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] (Internal Choice) **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the categories of attacks that an IDS in a WSN must detect. Classify them by network layer and provide one example detection strategy for each. **Model Solution:** **1. Physical Layer Attacks [2 marks]:** - *Jamming:* Adversary transmits high-power RF noise, raising the noise floor and preventing communication. - *Detection Strategy:* Each node monitors the Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) and packet delivery ratio. A sudden drop in delivery ratio with elevated RSSI noise floor triggers a jamming alarm. **2. MAC / Link Layer Attacks [2 marks]:** - *Exhaustion:* Attacker forces repeated retransmissions by sending RTS/CTS without corresponding data, draining victim battery. - *Detection Strategy:* Watchdog node counts neighbor's retransmission requests; if rate exceeds a threshold $\tau_{\text{rts}}$, alert is raised. **3. Network / Routing Layer Attacks [2 marks]:** - *Sinkhole Attack:* Compromised node advertises a high-quality (low-cost) route, attracting all traffic, then drops or alters it. - *Detection Strategy:* Each node compares the advertised route metric against expected values from a routing specification; significant deviation flags a sinkhole. **4. Application Layer Attacks [1 mark]:** - *False Data Injection:* Compromised node injects fabricated sensor readings (e.g., false temperature spikes). - *Detection Strategy:* Statistical consistency check — readings should lie within $\mu \pm k\sigma$ of neighbor consensus; outliers are rejected. **Conclusion [Bonus for completeness]:** A robust WSN IDS must deploy detection agents at all four layers; a single-layer IDS leaves blind spots. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the energy-aware lifetime ratio $\eta = \dfrac{T_n^{\text{new}}}{T_n}$ for a node running an IDS agent, and show that $\eta \ge 0.9$ requires $e_{\text{IDS}} \le 0.111 \cdot P_{\text{base}}$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Definitions [1 mark].** - $E_0$: total initial energy per node (J). - $P_{\text{base}}$: average power drain without IDS (W). - $e_{\text{IDS}}$: additional IDS-related power drain (W). - $T_n$: original node lifetime (s) $= E_0 / P_{\text{base}}$. **Step 2: New power consumption [1 mark].** $$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}$$ **Step 3: New lifetime [1 mark].** $$T_n^{\text{new}} = \frac{E_0}{P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}}$$ **Step 4: Lifetime ratio derivation [2 marks].** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta = \frac{T_n^{\text{new}}}{T_n} &= \frac{E_0 / (P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}})}{E_0 / P_{\text{base}}} \\ &= \frac{P_{\text{base}}}{P_{\text{base}} + e_{\text{IDS}}} \\ &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{e_{\text{IDS}}}{P_{\text{base}}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Imposing $\eta \ge 0.9$ [2 marks].** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{e_{\text{IDS}}}{P_{\text{base}}}} &\ge 0.9 \\ 1 + \frac{e_{\text{IDS}}}{P_{\text{base}}} &\le \frac{1}{0.9} = 1.1111 \\ \frac{e_{\text{IDS}}}{P_{\text{base}}} &\le 0.1111 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{e_{\text{IDS}} \le 0.111 \cdot P_{\text{base}}}$$ **Conclusion [Bonus]:** The IDS must be designed to consume less than ~11% of the node's base power budget to maintain 90% of the original network lifetime. This is the **fundamental energy design constraint** of any WSN IDS scheme. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Students Lose Marks in WSN IDS Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "Prevention" with "Detection":** A common KTU trap. Cryptography and key management are **prevention (Tier-1)**, NOT detection. Writing "IDS uses encryption" will fetch zero marks. > 2. **Wrong layer attribution:** Many students attribute Sybil and wormhole attacks to the MAC layer. Correct: they are **Network-layer** attacks. > 3. **Forgetting the energy constraint:** Any IDS design that ignores energy is incomplete. KTU examiners expect the $\eta \ge 0.9$ or equivalent energy argument in 14-mark answers. > 4. **Skipping "Why WSN IDS is unique":** WSN IDS is not the same as traditional network IDS. You must mention **resource constraints, wireless medium, and insider attacks** to score full marks. > 5. **Numerical mistakes in $P_D$ formula:** The correct formula is $P_D = 1 - (1-q)^{N-c}$. A frequent error is $(1-q)^{Nc}$ or $(1-q)^{N+c}$. > 6. **Insufficient diagrams in 14-mark answers:** Always include at least one labeled architecture or flow diagram — KTU examiners reward visual clarity. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Intrusion Detection in WSNs** - [ ] **Definition:** IDS = second line of defense that detects (does NOT prevent) intrusions. - [ ] **CIA Triad** must be explicitly stated in any WSN security answer. - [ ] **Three IDS Architectures:** Distributed (best for WSN), Centralized, Hybrid. Know pros/cons. - [ ] **Three Detection Methods:** Anomaly-based (preferred for WSN — detects novel attacks), Misuse-based (signature matching — too heavy for WSN), Specification-based (uses defined correct-behavior spec — very WSN-friendly). - [ ] **Layer-wise attack mapping** is critical: Jamming (PHY), Exhaustion (MAC), Sinkhole/Sybil/Wormhole (Network), False Data Injection (App). - [ ] **Master formula:** $P_D = 1 - (1-q)^{N-c}$ — system-level detection probability under collusion. - [ ] **Master formula:** $P_F = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — false positive rate (always minimize). - [ ] **Master formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + e_{\text{IDS}} / P_{\text{base}}}$ — energy-aware lifetime ratio; design rule: $e_{\text{IDS}} \le 0.1 \cdot P_{\text{base}}$. - [ ] **Master formula:** $\tau^{*} = \pi_0 / \pi_1$ (Bayes minimum-error threshold under equal cost). - [ ] **Watchdog concept:** A node overhears its neighbor's transmissions in promiscuous mode — the foundation of distributed IDS in WSNs. - [ ] **Insider vs. Outsider:** Prevention handles outsiders; IDS is essential for insiders. - [ ] **Key trade-off:** Detection accuracy vs. Energy consumption vs. Communication overhead — the central design tension in every WSN IDS scheme. - [ ] **Bayes' rule** explains why rare intrusions cause high false-positive rates in anomaly-based IDS. - [ ] **Always quote a real-world application** (battlefield, smart grid, WBAN, IIoT) to earn the "application" mark.

topics.title: Topology Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Topology Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology Control** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is defined as the deliberate, algorithmic manipulation of the network's logical and physical communication structure — typically by adjusting the **transmission power** of individual nodes, selecting a subset of **active (awake) nodes** through duty cycling, or imposing a **hierarchical clustering** overlay — to produce a connected, energy-efficient, and interference-aware sub-graph (the *controlling topology* $G'$) derived from the maximum-power graph $G_{max}$.

The objective is to **preserve global network connectivity and sensing coverage** while reducing redundant communication links, mitigating MAC-level contention, and extending network lifetime. Formally, given the maximum-power graph $G_{max}=(V,E_{max})$, topology control aims to compute a sub-graph $G'=(V,E')$ such that:

$$G' \subseteq G_{max} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \text{connectivity}(G') = \text{connectivity}(G_{max})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Street-Light Network"**
>
> Imagine a city where every street-light has a sensor, and they must collectively form a *lit-up communication backbone* (a connected graph). If every light shines at its **maximum brightness** in every direction, the city wastes enormous electricity, neighbouring lights blind each other (interference), and bulbs burn out fast.
>
> **Topology control** is like the city's *Energy Department* issuing a mandate:
> 1. **Dim the lights** (reduce transmission power) so each light just barely reaches a couple of strategic neighbours — this is *power-control topology*.
> 2. **Turn off some lights temporarily** on a rotating roster (sleep scheduling / duty cycling) — only the awake ones carry traffic.
> 3. **Promote a few "head-lights"** to form a *backbone* of district supervisors that coordinate all the rest — this is *cluster/hierarchical topology*.
>
> The result: the same area is illuminated (network is connected and covered), but with **far less energy, less glare, and a much longer system life**. That is precisely what topology control does in a WSN.

## 1.3 Why Topology Control is Indispensable in WSNs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three engineering realities force the use of topology control in any dense WSN:
>
> - **Energy Scarcity** — sensor nodes are battery-driven; wireless transmission is the dominant energy consumer. Topology control minimises the average transmission range $r_{avg} \le r_{max}$.
> - **Spatial Redundancy** — random deployment produces high node degree. Without control, every node contends with dozens of neighbours (the *hidden/exposed-terminal* problem).
> - **Scalability** — flat ad-hoc topologies do not scale beyond a few hundred nodes. Hierarchy (clustering) brings $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ rather than $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ overhead.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test these quantitative metrics:
> - **Network Lifetime** ($L_{net}$) — time until the first (or last) node exhausts its energy $E_0$.
> - **Average Node Degree** ($\bar{d}$) — average number of neighbours per active node.
> - **Stretch Factor** ($\rho$) — ratio of the shortest path in $G'$ to that in $G_{max}$; a *spanner* graph must satisfy $\rho \le t$ for a constant $t$.
> - **Energy per Bit** ($E_b$) — Joules consumed per successfully delivered information bit.
> - **Coverage Ratio** ($\eta_{cov}$) — fraction of the monitored region $A$ sensed by at least one active node.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Topology Control Strategies

Topology control in WSNs is implemented via three orthogonal mechanisms, which may be combined in practice:

| # | Strategy Class | Core Knob Tuned | Representative Algorithms | Key Trade-off |
|---|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Power Control** | Transmission radius $r_i$ per node | COMPOW, CLUSTERPOW, Cone-Based, LMST | Saves energy vs. risks disconnection |
| 2 | **Sleep Scheduling (Duty Cycling)** | Awake/Asleep state of nodes | SPAN, ASCENT, PEAS | Saves idle energy vs. latency |
| 3 | **Hierarchical / Clustering** | Cluster-head election & role assignment | LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED | Scalable routing vs. CH bottleneck |

## 2.2 The Three Operational Phases (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery & Maximum-Power Graph Construction**
> Each node $v_i$ broadcasts a *Hello* packet at $P_{max}$ and records the set of reachable nodes:
> $$N_{max}(v_i) = \left\{ v_j \in V \; \middle| \; d(v_i, v_j) \le r_{max} \right\}$$
> This yields the dense graph $G_{max}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 2 — Topology Decision (Algorithmic Filtering)**
> Based on the chosen scheme, each node:
> - (Power control) reduces $r_i$ to a *critical radius* $r_{crit}$ that still preserves a connected backbone, OR
> - (Sleep scheduling) marks itself *asleep* if its neighbours are already well-covered, OR
> - (Clustering) elects/propagates a cluster-head using a cost function $f(E_{res}, d_{to\_BS}, \text{role})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 3 — Maintenance & Repair**
> As nodes die, links are recomputed; affected nodes re-broadcast their updated radius or change state. This is the *reactive maintenance loop* of topology control.

## 2.3 Geometric / Spanner-Based Topology Construction

Several classical geometric graphs are used as *theoretical lower-bounds* for power-control topology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Neighborhood Graph (RNG)** — An edge $(u,v)$ survives iff there is no third node $w$ such that $\max\{d(u,w), d(v,w)\} < d(u,v)$.
> **Gabriel Graph (GG)** — An edge $(u,v)$ survives iff the disc with diameter $uv$ contains no other node.
> **Yao Graph** — Cone-based partition; each node keeps the shortest edge in each cone of angle $\alpha$.
> **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** — Globally minimum total-weight spanning tree.
> **Delaunay Triangulation (DT)** — A planar dual of the Voronoi diagram; the *strongest known spanner* among planar structures.

Containment hierarchy (with planar guarantee for some):

$$MST \subseteq RNG \subseteq GG \subseteq DT \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Yao}_{\alpha} \text{ is a } t\text{-spanner for } t = \frac{1}{1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorise the symbols, units, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ (free-space) | $k$ = bits, $d$ = distance, $E_{elec}$ = electronics energy/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs}$ = free-space amplifier factor | Energy spent transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$ in free-space |
| 2 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ (multi-path) | $\varepsilon_{mp}$ = multi-path amplifier factor | Energy spent transmitting $k$ bits over long distance |
| 3 | $E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | $E_{elec}$ = receiver electronics energy/bit | Energy spent receiving $k$ bits |
| 4 | $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$ | Threshold distance between FS and MP models | Decides which model to apply |
| 5 | $r_{crit} \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln n + \ln(1/\delta)}{n \cdot \pi \cdot \rho_s}}$ | Critical radius for $n$-node connectivity w.p. $\ge 1-\delta$; $\rho_s$ = node density | Connectivity-preserving minimum radius |
| 6 | $\rho_{spanner} = \dfrac{\ell_{G'}(u,v)}{\ell_{G_{max}}(u,v)} \le t$ | $t$-spanner property; $\ell$ = shortest path length | Quality measure of topology approximation |
| 7 | $L_{net}^{(first\;death)} = \dfrac{E_0}{\max_i \{ E_i(t) \}}$ | Time until first node drains | Network lifetime metric |
| 8 | $P_{opt} = P_{max} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\chi}$ | $\chi$ = number of redundant neighbours removed | COMPOW-style optimal uniform power |
| 9 | $\bar{d}_{Yao,\alpha}(v) \le \lceil 2\pi/\alpha \rceil + 1$ | Upper bound on Yao graph degree | Maximum neighbour count per node |
| 10 | $E_{cluster\_round} = n \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + 2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$ | LEACH energy per round (intra-cluster + BS uplink) | Hierarchical topology energy estimate |

> **Strict note on table syntax:** All absolute-value / set-membership dividers are written as `\vert` or `\mid` so the markdown table layout is never broken.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Topology control is not a textbook abstraction. It is the **operational backbone** of:
- **Smart-grid AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** — meshed radio meters with sleep-scheduled routers.
- **Precision agriculture** — soil-moisture sensor fields that must last 5+ years on a coin cell.
- **Structural Health Monitoring** of bridges/tunnels — densely deployed vibration nodes where power control prevents signal collisions.
- **Military battlefield surveillance** — clustered, duty-cycled motes extending mission duration from days to months.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **fully expanded derivations** — no step is skipped. Every algebraic manipulation is accompanied by its rationale.

## 3.1 Derivation: The First-Order Radio Energy Model (LEACH/Heinzelman Framework)

The radio energy model is the **foundation** for every energy-based topology control evaluation. We derive each term from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Transmission Energy $E_{Tx}(k,d)$

The transmitter expends energy in two functionally independent blocks:
- **Electronics block** — runs the encoder, modulator, filter, and digital-to-analog converter. This block is *distance-independent* and processes each bit once.
- **Amplifier block** — boosts the RF signal to overcome path loss. This block is *distance-dependent*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx}(k,d) &= E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k,d) \\
&= k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon \cdot d^{\alpha_{PL}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Electronics energy:** Every bit traverses the digital circuitry and consumes $E_{elec}$ joules.
**Step 2 — Amplifier energy:** The Friis free-space model gives a power density falloff of $1/d^{2}$ for the line-of-sight component, so the amplifier must inject energy proportional to $d^{2}$ (or $d^{4}$ in the multi-path regime).
**Step 3 — Path-loss exponent $\alpha_{PL}$:** Equals 2 for free-space and 4 for multi-path fading.
**Step 4 — Switch condition:** Choose the smaller of the two expressions, yielding the *crossover distance* $d_{crossover}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} &= k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \\
\Rightarrow \; 1 &= \dfrac{\varepsilon_{mp}}{\varepsilon_{fs}} \cdot d^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \; d_{crossover} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Critical Transmission Radius for Connectivity

> [!NOTE]
> A network of $n$ nodes uniformly distributed in a unit square with density $\rho_s$ is *asymptotically connected* w.h.p. if and only if the common radius $r$ satisfies the classical Gupta–Kumar condition.

**Step 1 — Expected neighbours per node:**
$$
E[N(r)] = (n-1) \cdot \pi r^{2} \rho_s \approx n \pi r^{2} \rho_s
$$
**Step 2 — Apply the 0-1 law / percolation threshold:** A random geometric graph $G(n,r)$ becomes connected when the *mean degree* $\lambda = n \pi r^{2} \rho_s$ exceeds $\ln n$.
**Step 3 — Rearrange for $r$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n \pi r^{2} \rho_s &= \ln n + c \\
r^{2} &= \dfrac{\ln n + c}{n \pi \rho_s} \\
r_{crit} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln n + \ln(1/\delta)}{n \pi \rho_s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — The $-\ln(1/\delta)$ term** emerges from applying a Chernoff bound so that *connectivity holds with probability at least $1 - \delta$*. This is the radius **below which the network is almost surely disconnected** and **above which it is almost surely connected**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stretch Factor of a Power-Controlled Sub-Graph

> [!NOTE]
> The Yao graph $Y_{\alpha}$ is a $t$-spanner for $t = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2)}$. We derive this constant.

**Step 1 — Cone partition:** Around each node $v$, divide the plane into $k = \lceil 2\pi/\alpha \rceil$ cones of angle $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Shortest-edge selection:** In each cone, retain only the *shortest* edge from $v$ to any neighbour. The discarded edges are all *longer* than the retained one.

**Step 3 — Path reconstruction:** Any long-range edge $(u,v)$ in the maximum-power graph can be *approximated* by a chain of Yao edges, each lying inside a cone, each subtending an angular deviation of at most $\alpha/2$ from the straight line.

**Step 4 — Geometric inequality:** For two consecutive edges of lengths $a$ and $b$ with included angle $\theta \le \alpha/2$, the third side of the triangle satisfies:
$$
c = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2} - 2ab\cos\theta} \le \dfrac{a + b}{1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2)}
$$
This follows from the law of cosines and the bound $\cos\theta \ge \cos(\alpha/2) \ge 1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2)$ for small $\alpha$.

**Step 5 — Telescoping sum over the $k$ cones** yields the **spanner bound**:
$$
\rho_{Yao} = \dfrac{\ell_{Yao}(s,t)}{\ell_{G_{max}}(s,t)} \le t = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: LEACH Energy Cost per Round (Clustering Topology)

LEACH is the canonical *cluster-based* topology control protocol. The per-round energy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= E_{cluster\_formation} + E_{steady\_state} \\
E_{steady\_state} &= n \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + 2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Intra-cluster aggregation cost:** Each of the $n$ non-CH nodes sends $k$ bits to its CH; the CH performs data-aggregation (compression factor $E_{DA}$ per bit).
**Step 2 — CH uplink cost:** Each CH transmits its aggregated frame to the base station over a long (multi-path) distance $d_{toBS}$.
**Step 3 — Receive cost:** Every non-CH transmission is received once by a CH ($E_{Rx} = k E_{elec}$).
**Step 4 — Factor 2 in $2nE_{elec}k$:** one for the source-side electronics, one for the CH-side electronics.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Topology Control Should I Choose?"

| Scenario | Dominant Constraint | Recommended Topology Control | Expected Lifetime Gain |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Dense, uniform, low traffic | Energy + interference | **Power Control (LMST / Yao)** | $2\times$ – $3\times$ |
| Sparse, event-driven, mobile nodes | Latency + coverage | **Sleep Scheduling (SPAN / PEAS)** | $3\times$ – $5\times$ |
| Large-scale static, periodic reports | Scalability + BS-bottleneck | **Hierarchical Clustering (LEACH / HEED)** | $5\times$ – $10\times$ |
| Harsh environment with frequent node death | Robustness + repair | **K-connected topology (k = 2 or 3)** | $1.5\times$ + fault-tolerance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below use **only alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain-text labels** (no markdown formatting inside the labels) so they render reliably in any Mermaid v10+ viewer.

## 4.2 Operational Flow of a Topology Control Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Bootstraps with Pmax] --> B[Node i Broadcasts Hello at Pmax]
    B --> C[Construct Nmax vi Neighbour List]
    C --> D{Topology Control Mode}
    D -- Mode 1 Power --> E[Compute rcrit via Yao or LMST]
    D -- Mode 2 Sleep --> F{All Neighbours Covered?}
    D -- Mode 3 Cluster --> G[Run LEACH HEED CH Election]

    E --> H[Set Ptx to Popt]
    F -- Yes --> I[Node i enters Sleep State]
    F -- No --> J[Node i stays Active]
    G --> K[Assign CH and Member Roles]

    H --> L[Topology G Prime Activated]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{Maintenance Trigger}
    M -- Energy Drop --> N[Recompute Topology]
    M -- Node Failure --> O[Trigger Repair Local]
    M -- Stable --> P[Continue Operation]
    N --> D
    O --> D
    P --> M
```

```mermaid
    TCENG --> NBRT[Neighbour Table G Prime]

    NBRT2 --> TCENG

    BAT --> RADIO

```

## 4.4 Geometric Spanner Containment Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    DT[Delaunay Triangulation] --> GG[Gabriel Graph]
    GG --> RNG[Relative Neighborhood Graph]
    RNG --> MST[Minimum Spanning Tree]
    MST --> POW[Critical Radius Controllable Subgraph]
    YAOY[Yao Graph alpha] -. .Parallel Spanner. .-> POW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The directed arrows $DT \supseteq GG \supseteq RNG \supseteq MST$ mean that each subsequent graph is a *sub-graph* of the one above. The Yao graph is a *parallel* (non-containing) spanner construction with bounded stretch.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define topology control in a Wireless Sensor Network. List its two primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Topology control is the algorithmic mechanism used to determine — for each sensor node — its neighbour set and its transmission power such that the resulting communication sub-graph is connected, energy-efficient, and interference-aware.
- **Primary Objective 1:** Reduce energy consumption (especially per-bit transmit energy $E_{Tx}$) by trimming redundant high-power links.
- **Primary Objective 2:** Preserve global network connectivity and sensing coverage while reducing MAC contention and extending the operational lifetime $L_{net}$ of the network.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between Power Control and Sleep Scheduling as topology control strategies.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Power Control | Sleep Scheduling |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Reduces $P_{tx}$ per node | Cycles nodes between Awake and Asleep states |
| **Energy saving source** | Lower amplifier drain | Eliminated idle listening |
| **Latency impact** | Low (still awake) | High (must wait for sleeping neighbour) |
| **Representative algorithms** | COMPOW, LMST, CBTC | SPAN, PEAS, ASCENT |
| **Topology type** | Connected power graph | Sparse active sub-graph |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the LEACH protocol as a hierarchical topology control mechanism. Describe its cluster-head (CH) election algorithm and the threshold $T(n)$ used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the total energy consumed per round by a LEACH network of $n = 100$ nodes deployed in a $100\;\text{m} \times 100\;\text{m}$ field with a base station located at $(50, 175)$ metres. Use the parameters: $k = 4000$ bits, $E_{elec} = 50\;\text{nJ/bit}$, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013\;\text{pJ/bit/m}^{4}$, $E_{DA} = 5\;\text{nJ/bit/signal}$. Assume 5% of nodes act as CHs per round and the average distance from a node to its CH is $d_{toCH} = 20$ m. Use the multi-path model for BS uplink.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) organises nodes into rotating clusters to evenly distribute the cluster-head energy burden.
- **Phase 1 — Advertisement:** Each node picks a random number $r \in [0,1]$ and becomes a CH if $r < T(n)$, where
$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
- **Phase 2 — Cluster Setup:** Non-CH nodes join the nearest CH based on received RSSI.
- **Phase 3 — Steady State:** CHs aggregate data and forward to the BS; TDMA scheduling avoids intra-cluster collisions.
- **Phase 4 — Rotation:** CH role rotates every $1/p$ rounds so energy drain is *amortised* across all nodes.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
Number of CHs: $n_{CH} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5$. Number of non-CH nodes: $n_{non} = 95$.

**Step 1 — Intra-cluster energy (free-space, $d_{toCH}=20$ m):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{non\_tx} &= n_{non} \cdot (k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}) \\
&= 95 \cdot (4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 20^{2}) \\
&= 95 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.6 \times 10^{-5}) \\
&= 95 \cdot 2.16 \times 10^{-4} = 2.052 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CH receive cost:**
$$
E_{ch\_rx} = 95 \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} = 95 \cdot 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1.9 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — CH data aggregation:**
$$
E_{DA\_total} = n_{non} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} = 95 \cdot 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — BS uplink (multi-path, $d_{toBS} = 175$ m):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{BS\_tx} &= n_{CH} \cdot (k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}) \\
&= 5 \cdot (4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 1.3 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 175^{4}) \\
&= 5 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4} + 4.876 \times 10^{-2}) \\
&= 5 \cdot 4.896 \times 10^{-2} = 2.448 \times 10^{-1}\;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total per-round energy:**
$$
E_{round} = 2.052 \times 10^{-2} + 1.9 \times 10^{-2} + 1.9 \times 10^{-3} + 2.448 \times 10^{-1} \approx 0.286\;\text{J}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of rounds until first node dies** (with $E_0 = 0.5$ J/node):
$$
N_{rounds} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n \cdot E_0}{E_{round}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{100 \cdot 0.5}{0.286} \right\rfloor = 174\;\text{rounds}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Cone-Based Topology Control (CBTC) algorithm. Explain how the value of the cone angle $\alpha$ influences the resulting topology.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the operational phases of a generic topology control engine and discuss the role of the critical radius $r_{crit}$ in maintaining connectivity.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
CBTC is a *distributed, asynchronous* power-control algorithm. Each node $u$ determines its neighbours by the rule: *an edge $(u,v)$ is kept if and only if there exists at least one cone of angle $\alpha$ centred at $u$ that contains $v$ and no other neighbour inside it.*
- **Asymmetry handling:** To make the resulting graph symmetric (so that a link exists in both directions), $u$ sets its power to the maximum of $\{ P_{uv}, P_{uw}, \ldots \}$ for all retained edges.
- **Effect of $\alpha$:** Smaller $\alpha$ → fewer cones → more aggressive edge retention → higher node degree and energy use, but **better spanner stretch**. Larger $\alpha$ → sparser graph → less interference, but **higher stretch** and possible disconnection if $\alpha > 5\pi/6$ (the theoretical bound for connectivity preservation).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
The operational phases are:
1. **Initialization:** All nodes transmit at $P_{max}$ and exchange *Hello* packets to populate the neighbour table $N_{max}$.
2. **Topology Decision:** The local algorithm (e.g., LMST, CBTC, or Yao) computes the target edge set and corresponding transmission power.
3. **Reconfiguration:** Each node applies the new $P_{tx}$ value.
4. **Maintenance Loop:** On detection of a failed link (via missing ACKs or NACKs), the affected node triggers a *localised recomputation*, possibly raising its power to $P_{max}$ temporarily.

**Role of $r_{crit}$:** The critical radius
$$
r_{crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln n + \ln(1/\delta)}{n \pi \rho_s}}
$$
guarantees (with probability $1-\delta$) that the network remains *asymptotically connected* when every node transmits at exactly this radius. Choosing $r < r_{crit}$ risks network partitioning; choosing $r \gg r_{crit}$ wastes energy. Hence $r_{crit}$ is the **sweet-spot design parameter** between connectivity and energy efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing RNG and GG:** Many students write "$GG \subseteq RNG$" — the correct inclusion is $RNG \subseteq GG$. Lose 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2nE_{elec}k$:** The receive side and transmit side electronics both consume $E_{elec}$ per bit. Many students compute only the transmit side.
> 3. **Using free-space model for BS uplink:** LEACH's BS distance is typically $> d_{crossover}$. Always switch to the multi-path $d^{4}$ model. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Examiners explicitly demand units (J, m, nJ/bit). Missing units → −0.5 mark per quantity.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of uniform random distribution** in $r_{crit}$ derivations. This is the precondition for the Gupta–Kumar result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Topology Control**

- [ ] **Definition** — Topology control = engineering a sub-graph $G' \subseteq G_{max}$ that preserves connectivity & coverage while minimising energy and interference.
- [ ] **Three classes** — (1) Power Control, (2) Sleep Scheduling, (3) Hierarchical Clustering.
- [ ] **Radio energy model** — Free-space $d^{2}$ for short range, multi-path $d^{4}$ for long range; switch at $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$.
- [ ] **Critical radius** — $r_{crit} = \sqrt{(\ln n + \ln(1/\delta))/(n\pi\rho_s)}$ is the connectivity threshold.
- [ ] **Spanner inclusion chain** — $MST \subseteq RNG \subseteq GG \subseteq DT$.
- [ ] **Yao graph stretch** — $\rho = 1/(1 - 2\sin(\alpha/2))$.
- [ ] **LEACH threshold** — $T(n) = p/(1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p))$ for $n \in G$, else 0.
- [ ] **Yao degree bound** — $\le \lceil 2\pi/\alpha \rceil + 1$.
- [ ] **Per-round LEACH energy** — sum of intra-cluster Tx, CH Rx, aggregation, and BS uplink.
- [ ] **Network lifetime** — typically defined as time until *first* or *last* node death.
- [ ] **Stretch factor** — must be $\le t$ for a $t$-spanner.
- [ ] **SPAN / PEAS / ASCENT** — duty-cycling protocols; SPAN preserves a connected awake backbone.
- [ ] **COMPOW** — every node uses the *minimum common* power that still keeps the network connected.
- [ ] **CBTC** — cone-based; $\alpha > 5\pi/6$ loses connectivity guarantee.
- [ ] **Hierarchical gain** — Clustering yields $5\times$ – $10\times$ lifetime over flat topology in dense static deployments.
- [ ] **Common pitfall** — Always state assumptions (uniform deployment, free-space vs. multi-path, ideal MAC) in derivations.

---
