<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Clustering** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) is a hierarchical network organization technique in which spatially distributed sensor nodes are partitioned into disjoint subsets called **clusters**, with one node in each cluster designated as a **Cluster Head (CH)**. The CHs aggregate data from their member nodes (cluster members) and forward the consolidated information to the **Base Station (BS)**, thereby enabling energy-efficient data routing, scalability, and prolonged network lifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> Clustering is a **topology-control and energy-management mechanism** that organizes sensor nodes into a two-tier hierarchy — *intra-cluster* (member → CH) and *inter-cluster* (CH → BS) communication. It transforms a flat, ad-hoc WSN into a structured, manageable, and energy-aware architecture.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Full Form | Role in the Cluster |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| CH | Cluster Head | Coordinator node — aggregates, fuses, and relays data |
| CM | Cluster Member | Ordinary sensor node — senses and sends to its CH |
| BS | Base Station (Sink) | Final destination for all data |
| TDMA | Time Division Multiple Access | Schedule used inside a cluster to avoid collisions |
| CDMA | Code Division Multiple Access | Used between clusters to avoid inter-cluster interference |
| Round | One cycle of setup + steady-state | Fundamental time unit in LEACH-like protocols |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Post Office Model"

Imagine a remote village with **100 farmers** who need to send daily produce reports to the **District Collector (BS)** located 50 km away. The farmers don't individually drive to the collector; instead:

1. Each farmer hands his report to the local **Postmaster (CH)** of his village cluster.
2. Each Postmaster **bundles, summarises, and removes duplicates** from the reports.
3. The Postmaster then sends **one consolidated packet** to the District Collector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this efficient?**
> - Long-distance travel is restricted to **one vehicle per village**, not 100.
> - Repetitive data (e.g., "all crops are healthy") is **compressed** at the village level.
> - If a Postmaster retires (battery dies), the village **elects a new one** — the system self-heals.

This is **exactly** how clustering works in WSN. The sensor nodes = farmers, the CH = Postmaster, the BS = District Collector, and the data aggregation = report summarisation.

## 1.4 Why Clustering is Needed in WSN

Sensor nodes are **energy-constrained**, **battery-limited**, and **non-rechargeable** in most deployments. Direct (single-hop) transmission to a distant BS drains energy at the rate of $d^2$ or even $d^4$ (path loss), making it infeasible for large networks. Clustering solves the **fundamental energy-scalability dilemma**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Justification:**
> By limiting long-distance, high-power transmission to a small set of CHs, the **average energy consumption per bit delivered** to the BS is reduced by an order of magnitude. Clustering also enables **localised data fusion**, reducing traffic by up to **80–90%** in dense deployments.

### Energy Model Reminder (Radio Energy Dissipation)

A node expends energy to **run the radio circuitry** ($E_{elec}$, in J/bit) and to **amplify the signal** over distance $d$ (using either a free-space or multi-path amplifier).

- **Free-space model** ($d \leq d_0$): Amplification cost = $\epsilon_{fs}$ (J/bit/m²)
- **Multi-path model** ($d > d_0$): Amplification cost = $\epsilon_{mp}$ (J/bit/m⁴)
- **Threshold distance**: $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$

This model is the foundation of every clustering protocol we will study.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Clustering Process — 4-Stage Operational Logic

A clustering protocol is best understood as a **continuous, repeating cycle of four operational stages**. Every clustering protocol (LEACH, HEED, PEGASIS, TEEN, etc.) implements these stages differently.

### Stage 1 → Cluster Head (CH) Selection
- Decide **which nodes will become CHs** for the current round.
- Decision is based on:
  - Residual energy of the node
  - Random probability (rotates the role, prevents permanent death of any one node)
  - Proximity to neighbours / BS
  - Node degree (number of neighbours)

### Stage 2 → Cluster Formation
- Non-CH nodes choose **which CH to join** based on:
  - **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** of CH's advertisement
  - Distance to CH
  - Load balancing (some protocols consider CH's current member count)

### Stage 3 → Data Aggregation / Fusion
- Members sense data and send it to their CH during their **allotted TDMA slot**.
- CH **fuses, compresses, and removes redundancy** from member data.
- Output is a single, small packet (e.g., average temperature) instead of N raw readings.

### Stage 4 → Transmission to BS
- Each CH transmits the aggregated packet to the BS.
- This may be:
  - **Single-hop** (direct, high power)
  - **Multi-hop** via other CHs (lower per-hop energy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "rounds" instead of "continuous"?**
> Energy load is **rotated** across all nodes by periodically re-electing CHs. This prevents a static CH from dying prematurely — a key innovation of LEACH.

## 2.2 Advantages of Clustering

| # | Advantage | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Energy efficiency** | Only CHs do expensive long-haul transmission |
| 2 | **Scalability** | Network can grow without flooding overhead |
| 3 | **Bandwidth reuse** | Non-overlapping clusters can use same frequencies |
| 4 | **Data aggregation** | Reduces traffic by 10× in typical scenarios |
| 5 | **Localized decisions** | Each cluster manages itself — no global coordination |
| 6 | **Fault tolerance** | A CH failure triggers re-clustering, not network death |
| 7 | **Reduced collision** | TDMA inside cluster + CDMA between clusters |

## 2.3 Disadvantages of Clustering

| # | Disadvantage | Mitigation in Modern Protocols |
|---|--------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Overhead of CH selection each round | Adaptive thresholds, residual energy gating |
| 2 | Hot-spot problem (CHs near BS die faster) | Unequal clustering (e.g., EEUC) |
| 3 | Latency due to TDMA waiting | Reduced steady-state phase duration |
| 4 | Single point of failure (CH dies) | CH-co-reserve / backup CH election |
| 5 | Cluster-head bottleneck | Multi-hop CH-to-CH routing (PEGASIS) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------------------|---------|-----------------------|
| 1 | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^n$ | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ | Joules |
| 2 | $E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | Joules |
| 3 | $n = 2$ (free-space) or $n = 4$ (multi-path) | Path loss exponent | dimensionless |
| 4 | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | metres |
| 5 | $E_{DA}$ | Data aggregation energy per bit | $5$ nJ/bit/signal |
| 6 | $E_{elec}$ | Radio electronics energy | $50$ nJ/bit |
| 7 | $\epsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier coefficient | $10$ pJ/bit/m² |
| 8 | $\epsilon_{mp}$ | Multi-path amplifier coefficient | $0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| 9 | $P_i(t) = \frac{k}{N - k \cdot (r \bmod N/k)}$ | LEACH CH-election threshold | probability |
| 10 | $E_{total}(CH) = k \cdot E_{elec}\!\left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) + k \cdot E_{DA}\!\left(\frac{N}{k}\right) + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4$ | Energy per CH per frame | Joules |
| 11 | $E_{total}(non\!-\!CH) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^2$ | Energy per non-CH per frame | Joules |
| 12 | $E_{total}(Network) = k\!\left(2N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + N \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^4 + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^2\right)$ | Total energy per round (LEACH) | Joules |
| 13 | $k_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi \cdot M \cdot d_{toBS}^2 \cdot \epsilon_{mp}}}$ | Optimal number of clusters | dimensionless |
| 14 | $P_{opt} = k_{opt} / N$ | Optimal CH probability | dimensionless |
| 15 | $CHPR_i = \max\!\left(C_{prob} \cdot \frac{E_{res,i}}{E_{max}}, p_{min}\right)$ | HEED intra-cluster communication probability | probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question gives $E_{elec}, \epsilon_{fs}, \epsilon_{mp}$, ALWAYS compute $d_0$ first, then choose the right amplifier term based on the actual $d$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **LEACH** powers the famous MIT $\mu$AMPS testbed.
- **Clustering ideas** are used in **smart agriculture** (soil-moisture clusters), **structural health monitoring** (vibration clusters), **precision military surveillance**, and **IoT smart-city deployments** (ZigBee PRO cluster tree).
- Modern **5G/6G cell-free massive MIMO** inherits clustering concepts for user-centric cell-free architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Number of Clusters ($k_{opt}$)

The cornerstone derivation in WSN clustering — every KTU exam expects this. We minimise the total network energy per round with respect to $k$, the number of clusters.

### Step 1: Assumptions
- $N$ sensor nodes are uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region.
- $k$ clusters, each with on average $N/k$ nodes (one CH + $N/k - 1$ members).
- Each CH aggregates data from its $(N/k - 1)$ members into a $k$-bit packet.
- BS is at the centre of the field at distance $d_{toBS}$.

### Step 2: Energy spent by a non-CH (member) node per frame

A member sends a $k$-bit packet to its CH at distance $d_{toCH}$. Members are close to their CH, so $d_{toCH}$ is small; we use the free-space model:

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}$$

The expected squared distance from a member to its CH, assuming uniform distribution of $N/k$ nodes over a region of area $M^2/k$, is:

$$E\!\left[d_{toCH}^{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{k}$$

(derived by integrating $r^{2}$ over a uniform disk and using $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{R} r^{2} \cdot r \,dr\,d\theta / \pi R^{2} = R^{2}/2$, with $R = M/\sqrt{\pi k}$.)

### Step 3: Energy spent by a CH per frame

The CH must (i) receive $N/k - 1$ packets, (ii) aggregate them, and (iii) transmit one fused packet to the BS at distance $d_{toBS}$. The BS is far, so we use the multi-path model:

$$E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{elec}\!\left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) + k \cdot E_{DA}\!\left(\frac{N}{k}\right) + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

### Step 4: Total network energy per round

There are $k$ CHs and $(N - k)$ non-CHs. Multiplying through:

$$E_{total} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{nonCH}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3 and simplifying (assuming $k \ll N$, so $N - k \approx N$):

$$E_{total} = k \cdot \left[k E_{elec}\frac{N}{k} + k E_{DA}\frac{N}{k} + k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}\right] + N \cdot \left[k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2}\right]$$

$$E_{total} = 2N \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k^{2} \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} + N \cdot k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{M^{2}}{k}$$

After algebra (combining like terms):

$$E_{total} = k \cdot \left(2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}\right) + \frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{k}$$

### Step 5: Minimise $E_{total}$ with respect to $k$

Take the derivative $\dfrac{dE_{total}}{dk}$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} - \frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} = 0$$

Solving for $k^{2}$:

$$k^{2} = \frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi \cdot \left(2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}\right)}$$

When the BS is far away, $\epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \gg 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA}$, so this reduces to the famous KTU formula:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi \cdot M \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} \cdot \epsilon_{mp}}}$$

### Step 6: Optimal CH selection probability

Since there are $N$ nodes and $k_{opt}$ clusters, the probability that any node becomes a CH is:

$$P_{opt} = \frac{k_{opt}}{N} = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi \cdot N \cdot M \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} \cdot \epsilon_{mp}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** This derivation appears in nearly every previous year question paper. Memorise Steps 4 and 5 — examiners allocate **3–4 marks** specifically to the differentiation step.

## 3.2 The LEACH Threshold Formula — Derivation of Fair Rotation

LEACH rotates the CH role every round so that no single node is perpetually burdened. The threshold $T(n)$ is the probability that a node that **has not been a CH in the last $1/P$ rounds** becomes a CH in the current round $r$.

**Step 1:** Suppose we want exactly $k = N \cdot P$ CHs per round, and the role rotates every $N/k = 1/P$ rounds.

**Step 2:** Let $G$ be the set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds. In steady state, there are approximately $N - k \cdot r \cdot P = N \cdot (1 - P \cdot r \bmod (1/P))$ such nodes — but LEACH simplifies this.

**Step 3:** The node picks a random number $u \in [0, 1]$. If $u < T(n)$, it declares itself a CH; else it remains a member.

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot \left(r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Sanity check** — Sum of $T(n)$ over all $n \in G$ equals the expected number of CHs:

$$\sum_{n \in G} T(n) = \vert G \vert \cdot \frac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)} \approx k$$

This guarantees **energy-load balance** across the network.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \times 100$ m² field. BS is at the centre. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit, packet size $k = 4000$ bits, $d_{toBS} = 100$ m. Find $k_{opt}$, $P_{opt}$, and total energy per round.

**Step 1:** $d_0 = \sqrt{10 \text{ pJ} / 0.0013 \text{ pJ/m}^4} \approx \sqrt{7692} \approx 87.7$ m. Since $d_{toBS} = 100$ m $> d_0$, we use the **multi-path model** for CH-to-BS, confirmed.

**Step 2:** Compute $k_{opt}$:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100 \cdot 10 \text{ pJ}}{2\pi \cdot 100 \text{ m} \cdot (100 \text{ m})^{2} \cdot 0.0013 \text{ pJ/m}^{4}}}$$

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{1000}{2\pi \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.0013}} = \sqrt{\frac{1000}{81.68}} = \sqrt{12.24} \approx 3.5 \approx 4 \text{ clusters}$$

**Step 3:** $P_{opt} = 4/100 = 0.04$ → **4% probability per node per round**.

**Step 4:** Total energy per round (using the formula from §2.4 row 12):

$$E_{total} = 4 \cdot \left[2 \cdot 100 \cdot 50\text{nJ} + 100 \cdot 5\text{nJ} + 0.0013\text{pJ} \cdot (100)^4\right] \cdot 4000 + \frac{100 \cdot 10\text{pJ} \cdot 10000}{2\pi \cdot 4} \cdot 4000$$

Computing each term in Joules:

- $2 \cdot 100 \cdot 50\text{nJ} = 10 \mu\text{J/bit}$ → $10\text{e-}6 \cdot 4000 = 0.04$ J
- $100 \cdot 5\text{nJ} = 0.5 \mu\text{J/bit}$ → $0.5\text{e-}6 \cdot 4000 = 0.002$ J
- $0.0013\text{e-}12 \cdot 10^{8} = 1.3\text{e-}7$ J/bit → $1.3\text{e-}7 \cdot 4000 = 5.2\text{e-}4$ J
- Sum (per CH) ≈ $0.04 + 0.002 + 0.00052 \approx 0.0425$ J
- Multiplied by 4 CHs: $0.17$ J
- Member energy: $\frac{100 \cdot 10\text{e-}12 \cdot 10000}{2\pi \cdot 4} = \frac{1\text{e-}5}{25.13} \approx 3.98\text{e-}7$ J/bit
- Times 4000 bits: $\approx 1.59\text{e-}3$ J
- **Total ≈ 0.172 J per round**

This quantitative style is exactly what KTU 14-mark questions demand.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Cluster Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical two-tier clustering architecture that KTU examiners expect students to draw or describe in the exam.

```mermaid
graph TD
    BSnode["BASE STATION SINK"]
    CH1["CLUSTER HEAD 1"]
    CH2["CLUSTER HEAD 2"]
    CH3["CLUSTER HEAD 3"]
    CM1A["MEMBER NODE A"]
    CM1B["MEMBER NODE B"]
    CM1C["MEMBER NODE C"]
    CM2A["MEMBER NODE D"]
    CM2B["MEMBER NODE E"]
    CM3A["MEMBER NODE F"]
    CM3B["MEMBER NODE G"]
    CM3C["MEMBER NODE H"]

    BSnode --- CH1
    BSnode --- CH2
    BSnode --- CH3

    CH1 --- CM1A
    CH1 --- CM1B
    CH1 --- CM1C

    CH2 --- CM2A
    CH2 --- CM2B

    CH3 --- CM3A
    CH3 --- CM3B
    CH3 --- CM3C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The three CHs form the **upper tier** (inter-cluster backbone) connected directly to the BS. The eight member nodes form the **lower tier** (intra-cluster) sending data only to their own CH. Member-to-BS direct links do **not** exist — this is the defining property of clustered WSN.

## 4.2 LEACH Protocol Operational Cycle (Setup & Steady-State Phases)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["START OF ROUND R"]
    SetupA["SETUP PHASE - ADVERTISEMENT"]
    SetupB["SETUP PHASE - CLUSTER JOIN"]
    SetupC["SETUP PHASE - SCHEDULE CREATION"]
    SteadyA["STEADY STATE - DATA SENSING"]
    SteadyB["STEADY STATE - TDMA TRANSMISSION TO CH"]
    SteadyC["STEADY STATE - DATA FUSION AT CH"]
    SteadyD["STEADY STATE - CH TRANSMITS TO BS"]
    Decision{"END OF ROUND R ?"}
    NextRound["R = R + 1"]
    Stop["REPEAT FROM SETUP PHASE"]

    Start --> SetupA
    SetupA --> SetupB
    SetupB --> SetupC
    SetupC --> SteadyA
    SteadyA --> SteadyB
    SteadyB --> SteadyC
    SteadyC --> SteadyD
    SteadyD --> Decision
    Decision -- NO --> SteadyA
    Decision -- YES --> NextRound
    NextRound --> Stop
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** LEACH divides **time** into rounds, and each round has two phases: a short **Setup phase** (CH election + cluster formation) and a much longer **Steady-state phase** (data transfer). The ratio is typically ~1:19 to minimise control overhead.

## 4.3 Comparison of Major Clustering Protocols

```mermaid
graph LR
    ProtoLEACH["LEACH"]
    ProtoLEACHC["LEACH C CENTRALISED"]
    ProtoLEACHF["LEACH F FIXED CLUSTERS"]
    ProtoPEGASIS["PEGASIS CHAIN BASED"]
    ProtoHEED["HEED HYBRID"]
    ProtoTEEN["TEEN THRESHOLD"]
    ProtoSEP["SEP HETEROGENEOUS"]
    ProtoEEUC["EEUC UNEQUAL CLUSTERS"]

    ProtoLEACH --> ProtoLEACHC
    ProtoLEACH --> ProtoLEACHF
    ProtoLEACH --> ProtoPEGASIS
    ProtoLEACH --> ProtoHEED
    ProtoLEACH --> ProtoTEEN
    ProtoHEED --> ProtoSEP
    ProtoLEACHC --> ProtoEEUC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** LEACH is the **ancestor** of almost every modern WSN clustering protocol. KTU questions frequently ask students to compare LEACH with PEGASIS, HEED, or TEEN.

## 4.4 LEACH CH Selection — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    NodeStart["NODE N AT ROUND R"]
    CheckCH{"WAS N A CH IN LAST 1 OVER P ROUNDS ?"}
    GenRand["GENERATE RANDOM U IN 0 1"]
    ComputeT["COMPUTE THRESHOLD T N"]
    CompareT{"U LESS THAN T N ?"}
    BecomeCH["NODE N BECOMES CH FOR ROUND R"]
    StayCM["NODE N STAYS CLUSTER MEMBER"]
    Advertise["BROADCAST ADVERTISE MSG"]
    WaitJoin["WAIT FOR JOIN REQUESTS"]
    CreateSched["CREATE TDMA SCHEDULE"]

    NodeStart --> CheckCH
    CheckCH -- YES --> StayCM
    CheckCH -- NO --> GenRand
    GenRand --> ComputeT
    ComputeT --> CompareT
    CompareT -- YES --> BecomeCH
    CompareT -- NO --> StayCM
    BecomeCH --> Advertise
    Advertise --> WaitJoin
    WaitJoin --> CreateSched
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** LEACH's CH selection is **distributed, probabilistic, and self-elected** — there is **no central controller**. This is what makes LEACH scalable to thousands of nodes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECAT634]**
**Define clustering in WSN. Why is it preferred over direct transmission for large networks?**

**Model Answer:**
Clustering is a hierarchical topology-management technique in which sensor nodes are grouped into clusters, each governed by a Cluster Head (CH). The CH aggregates data from cluster members and forwards a single, compressed packet to the Base Station (BS).

It is preferred over direct transmission because:
1. **Energy efficiency** — only CHs do expensive long-haul transmission to the BS, reducing the average energy per bit.
2. **Scalability** — the BS is not flooded with $N$ individual packets but with only $k$ aggregated packets.
3. **Data fusion** — eliminates redundancy, reducing traffic by 80–90% in dense deployments.
4. **Bandwidth reuse** — non-overlapping clusters can transmit concurrently using different CDMA codes.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECAT634]**
**What is the role of a Cluster Head (CH)? Compare TDMA and CDMA usage inside a clustering framework.**

**Model Answer:**
**Role of a CH:**
- Acts as a local coordinator for its cluster.
- Receives sensed data from all cluster members.
- Performs data aggregation/fusion to remove redundancy.
- Forwards the consolidated data to the BS (single or multi-hop).
- Manages intra-cluster communication schedule (TDMA).

**TDMA vs CDMA in Clustering:**

| Aspect | TDMA (Intra-cluster) | CDMA (Inter-cluster) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Purpose | Avoid collision among members of the same cluster | Avoid interference between adjacent clusters |
| Allocation | Time slots per member | Unique spreading code per cluster |
| Benefit | Energy savings (nodes sleep in non-slots) | Concurrent transmission by all CHs |
| Drawback | Latency grows with cluster size | Code management complexity |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECAT634, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) protocol**, including its two operational phases, the CH selection mechanism using the threshold formula, and the steady-state data flow. Discuss how LEACH achieves energy efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimal number of clusters ($k_{opt}$)** that minimises the total energy consumption per round of LEACH. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — LEACH Protocol

**Introduction (1 mark):**
LEACH is a **distributed, self-organising, adaptive clustering protocol** proposed by Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, and Balakrishnan (2000). It is the foundational clustering protocol for homogeneous WSNs.

**Cluster Head Selection (2 marks):**
At the start of each round $r$, every node generates a random number $u \in [0, 1]$. The node becomes a CH if $u < T(n)$, where

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot \left(r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- $P$ = desired CH probability (typically 0.05, i.e., 5%).
- $G$ = set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds.
- This guarantees that every node becomes a CH approximately once every $1/P$ rounds, achieving **rotational energy balance**.

**Setup Phase (1.5 marks):**
1. CHs broadcast an **ADV** message using CSMA MAC.
2. Non-CH nodes choose the CH with the **strongest received signal** (lowest $d_{toCH}$) and reply with a **Join-REQ** message.
3. CHs create a **TDMA schedule** and broadcast it to all members.
4. Members turn off their radio except in their allotted slot (saves energy).

**Steady-State Phase (1.5 marks):**
1. Each member senses data and sends it to its CH during its TDMA slot.
2. The CH **aggregates/fuses** data using signal-processing functions (e.g., averaging, suppression).
3. The CH transmits the aggregated packet to the BS using a **single-hop, high-power** transmission (spread-spectrum if CDMA is used).
4. The phase is longer than setup to amortise the control overhead.

**Energy Efficiency Achievements (1 mark):**
- Only $k = NP$ CHs perform the expensive CH-to-BS transmission.
- TDMA sleeping saves member energy.
- Data fusion compresses traffic.
- Rotational role prevents single-node premature death.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Derivation of $k_{opt}$

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ field.
- $k$ clusters, each with $N/k$ nodes.
- BS is at the centre, distance $d_{toBS}$.
- Homogeneous initial energy.

**Step 2 — Energy per non-CH (1.5 marks):**
Using the free-space model for short intra-cluster distance $d_{toCH}$:

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}, \quad E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per CH (1.5 marks):**
Receives $(N/k - 1)$ packets, aggregates, then sends to BS using the multi-path model:

$$E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{elec}\!\left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) + k \cdot E_{DA}\!\left(\frac{N}{k}\right) + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy (1 mark):**

$$E_{total} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{nonCH} \approx k \cdot \left(2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}\right) + \frac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

**Step 5 — Minimisation (1.5 marks):**
$\dfrac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 0$ gives:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi \cdot M \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} \cdot \epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Step 6 — Concluding statement (0.5 mark):**
The corresponding optimal CH probability is $P_{opt} = k_{opt}/N$. For typical WSN parameters, $k_{opt} \approx 3$–$5\%$ of $N$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECAT634, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **PEGASIS (Power-Efficient Gathering in Sensor Information Systems)** protocol. Compare it with LEACH in terms of energy efficiency, latency, and scalability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **HEED (Hybrid Energy-Efficient Distributed clustering)** protocol. How does it improve upon LEACH's CH selection? Discuss the role of the intra-cluster communication probability $CHPR$ in HEED. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — PEGASIS

**PEGASIS Overview (2 marks):**
PEGASIS, proposed by Lindsey & Raghavendra (2002), is a chain-based protocol that forms a **greedy chain** of all sensor nodes. Each node communicates only with its **nearest neighbour** and takes turns being the **chain leader** that transmits to the BS.

**Chain Formation (1.5 marks):**
- The farthest node from BS starts the chain.
- The next-closest unvisited node is appended to the chain.
- Greedy algorithm repeats until all $N$ nodes are linked.
- Each node has at most 2 neighbours in the chain (degree ≤ 2).

**Data Transmission (2 marks):**
- Token-based, round-robin leadership: in round $i$, node $i \bmod N$ is the leader.
- Each node fuses its own data with the data received from its downstream neighbour (data aggregation at every hop).
- Only the leader sends the final fused packet to the BS.

**Comparison with LEACH (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | LEACH | PEGASIS |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Topology | Multiple clusters | Single chain |
| Aggregation | Once per CH (at CH) | At every hop in the chain |
| Distance amplification | Moderate (CH to BS) | High (multi-hop chain) |
| Latency | Low (parallel clusters) | High (sequential chain) |
| Scalability | Better | Poor for large $N$ |
| Energy per round | Higher | ~2× lower than LEACH (no clustering overhead) |

**Conclusion:** PEGASIS saves energy by reducing the number of transmissions to the BS to **one per round** and by aggressive per-hop fusion, but it suffers from **high latency** due to the sequential chain.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — HEED

**HEED Overview (1.5 marks):**
HEED, proposed by Younis & Fahmy (2004), is a **distributed clustering protocol** that selects CHs based on a hybrid of **residual energy** and **intra-cluster communication cost**. It overcomes LEACH's weakness of selecting CHs purely at random.

**CH Selection in HEED (2.5 marks):**
1. **Initial probability:** $CHPR_i = C_{prob} \cdot \frac{E_{res,i}}{E_{max}}$, where $C_{prob}$ is the initial percentage of CHs, $E_{res,i}$ is the residual energy of node $i$, and $E_{max}$ is the maximum (initial) energy.
2. **Iterative refinement:** In each iteration, node $i$ becomes a **tentative CH** if $CHPR_i \geq 1$. Otherwise, it joins the CH with the **lowest intra-cluster cost** (sum of AMRP — Average Minimum Reachability Power — to all potential members).
3. **Finalisation:** Tentative CHs become permanent CHs when $CHPR_i = 1$ in some iteration; others become members.
4. **Probability floor:** $CHPR_i = \max(CHPR_i, p_{min})$ ensures the protocol terminates in $O(1)$ iterations.

**Improvement over LEACH (2 marks):**

| Aspect | LEACH | HEED |
|--------|-------|------|
| CH criterion | Random (probabilistic) | Residual energy + communication cost |
| Cluster shape | Random | Compact, balanced |
| Energy balance | Probabilistic | Deterministic, based on actual energy |
| Multi-hop support | No (direct CH→BS) | Yes (CHs can route via other CHs) |
| Termination | Not guaranteed | $O(1)$ iterations guaranteed |
| Load distribution | Possible imbalance | Balanced via AMRP |

**Role of CHPR (1 mark):**
$CHPR$ (Cluster Head Probability) directly couples the CH candidacy to the node's residual energy. High-energy nodes are more likely to become CHs, while low-energy nodes are more likely to join, ensuring **fairness and longevity** of the network.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing amplifier models** — many students use free-space ($\epsilon_{fs}$) for CH-to-BS when $d_{toBS} > d_0$. **Always compute $d_0$ first.** Mark deduction: **1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Skipping the expectation $E[d_{toCH}^2] = M^2/(2\pi k)$** — this is a non-trivial step. Skipping it leads to wrong $k_{opt}$. Mark deduction: **1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting the TDMA schedule creation** in LEACH's setup phase — many students omit this. Mark deduction: **0.5–1 mark.**
> 4. **Conflating PEGASIS and LEACH** — PEGASIS has no clusters and no CHs in the LEACH sense; the "leader" rotates but the chain is fixed. Mark deduction: **1 mark.**
> 5. **Writing $T(n) = P$** (forgetting the rotation term) — full marks loss on the CH-selection sub-part.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ✅ Clustering = two-tier hierarchy (members → CH → BS).
- ✅ LEACH = **Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy**; uses **probabilistic CH rotation** via threshold $T(n)$.
- ✅ Two phases: **Setup** (CH election + cluster formation + TDMA schedule) and **Steady-state** (data transfer + fusion + CH→BS).
- ✅ Energy model: $E_{TX} = k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{amp} d^n$ with $n=2$ (free-space) or $n=4$ (multi-path).
- ✅ Threshold distance: $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$.
- ✅ **Optimal clusters:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs}}{2\pi M d_{toBS}^{2} \epsilon_{mp}}}$.
- ✅ **Optimal CH probability:** $P_{opt} = k_{opt}/N \approx 3\%-5\%$.
- ✅ LEACH = distributed, single-hop, homogeneous.
- ✅ LEACH-C = centralised version (BS picks CHs using global info).
- ✅ LEACH-F = fixed clusters (rotates CH role inside a fixed cluster).
- ✅ **PEGASIS** = chain-based, greedy chain, 1 transmission to BS per round, lower energy but higher latency.
- ✅ **HEED** = residual-energy + AMRP based, terminates in $O(1)$ iterations, balanced clusters.
- ✅ **TEEN** = threshold-sensitive, reactive (sends only when sensed value crosses hard/soft threshold).
- ✅ **SEP** = heterogeneous extension of LEACH with normal and advanced nodes.
- ✅ **EEUC** = unequal clustering to mitigate the hot-spot problem.
- ✅ Energy savings of clustering over direct transmission: up to **70–90%** depending on $d_{toBS}$ and $N$.
- ✅ Always state **all assumptions** before any derivation (1 mark easy gain in 14-mark questions).
- ✅ Always mention the **TDMA/CDMA duality** when comparing intra- and inter-cluster communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark derivation questions, structure your answer as **(1) Assumptions → (2) Energy equations → (3) Substitution → (4) Differentiation → (5) Final formula**. Examiners allocate marks proportionally to these five steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Synchronization
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT634 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Synchronization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Synchronization** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) is the process of achieving and maintaining a common notion of time across all sensor nodes in a distributed network, despite the absence of a centralized global clock, the presence of clock drift, propagation delays, and unpredictable wireless channel conditions. It enables sensor nodes to coordinate sensing, communication, data fusion, scheduling, localization, and event ordering with bounded temporal accuracy.

In a WSN, every node *i* maintains its own local hardware clock $C_i(t)$, which is modeled as an affine transformation of true global time $t$:

$$C_i(t) = a_i \cdot t + b_i$$

where $a_i$ is the **skew** (rate deviation) and $b_i$ is the **offset** (time difference at $t = 0$). Time synchronization is the operation of estimating and compensating for the parameters $(a_i, b_i)$ such that all nodes share a common time reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Time Synchronization is a high-weightage topic under Module 4. Students must clearly distinguish between **offset estimation** (single correction) and **skew compensation** (rate correction) for full marks in ESE questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a group of friends spread across Kerala trying to coordinate the exact moment they will all light diyas for Onam. Each friend has a slightly inaccurate wristwatch. Some watches are slow, some fast, and the *amount* of slowness/fastness changes over time (drift).

- **Offset** = how much ahead or behind each watch is right now.
- **Skew** = how fast or slow each watch is *ticking* compared to real time.
- **Drift** = a watch that gains/loses **2 seconds per hour** has a drift — fixed by recalibration.
- **Time Synchronization** = the protocol by which friends exchange messages ("what time does your watch show?") and mathematically adjust their watches to agree within microseconds.

In WSNs, this exchange happens via wireless packets, and the "delay" between sending and receiving is analogous to a phone call echo — engineers must subtract this echo to find the true time gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why WSNs are different from the Internet?** The Internet uses **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** with millisecond accuracy. WSNs demand **microsecond to sub-microsecond** accuracy under severe energy, bandwidth, and computational constraints, making synchronization a fundamentally harder problem.

### 1.3 The Need for Time Synchronization in WSNs

| Application | Why Synchronization is Critical |
|-------------|--------------------------------|
| **Data Fusion / Aggregation** | Correlating events detected by spatially separated sensors |
| **TDMA Scheduling** | Allocating collision-free time slots to nodes |
| **Target Tracking** | Estimating velocity via Doppler/time-of-arrival differences |
| **Power Management (S-MAC, B-MAC)** | Coordinated sleep/wake cycles |
| **Localization (TDoA, ToA)** | Trilateration using propagation time differences |
| **Event Ordering & Logging** | Detecting sequence of alarm triggers |
| **Security** | Authenticating timestamped messages, replay-attack prevention |

### 1.4 Fundamental Challenges Unique to WSNs

- **Energy constraints** — frequent synchronization wastes battery.
- **Limited bandwidth** — control packets compete with data.
- **Ad-hoc deployment** — no fixed infrastructure.
- **Harsh environments** — temperature, humidity cause crystal oscillator drift.
- **Resource-poor hardware** — limited memory, 8/16-bit CPUs.
- **Multi-hop networks** — synchronization error *accumulates* per hop.
- **Unreliable wireless links** — packet loss, asymmetry, interference.
- **No global view** — distributed algorithms only.

### 1.5 Sources of Synchronization Error

Every packet exchange suffers from four cumulative delay components, first identified in the foundational NTP literature and adapted to WSNs by Ganeriwal, Kumar, and Srivastava:

$$\text{Total Delay} = T_{send} + T_{access} + T_{prop} + T_{recv}$$

| Delay Component | Description | Typical Magnitude (WSN) |
|-----------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| $T_{send}$ | Time to construct the packet at the application layer and pass to MAC | **0–100 ms** (highly variable) |
| $T_{access}$ | MAC-layer contention, back-off, channel acquisition (CSMA) | **0–500 ms** (variable) |
| $T_{prop}$ | Physical signal propagation through air | **≈ 1 µs / 300 m** (deterministic) |
| $T_{recv}$ | Reception processing at the receiver MAC/PHY | **0–100 ms** (variable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dominant errors are $T_{send}$, $T_{access}$, and $T_{recv}$ because they are **non-deterministic**. $T_{prop}$ is essentially negligible for short-range WSN radios. Effective protocols aim to **timestamp at the MAC layer** to eliminate $T_{send}$ and most of $T_{access}/T_{recv}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Clock Model — Mathematical Foundation

A sensor node's local clock is governed by a quartz-crystal oscillator whose frequency drifts due to aging, temperature, voltage variation, and manufacturing tolerances. The generalized model is:

$$C_i(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} a_i(\tau)\,d\tau + b_i(t_0)$$

In practice, for short synchronization intervals, $a_i$ is treated as a **constant** (linear model):

$$C_i(t) = a_i \cdot t + b_i$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Goal of Synchronization |
|-----------|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Skew** | $a_i$ | Rate of node clock relative to true time | $a_i \to 1$ |
| **Offset** | $b_i$ | Constant time difference at $t = t_0$ | $b_i \to 0$ |
| **Drift** | $\rho_i = a_i - 1$ | Fractional rate error (ppm) | $\vert \rho_i \vert \to 0$ |
| **Maximum Drift** | $\rho_{max}$ | Worst-case bound from crystal datasheet | Used for guard-bands |

### 2.2 Classification of Time Synchronization Protocols

#### A. By Architecture
1. **Centralized / Master-Slave** — One reference node broadcasts time; slaves correct. (e.g., TPSN, FTSP)
2. **Distributed / Peer-to-Peer** — All nodes equal; consensus-based. (e.g., GTSP, ATS)
3. **Cluster-based / Hierarchical** — Network divided into clusters; intra-cluster + inter-cluster sync. (e.g., CHTS)

#### B. By Mechanism
1. **Sender–Receiver Synchronization** — One-way or two-way message exchange between two nodes.
2. **Receiver–Receiver Synchronization** — A reference broadcast is heard by multiple receivers; they compare reception timestamps.
3. **Sender–Receiver–Receiver (Hybrid)** — Combines both.

#### C. By Operation Mode
1. **Continuous Synchronization** — Always running (high overhead).
2. **On-Demand Synchronization** — Triggered by an event or threshold drift.
3. **Post-facto Synchronization** — Nodes use unsynchronized local clocks during sensing; *after* an interesting event, they exchange timestamps to align. Suited for event-driven WSNs.

#### D. By Scope
1. **Pairwise** — Between two nodes.
2. **Group-wise** — All nodes to a common reference.
3. **Global** — Every node synchronized to all others.

### 2.3 Error Sources Decomposed in a Two-Way Message Exchange

Consider node $A$ (master) and node $B$ (slave) exchanging two messages to estimate offset $\theta$ and propagation delay $d$.

| Time | Event at A | Event at B |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| $T_1$ | A sends request, stamps $T_1$ | — |
| $T_2$ | — | B receives, stamps $T_2$ |
| $T_3$ | — | B sends reply, stamps $T_3$ |
| $T_4$ | A receives reply, stamps $T_4$ | — |

Assuming symmetric propagation delay $d$:

$$T_2 = T_1 + d + \theta$$

$$T_4 = T_3 - d + \theta$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$\theta = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The symmetric-path assumption is the **Achilles heel** of two-way protocols. In reality, uplink and downlink delays differ due to channel asymmetry, mobility, and traffic. Protocols like TPSN attempt to model this with linear regression over multiple exchanges.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Clock model | $C_i(t) = a_i t + b_i$ | $a_i$: dimensionless; $b_i$: seconds |
| 2 | Skew | $a_i = \frac{dC_i(t)}{dt}$ | Unitless ratio |
| 3 | Drift | $\rho_i = a_i - 1$ | ppm ($\times 10^{-6}$) |
| 4 | Offset (single pairwise) | $\theta = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Round-trip delay | $d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Clock reading difference | $\Delta C = a_i(t_2 - t_1) + b_i$ | seconds |
| 7 | Skew compensation | $t_{global} = \frac{C_i(t) - b_i}{a_i}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Accumulated error over *n* hops | $E_n \approx n \cdot \varepsilon_{single-hop}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Maximum clock drift guard-band | $\Delta t_{max} = \rho_{max} \cdot T_{resync}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Skew correction factor | $\hat{a}_i = \frac{N \sum C_i T_{real} - \sum C_i \sum T_{real}}{N \sum C_i^2 - (\sum C_i)^2}$ | Least-squares slope |
| 11 | Velocity of light / RF | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | 1 m / 3.33 ns |
| 12 | Hop count error growth (RBS-like) | $E_{k} \approx E_{k-1} + \sigma_{k}$ | Additive Gaussian model |
| 13 | TPSN re-sync drift accumulation | $E(t) = \int_0^t \vert a_i(\tau) - 1 \vert d\tau$ | Continuous model |
| 14 | Confidence interval for offset | $\theta \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | With $N$ samples |
| 15 | Energy per synchronization packet | $E_{sync} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{tx}$ | Joules |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use bare `|` inside cells. Use `\vert` for mathematical absolute value (e.g., $\vert a_i - 1 \vert$).

### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equation 2 (skew) and Equation 7 (skew compensation)** underpin every long-term WSN deployment (e.g., **agricultural sensor networks** in Kerala's rubber plantations). Without skew correction, drift would exceed hours per day.
- **Equation 4 (offset)** is the heart of NTP, PTP (IEEE 1588), and TPSN — all industrial automation, financial trading, and telecom networks.
- **Equation 8 (multi-hop error growth)** drives the design of hierarchical protocols like **GTSP** that limit hop depth.
- **Equations 11–12** are critical for **acoustic / underwater WSNs** where propagation delay is no longer negligible (≈ 1500 m/s vs. $3\times 10^8$ m/s).
- **Equation 15** is used in **energy-aware synchronization scheduling** (LTS — Lightweight Time Synchronization) which sacrifices accuracy for longevity.

### 2.6 Logical Operational Steps — The "How" Behind a Typical Sync Protocol

1. **Discovery phase** — Find neighbors (beacon exchange).
2. **Hierarchy construction** — Root election, BFS tree (TPSN) or cluster formation (cluster-head protocols).
3. **Pairwise exchange** — Two-way message exchange at MAC layer to minimize non-determinism.
4. **Timestamp processing** — Compute offset $\theta$ and skew $a_i$ via least-squares regression.
5. **Compensation** — Apply correction: $C_{i,new}(t) = a_i C_i(t) + b_i - \theta$.
6. **Resync scheduling** — Trigger next round based on drift guard-band.
7. **Dissemination** — Propagate corrected time down the tree (for hierarchical protocols).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MAC-layer timestamping** is the single biggest source of accuracy improvement. Moving the timestamp from the application layer (where queueing dominates) to the MAC layer (where transmission begins) eliminates up to **hundreds of microseconds** of jitter per packet.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Offset and Propagation Delay from a Two-Way Exchange

**Given:** Nodes A and B with unknown true offset $\theta = C_A(0) - C_B(0)$ and symmetric propagation delay $d$. Timestamps $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ as defined in §2.3.

**Step 1 — Write the relationship for the forward path (A → B):**
The packet is sent at A's time $T_1$, travels for $d$ seconds, and is received at B's time $T_2$. Since B's clock is offset by $\theta$ relative to A:

$$T_2 = T_1 + d + \theta$$

**Step 2 — Write the relationship for the return path (B → A):**
B sends at its time $T_3$, the packet travels for $d$ seconds, and A receives at $T_4$:

$$T_4 = T_3 - d + \theta$$

> Note: The minus sign in $T_4$ accounts for B being *behind* A (if $\theta > 0$); the A's clock reads a value that already includes the offset.

**Step 3 — Add the two equations to eliminate $d$:**

$$T_2 + T_4 = T_1 + T_3 + 2\theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for offset $\theta$:**

$$\theta = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to eliminate $\theta$ and solve for $d$:**

$$T_2 - T_4 = T_1 - T_3 + 2d$$

$$d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}}{2}$$

> Final boxed results:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2} \quad\quad d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Skew and Offset via Linear Least-Squares Regression

**Given:** $N$ pairs of measurements $(C_i^{(k)}, T_{real}^{(k)})$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, where $C_i^{(k)}$ is node $i$'s local clock reading and $T_{real}^{(k)}$ is the corresponding true global time (e.g., from a master beacon).

**Model:** Assume linear relationship $T_{real} = a \cdot C_i + b$ (note: regression of true time on local time for numerical stability).

**Step 1 — Minimize sum of squared errors:**

$$J(a, b) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( T_{real}^{(k)} - a C_i^{(k)} - b \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial a} = -2 \sum_k C_i^{(k)} \left( T_{real}^{(k)} - a C_i^{(k)} - b \right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_k \left( T_{real}^{(k)} - a C_i^{(k)} - b \right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the normal equations:**

$$a \sum_k (C_i^{(k)})^2 + b \sum_k C_i^{(k)} = \sum_k C_i^{(k)} T_{real}^{(k)}$$

$$a \sum_k C_i^{(k)} + b N = \sum_k T_{real}^{(k)}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-form solution (standard linear regression):**

$$\hat{a} = \frac{N \sum_k C_i^{(k)} T_{real}^{(k)} - \sum_k C_i^{(k)} \sum_k T_{real}^{(k)}}{N \sum_k (C_i^{(k)})^2 - \left(\sum_k C_i^{(k)}\right)^2}$$

$$\hat{b} = \frac{\sum_k T_{real}^{(k)} - \hat{a} \sum_k C_i^{(k)}}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Conversion back to the clock-model form:**
The recovered parameters can be used to correct node $i$'s clock via:

$$T_{global} = \frac{C_i - \hat{b}}{\hat{a}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Error Accumulation Across *N* Hops

**Given:** Each pairwise synchronization has independent zero-mean Gaussian error $\varepsilon_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1 — Define the error after hop $k$:**
$e_k = e_{k-1} + \varepsilon_k$ with $e_0 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Mean:**

$$E[e_k] = E[e_{k-1}] + E[\varepsilon_k] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Variance (errors uncorrelated):**

$$\sigma_k^2 = \text{Var}(e_{k-1}) + \text{Var}(\varepsilon_k) = \sigma_{k-1}^2 + \sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Induction yields:**

$$\sigma_k^2 = k \cdot \sigma^2$$

$$\sigma_k = \sigma \sqrt{k}$$

> **Conclusion:** Standard deviation grows as the **square root of hop count**, not linearly. This is why hierarchical protocols (FTSP, GTSP) limit tree depth.

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Drift-Based Re-Sync Interval

**Given:** Crystal oscillator maximum drift specification $\rho_{max}$ (in ppm), tolerable maximum error $E_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum drift rate in seconds per second:**

$$R_{max} = \rho_{max} \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case time error after interval $T$:**

$$E(T) = R_{max} \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the longest tolerable resync interval $T_{resync}$:**

$$T_{resync} = \frac{E_{max}}{R_{max}} = \frac{E_{max}}{\rho_{max} \times 10^{-6}}$$

> **Example:** A **Mica2 mote** uses a **7.37 MHz** crystal with $\rho_{max} = 40$ ppm. To keep error below **1 ms**:

$$T_{resync} = \frac{10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 25 \text{ seconds}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major WSN Time-Sync Protocols

| Protocol | Mechanism | Pairwise Error | Energy Cost | Multi-hop Scalability | Skew Compensation |
|----------|-----------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **NTP** (legacy) | Internet two-way | ≈ **1–10 ms** | High (continuous) | Poor for WSN | Limited |
| **RBS** (Reference Broadcast) | Receiver–receiver | ≈ **1.85 µs** (Berkeley motes) | Medium | Good (broadcast) | Yes (least-squares) |
| **TPSN** (Timing-sync Protocol) | Sender–receiver two-way | ≈ **17.5 µs** | High (per-pair) | Moderate (tree-based) | Partial |
| **FTSP** (Flooding Time Sync) | MAC-timestamp flooding | ≈ **1.48 µs** | Low (flood) | Excellent (linear regression) | Yes (regression) |
| **LTS** (Lightweight Time Sync) | Pairwise + central | ≈ **O(√n) µs** | **Very low** | Excellent (targeted) | Optional |
| **GTSP** (Group Time Sync) | Clustered hierarchical | ≈ **few µs** | Low | Excellent | Yes |
| **DMTS** (Delay Measurement Time Sync) | One-way with delay estimation | ≈ **30–50 µs** | Very low | Moderate | No |
| **PBS** (Pairwise Broadcast) | Pairwise broadcast | ≈ **1 µs** | Low | Good | Yes |
| **ATS** (Asynchronous Time Sync) | Receiver–receiver + regression | ≈ **2 µs** | Very low | Excellent | Yes |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (TPSN-Style)

> A master node A and slave node B perform a two-way exchange. Timestamps (in microseconds, A's clock domain) are: $T_1 = 1000$, $T_2 = 1015$, $T_3 = 1020$, $T_4 = 1040$. Compute offset and delay.

**Step 1 — Forward path elapsed at B:**

$$T_2 - T_1 = 1015 - 1000 = 15 \,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Return path elapsed at A:**

$$T_4 - T_3 = 1040 - 1020 = 20 \,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Apply the offset formula:**

$$\theta = \frac{15 - 20}{2} = -2.5 \,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Apply the delay formula:**

$$d = \frac{15 + 20}{2} = 17.5 \,\mu s$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** B is ahead of A by **2.5 µs**; one-way propagation plus processing delay is **17.5 µs**.

**Step 6 — Compensation at B:** B must subtract 2.5 µs from every future timestamp it generates (or equivalently, set its clock back by 2.5 µs).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure A — Two-Way Message Exchange (TPSN-Style Sequence)

The diagram below shows the chronological sequence of four timestamps in a classic two-way sender–receiver synchronization. Time flows from top to bottom. Each node has its own local time axis; the difference $\theta$ is the unknown offset.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant NodeA as Node A (Master)
    participant NodeB as Node B (Slave)
    participant Channel as Wireless Channel

    Note over NodeA: Local clock CA(t)
    Note over NodeB: Local clock CB(t) = CA(t) - theta

    NodeA->>NodeA: Capture T1 at MAC layer
    NodeA->>Channel: Transmit SYNC_REQ
    Channel-->>NodeB: Signal in flight (delay = d)
    NodeB->>NodeB: Capture T2 at MAC layer
    NodeB->>NodeA: Transmit SYNC_ACK
    Note over NodeB: Captures T3 just before transmit
    NodeA->>NodeA: Capture T4 at MAC layer

    Note over NodeA,NodeB: theta = ((T2 - T1) - (T4 - T3)) / 2
    Note over NodeA,NodeB: d = ((T2 - T1) + (T4 - T3)) / 2
```

### 4.2 Figure B — Receiver–Receiver Synchronization (RBS Concept)

In RBS, a transmitter broadcasts a reference packet. Multiple receivers note their local arrival times, then exchange these timestamps among themselves to compute pairwise phase offsets. The transmitter's role is *eliminated* from the critical path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX[Reference Transmitter] -->|Broadcast Beacon| RX1[Receiver Node 1]
    TX -->|Broadcast Beacon| RX2[Receiver Node 2]
    TX -->|Broadcast Beacon| RX3[Receiver Node 3]
    TX -->|Broadcast Beacon| RX4[Receiver Node 4]

    RX1 -->|Exchange TS via Data Msg| RX2
    RX2 -->|Exchange TS via Data Msg| RX3
    RX3 -->|Exchange TS via Data Msg| RX4

    RX1 -.->|Compute Offset Relative to Ref| OUT1[Offset Matrix 1]
    RX2 -.->|Compute Offset Relative to Ref| OUT2[Offset Matrix 2]
    RX3 -.->|Compute Offset Relative to Ref| OUT3[Offset Matrix 3]
    RX4 -.->|Compute Offset Relative to Ref| OUT4[Offset Matrix 4]
```

### 4.3 Figure C — Hierarchical (Tree-Based) Synchronization Architecture

A representative architecture used by TPSN / FTSP. A root gateway node (connected to GPS or NTP) is the time reference. The network is organized as a BFS tree; each level synchronizes to its parent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[ROOT Gateway<br/>GPS or NTP Source] --> L1A[Level 1 Node A]
    ROOT --> L1B[Level 1 Node B]
    L1A --> L2A[Level 2 Node A1]
    L1A --> L2B[Level 2 Node A2]
    L1B --> L2C[Level 2 Node B1]
    L1B --> L2D[Level 2 Node B2]
    L2A --> L3A[Level 3 Leaf A1a]
    L2A --> L3B[Level 3 Leaf A1b]
    L2B --> L3C[Level 3 Leaf A2a]
    L2C --> L3D[Level 3 Leaf B1a]
    L2D --> L3E[Level 3 Leaf B2a]
    L2D --> L3F[Level 3 Leaf B2b]
```

### 4.4 Figure D — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Time-Sync Stack

This block diagram represents the logical layers involved in a node producing a globally-synchronized timestamp, from the physical radio up to the application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHY["Physical Layer"]
        ANT[Antenna and Radio Front End]
    end

    subgraph MAC["MAC Layer - Timestamp Origin"]
        TXTS[Transmit Timestamp Unit]
        RXTS[Receive Timestamp Unit]
        CCA[Clear Channel Assessment CCA]
    end

    subgraph NWK["Network Layer - Time Sync Engine"]
        TMOD[Time Sync Module Core]
        TREE[Hierarchy and Tree Manager]
        LSQ[Least Squares Skew Estimator]
        COMP[Clock Compensation Unit]
    end

    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        SENS[Sensor Reading Capture]
        TS[Timestamp Annotation]
        EVT[Event Detection]
    end

    ANT --> RXTS
    ANT --> TXTS
    TXTS --> TMOD
    RXTS --> TMOD
    CCA --> TMOD
    TREE --> TMOD
    TMOD --> LSQ
    LSQ --> COMP
    COMP --> TS
    SENS --> TS
    EVT --> TS
```

### 4.5 Figure E — End-to-End Synchronization Lifecycle (FSM)

The lifecycle of a single synchronization round, expressed as a state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Discovery: Periodic Beacon Trigger
    Discovery --> HierarchyBuild: Neighbor List Ready
    HierarchyBuild --> ParentSync: Tree Constructed
    ParentSync --> OffsetCompute: Two Way Exchange Complete
    OffsetCompute --> SkewEstimate: Collect N Samples
    SkewEstimate --> Compensate: Solve Least Squares
    Compensate --> Operating: Apply Correction to Local Clock
    Operating --> Idle: Resync Timer Expires
    Operating --> PostFactoSync: Event Detected
    PostFactoSync --> Operating: After The Fact Alignment Done
```
